regulations.name: General Data Protection Regulation
regulations.authority: European Data Protection Board (EDPB)
regulations.summary: The world's most comprehensive data protection law, governing how organizations collect, store, process, and transfer personal data of individuals in the EU. It applies to any organization worldwide that handles EU resident data, regardless of where the organization is based.

key_requirements.text: Establish and document a lawful basis for every personal data processing activity
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful processing; fines up to €20M or 4% global annual turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Records of Processing Activities (RoPA); legitimate interest assessments; consent records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a comprehensive data inventory mapping all processing activities, data categories, and purposes. 2. Document the legal basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interest) for each processing activity in a Records of Processing Activities (ROPA) register. 3. Perform and retain Legitimate Interest Assessments (LIA) for any processing relying on Article 6(1)(f).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Records of Processing Activities template and completed ROPA for all departments. Compliance team: maintain master register of lawful basis determinations and conduct annual review.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide written confirmation of the lawful basis under which they process data on your behalf and sign Data Processing Addendum (DPA) explicitly stating their processing activities and legal basis.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Records of Processing Activities register, dated Legitimate Interest Assessments, Data Processing Agreements with all vendors, internal policy document citing legal basis for each processing activity.

key_requirements.text: Honour all data subject rights: access (Art.15), erasure (Art.17), portability (Art.20), rectification (Art.16), restriction (Art.18), objection (Art.21)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory authority enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DSR handling procedures; documented response timelines; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal Data Subject Rights request procedure with defined timelines (30 days, extendable by 60 days) and assign ownership to a nominated team (e.g. Data Protection or Customer Service). 2. Create standardized response templates and workflows for each right (access, erasure, portability, rectification, restriction, objection) with approval checkpoints. 3. Implement a tracking system or register to log all requests, response dates, and actions taken.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Data Subject Rights policy and response templates. IT team: document data location and retrieval procedures for access requests and confirm technical capability for data portability export. HR/Finance: identify data held and implement processes for rectification and erasure in relevant systems.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: confirm in DPA their ability to support access, erasure, portability, and restriction requests within defined timelines; provide evidence of their data retrieval and deletion procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published Data Subject Rights policy, sample completed request tracking log with dates and responses, copies of responses sent to data subjects, audit trail of system deletions, data portability export examples in common formats (CSV, XML).

key_requirements.text: Report personal data breaches to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; possible criminal liability in some member states
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach notification log; incident response runbook; DPA submission receipts
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a breach detection and response procedure defining roles, escalation paths, and the 72-hour notification trigger (calculated from discovery of the breach). 2. Document the supervisory authority contact details and required breach notification content (nature, scope, likely consequences, mitigation measures taken). 3. Maintain a Breach Register with entry date, discovery date, notification date, and authority response.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: establish breach detection controls (logs, alerts, monitoring) and provide evidence of time-of-discovery recording procedures. Legal team: confirm supervisory authority contact details and draft breach notification template. Incident response team: create and test escalation workflow with defined decision-makers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: commit contractually to notify your organisation immediately (not exceeding 24 hours) of any breach involving your data and provide a breach notification template.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Breach Response Procedure document, populated Breach Register with discovery dates and notification evidence, copies of breach notifications sent to supervisory authority with transmission receipts, incident logs showing detection timestamps.

key_requirements.text: Notify affected data subjects without undue delay when a breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights and freedoms
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; reputational and legal exposure
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach notification letters; risk assessment records; communication logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Integrate data subject breach notification into your breach response procedure, including a risk assessment step to determine if the breach poses high risk to rights and freedoms. 2. Define communication channels (email, SMS, phone, registered mail) and ensure notifications are sent in plain language, without undue delay. 3. Document the decision rationale (risk assessment outcome) and proof of notification (delivery receipts, bounce-back records).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: develop high-risk assessment criteria and decision template. Communications team: draft clear, plain-language notification templates and identify contact databases. IT team: provide delivery logs and proof of transmission.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide documented evidence of their data subject notification capability if they are responsible for notification; confirm communication channels and timeline commitments in the DPA.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Risk assessment template and completed risk assessments for each breach, copies of notifications sent to data subjects with delivery proof (email read receipts, postal delivery confirmations), template notification text in plain language, dated decision records.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) for public authorities, large-scale systematic monitoring, or large-scale sensitive data processing
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; inability to demonstrate accountability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DPO appointment letter; DPO contract or job description; registration with supervisory authority
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Determine applicability: confirm whether the organisation is a public authority or performs large-scale systematic monitoring or processing of sensitive data; document this assessment. 2. If applicable, appoint a qualified Data Protection Officer (internal or external) and notify the supervisory authority of the appointment within a defined timeframe. 3. Ensure the DPO has adequate resources, independence, and reporting lines directly to senior management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Legal team: conduct applicability assessment and document findings. Executive leadership: formally appoint the DPO (name, contact details) and confirm DPO placement and independence in organizational structure. Finance team: budget and allocate sufficient resources and tools for DPO function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: If DPO is external, execute a service agreement defining scope, independence, access rights, and resource allocation; confirm supervisory authority notification will be handled by the vendor or organisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed applicability assessment memo, DPO appointment letter or contract, evidence of supervisory authority notification (email confirmation or reference number), organizational chart showing DPO reporting lines, DPO contact details on company website or privacy notice.

key_requirements.text: Implement Privacy by Design and Privacy by Default in all systems and processes involving personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory remediation orders by regulators
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy impact review in SDLC; default settings documentation; design decisions log
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Privacy by Design standard requiring Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for new systems or high-risk processing before implementation. 2. Embed privacy requirements into system design specifications: data minimization, encryption, access controls, retention limits, and audit logging. 3. Conduct privacy-focused code reviews and penetration testing; document privacy features in system architecture documentation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Development team: integrate DPIA template into project initiation; provide technical design documents showing privacy controls and encryption implementation. Security team: perform privacy-focused threat modeling and provide test results. Product team: confirm default settings minimize data collection and provide evidence of privacy-first feature design.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide evidence of Privacy by Design in their product/service (security certifications, privacy audit reports, architectural documentation); confirm default privacy settings in their systems; commit to supporting data minimization and retention policies through contractual terms.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed DPIA documents (signed and dated), system architecture diagrams showing encryption and access controls, privacy requirements in design specifications, penetration test reports, code review records, screenshots of default privacy settings, privacy feature documentation.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) before commencing high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; enforcement action; processing suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Completed DPIA templates; evidence of prior consultation with DPA where required
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all processing activities classified as high-risk (e.g., large-scale processing of sensitive data, automated decision-making, systematic monitoring). 2. Designate a Data Protection Officer or compliance lead to conduct a formal DPIA template for each activity, documenting purpose, necessity, risk assessment, and mitigation measures. 3. Review and approve DPIAs before implementation; escalate to supervisory authority if residual risks remain high.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide list of processing activities and risk classification criteria. Data Protection Officer or equivalent: complete DPIA templates and maintain version control. IT team: document technical and organisational measures implemented as risk mitigations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide details of their processing methods, sub-processors, and security controls relevant to your DPIA. Vendors: confirm any data transfers outside the EEA and associated safeguards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPIA templates (one per high-risk activity) with documented risk assessment, mitigation measures, approval sign-off, and date of completion. Supervisory authority consultation records (if applicable). List of processing activities cross-referenced to DPIA coverage.

key_requirements.text: Ensure personal data is not transferred outside the EEA without an adequate safeguard (adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs, or derogation)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €20M or 4% global turnover; injunctions on data flows
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Transfer impact assessments; executed SCCs; adequacy decision references; BCR approvals
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit all data flows to identify recipients and destinations outside the EEA; document the legal basis for each transfer (e.g., adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs). 2. Obtain executed Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) from vendors; confirm alignment with current ECJ case law (Schrems II). 3. Conduct a supplementary transfer impact assessment if using SCCs; implement additional technical controls (encryption, access restrictions) where necessary.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: map all data flows and identify EEA vs. non-EEA destinations; provide transfer documentation. Legal team: review and classify each transfer mechanism; document supplementary safeguards and impact assessments. Compliance lead: maintain a register of all transfers and their legal basis.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All processors and sub-processors outside the EEA: provide executed SCCs or confirmation of BCRs; declare the jurisdiction they operate in and applicable data protection laws. Vendors: confirm implementation of supplementary technical measures (encryption, contractual commitments from their own sub-processors).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Executed Standard Contractual Clauses (signed copies). BCR certification documentation (where applicable). Data transfer impact assessment and supplementary measures log. Register of international transfers (list of vendors, destinations, legal basis, and effective dates).

key_requirements.text: Enter into binding Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all processors handling personal data on your behalf
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; joint liability exposure with processors
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Signed DPA index; processor audit reports; sub-processor approval records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third parties who process personal data on your behalf (processors and joint controllers); classify by criticality and data sensitivity. 2. Use a template DPA or processor addendum that includes mandatory Article 28 clauses (processing instructions, confidentiality, security obligations, sub-processor rules, audit rights). 3. Obtain signed DPAs before data transfer commences; maintain a current inventory and refresh agreements annually or when processing scope changes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: develop or procure GDPR-compliant DPA template and maintain version control. Procurement or vendor management: identify all processors and maintain a current vendor schedule. Compliance lead: track DPA execution dates and expiry/renewal dates; flag missing agreements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: execute the DPA or processor addendum prior to processing; confirm acceptance of all Article 28 mandatory clauses. Vendors: provide a signed list of sub-processors and notify of any changes to sub-processor list within 30 days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Agreements (one per processor) with Article 28 mandatory clauses visible. Processor inventory spreadsheet (vendor name, processing scope, DPA execution date, sub-processors listed). Email confirmations or formal notices of sub-processor changes.

key_requirements.text: Maintain Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) as required by Article 30
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory authority audit findings
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Up-to-date RoPA spreadsheet or register; review date and owner records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all processing activities across the organisation (data collection points, use cases, retention periods, legal basis, recipients, security measures); document in a structured Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) template. 2. Assign ownership by department; establish a review cycle to update RoPA at least annually or when processing scope changes. 3. Ensure RoPA is available and can be produced to the supervisory authority within a reasonable timeframe.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Protection Officer or compliance lead: design and maintain the RoPA template and central register. All departments (IT, HR, Finance, Marketing, etc.): provide processing activity descriptions, retention schedules, and legal basis. IT team: document all security measures and access controls for each processing activity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors and sub-processors: provide descriptions of their processing scope, locations, retention periods, and security measures to enable accurate RoPA documentation. Vendors: confirm any data sharing arrangements that must be disclosed in the RoPA.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) register—a documented list of all processing activities with: processing purpose, legal basis, data categories, recipient categories, retention period, and security measures. Version-controlled RoPA with documented review and update dates. Signed acknowledgment from department heads confirming accuracy of their processing entries.

regulations.penalties: Up to 20 million EUR or 4% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher)

milestones.label: EDPB coordinated enforcement on right of access

regulations.name: Network and Information Security Directive 2
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A landmark EU directive that significantly expands cybersecurity obligations for organizations operating critical infrastructure and essential services. NIS2 broadens the scope of its predecessor to include more sectors and introduces stricter requirements for risk management, incident reporting, and supply chain security.

key_requirements.text: Implement cybersecurity risk management measures: policies, incident handling, supply chain security, network security, encryption, MFA, access control
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; operational suspension orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Cybersecurity policy; risk register; ISO 27001 or equivalent audit; MFA deployment records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT Security team: draft and adopt a comprehensive cybersecurity policy covering risk assessment, incident handling, supply chain vetting, network segmentation, encryption standards, multi-factor authentication, and role-based access control. 2. Legal and IT: create a risk register documenting identified threats, mitigations, and responsible owners reviewed quarterly. 3. IT: implement technical controls (MFA on all critical systems, encryption at rest and in transit, network access logs, vulnerability scanning).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security: risk assessment report with identified vulnerabilities and remediation timelines; IT: MFA deployment checklist and encryption inventory; Legal: signed cybersecurity policy with board approval date; HR: staff access control matrix and termination procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Procurement/Legal: request from all vendors a completed security questionnaire covering encryption, access controls, incident response capability, and audit rights; Cloud providers: provide SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificate; managed security providers: furnish detailed security architecture documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed cybersecurity policy document; risk register with review dates; MFA implementation logs; encryption audit report; vendor security questionnaires with completion dates; SOC 2/ISO 27001 certificates from critical vendors; network segmentation diagrams; access control policy with approval signatures.

key_requirements.text: Report significant incidents to national CSIRT/NCA within 24 hours (early warning) and 72 hours (notification)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; public disclosure requirements
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident log; CSIRT notification receipts; post-incident analysis reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Security Operations and Legal: establish an incident classification matrix and escalation procedure defining what constitutes a 'significant incident' requiring 24-hour and 72-hour reporting thresholds. 2. IT: configure automated alerting to notify the incident response team and legal department when triggering incidents are detected, with timestamped log records. 3. Legal: prepare incident notification templates pre-approved by national CSIRT/NCA and maintain a signed communication protocol with the authority.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security Operations: incident classification policy and escalation procedures; IT: incident detection and logging infrastructure documentation; Legal: pre-drafted notification template; Finance: budget allocation for incident response retainer or resources.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed Security Service Providers (MSSP): provide service level agreement (SLA) confirming detection and alert capability within defined time thresholds; incident response vendors: confirm 24/7 availability and escalation protocols.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident classification policy; incident handling procedure document with board approval; notification template approved by national authority; sample incident logs showing timestamps of detection and escalation; evidence of submission to CSIRT/NCA (email receipts, portal confirmations); incident register with notification dates.

key_requirements.text: Ensure supply chain cybersecurity: assess security practices of direct suppliers and service providers
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory orders to remediate third-party risks
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Supplier security assessment questionnaires; contract security clauses; audit rights
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Procurement/Legal: create and deploy a vendor security assessment questionnaire covering data handling, encryption, access controls, incident response, and audit rights; require completion and board-level sign-off before contract execution. 2. IT: establish a vendor management inventory listing all critical suppliers and their assessed risk level, updated annually or after significant changes. 3. Legal: incorporate security requirements and audit rights clauses into all new and renewal vendor contracts, with specific mention of NIS2 obligations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement: vendor assessment questionnaire template and completion tracking spreadsheet; IT: vendor risk register with assessment scores and review dates; Legal: contract language library with mandatory security clauses; Compliance: annual vendor reassessment schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All direct suppliers: complete security questionnaire with certification of accuracy; Cloud/SaaS providers: provide current SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or equivalent audit report; service providers: confirm security incident reporting obligations in writing and provide proof of cyber liability insurance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed vendor security questionnaires with submission dates; signed vendor risk register; contracts with embedded security and audit clauses; SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certificates; vendor insurance certificates; evidence of annual reassessment (dated questionnaire responses); audit reports from critical suppliers.

key_requirements.text: Management body must receive training in cybersecurity risk management and oversee its implementation
key_requirements.consequence: Personal liability of management; fines for organisation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/management training records; board cybersecurity agenda items; meeting minutes
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR/Compliance: schedule and deliver cybersecurity risk management training to all board members, covering NIS2 obligations, incident response roles, supply chain risks, and oversight responsibilities; maintain attendance records and require annual refresher training. 2. Board Secretary: establish a standing cybersecurity agenda item in quarterly board meetings with documented discussions and decisions. 3. CISO/Chief Security Officer: report directly to board (or board subcommittee) on risk posture, incident trends, and regulatory compliance status at least quarterly.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR: training vendor selection and course schedule; Compliance: training attendance records with sign-off; Board Secretary: board meeting agendas and minutes showing cybersecurity discussions; CISO: quarterly cybersecurity status reports with risk metrics and board presentation decks.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training provider: deliver customized NIS2 and cybersecurity risk management course; confirm attendance certificates issued.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board training certificates with dates and attendee names; board meeting minutes referencing cybersecurity oversight and decisions; CISO quarterly reports presented to board (with meeting minutes confirming receipt); training attendance sign-in sheets; role and responsibility document signed by board members.

key_requirements.text: Register with the national competent authority and provide required organisational information
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; non-compliant operation of essential/important entity
key_requirements.evidence_guide: NCA registration confirmation; entity classification documentation
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal/Compliance: determine organisation's classification as essential entity, important entity, or non-regulated entity under NIS2 and identify the applicable national competent authority (NCA). 2. Compliance: submit formal registration or notification to the NCA via the designated portal or process, providing required organisational information (name, address, sector, essential services provided, contact details). 3. Legal: maintain registration confirmation and update NCA of any material changes (ownership, scope of services, contact persons) within the required timeframe.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal: NIS2 entity classification analysis and determination; Compliance: completed NCA registration form with supporting organisational documents; Finance: organisation registration certificate and sector classification letter; HR: designated contact persons for NCA communications.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not typically applicable for this requirement; however, if outsourcing registration administration, obtain service provider confirmation of submission to NCA.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: NCA registration confirmation letter or portal submission receipt; organisation registration certificate; NIS2 entity classification determination document signed by Legal; proof of submission (email, portal screenshot with timestamp); evidence of any updates filed with NCA; correspondence with NCA on file.

key_requirements.text: Test incident response and business continuity plans regularly through exercises and drills
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; inability to demonstrate resilience capability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Table-top exercise reports; drill schedules; BCP test results; lessons-learned records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT/Business Continuity: develop or update incident response and business continuity plans, document them formally, and assign owners and recovery time objectives (RTOs) for critical systems. 2. Security team: conduct at least two full-scale exercises per year (one incident response tabletop drill, one business continuity failover test), document results, and track remediation of identified gaps. 3. Management: review and approve exercise reports, verify evidence of testing completion, and ensure action items are resolved within defined timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT: incident response plan and business continuity plan documents with version history and approval signatures; Security: exercise schedule and test scenarios; IT Operations: failover test results and recovery time measurements; Compliance: exercise findings report and remediation tracking log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Disaster recovery service providers: confirm failover testing capability and provide test execution certificates; backup vendors: furnish restoration test reports with RTO/RPO validation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response and business continuity plan documents; exercise schedule calendar; tabletop drill agenda, participant list, and findings report; failover test results with system recovery times; photographic or video evidence of drills; remediation action register with closure dates; management sign-off on exercise completion; post-exercise metrics and lessons learned documentation.

regulations.penalties: Up to 10 million EUR or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities

regulations.name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
regulations.authority: European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, ESMA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that establishes uniform requirements for the digital operational resilience of financial entities in the EU. DORA consolidates and upgrades ICT risk requirements previously scattered across multiple sectoral regulations, specifically for banks, insurers, investment firms, and their critical ICT third-party providers.

key_requirements.text: Establish an ICT risk management framework with board-approved policies, risk appetite, and governance structure
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory orders; management personal liability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT risk management framework document; board approval minutes; risk appetite statement
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft ICT Risk Management Framework document defining risk appetite statements, governance roles, and escalation procedures; submit to board for formal approval and sign-off. 2. Establish a governance structure with defined ownership (e.g., Chief Information Security Officer, Risk Committee) and document decision-making authorities. 3. Embed the approved framework into internal policies and ensure annual board review and updates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretariat: obtain board meeting minutes and formal approval resolution. Legal team: draft and maintain framework document including risk appetite statement. IT/Risk team: establish governance structure diagram with roles and responsibilities; maintain register of framework updates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable — this is an internal governance requirement with no direct vendor involvement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved ICT Risk Management Framework document (signed and dated), governance structure chart, board resolution/minutes confirming approval, annual board review records, framework version control log.

key_requirements.text: Classify and report major ICT-related incidents to competent authority within prescribed timelines (initial: 4 hours, intermediate: 72 hours, final: 1 month)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 1% average daily worldwide turnover (banks); supervisory scrutiny
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT incident classification methodology; incident log; NCA submission receipts
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define incident classification criteria (major vs. minor) aligned with DORA thresholds and document the triage process. 2. Establish an automated incident notification workflow with timestamped alerts to competent authority at T+4 hours (initial), T+72 hours (intermediate), and T+30 days (final). 3. Maintain incident log with evidence of reporting timestamps and competent authority acknowledgements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: establish incident classification matrix and triage procedures; configure alerting system with timestamp proof. Legal/Compliance team: identify competent authority contact details and submission method; create incident reporting template. Incident Response team: maintain timestamped incident log with all three notification milestones recorded.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: ICT service providers (if incident involves third-party systems): commit to immediate incident notification to organisation within 1 hour of discovery, including impact assessment.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Incident classification policy document, incident log with timestamped entries, screenshots of incident tickets/alerts, competent authority submission confirmations (with receipt timestamps), correspondence showing authority acknowledgements, incident severity assessments.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Digital Operational Resilience Testing (DORT) annually — basic testing and TLPT for significant entities
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory finding; mandatory remediation; fines
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Annual test plan; penetration test reports; TLPT scoping documentation; remediation tracker
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct annual Digital Operational Resilience Testing: perform basic testing (vulnerability scans, data recovery drills, business continuity exercises) and TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) if classified as significant entity. 2. Engage an independent third party to execute TLPT if required, or conduct basic testing internally with documented scope and findings. 3. Document all test results, identified weaknesses, remediation actions, and evidence of completion in an annual testing report.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: plan and execute annual testing calendar, perform basic resilience tests (backups, failover, incident simulation); maintain detailed test plans and execution logs. Risk/Compliance team: determine if entity is significant per regulatory criteria; commission independent TLPT provider if required. Management: review testing results and approve remediation plans; track closure of identified issues.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Independent TLPT provider (if applicable): provide TLPT execution contract, detailed test plan, rules of engagement, and comprehensive final report with findings, impact assessment, and proof of testing completion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual Digital Operational Resilience Testing Report (signed by management and testing provider), TLPT engagement contract and scope document, test execution logs with timestamps, vulnerability/weakness findings list, remediation action plan with closure evidence, proof of independent provider credentials (if TLPT conducted).

key_requirements.text: Manage ICT third-party risk through rigorous pre-contractual due diligence, contract provisions, and ongoing monitoring
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory action; withdrawal of authorisation; fines
key_requirements.evidence_guide: TPPP (Third-Party Provider Policy); DDQ responses; contract review records; exit plans
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a pre-contractual due diligence process: assess vendor ICT capabilities, security maturity, financial stability, and regulatory compliance using a structured questionnaire and on-site review (for critical vendors). 2. Establish ongoing monitoring controls: quarterly compliance reviews, annual audit rights execution, and real-time security incident reporting channels. 3. Maintain a decision log documenting risk acceptance and mitigation measures for each third-party arrangement.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: develop and apply vendor due diligence questionnaire; maintain scored vendor assessment records. Legal team: review vendor contracts for compliance with DORA requirements before execution. Risk/Compliance team: establish vendor risk register with scoring, classification (critical/important/standard), and monitoring schedule. IT/Security team: execute annual audits/assessments and document findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All ICT third-party providers: complete due diligence questionnaire (security controls, certifications, incident history), provide evidence of regulatory compliance, submit to annual security assessment/audit, commit to immediate notification of material security incidents, provide references or auditor reports on request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed vendor due diligence questionnaires (signed and dated), vendor risk register with assessment scores and classifications, pre-contractual risk assessment memos, vendor audit reports or on-site review records, vendor compliance evidence (ISO 27001, SOC 2, regulatory licenses), vendor risk acceptance documentation, monitoring schedule and execution records.

key_requirements.text: Ensure contractual provisions with ICT third-party providers include: SLAs, audit rights, exit strategies, data portability, incident cooperation
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory contract renegotiation; supervisory orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT contract register; contract review checklist; executed contract clauses
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a Master ICT Vendor Terms document (or template) incorporating mandatory DORA clauses: SLAs (uptime, response times), audit rights (right to inspect, obtain reports), exit strategy (wind-down period, data return), data portability terms, and incident cooperation obligations. 2. Embed these clauses into all new vendor contracts; conduct a gap analysis on existing contracts and execute amendments for critical vendors. 3. Maintain a clause compliance matrix documenting which vendors have each mandatory provision in place.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Master ICT Vendor Terms or contract template with all DORA clauses (SLAs, audit rights, exit, data portability, incident cooperation); maintain clause compliance matrix. Procurement team: ensure all new vendor contracts reference or incorporate the master terms; track implementation status. Contracts team: identify and prioritise existing contracts for amendment.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All ICT third-party providers: accept and execute contracts containing DORA-mandated clauses; confirm in writing acceptance of: defined SLAs, audit rights, exit strategy and data return timelines, data portability requirements, incident notification obligations, and key person/subcontracting restrictions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Master ICT Vendor Terms document (legal-reviewed and approved), executed vendor contracts with embedded/referenced DORA clauses (signed by both parties), clause compliance matrix (listing each vendor and which provisions are in place), amendment agreements for critical existing vendors, vendor written acceptance of mandatory terms, specific SLA agreements with measurable metrics.

key_requirements.text: Maintain an updated register of all ICT third-party arrangements and report concentration risks
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory findings; fines; forced diversification
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT third-party register; critical third-party assessment; concentration risk analysis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain a comprehensive ICT Third-Party Register listing all vendors, service type, criticality classification, contract dates, and concentration risk indicators (e.g., single vendor for core function, geographic concentration). 2. Conduct quarterly reviews to identify and assess concentration risks (dependency on single vendors, service redundancy gaps, geographic clustering). 3. Report concentration risks to senior management and competent authority as required, documenting risk mitigation actions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement/Vendor Management team: establish and maintain ICT Third-Party Register in a controlled system (e.g., database or spreadsheet with access controls); assign unique vendor IDs and categorise by service type and criticality. Risk/Compliance team: perform quarterly concentration risk analysis (identify single points of failure, redundancy gaps, geographic/sectoral clustering); document findings in concentration risk report. Management: review concentration risk reports and approve mitigation strategies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not directly applicable — this is an internal inventory and analysis requirement. Vendors may be asked to provide: confirmation of their geographic locations, data centre redundancy, and any subcontracting arrangements as part of due diligence.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ICT Third-Party Register (current version with timestamp, showing all vendors, services, criticality levels, contract dates), quarterly concentration risk analysis reports, concentration risk assessment methodology document, risk mitigation action plans (for identified high-concentration risks), senior management meeting minutes confirming review and approval, competent authority reporting correspondence (if required).

regulations.penalties: Up to 2% of global annual turnover; periodic penalty payments up to 1% of average daily turnover

milestones.label: Full application date — ICT risk, incident reporting, testing, third-party risk
First round of TLPT (Threat-Led Pen Testing) required

regulations.name: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
regulations.authority: EU AI Office (European Commission)
regulations.summary: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act is the world's first comprehensive AI regulation, entered into force on 1 August 2024. It applies a risk-based approach: prohibited AI practices banned from 2 February 2025; rules for General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models apply from 2 August 2025; high-risk AI system requirements apply from 2 August 2026. The AI Act covers providers, deployers, and importers of AI systems in the EU/EEA regardless of where the provider is established.

key_requirements.text: Identify and cease any use of prohibited AI systems (subliminal manipulation, social scoring, real-time remote biometric ID in public spaces without exception)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €35M or 7% global annual turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: AI system inventory; prohibited AI classification assessment; decommission records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct an inventory audit of all AI systems currently in use across the organisation, mapping their intended purpose and capabilities. 2. Cross-reference each system against the EU AI Act's prohibited list (subliminal manipulation, social scoring, real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces). 3. Document any identified prohibited systems and execute immediate decommissioning or modification plans with signed-off timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: issue a formal notice to all business units requesting a complete AI system inventory with use-case descriptions. Compliance team: maintain a prohibited AI register documenting any systems identified, their risk level, and decommissioning status. IT team: provide system ownership details and technical specifications for each AI application.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All AI vendors and service providers: provide written confirmation that supplied systems do not incorporate prohibited capabilities; submit architectural documentation or third-party certifications confirming compliance with prohibited-system restrictions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed inventory audit report dated and approved by Legal/Compliance; decommissioning logs with timestamps; vendor attestation letters confirming non-use of prohibited techniques; board minutes or management sign-off confirming policy adoption.

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI systems: establish a risk management system and conduct a conformity assessment before market placement or putting into service
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €15M or 3% global annual turnover; market withdrawal orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk management system documentation; conformity assessment report; EU database registration
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Classify all AI systems by risk level (prohibited, high-risk, limited-risk, minimal-risk) using the Act's risk categories and document the classification rationale. 2. For each high-risk system, establish a documented risk management system covering hazard identification, risk evaluation, and mitigation controls across the system lifecycle. 3. Appoint a qualified individual to oversee conformity assessment and ensure pre-deployment sign-off before any high-risk system enters service.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain a risk classification register with justifications for each AI system's category. Legal team: draft a risk management procedure document specific to high-risk AI systems. Quality Assurance team: design and execute a conformity assessment checklist and maintain assessment records. Management: appoint a named responsible individual for high-risk AI oversight with documented authority and accountability.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: High-risk AI vendors: provide a pre-completed risk assessment template, evidence of hazard identification and mitigation measures, and a declaration of conformity or equivalent third-party assessment report; supply any relevant ISO certifications (e.g., ISO 42001) or external audit reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed risk classification register; documented risk management plan per high-risk system; completed conformity assessment reports with sign-off dates; named responsible individual appointed by management (HR record); vendor conformity declarations and assessment evidence filed in a central compliance repository.

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI: implement data governance practices for training, validation, and testing datasets including bias testing
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory withdrawal from market
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Dataset documentation; bias testing reports; data quality assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map and document all datasets used in training, validation, and testing phases of high-risk AI systems, including data provenance, volume, and characteristics. 2. Implement a documented bias testing regime that includes statistical tests for demographic parity, disparate impact, and fairness metrics, with results recorded before model deployment. 3. Establish a data governance policy covering dataset quality checks, bias mitigation procedures, and sign-off requirements before model updates or retraining.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data team: provide dataset inventory with lineage, version control records, and access logs for all training/validation/test datasets. ML/Data Science team: produce documented bias testing reports including methodology, test results, and mitigation actions taken. Compliance team: draft a data governance policy addressing dataset curation, bias testing frequency, and sign-off workflows. Quality team: establish a testing log template and maintain records of all bias assessments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: AI/ML vendors: provide detailed dataset documentation (provenance, demographics, size), evidence of bias testing methodologies applied, copies of bias test results for the supplied model, and confirmation of any known limitations or fairness constraints.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Dataset inventory and lineage documentation; bias testing methodology document and test reports signed by a qualified data scientist; data governance policy signed by Legal/Compliance; dataset version control logs; audit trail of model retraining/updates with associated bias re-testing records.

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI: maintain technical documentation, generate logs automatically, and enable meaningful human oversight
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; market access restrictions; supervisory investigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Technical documentation file; logging configuration; human oversight procedures
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain comprehensive technical documentation for each high-risk AI system covering system purpose, performance specifications, decision logic, and known limitations. 2. Configure automatic logging for all high-risk AI inferences, including inputs, outputs, user identifiers, and timestamps, with secure audit logs retained for audit purposes. 3. Design and document a human oversight procedure that specifies when and how humans review, override, or intervene in AI decisions, with a named individual accountable for oversight implementation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Engineering team: maintain technical documentation in a version-controlled repository with change history; configure logging systems and provide audit log exports demonstrating continuous capture of high-risk AI transactions. ML team: document system architecture, model card, and known limitations in an agreed format. HR/Management: assign named individuals responsible for human oversight of each high-risk system and document their authority and training.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: AI system vendors: deliver comprehensive technical documentation including system purpose, input/output specifications, and known performance limitations; confirm logging capability and provide sample audit logs; describe the human oversight features available in the system and any configuration recommendations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Technical documentation repository with version control history; sample audit logs covering at least 90 days of system operation; human oversight procedure document with named responsible individuals and sign-off; training records for oversight personnel; system architecture diagrams or model cards filed in compliance records.

key_requirements.text: GPAI model providers must comply with transparency obligations, copyright law, and publish training data summaries
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €15M or 3% global turnover; systemic risk obligations for frontier models
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Model technical documentation; training data summary; copyright compliance assessment
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. If providing a general-purpose AI (GPAI) model, compile a summary document describing training data sources, volume, key characteristics, and filtering or processing applied. 2. Conduct a legal review to identify and document training data licensing and copyright status; implement contractual or technical controls to prevent unlicensed training data use. 3. Publish the training data summary in an accessible format (e.g., on your website or in a regulatory repository) and maintain records of publication.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data/ML team: prepare a training data summary document including data sources, volumes, and preprocessing steps; provide evidence of data licensing review (e.g., legal memoranda). Legal team: conduct copyright and IP review of all training datasets and document licensing status and any restrictions. Compliance team: establish a publication process and maintain records of summary publication and updates. Product team: implement technical controls (if applicable) to restrict use of non-licensed data.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: GPAI model providers: deliver a comprehensive training data summary covering sources, volumes, key characteristics, and filtering/processing methods; provide evidence of copyright/IP clearance or licensing for training data; commit to ongoing transparency updates as model versions evolve.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published training data summary document with date of publication and distribution method; legal memorandum confirming copyright and licensing review; evidence of publication (e.g., website screenshot, repository entry with timestamp); records of any updates or revisions to the summary over time.

key_requirements.text: Disclose to users when they are interacting with an AI system unless the context makes it obvious
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €7.5M or 1.5% global turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: AI disclosure notices; chatbot/voice bot disclosure logs; UI design evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all user-facing touchpoints where AI systems make decisions or generate content and assess whether the context makes AI use obvious to the user (e.g., explicitly branded AI chatbot). 2. For systems where AI use is not obvious from context, implement a clear disclosure notice (text, icon, or audio) presented before or at the moment of interaction, with evidence of user acknowledgement where feasible. 3. Document the disclosure approach for each system and maintain logs showing that disclosures were presented to users.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Product/UX team: map all user-facing AI interactions and assess which require explicit disclosure; design and implement disclosure notices (text, pop-ups, or icons) for non-obvious AI use. Legal team: draft disclosure language compliant with Act requirements and review for clarity. Compliance team: create a disclosure register documenting each AI system and its disclosure method. IT team: log all disclosures presented to users with timestamps and user acceptance records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: AI service providers: confirm the visibility and obviousness of AI use in their systems; provide sample screenshots or descriptions of how their systems present themselves to users; where disclosure is required, supply recommended disclosure wording or agree to embed client-provided disclosures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: User-facing AI interaction map with documented assessment of disclosure necessity per system; screenshots or prototypes of disclosure notices; disclosure register signed by Legal/Compliance; sample user logs showing that disclosures were presented; user interface documentation confirming persistent disclosure placement; training records confirming user-facing staff understand disclosure requirements.

regulations.penalties: Up to 35 million EUR or 7% of global annual turnover for prohibited AI violations

milestones.label: All high-risk AI (Annex III) obligations apply

regulations.name: EU Cybersecurity Act
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that grants ENISA a permanent mandate and establishes an EU-wide cybersecurity certification framework for ICT products, services, and processes.

key_requirements.text: Voluntary European cybersecurity certification schemes for ICT products, services, and processes
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify which European cybersecurity certification scheme(s) apply to your ICT products, services, or processes (e.g., EUCC, SOC 2+, sector-specific schemes). 2. Assess your current security posture against the scheme's technical requirements. 3. Engage a notified body or approved assessor to conduct evaluation and issue a certificate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: compile current security certifications and gap analysis report. IT team: document security architecture, controls inventory, and incident response procedures. Compliance team: map products/services to applicable certification schemes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Certification body: provide scheme documentation, assessment criteria, and fee schedule. Third-party vendors: supply existing certificates, security attestations, and conformity declarations relevant to your ICT supply chain.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Valid European cybersecurity certificate issued by a notified body, conformity assessment report, certificate registration confirmation from ENISA database, and gap analysis demonstrating alignment with scheme requirements.

key_requirements.text: Three assurance levels: basic, substantial, and high based on risk profile
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a formal risk assessment to determine whether your ICT product/service requires basic, substantial, or high assurance level. 2. Document risk factors (data sensitivity, user base, criticality, attack likelihood). 3. Select and pursue certification at the appropriate level based on regulatory obligations and business context.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk team: produce risk assessment report with threat model and impact analysis. Compliance team: document rationale for assurance level selection and approval from leadership. Security team: map existing controls to each assurance level's requirements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Certification body: provide assurance level mapping document and risk-based selection guidance. Risk assessment consultants: deliver independent risk rating and assurance level recommendation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Formal risk assessment report signed by risk owner, assurance level justification memo, and certificate identifying the achieved assurance level (basic, substantial, or high).

key_requirements.text: Manufacturers can self-declare conformity at the basic level; independent assessment required for high level
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. For basic-level certification, complete a self-declaration of conformity (SDoC) documenting compliance with scheme requirements without third-party audit. 2. For substantial or high levels, engage an independent notified body to conduct a full conformity assessment. 3. Maintain all supporting evidence and submit appropriate documentation to ENISA.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and sign self-declaration of conformity (for basic level only). IT team: compile technical documentation, test results, and security architecture evidence. Compliance team: prepare submission package and track certificate lifecycle.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Notified body (for substantial/high): conduct independent assessment and issue conformity assessment report. Certification auditors: provide detailed audit findings and remediation guidance if required.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Self-declaration of conformity with technical file (for basic), or notified body conformity assessment report and test results (for substantial/high), and issued certificate from ENISA database.

key_requirements.text: National certification supervisory authorities must be designated by each member state
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify the competent national authority designated by your member state as the cybersecurity certification supervisory body. 2. Establish point of contact and communication channel with the designated authority. 3. Comply with any national registration, notification, or reporting requirements they impose.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: identify designated national supervisory authority and obtain contact details and requirements. Compliance team: establish notification and reporting procedures and assign responsibility for ongoing liaison. IT team: prepare information for any national authority audits or inspections.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: National supervisory authority: provide written guidance on member state-specific requirements, timelines, and documentation standards. Certification body: confirm coordination with national authority and provide attestation of their notified status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Correspondence with national supervisory authority confirming registration/notification, authority's written requirements document, and proof of ongoing compliance (e.g., notification submissions, inspection responses).

key_requirements.text: ENISA maintains a database of all issued, amended, and withdrawn certificates
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Verify that your issued certificate is recorded in the official ENISA cybersecurity certification database immediately upon issuance. 2. Monitor the database to confirm certificate status remains active and accurate. 3. Notify ENISA of any material changes (amendments, suspensions, withdrawals) through your notified body.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: confirm certificate entry in ENISA database upon issuance. Legal team: document certificate details (number, issue date, expiry date, assurance level). IT team: establish process to alert compliance of any changes triggering ENISA notification.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Notified body: submit certificate to ENISA database and provide confirmation of registration. ENISA: acknowledge certificate registration and provide database record reference.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Certificate registration confirmation from ENISA database (screenshot or official export), certificate number and details, and any amendment or withdrawal notices registered in the database.

key_requirements.text: Cybersecurity certification does not replace sector-specific requirements (e.g., NIS2, DORA)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map your organisation to all applicable sector-specific regulatory frameworks (NIS2, DORA, financial sector, energy, healthcare, etc.) in addition to cybersecurity certification. 2. Conduct gap analysis to identify requirements not covered by certification alone. 3. Implement supplementary controls and governance to satisfy all applicable regulations beyond the scope of the chosen certification scheme.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: produce regulatory landscape map identifying NIS2, DORA, and sector-specific obligations. Legal team: document gaps between certification scheme and applicable sector-specific requirements. Security team: develop remediation plan and controls roadmap for non-certification requirements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Regulatory consultants: provide detailed gap analysis between chosen certification and NIS2/DORA requirements. Sector regulators (if applicable): clarify interpretive guidance on interaction between certification and sector-specific mandates.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Regulatory mapping document showing all applicable frameworks, gap analysis report, supplementary controls inventory, sector-specific compliance artefacts (e.g., NIS2 incident response plan, DORA controls framework), and audit trail showing implementation of non-certification requirements.

regulations.penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims

regulations.name: California Consumer Privacy Act (as amended by CPRA)
regulations.authority: California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA)
regulations.summary: California's comprehensive privacy law giving residents broad rights over their personal information. Considered the most stringent U.S. state privacy law and a model for other states.

key_requirements.text: Disclose categories of personal information collected and purposes at or before point of collection
key_requirements.consequence: AG enforcement; $2,500/unintentional or $7,500/intentional violation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy notice; collection point disclosures; consent forms; website audit
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory audit to identify all personal information categories collected across touchpoints (web forms, apps, offline channels). 2. Map each category to its lawful purpose and retention period. 3. Embed disclosure notices in privacy policies, cookie banners, and collection forms with clear, plain-language descriptions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and version-control privacy policy disclosures and collection notices. IT team: map data flows in system architecture documentation showing what categories are collected at each touchpoint. Marketing/Product team: provide list of all data collection points and their intended uses.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: confirm in writing what personal information categories they receive and their stated processing purposes. Marketing vendors: provide documentation of data categories used for targeting, profiling, or analytics.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed privacy policy with dated version history, screenshots of collection point notices with timestamps, data inventory spreadsheet with categories and purposes, collection form source code or wireframes showing disclosure placement.

key_requirements.text: Respond to consumer rights requests (know, delete, opt-out of sale, non-discrimination) within 45 days
key_requirements.consequence: Enforcement by CA AG; private right of action for security breaches
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consumer request logs; response time records; request fulfilment documentation
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented request intake process (web portal, email address, or toll-free number) and log all consumer requests with receipt timestamps. 2. Implement a verification protocol to authenticate the requester's identity and assign unique case identifiers. 3. Create a fulfillment workflow with 45-calendar-day deadline tracking, escalation procedures for complex requests, and documented denial reasons if applicable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: establish written consumer request procedures document and train staff on verification standards. IT team: implement request management system with audit logs, deadline alerts, and fulfillment tracking. Data owners across departments: provide list of systems housing relevant data and estimated retrieval timelines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors and service providers: sign Data Processing Addendum (DPA) with commitment to assist in fulfilling consumer requests within 15 days of notice. Request written confirmation of their data retention schedules and deletion procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Consumer request intake portal or email documentation, signed request procedures manual with effective date, sample request files showing receipt timestamp, verification checklist, and fulfillment documentation, system logs showing 45-day deadline tracking, signed DPAs with all processors.

key_requirements.text: Provide a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" opt-out mechanism
key_requirements.consequence: $7,500 per intentional violation; AG enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Opt-out link on website; opt-out request logs; downstream vendor notification records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and deploy a prominent, persistent opt-out link or button on the website homepage and mobile app that is equally or more conspicuous than other calls-to-action. 2. Configure the backend to flag opt-out preferences in the consumer's account or CRM and communicate this to all authorized vendors. 3. Honor opt-out signals within 15 days and implement quarterly audits to verify no sales or sharing occur post-opt-out.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Web/Product team: implement opt-out button/link with UX testing documentation proving equal or greater visibility. Marketing team: audit vendor data-sharing agreements and provide updated list of all third parties receiving personal information. IT team: deploy preference management system storing opt-out flags and provide monthly reports of requests processed.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data buyers and marketing service providers: sign addendum confirming they will cease receiving personal information for a consumer who has opted out and provide deletion confirmation within 30 days. Request written copies of their data deletion procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Opt-out mechanism screenshot or live link, UX testing report showing button visibility metrics, preference management system schema and audit logs, signed vendor addenda, monthly fulfillment reports showing opt-out requests processed, third-party certification letters confirming deletion of opted-out consumer data.

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Private right of action for breaches; AG enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security risk assessment; NIST CSF or ISO 27001 mapping; pen test reports; incident logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a baseline security risk assessment identifying systems, databases, and networks storing personal information, then document vulnerabilities and remediation priority. 2. Implement administrative, technical, and physical safeguards (encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, multi-factor authentication, audit logging). 3. Establish a vulnerability management program with annual penetration testing, incident response procedures, and documented security training for staff with access to personal information.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: provide current security audit report, penetration test results, and system architecture diagrams showing encryption deployment. Compliance team: document security policies and access control procedures. HR team: provide staff training records for data handling and incident reporting protocols.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and data processors: provide SOC 2 Type II report, ISO 27001 certification, or equivalent third-party security audit confirming encryption, access controls, and vulnerability management. Request certificates of insurance covering data breach liability.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Security risk assessment report with dated completion and sign-off, annual penetration test report, encryption deployment documentation, multi-factor authentication system logs, access control policy and role-based access matrices, staff security training completion records, incident response plan, signed vendor security attestations or SOC 2 reports.

key_requirements.text: Update Privacy Policy at least every 12 months to reflect current practices
key_requirements.consequence: AG enforcement; consumer complaints
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy policy version history; legal review records; publication confirmation
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a privacy policy review schedule tied to the calendar (e.g., Q4 each year) and assign ownership to Legal and Compliance teams. 2. Conduct annual data practice inventory and cross-reference against current policy language to identify gaps or changes. 3. Document all updates (change logs with effective dates, version numbers), distribute to stakeholders, and publish the updated policy on the website.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: maintain privacy policy master document with version control, change logs, and dated signatures approving updates. Compliance team: create annual review checklist comparing policy against current data collection and processing practices. IT team: provide list of new systems, integrations, or service providers added in the past 12 months affecting privacy practices.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors and service providers: notify compliance team of any material changes to their data processing, sub-processor additions, or security practices that may require privacy policy amendments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Privacy policy with version number and effective date, timestamped change log documenting each update and reason, signed authorization for publication, archived copies of previous versions, annual review checklist confirming data practice inventory was completed, communication records showing policy distribution to internal stakeholders and posting on public website.

regulations.penalties: Up to $2,500 per unintentional violation; $7,500 per intentional violation

regulations.name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) - Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing national standards for protecting the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI). Applies to covered entities and business associates.

key_requirements.text: Conduct an accurate and thorough risk analysis of potential risks and vulnerabilities to ePHI
key_requirements.consequence: Civil penalties up to $1.9M per category per year; criminal prosecution
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk analysis report; asset inventory; threat assessment documentation; annual review records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage an external healthcare compliance auditor to conduct a comprehensive ePHI risk assessment covering systems, network infrastructure, workforce access patterns, and third-party integrations. 2. Document all identified vulnerabilities, likelihood ratings, and potential impact on PHI confidentiality, integrity, and availability. 3. Formally review and sign off on the assessment findings with senior management and legal counsel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide network topology diagram, system inventory with data classifications, user access matrices, and current security control documentation. Legal/Compliance team: deliver existing policies, prior audit reports, and list of all systems processing ePHI.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers and cloud vendors: submit completed HIPAA risk assessment questionnaires, vulnerability scan reports, and current SOC 2 Type II or equivalent security certifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed risk analysis report dated and approved by senior management; vulnerability inventory with risk ratings; system inventory with ePHI classifications; management sign-off memo confirming assessment completion.

key_requirements.text: Implement risk management measures to reduce identified risks to a reasonable and appropriate level
key_requirements.consequence: Civil money penalties; mandatory corrective action plan
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk management plan; remediation tracking; control implementation evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a risk remediation roadmap prioritizing high-risk vulnerabilities by impact and feasibility, with assigned owners and remediation deadlines. 2. Implement specific technical, administrative, and physical controls (e.g., encryption, role-based access control, staff training) aligned to each identified risk. 3. Conduct post-implementation testing and document that residual risk levels are reasonable and proportionate to organizational operations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide remediation project plan with timelines, completion reports for control implementations, and test results demonstrating control effectiveness. Security/Compliance team: deliver updated risk register with residual risk assessments and management approval of risk tolerance decisions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: System vendors and integrators: provide remediation timelines, patch deployment confirmations, security control implementation certifications, and evidence of testing completion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed risk remediation roadmap; control implementation project plan with completion dates; post-implementation test reports and sign-offs; updated risk register with documented residual risk acceptance by management.

key_requirements.text: Implement access controls limiting ePHI access to authorized persons and software programs only
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; corrective action plan; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; active directory / IAM audit reports; least-privilege reviews
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all authorized users and software programs requiring ePHI access, mapped to specific data types and systems. 2. Implement technical access controls (role-based access control, database encryption, API authentication) and enforce multi-factor authentication for all ePHI system access. 3. Establish a quarterly access review process requiring managers to certify that access rights remain appropriate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide current access control configuration screenshots, user role definitions, multi-factor authentication implementation evidence, and access review schedules. HR team: supply list of active employees and contractors with associated job functions and required ePHI access levels.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Application and EHR vendors: provide documentation of available access control mechanisms, role-based permission matrices, API authentication capabilities, and multi-factor authentication support.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Role-based access control (RBAC) configuration documentation; multi-factor authentication deployment certificate; quarterly access review certifications signed by managers; user access matrix showing authorized individuals and programs by system.

key_requirements.text: Implement audit controls to record and examine activity on systems containing ePHI
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; inability to detect or investigate breaches
