key_requirements.evidence_guide: Audit log configuration; log retention policy; SIEM alerts; periodic log review records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Configure comprehensive logging on all systems processing ePHI (databases, EHR, cloud platforms, network devices) capturing user identity, timestamp, action performed, and outcome. 2. Establish a centralized log aggregation and monitoring system with automated alerting for unauthorized access attempts, bulk data exports, or privilege escalations. 3. Implement a log retention policy preserving audit records for a minimum of six years and restrict log modification to authorized IT staff only.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide audit logging configuration for all ePHI systems, log aggregation tool specifications, alert rule definitions, and retention policy documentation. Security team: deliver log review procedures, alert triage and investigation protocols, and evidence of monthly log review summaries.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and application vendors: confirm audit logging is enabled and cannot be disabled by non-administrative users, provide log export APIs or scheduled reports, and deliver encryption specifications for logs in transit and at rest.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Audit logging configuration reports for each ePHI system; log retention policy document; automated alert rule definitions and test results; sample monthly log review reports with signatures; evidence of log immutability controls.

key_requirements.text: Notify affected individuals, HHS, and (where applicable) media within required timeframes following a breach of unsecured PHI
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties up to $1.9M per incident category; OCR investigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach assessment log; HHS notification receipts; individual notification records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a breach response plan documenting discovery procedures, notification timelines (individuals within 60 days, HHS without unreasonable delay, and media for breaches affecting 500+ individuals in a jurisdiction), and content requirements. 2. Designate a breach response team with roles for investigation, notification, regulatory coordination, and documentation. 3. Conduct a mock breach scenario annually and update the plan based on findings.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide final breach notification plan, breach log template, and pre-approved notification letter templates. Communications team: supply media contact lists and stakeholder notification procedures. IT team: define breach detection procedures and forensic investigation processes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors managing ePHI: provide incident notification timelines, commit to forensic log preservation, and confirm notification procedures if a breach originates from their systems.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response plan with effective date; breach notification templates approved by legal counsel; breach log showing any incidents, investigation dates, and notification dates; annual mock breach exercise report with findings and remediation; documentation of HHS and media notifications for any breaches that occurred.

key_requirements.text: Provide patients with Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) describing how PHI is used and disclosed
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; complaints to OCR
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Current NPP document; distribution records; website posting evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft or update the Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) documenting all uses and disclosures of PHI (treatment, payment, operations, research, legal), patient rights (access, amendment, accounting of disclosures), and contact information for privacy concerns. 2. Have the NPP reviewed and approved by legal counsel and privacy officer, then distribute in writing to all new patients and make it readily available on websites and in waiting areas. 3. Maintain records documenting that patients have received and acknowledged the NPP at the first encounter.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Privacy team: deliver finalized NPP document aligned with current business practices and HIPAA requirements. Patient Services/Registration team: establish process for distributing NPP at patient intake and maintaining signed acknowledgment forms. Compliance team: conduct annual NPP review to ensure accuracy.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Business associates (EHR vendors, billing services, cloud platforms): confirm that your NPP accurately reflects their use and disclosure of PHI on your behalf, and request written certification of compliance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed and dated Notice of Privacy Practices document; evidence of legal review and approval; copies of NPP posted on website and in physical locations; sample patient acknowledgment forms signed and dated; documentation of annual NPP review completion.

key_requirements.text: Enter into Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with all vendors who access, create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI on your behalf
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; joint liability; OCR enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Executed BAA index; vendor assessment records; BAA review dates
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a comprehensive audit of all third-party vendors and service providers to identify those who handle, store, or transmit PHI in any form.
2. Draft or obtain a standard Business Associate Agreement template that includes required HIPAA safeguards, breach notification obligations, and permitted uses/disclosures.
3. Execute signed BAAs with each identified vendor before they access PHI; maintain a central registry documenting BAA status, effective dates, and renewal schedules.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide executed BAA templates with HIPAA-required clauses and renewal tracking procedures.
IT team: supply vendor inventory listing all systems and platforms that process or store PHI.
Compliance team: maintain and update a BAA registry with execution dates and vendor contact information.
vendor_actions: All PHI vendors: sign and return executed Business Associate Agreement before system access is granted.
Vendors: provide attestation of their own subcontractor BAAs and evidence of HIPAA compliance certifications (if applicable).
Vendors: confirm breach notification procedures and contact details in writing.
compliance_evidence: Executed, signed Business Associate Agreements for all vendors with PHI access.
Vendor inventory spreadsheet with BAA status, execution dates, and effective/expiration dates.
Email or written vendor confirmations of breach notification procedures.
Documentation of vendor risk assessments performed prior to BAA execution.

key_requirements.text: Train all workforce members on HIPAA policies and procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; inability to invoke workforce training defence
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; training materials; assessment scores; annual refresh evidence
how_to_meet: 1. Develop or procure HIPAA-specific training curriculum covering privacy rules, security rules, breach response, and workforce roles; ensure content reflects your organization's policies and workflows.
2. Implement mandatory annual training for all workforce members (employees, contractors, volunteers, trainees) with documented completion and assessment records.
3. Establish a tracking system to monitor training completion, identify gaps, and enforce refresher training; document attendance rosters and test scores.
internal_actions: HR team: maintain training enrollment lists and completion records with dates, attendee names, and job titles.
Compliance team: design or curate HIPAA training content aligned to organization-specific workflows and deliver via learning management system.
IT team: configure access controls to restrict PHI access until required training is certified complete.
vendor_actions: Training vendor (if outsourced): deliver HIPAA curriculum and provide completion certificates with participant names and dates.
Training vendor: supply roster reports and test score documentation for audit and internal tracking purposes.
compliance_evidence: Training completion certificates or transcripts for 100% of workforce members dated within the past 12 months.
Attendance rosters or Learning Management System (LMS) logs showing enrollment, completion dates, and assessment scores.
Training curriculum document outlining HIPAA topics covered, learning objectives, and organization-specific policies.
Records of refresher training completion and a documented policy on training frequency and mandatory participation.

regulations.penalties: Tiered: $100-$50,000 per violation; maximum $1.9 million per violation category per year

milestones.label: HIPAA Security Rule NPRM published — major update
Proposed updated Security Rule compliance deadline

regulations.name: Sarbanes-Oxley Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing enhanced standards for all U.S. publicly traded companies and their auditors, focused on financial reporting integrity and internal controls.

key_requirements.text: CEO and CFO must personally certify accuracy of financial statements (Section 302)
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal penalties up to $5M and 20 years imprisonment for false certifications
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Signed Section 302 certifications; sub-certifications from business unit leaders
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. CEO and CFO jointly review the complete financial statements and supporting schedules for accuracy and completeness before quarter-end. 2. Both executives sign the Section 302 certification statement attesting to the accuracy of financial statements and effectiveness of disclosure controls. 3. Legal team files the signed certification with the SEC as part of the 10-Q or 10-K submission.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CFO: provide draft financial statements with reconciliation schedules 30 days prior to filing deadline. Legal team: prepare the Section 302 certification template and maintain execution evidence. Accounting team: provide management representation letter confirming no material misstatements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: deliver audit clearance letter and management letter of comments prior to certification signing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Section 302 certification statements filed with SEC; copy in company records. Email chain showing both CEO and CFO review and approval. Management representation letter. SEC EDGAR filing receipt confirmation.

key_requirements.text: Assess and report on effectiveness of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) annually (Section 404)
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; restatements; shareholder litigation; delisting risk
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Management assessment report; external auditor attestation; control testing workpapers
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Finance and IT teams conduct a documented assessment of all key controls over financial reporting, including system access, transaction authorization, and reconciliation procedures. 2. Management documents control deficiencies identified and certifies the overall effectiveness of ICFR in the annual Form 10-K. 3. External auditor performs an independent audit of ICFR and issues an attestation report on management's assessment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: map all financial reporting processes and execute ICFR control testing workpapers with documented results. IT team: provide system access logs and change management records for financial systems. Internal Audit: prepare control deficiency log and remediation tracking. Legal: prepare ICFR management certification narrative.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: deliver ICFR attestation report and identified deficiency summary in accordance with PCAOB AS 2201.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ICFR control testing workpapers with evidence of execution and sign-off. Control deficiency register with remediation plans. Management's ICFR assessment statement in Form 10-K. External auditor's ICFR attestation report. Board audit committee approval minutes.

key_requirements.text: Establish and maintain disclosure controls and procedures to ensure timely and accurate reporting
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; restatements; D&O liability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Disclosure committee charters; meeting minutes; controls inventory; gap remediation tracking
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and document disclosure controls and procedures covering financial reporting process, period-end close activities, and external communication protocols. 2. Assign ownership to specific roles (e.g., disclosure committee chair, CFO) and establish a quarterly certification process. 3. Test disclosure procedures quarterly to confirm timeliness and accuracy, documenting results and any remediation actions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Investor Relations: document the disclosure policy and procedures manual, including roles and escalation paths. CFO office: execute quarterly disclosure control certifications with supporting test evidence. Accounting team: maintain period-end close checklist and approval sign-offs. Internal Audit: perform quarterly testing of disclosure timeliness and prepare findings report.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: provide feedback on disclosure control design and effectiveness during audit planning and year-end review.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Disclosure Controls and Procedures policy document with signed acknowledgment from key personnel. Quarterly certification memos signed by CFO/Controller. Test evidence workpapers showing control execution and results. Management's disclosure controls effectiveness statement included in 10-K/10-Q. Audit committee meeting minutes documenting review and approval.

key_requirements.text: Maintain audit committee independence — all members must be independent directors
key_requirements.consequence: Exchange delisting; SEC investigation; investor litigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board composition records; independence declarations; committee charter
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Board governance team conducts independence assessment of all audit committee nominees using SEC and stock exchange independence standards. 2. Document independence analysis for each director covering employment history, family relationships, and material financial interests. 3. Obtain signed attestations from each director confirming their independent status and file documentation with the board.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Corporate Secretary: maintain director questionnaires and independence certification forms. Legal team: prepare written independence analysis for each audit committee member. Board: document independence determinations in meeting minutes and board resolutions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: confirm understanding of audit committee independence status at audit planning meeting.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Director independence certification forms signed by each audit committee member. Legal opinion letter on independence determinations. Board resolution documenting independence approval. Director questionnaires with complete biographical and conflict disclosures. Proxy statement (DEF 14A) disclosing audit committee composition and independence status. Audit committee charter confirming independence requirements.

key_requirements.text: Retain all audit-related records for seven years and prohibit destruction during investigations (Section 802)
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal obstruction charges; evidence spoliation sanctions
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Records retention policy; legal hold procedures; destruction logs; storage certificates
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT and Legal teams establish a document retention policy requiring all audit-related records to be preserved for a minimum of seven years from creation. 2. Implement archive controls in accounting systems and email platforms to prevent deletion of audit workpapers, correspondence, and financial records. 3. Communicate the retention hold protocol to all staff and place a litigation hold on all records immediately upon notification of investigation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure archive policies in email and document management systems preventing deletion for 7 years. Legal team: create and distribute audit record retention policy and investigation hold procedures. Finance: establish audit workpaper repository with access controls. Compliance: maintain destruction certificate log and audit trail of retention compliance.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Document management vendor: deliver system configuration confirmation and audit trail reports showing retention settings. External auditor: confirm receipt and secure storage of their workpapers with signed retention agreement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Document Retention Policy signed by CFO and General Counsel. System configuration screenshots showing 7-year hold settings. Email and file server archive reports. Litigation hold notices with distribution logs. SEC 802 certification attached to audit workpapers. Destruction log showing no deletion during relevant periods.

key_requirements.text: Implement and protect whistleblower protections for employees reporting suspected fraud
key_requirements.consequence: Civil liability; DOL enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Whistleblower policy; hotline records; non-retaliation training; investigation logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a confidential whistleblower hotline (internal or third-party) and publicize contact information to all employees through orientation, intranet, and regular communications. 2. Develop written whistleblower policy prohibiting retaliation and detailing investigation procedures, reporting to audit committee, and remediation protections. 3. Train managers and HR on non-retaliation obligations and document all received reports with investigation outcomes and case closure records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: design and distribute whistleblower protection policy with mandatory acknowledgment forms. Compliance/Internal Audit: implement hotline system and assign case management responsibilities. Legal: prepare retaliation prevention guidance and communicate to management. Audit committee: receive quarterly reports on whistleblower cases and investigation status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hotline vendor: provide service description, case numbering system, and secure report delivery to company. External legal counsel: conduct training for management on retaliation liability and investigation best practices.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Whistleblower protection policy document with employee acknowledgment signatures. Hotline registration and usage reports. Case intake logs with investigation closure documentation. Audit committee meeting minutes documenting whistleblower case reviews. Employee training attendance records and signed acknowledgments. Hotline vendor SAS 70 or SOC 2 compliance report.

regulations.penalties: Up to $5 million in fines and 20 years imprisonment for securities fraud

regulations.name: Executive Order 14028 - Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity
regulations.authority: U.S. Office of Management and Budget (OMB), CISA, NIST
regulations.summary: U.S. presidential executive order mandating sweeping cybersecurity improvements across the federal government, catalyzing Zero Trust architecture, SBOM requirements, and software supply chain security.

key_requirements.text: Federal agencies must adopt Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) following NIST SP 800-207
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a current-state architecture assessment against NIST SP 800-207 ZTA pillars (identity verification, least-privilege access, microsegmentation). 2. Develop a ZTA implementation roadmap with phased milestones for network, application, and data layers. 3. Deploy identity and access management (IAM) controls, network segmentation tools, and continuous monitoring systems aligned to the standard.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide network topology diagrams, IAM system inventory, and access control documentation. Security team: deliver gap analysis report comparing current architecture to NIST SP 800-207 requirements and ZTA transition plan with resource allocation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Infrastructure vendors: provide ZTA-compliant product configuration guides and integration support. Consulting partners: deliver ZTA assessment report and implementation blueprint signed by authorized representatives.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed ZTA implementation roadmap, NIST SP 800-207 gap analysis report, network segmentation configuration files, IAM policy documentation, continuous monitoring dashboards showing enforcement of Zero Trust principles, and implementation completion certificate.

key_requirements.text: Software vendors selling to the federal government must provide a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vendor software onboarding process requiring SBOM submission prior to contract execution. 2. Define SBOM format requirements (SPDX, CycloneDX, or SWID) and detail level (component name, version, license, supplier). 3. Implement a centralized repository for managing and tracking SBOMs across all software procurement contracts.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: establish SBOM requirement in all RFPs and contract templates. Security team: create SBOM validation checklist and assign responsibility for SBOM review and storage.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Software vendors: provide SBOM in required format (SPDX or CycloneDX JSON/XML) prior to deployment, signed attestation of SBOM accuracy, and commitment to update SBOM for patches and updates.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed vendor contracts with SBOM clause, completed SBOMs for all software products in standardized format, vendor attestation letters confirming SBOM accuracy, centralized SBOM inventory registry, and evidence of SBOM review sign-off by security team.

key_requirements.text: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) and encryption for federal systems within 180 days
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Deploy MFA solutions to all federal systems and user accounts, prioritizing privileged accounts and remote access within 60 days. 2. Implement encryption at rest and in transit for all federal data systems using NIST-approved algorithms (AES-256, TLS 1.2+). 3. Establish monitoring and enforcement controls to verify MFA adoption and encryption deployment across all systems by day 180.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: conduct inventory of all systems requiring MFA and encryption, provide implementation timeline, and deploy MFA and encryption technologies. Security team: define MFA policy (authentication factors accepted), encryption standards, and compliance verification procedures with monthly attestation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: MFA/encryption vendors: provide implementation roadmap, integration support, and proof of NIST algorithm compliance. Managed security service providers: deliver monthly attestation reports confirming MFA and encryption deployment status across monitored systems.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed MFA and encryption deployment plan with day-180 deadline, vendor product certifications, configuration audit logs, MFA enrollment reports showing >95% coverage, encryption key management documentation, monthly compliance attestations from IT/security teams, and final completion certificate dated within 180 days.

key_requirements.text: Establish a Cyber Safety Review Board (CSRB) to review significant cyber incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Cyber Safety Review Board (CSRB) through formal charter defining membership (agency heads, CISA, ODNI, other senior officials), governance, and incident review criteria. 2. Define incident significance thresholds (impact scope, data sensitivity, national security relevance) triggering CSRB activation. 3. Develop incident review and reporting procedures including root cause analysis, remediation recommendations, and lessons-learned dissemination.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Executive team: draft and approve CSRB charter defining mission, composition, decision authority, and escalation procedures. HR team: identify and assign senior officials as CSRB members. Security team: develop incident significance criteria and review process documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response consultants: provide template for incident review procedures and facilitate CSRB member training on review methodologies.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed CSRB charter document, list of appointed CSRB members with roles and contact information, incident significance criteria matrix, CSRB meeting minutes from incident reviews, documented root cause analysis findings, remediation tracking logs, and annual CSRB activity summary report.

key_requirements.text: Mandate use of endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions across federal agencies
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct inventory of all endpoints (desktops, laptops, servers, IoT devices) across federal agencies and classify by criticality and operating system. 2. Procure and deploy EDR solutions to all endpoints, prioritizing high-risk and critical systems first. 3. Configure EDR agent settings, enable threat detection and response rules, and integrate with security operations center (SOC) for centralized monitoring.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide comprehensive endpoint inventory, deploy EDR agents, and report deployment completion status. Security/SOC team: configure EDR detection rules aligned to federal threat intelligence, establish EDR alert review procedures, and deliver monthly EDR coverage and threat detection reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: EDR vendors: provide agent deployment tools, configuration guides, and proof of NIST Cybersecurity Framework alignment. SOC service providers: commit to 24/7 EDR alert monitoring and incident response initiation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed EDR procurement contract, endpoint inventory with EDR deployment status per system, EDR agent installation logs, EDR configuration documentation, threat detection rule set, monthly EDR coverage reports (% of endpoints protected), sample alert and response logs, and vendor product certification.

key_requirements.text: Standardize cybersecurity incident playbooks across federal agencies
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit existing incident playbooks across federal agencies and document variations in structure, response procedures, and escalation timelines. 2. Develop a standardized federal incident response playbook template covering detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and communication steps. 3. Deploy standardized playbooks to all agencies via CISA, provide mandatory training, and establish quarterly playbook review cycles.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: collect and document all current incident playbooks, conduct playbook effectiveness assessment, and deploy standardized federal playbook to all responders. HR/Training team: conduct mandatory incident response training for SOC and response personnel, track completion, and schedule annual refresher training.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: CISA: publish standardized incident response playbook template aligned to NIST SP 800-61 and federal threat landscape. Incident response consultants: review and validate standardized playbook effectiveness through tabletop exercises.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Standardized federal incident response playbook document (signed by CISA and participating agencies), current-state playbook inventory showing pre-standardization variations, staff training completion records, tabletop exercise reports validating playbook effectiveness, quarterly playbook review meeting minutes, and signed agency attestations confirming playbook adoption.

regulations.penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors)

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection Law
regulations.authority: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC)
regulations.summary: China's first comprehensive personal information protection law, drawing inspiration from the GDPR while incorporating distinctive Chinese characteristics. Imposes strict cross-border data transfer restrictions and data localization requirements.

key_requirements.text: Obtain separate, specific consent for each personal information processing purpose; consent must be voluntary and unambiguous
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 5% of prior-year revenue; business suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consent records; consent capture mechanisms; withdrawal records; consent management platform
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all personal information processing activities and identify distinct purposes (e.g. account creation, marketing, analytics). 2. Draft purpose-specific consent forms with granular opt-in checkboxes—no pre-ticked boxes or bundled consents. 3. Retain signed consent records with timestamp, purpose, and user identifier for each transaction.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide purpose inventory and consent template review; IT team: configure consent management system to record separate opt-ins per purpose with audit logs; Compliance team: establish consent record retention schedule (minimum 3 years).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Marketing/Analytics vendors: provide contractual confirmation that they will not process personal information beyond stated purposes and will not combine consents across services.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms (digital or paper) showing granular, separate checkboxes per purpose; consent management system audit logs showing date, purpose, and individual identifier; consent template approval email from Legal.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Personal Information Protection Impact Assessments (PIPIAs) before high-risk processing including sensitive data, automated decisions, or cross-border transfers
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; processing orders; suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: PIPIA report templates; completed PIPIA records; review by Person-in-Charge
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify high-risk processing triggers: sensitive data categories (biometric, health, financial), automated decision-making, and cross-border transfers. 2. Commission a formal Personal Information Protection Impact Assessment (PIPIA) document for each high-risk activity, covering data flows, controls, and mitigation measures. 3. Obtain sign-off from the Person-in-Charge and Legal team before processing commences.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: develop PIPIA template aligned with PIPL Article 55 requirements; Risk/Compliance team: conduct and document PIPIA for each identified high-risk processing activity; IT team: map data flows and security controls to support PIPIA evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide their own PIPIA or security assessment report if they will handle sensitive data or make automated decisions on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed PIPIA reports for each high-risk processing activity; sign-off emails from Person-in-Charge and Legal; data flow diagrams; control matrices showing mitigation measures.

key_requirements.text: Designate a Person-in-Charge (PIC) for personal information protection within the organisation
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; regulatory inspection
key_requirements.evidence_guide: PIC appointment letter; PIC responsibilities documentation; reporting structure
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a senior individual (typically from Legal, Compliance, or Chief Data Officer office) as Person-in-Charge (PIC) of personal information protection and document the appointment in writing. 2. Publish the PIC's name, title, and contact details internally and on the company website or privacy notice. 3. Define the PIC's responsibilities in writing: oversight of compliance activities, incident response coordination, and liaison with regulators.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Legal team: issue formal appointment letter for PIC with defined responsibilities and reporting line; Compliance team: document PIC responsibilities and escalation procedures in a written charter; Communications team: publish PIC contact details on company website and internal intranet.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable—this is an internal governance requirement. However, vendors should be notified of the PIC's contact details for handling data protection inquiries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter or board resolution naming the PIC; PIC job description or charter document outlining responsibilities; screenshot of website/intranet showing PIC contact details; organizational chart showing reporting line.

key_requirements.text: For cross-border transfers: obtain prior CAC approval, pass a security assessment, or use standard contract (PIC); restricted transfers of "important data"
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 5% revenue; data flow injunctions; business suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: CAC approval certificates; security assessment report; executed SCCs; transfer records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Before any cross-border transfer of personal information, determine whether the destination country is equivalent to China's data protection level; if not, obtain CAC approval, pass a security assessment, or execute a PIC-approved Standard Contract. 2. For "important data" transfers, apply stricter controls: only proceed if the PIC has explicitly approved the transfer and documented the business justification. 3. Maintain a registry of all cross-border transfers with destination country, data category, transfer mechanism, and approval evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: review destination country equivalence status and select appropriate transfer mechanism (CAC approval, security assessment, or standard contract); Compliance/PIC team: maintain cross-border transfer registry and approval log; IT team: implement technical controls to prevent unauthorized transfers and log all cross-border data flows.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Any recipient of personal information outside China: provide Data Processing Agreement or signed Standard Contract (approved by PIC); confirm their country of operation and data protection regime; submit to CAC security assessment if required by PIC.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-border transfer registry with destination country, data category, and transfer mechanism; CAC approval letter (if applicable); executed Standard Contract or Data Processing Agreement; security assessment report; PIC approval memo documenting business justification for 'important data' transfers.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals and competent authority within prescribed timeline following personal information security incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; regulatory investigation; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident notification log; CAC/local authority submission receipts; individual notification records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a written incident response plan defining the timeline for detection (immediate upon discovery), internal notification (within 24 hours to PIC and legal), and external notification (within prescribed timeline to individuals and CAC/competent authority, typically within 30 days for material breaches). 2. Create a data breach notification template with mandatory content: incident description, data categories affected, likely consequences, and mitigation measures. 3. Implement event logging and monitoring to detect security incidents and trigger the notification workflow automatically.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: deploy monitoring and logging to detect incidents; establish incident severity classification and notification trigger procedures; Legal team: draft notification template and approval workflow; Compliance/PIC team: maintain incident log with detection date, notification date, and evidence of authority notification.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Any data processors or vendors: contractually commit to notifying the organization within 24 hours of discovering a security incident affecting personal information; provide incident reporting procedures and contact details.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response plan with timelines; incident notification template approved by Legal; incident log entries showing detection date, individuals notified, notification date, and content sent; CAC/authority notification letters or emails with timestamps; proof of individual notifications (email receipts or SMS logs).

regulations.penalties: Up to RMB 50 million (approx. USD 7 million) or 5% of prior year's annual turnover

regulations.name: Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados (Brazilian General Data Protection Law)
regulations.authority: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados (ANPD)
regulations.summary: Brazil's comprehensive data protection law, heavily influenced by the GDPR. Applies to any processing of personal data performed in Brazil or directed at individuals in Brazil.

key_requirements.text: Establish a legal basis for each personal data processing activity from the 10 LGPD legal bases
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 2% of Brazil revenues (max R$50M per violation); suspension of processing
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data processing register showing legal basis per activity; legitimate interest assessments
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory audit across all business units to identify each processing activity and map it to one of the 10 LGPD legal bases (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interests, credit protection, health/safety, research, or credit information).
2. Document the legal basis selection rationale for each activity in a Data Processing Register, including data categories, retention periods, and recipients.
3. Review and obtain sign-off from Legal and Business owners on the basis assignments, and update the register quarterly as new processing activities are introduced.
internal_actions: Legal team: prepare a documented legal basis assessment matrix for each processing activity with justification and approval sign-off.
IT team: export a complete data inventory report identifying all systems, databases, and applications that process personal data.
Business unit heads: confirm the business purpose and legal basis for each processing activity owned by their department.
vendor_actions: Third-party vendors/processors: provide written confirmation of the legal basis under which they process any personal data on behalf of the organization, and certify this in a Data Processing Addendum (DPA).
compliance_evidence: Data Processing Register signed by Legal and Business owners, with documented legal basis for each activity.
Legal basis assessment memo issued by Legal counsel detailing the rationale for each of the 10 bases applied.
Signed Data Processing Addendums (DPAs) from all third-party processors confirming legal basis alignment.
Audit trail showing quarterly review and update of the register with date and approver.

key_requirements.text: Designate a Data Protection Officer (Encarregado) and publish their contact details
key_requirements.consequence: ANPD enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DPO appointment record; contact details on company website; ANPD notification
how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally appoint a Data Protection Officer (Encarregado) with the authority and resources to oversee LGPD compliance, and document this appointment in an internal resolution or board decision.
2. Publish the DPO's name, title, email, and phone number on the organization's website privacy page, in privacy notices, and in the ANPD notification (Comunicação de Encarregado).

3. File a formal notification with ANPD via the official register (https://www.gov.br/cidadania/pt-br/acesso-a-informacao/lgpd) within 30 days of appointment, and maintain evidence of the registration.
internal_actions: HR/Legal team: issue a formal appointment letter or board resolution designating the DPO, including their responsibilities and direct reporting line.
Communications team: ensure DPO contact details are published and maintained on the organization's website, privacy center, and all privacy notices.
Compliance team: prepare and submit the ANPD Comunicação de Encarregado form with the DPO's contact information and organization details.
vendor_actions: Designated DPO (internal or external): provide a formal acceptance letter confirming willingness to serve and certifying independence and access to necessary resources.
compliance_evidence: Board resolution or formal appointment letter naming the DPO, dated and signed by authorized leadership.
Screenshot or PDF of website/privacy page showing published DPO contact details.
ANPD registration confirmation (Comprovante de Comunicação) or reference number from the official ANPD registry.
Privacy notice template showing DPO contact information (email, phone) in each document.

key_requirements.text: Notify ANPD and data subjects of security incidents likely to cause significant harm within a reasonable period
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; ANPD orders; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident notification log; ANPD submission records; individual notification evidence
how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident response plan that defines 'significant harm' thresholds (e.g., unauthorized access to sensitive data, exposure of financial or health information), designates a response team, and sets clear notification timelines (notification within a reasonable period, typically 10–30 days).

2. Create an incident log template capturing incident date, description, data categories affected, number of subjects impacted, remediation steps, and harm assessment; ensure IT, Security, and Legal jointly assess whether notification is required.
3. Prepare breach notification templates for both ANPD and data subjects, and conduct a test notification drill at least annually to validate timelines and content accuracy.
internal_actions: Security/IT team: document and maintain an incident register with all detected or reported data breaches, including technical details, containment measures, and root cause analysis.
Legal team: provide written guidance on harm assessment criteria and draft notification templates for ANPD and affected data subjects, ensuring compliance with LGPD Article 34.
Compliance/Risk team: establish and publish the incident escalation policy detailing roles (Chief Information Security Officer, DPO, Legal counsel) and decision timelines for notification.
vendor_actions: Third-party processors and hosting providers: contractually commit to notify the organization of any security incident affecting personal data within 24–48 hours, and provide incident logs and forensic reports upon request.
Cyber insurance provider: confirm coverage terms and notification requirements in the policy documentation.
compliance_evidence: Signed incident response and breach notification policy approved by Legal and Security leadership.
Incident register (log) showing date of discovery, harm assessment, notification date to ANPD and subjects, and reference number for each breach.
ANPD breach notification submission with timestamp and official ANPD acknowledgment (if applicable).
Sample breach notification letter(s) sent to affected data subjects, including date sent and evidence of delivery.
Signed Data Processing Addendums (DPAs) with processors requiring 24–48-hour incident notification.
Minutes of annual breach response drill testing notification timelines and procedures.

regulations.penalties: Up to R$50 million (~USD 10 million) per violation or 2% of Brazilian revenue

regulations.name: Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA) / National Data Management Office (NDMO)
regulations.summary: Saudi Arabia's first comprehensive data protection law establishing rights for individuals and obligations for organizations processing personal data of individuals in Saudi Arabia.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit consent for processing personal data or rely on alternative legal bases
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a consent management system documenting the legal basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, or public task) for each data processing activity. 2. Create consent forms or records evidencing explicit opt-in or reliance on alternative legal bases before data collection. 3. Maintain a register of processing activities (ROPA) mapping each dataset to its documented legal basis.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written analysis of applicable legal bases for each processing activity and draft consent templates. Compliance team: establish and maintain the register of processing activities with legal basis justifications. IT team: configure systems to capture and log consent timestamps and versions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) confirming their processing activities and legal bases under your instruction. Third parties: submit written confirmation of the lawful basis they rely upon for any personal data they handle on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or evidence records with timestamps, finalized ROPA with legal basis column completed, Data Processing Agreements with all active vendors, and legal memo justifying alternative bases (if used).

key_requirements.text: Notify data subjects of processing purposes and their rights
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a standardized privacy notice detailing processing purposes, data categories, retention periods, recipient categories, and individual rights (access, correction, erasure, objection). 2. Integrate delivery of the privacy notice into your data collection touchpoints (registration forms, consent forms, website) before or at the point of collection. 3. Maintain evidence logs confirming when and how each data subject received the notice.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain the master privacy notice document in Arabic and English. Marketing/Communications team: integrate privacy notices into all customer-facing channels and forms. IT team: implement logging of privacy notice delivery and confirmation receipts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors processing personal data: provide signed confirmation they have received and will comply with the privacy notice terms. Third-party platforms (e.g., email, CRM): provide audit trail exports showing delivery timestamps of privacy notices to data subjects.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current master privacy notice document (Arabic/English versions), screenshots or recordings of privacy notice placement on all collection channels, timestamped delivery logs, and signed vendor acknowledgments.

key_requirements.text: Respect individual rights: access, correction, erasure, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal data subject rights request process with defined timelines (typically 30 days) and escalation procedures. 2. Create documented procedures for fulfilling access requests (providing a copy of personal data), correction requests (amending inaccurate data), erasure requests (deleting data where lawful), and objection requests (ceasing processing). 3. Maintain a register of all rights requests received, actions taken, and evidence of fulfillment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document data subject rights procedures and timelines in a formal policy. HR/Operations team: designate a privacy officer or request handler and train them on procedures. IT team: create a ticketing system to log, track, and evidence all rights requests and responses with timestamps.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide written commitment to fulfill data subject rights requests within 10 business days and cooperate fully with your responses to individuals. Cloud/storage providers: confirm they can retrieve, export, or delete personal data upon instruction within agreed timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published data subject rights policy, populated rights request register showing request date, type, response date, and action taken, sample completed requests with data exports or correction evidence, and signed vendor commitment letters.

key_requirements.text: Report data breaches to SDAIA and affected individuals within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident response plan defining breach discovery procedures, impact assessment steps, and notification workflow to SDAIA and affected individuals. 2. Configure system monitoring and logging to detect unauthorized access or data anomalies; document detection and investigation timelines. 3. Draft and pre-approve breach notification templates in Arabic meeting SDAIA requirements, and maintain a breach incident log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: configure audit logs, intrusion detection systems, and data loss prevention tools with alerting. Legal/Compliance team: draft breach notification templates and flowcharts, define escalation contacts. Finance team: budget resources for incident investigation and external forensics if needed.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting/cloud providers: commit to detecting and reporting suspected breaches to you within 24 hours with full technical details. Security/backup vendors: provide evidence of monitoring and incident response procedures; confirm breach notification obligations in DPAs.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Incident response plan with timelines and SDAIA contact details, configured monitoring/logging dashboards with sample alert exports, pre-approved breach notification templates, completed breach incident log entries with investigation reports and SDAIA notification receipts.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Transfer Impact Assessments before cross-border transfers
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all cross-border data transfers (personal data leaving Saudi Arabia) and document the destination country, data categories, and recipients. 2. Conduct a Data Transfer Impact Assessment (DTIA) for each destination evaluating its data protection laws, government access risks, and safeguards (e.g., adequacy decisions, contractual clauses). 3. Document approved transfer mechanisms (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules) and obtain legal sign-off before transfers commence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain a cross-border data transfer inventory with destination countries and justification. Legal team: conduct and document DTIAs for each destination, conclude on transfer mechanism, and provide formal approval memo. IT/Privacy team: map data flows and systems to identify where transfers occur.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors/subprocessors in other countries: provide their country location, data processing agreement with transfer clauses (SCCs or equivalent), and written confirmation of their jurisdiction and data protection compliance status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed cross-border transfer inventory spreadsheet, signed DTIA reports for each destination, executed Data Processing Agreements with transfer mechanism clauses (SCCs), legal approval memos, and evidence of contractual safeguards in place.

key_requirements.text: Store data relating to Saudi citizens or residents within Saudi Arabia (data localization)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit all data storage systems and document where personal data relating to Saudi citizens/residents is currently stored (cloud regions, server locations, backup sites). 2. For any data stored outside Saudi Arabia, establish a plan to migrate it to a Saudi-approved data center or secure a formal exemption/approval from SDAIA. 3. Implement technical controls (encryption keys, access restrictions, jurisdiction tags) ensuring Saudi resident data cannot be accessed from or transferred outside Saudi Arabia without explicit authorization.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Infrastructure team: provide complete inventory of storage locations, cloud regions, and backup servers with data residency classifications. Compliance team: contact SDAIA if seeking exemptions or clarification; document approval. Legal team: update DPAs and internal policies to mandate Saudi data residency.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: confirm in writing which data centers are located in Saudi Arabia and offer SLAs guaranteeing Saudi residency. Provide technical documentation showing data replication and backup policies comply with localization. Subprocessors: certify their Saudi Arabia data center locations and restrictions on data egress.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data residency audit report mapping each system to storage location, cloud provider SLA extracts specifying Saudi regions, executed vendor agreements with data localization clauses, SDAIA exemption letter (if applicable), and evidence of technical controls (encryption policies, access logs, jurisdiction tags in systems).

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all high-risk processing activities (cross-border transfers, automated decision-making, large-scale sensitive data handling) and document risk classifications in a processing register.
2. Appoint a qualified individual as Data Protection Officer with documented authority, responsibility scope, and direct reporting line to senior management.
3. Establish a DPO charter defining duties, independence, resource allocation, and escalation procedures for data protection issues.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: provide written high-risk processing activity inventory with risk assessment justifications.
HR team: submit DPO appointment letter, CV verification, and role descriptor confirming independence and seniority.
Finance team: confirm DPO budget allocation and dedicated resource hours in current fiscal plan.
vendor_actions: External DPO provider (if outsourced): submit service agreement specifying confidentiality obligations, indemnification, and compliance with PDPL Article 6 independence requirements.
Data processors and joint controllers: acknowledge DPO contact details and commit to cooperating with DPO requests in processor agreements.
compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment memo or employment contract dated and board-approved.
Data Protection Officer role charter or job specification document.
High-risk processing activities register with risk ratings and DPO approval sign-offs.
Proof of DPO training or qualifications (certification, experience documentation).
Organizational chart showing DPO's direct reporting line to board/senior management.
Data processing agreement amendments or acknowledgments from third parties confirming DPO liaison arrangement.

regulations.penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations

regulations.name: Protection of Privacy Law and Privacy Protection Regulations (Data Security)
regulations.authority: Privacy Protection Authority (PPA)
regulations.summary: Israel's foundational data protection framework combining the Protection of Privacy Law (1981) with the comprehensive 2017 Data Security Regulations, adding prescriptive security requirements including database classification, security officer requirements, risk assessments, and breach notification obligations.

key_requirements.text: Register databases containing personal information with the Registrar of Databases (PPA)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory across all business units to identify all databases containing personal information. 2. Complete the PPA registration form with database details (purpose, data categories, retention periods, security measures). 3. Submit the completed registration to the Israeli Privacy Protection Authority and maintain proof of registration.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide data inventory spreadsheet listing all databases with personal data, ownership, and purposes. IT team: confirm database locations, types, and data subject categories. Compliance team: prepare and submit registration documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: confirm in writing which databases they host or process on behalf of the organisation and provide technical specifications required for PPA registration.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: PPA registration certificate or confirmation letter, signed data inventory spreadsheet, copies of submitted registration forms, internal sign-off memo from Legal/Compliance teams.

key_requirements.text: Classify data sensitivity level and implement proportionate security controls
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Classify each registered database by sensitivity level (low, medium, high) based on data type, volume, and risk of harm to data subjects. 2. Document classification rationale and map each level to specific technical controls (encryption, access restrictions, monitoring). 3. Implement controls and document in a Data Classification and Security Controls Matrix signed by IT and Legal leads.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide technical assessment of current controls for each database and gaps analysis. Security team: develop data classification framework and control mapping. Legal team: advise on regulatory sensitivity and data subject risk factors.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: supply capability documentation confirming available encryption, isolation, and monitoring features for each classification level.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Classification Matrix, risk assessment worksheets, security control implementation checklists, configuration documentation for encryption and access controls, audit logs showing control activation dates.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Database Security Officer for medium and high-security databases
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all medium and high-security databases from the classification exercise. 2. Formally appoint a Database Security Officer (DSO) in writing, confirming their authority, responsibilities, and reporting line to senior management. 3. Ensure DSO has documented accountability for access reviews, security incident response, and compliance with PPA requirements for assigned databases.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: prepare appointment letter and confirm DSO role in organizational chart. IT/Security team: document DSO responsibilities matrix. Legal team: confirm DSO authority and reporting obligations to board/management.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External security consultants (if appointed as DSO): sign a service agreement defining scope, confidentiality obligations, and reporting procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter naming the DSO, documented DSO responsibilities and authority memo, organizational chart showing reporting line, board resolution or management directive confirming appointment.

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual internal audits and risk assessments
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Schedule annual internal audits of all databases covering access controls, encryption, incident logs, and compliance with registered security measures. 2. Conduct a separate annual risk assessment identifying threats, vulnerabilities, and likelihood of unauthorized disclosure. 3. Document findings in audit and risk assessment reports with remediation plans and management sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Internal Audit team: conduct annual database security audit covering control operation and configuration. Security team: perform annual risk assessment with threat modeling and residual risk scoring. Management: review and approve findings and remediation timelines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors or penetration testers (if engaged): deliver audit report covering assigned systems, vulnerability findings, and remediation recommendations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual audit reports with scope and methodology, risk assessment documents with risk ratings and mitigation actions, management sign-off on findings, remediation tracking log with completion dates.

key_requirements.text: Report severe security incidents to PPA and affected individuals within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident response procedure defining 'severe security incident' (unauthorized access, data breach, loss of confidentiality/integrity). 2. Create a 72-hour notification workflow: detect/confirm incident, assess severity, notify PPA and affected individuals within 72 hours, document all steps. 3. Maintain an incident log with notification dates, PPA reference numbers, and communications sent.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: establish incident detection and escalation procedures with timing requirements. Legal team: draft notification templates for PPA and individuals. Compliance team: maintain incident register and ensure 72-hour deadline tracking.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response providers or forensics firms (if engaged): commit contractually to support severity assessment and notification timeline within the 72-hour window.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response policy, incident log entries with timestamps of detection/confirmation/notification, PPA notification letters and acknowledgment receipts, individual notification emails with proof of delivery, incident investigation reports.

key_requirements.text: Implement access controls, logging, and user authorization procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement role-based access control (RBAC) with documented user-to-role mappings and segregation of duties; configure system access to enforce least privilege. 2. Enable and retain comprehensive logging of all data access and modifications with user identity, timestamp, action, and data accessed. 3. Establish a quarterly user access review process with sign-off confirming access remains necessary and appropriate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: implement RBAC configuration, document role definitions and approve user access assignments. Audit/Compliance team: design and execute quarterly user access reviews with management attestation. IT team: configure logging systems and validate log completeness.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Identity and access management (IAM) or database vendors: confirm RBAC and logging capabilities meet requirements, provide configuration guides and ensure logs are retained per data retention policy.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: RBAC policy document with role definitions, current user-role assignment matrix, signed quarterly access review reports, system configuration documentation showing RBAC and logging activation, sample access logs with audit trails, segregation of duties matrix.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, correction, and deletion of personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented data subject request procedure specifying timelines (typically 30 days), request channels (email, portal, phone), and verification steps to confirm identity before releasing personal data.
2. Implement a technical system or manual process to locate, extract, correct, and securely delete personal information across all systems upon validated request.
3. Maintain a request log recording each subject access, correction, and deletion request with dates, requestor identity, data processed, and completion confirmation.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve data subject rights request policy and template letters for access/correction/deletion responses.
IT team: document all systems storing personal data, data retention schedules, and technical capabilities for data export and deletion (including backups and archives).
Operations team: establish request intake, verification, and fulfillment workflow with assigned owners and SLAs.
vendor_actions: All data processors and service providers: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPA) confirming their obligation to facilitate data subject rights requests within the same 30-day timeline.
Cloud and SaaS vendors: provide technical documentation or API specifications demonstrating ability to export, correct, and delete personal data upon instruction.
compliance_evidence: Signed data subject rights policy and procedure document.
Data subject request log (minimum 12 months) showing request type, date received, date completed, and confirmation of action taken.
Sample completed requests with supporting correspondence (access reports, correction confirmations, deletion certificates).
System inventory documenting where personal data is stored and deletion/export capabilities.
Signed Data Processing Agreements with all processors confirming rights fulfillment obligations.

key_requirements.text: Restrict transfer of data to countries without adequate protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Maintain an inventory of all personal data transfers (including to cloud providers, subsidiaries, and service providers) documenting recipient country, data category, and legal basis (adequacy decision, standard contractual clauses, or binding corporate rules).

2. For transfers to countries without adequate protection (outside EU/EEA and jurisdictions recognized by Israeli authority), execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent legally enforceable safeguards approved by relevant regulators.
3. Conduct and document transfer impact assessments for high-risk jurisdictions, evaluating surveillance laws, government access, and technical security, with remedial measures recorded.
internal_actions: Legal team: identify all countries where personal data is transferred and verify adequacy status; prepare or obtain executed Standard Contractual Clauses and supplementary measures documentation.
Data Protection Officer / Compliance: maintain a data transfer registry with recipient country, data categories, legal basis, and renewal dates for all agreements.
IT and Security team: document technical safeguards (encryption, pseudonymization, access controls) used to mitigate risks in transfers to non-adequate jurisdictions.
vendor_actions: All third-party vendors and cloud providers receiving personal data: provide signed Data Processing Agreements incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses appropriate for their location and confirm no onward transfers without prior written consent.

Vendors in non-adequate jurisdictions: provide certification of compliance with SCCs, evidence of sub-processor agreements with equivalent clauses, and written confirmation of data location and access restrictions.
compliance_evidence: Data transfer inventory/register listing all recipients, countries, data categories, transfer mechanisms (adequacy decisions, SCCs, BCRs), and effective dates.
Executed and dated Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent safeguard agreements signed by all receiving parties.
Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) for non-adequate jurisdictions documenting legal analysis and residual risks with documented remediation measures.
Data Processing Agreements with all vendors confirming SCCs or other approved mechanisms and prohibiting onward transfers.
Evidence of regular review and update of transfer mechanisms (e.g., Board minutes or compliance reports documenting annual reassessment).

regulations.penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages

regulations.name: Israel National Cyber Directorate Cyber Defense Directive
regulations.authority: Israel National Cyber Directorate (INCD)
regulations.summary: Framework issued by the Israel National Cyber Directorate establishing binding cybersecurity requirements for critical infrastructure organizations across all sectors including energy, water, finance, health, transportation, and communications.

key_requirements.text: Annual cyber risk assessment and security program update
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage an internal risk assessment team or external assessor to conduct a comprehensive cyber risk assessment covering all critical assets, threats, and vulnerabilities annually. 2. Document findings in a formal Risk Assessment Report and use results to update the organization's Information Security Program with new controls, policies, and remediation timelines. 3. Obtain board or senior management sign-off on the updated program and resource allocation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk & Compliance team: provide previous year's risk assessment and current asset inventory; IT team: supply system architecture diagrams, vulnerability scan reports, and change logs; Finance team: confirm budget allocation for remediation activities.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Security assessor or consultant: deliver signed Risk Assessment Report (ISO 27001/NIST aligned); any managed security service providers: supply annual threat intelligence summaries and vulnerability intelligence relevant to the organization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual Risk Assessment Report with executive summary and board approval minutes; updated Information Security Program document with signature and date; evidence of remediation tracking and resource allocation.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) with defined authorities
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Issue a formal Board resolution or Executive appointment letter naming the CISO role, specifying direct reporting line to CEO or Board committee, and defining explicit authorities (budget control, policy override, incident escalation rights). 2.
