Document the CISO's role, responsibilities, and decision-making scope in a signed Charter or Terms of Reference. 3. Announce the appointment internally and ensure all departments acknowledge the CISO's authority in writing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed appointment letter and employment contract; Legal team: draft and approve CISO Charter defining authorities and reporting structure; Board/Executive office: issue formal resolution or board minutes documenting the appointment and delegation of authority.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External governance consultant (if used): provide recommendations on CISO role scope and authority structure aligned with INCD expectations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or signed Executive appointment letter; CISO Charter or Terms of Reference document; organizational chart showing CISO reporting line to Board/CEO; signed acknowledgment from department heads confirming CISO authority.

key_requirements.text: Implement network segmentation between operational technology (OT) and IT systems
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a network discovery exercise to identify all IT and OT systems, document data flows, and classify systems by function and criticality. 2. Design and implement network segmentation using firewalls, VLANs, or air-gapped networks to logically (or physically) isolate OT from IT infrastructure, with defined ingress/egress rules. 3. Test segmentation by attempting cross-segment communication and document that unauthorized traffic is blocked; perform quarterly validation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Network team: provide current network topology, system inventory, and OT asset list; Security team: document segmentation requirements and access control rules; IT team: implement firewall rules and VLAN configurations with audit logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Network integrator or security consultant: deliver network segmentation design document with justified isolation boundaries; firewall vendor or managed service provider: supply firewall rule documentation and quarterly verification reports confirming blocks.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Network segmentation design document and architecture diagram; firewall rule sets and access control lists (ACLs); penetration test or segmentation validation report showing isolated OT/IT traffic; quarterly firewall audit logs confirming denied cross-segment attempts.

key_requirements.text: Report significant cybersecurity incidents to INCD within 24 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define a 'significant incident' threshold in the organization's Incident Response Plan (e.g., data breach, critical system compromise, ransomware) and establish a dedicated incident response team. 2. Implement a notification procedure that requires the CISO or Incident Commander to formally notify the Israel National Cyber Directorate within 24 hours of incident discovery using the official INCD reporting channel. 3. Maintain a signed incident log with timestamp of discovery, notification time, INCD reference number, and evidence of submission.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/Incident Response team: create Incident Response Plan with incident classification criteria and 24-hour notification trigger; Legal team: prepare incident notification template compliant with INCD requirements; CISO office: establish secure communication channel (phone, encrypted email) to INCD.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response consultant or forensics firm (if engaged): include in Incident Response Plan and confirm 24-hour availability; managed security service provider: commit to incident alert escalation within 2 hours of detection.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Incident Response Plan with incident classification matrix and INCD notification procedure; dated incident log entries showing discovery time and 24-hour notification submission; INCD confirmation receipts or reference numbers; call logs or email evidence of notification.

key_requirements.text: Maintain and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop or update the Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) covering critical business functions, recovery time objectives (RTOs), and recovery point objectives (RPOs), with annual review and update cycles. 2. Conduct tabletop exercises, failover tests, or full-scale recovery drills at least annually, documenting results, gaps, and remediation actions. 3. Maintain recovery infrastructure (backup systems, alternate sites, or cloud failover) and verify it is operational quarterly through restore tests.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Business Continuity/Disaster Recovery team: provide current BCP and DRP documents with RTO/RPO definitions; IT team: supply backup infrastructure documentation and quarterly restore test evidence; Finance team: confirm budget for recovery site or cloud DR subscriptions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: BCP consultant: review and validate BCP/DRP for completeness; backup vendor or cloud DR provider: provide quarterly test results and Recovery Time Guarantee commitments; alternate data center or cloud provider: confirm facility readiness and failover capability annually.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current Business Continuity Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan with signatures and review dates; annual test schedule and dated test execution reports showing results, issues found, and remediation status; recovery infrastructure inventory and quarterly operational verification records; vendor Service Level Agreements (SLAs) with RTO/RPO commitments.

key_requirements.text: Employee cybersecurity awareness training program
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Cybersecurity Awareness Training Program specifying mandatory annual training content (phishing, password hygiene, data protection, incident reporting) for all employees, with tracking of completion. 2. Select or develop training delivery method (online module, in-person session, or combination) and schedule initial training within 30 days of hire and refresher annually. 3. Maintain records of completion, scores/assessments, and include periodic simulated phishing exercises to reinforce learning and measure effectiveness.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Training team: integrate cybersecurity awareness training into onboarding and annual compliance calendars; IT/Security team: select training platform (e.g., SANS Security Awareness, KnowBe4) and develop organization-specific content; Compliance team: create and maintain training attendance and completion register.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training platform or security awareness vendor: deliver annual training modules and provide completion reporting; external simulated phishing provider: conduct quarterly campaigns and supply metrics on click rates and reporting; training content provider: ensure content reflects INCD Cyber Defense Directive scope.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cybersecurity Awareness Training Program document outlining scope, frequency, and content; annual training completion register (spreadsheet or LMS export) showing all employees and completion dates; simulated phishing campaign results and metrics; training assessment scores or quiz results; signed attendance records or certificate of completion.

key_requirements.text: Third-party and supply chain risk management requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vendor risk assessment framework that evaluates cybersecurity maturity, certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), and financial stability before onboarding any third party with system access or data handling responsibilities.
2. Document all supply chain dependencies in a centralized registry, including contractual security obligations, data residency requirements, and incident notification terms.
3. Conduct annual re-assessments of active vendors and maintain an audit trail of security incidents, breaches, or non-compliance incidents involving third parties.
internal_actions: Procurement team: provide signed vendor contracts and evidence of security requirement clauses (data protection, breach notification, audit rights).
IT team: deliver complete inventory of all third-party systems, APIs, and data access points with ownership and criticality classifications.
Legal team: supply template vendor security addendum and records of vendor security questionnaire responses.
Compliance team: provide vendor risk assessment scores, re-assessment schedules, and any documented vendor non-compliance incidents from the past 24 months.
vendor_actions: Vendors: provide current ISO 27001 certificate, SOC 2 Type II report, or equivalent cybersecurity certification dated within the last 12 months.
Vendors: sign data processing agreement (DPA) or security addendum confirming incident notification within 48 hours, annual security audits, and audit access rights.
Vendors: complete organization-provided security questionnaire (CAIQ or equivalent) and declare sub-processor arrangements and data location.
Vendors: provide evidence of cyber insurance with minimum coverage limits and confirm breach notification procedures in writing.
compliance_evidence: Signed vendor security assessment report with risk rating and board or management approval sign-off.
Centralized vendor registry (spreadsheet or risk management system export) listing all third parties, access scope, and re-assessment dates.
Executed data processing agreements or security addenda for all vendors handling sensitive data or system access.
Vendor certification copies (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, or equivalent) with valid dates.
Documented incident log or summary showing any third-party security incidents, breach notifications received, and remediation actions taken in the past 24 months.
Vendor re-assessment schedule and completed re-assessment forms for at least the most recent assessment cycle.

key_requirements.text: Vulnerability management and patching program with defined timelines
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Define and document a vulnerability management policy that specifies patch timelines by severity: critical patches within 72 hours, high within 14 days, medium within 30 days, and low within 90 days, with documented exceptions and management approval for delays.
2. Implement automated patch deployment tooling (e.g., WSUS, Mobile Device Management, or enterprise patch management platform) with monthly testing cycles and a documented rollback procedure.

3. Conduct monthly vulnerability scans across all assets, log all detected vulnerabilities with CVSS scores, assign remediation owners, and track closure status until patched or formally accepted as risk.
internal_actions: IT team: provide vulnerability management policy document, patch deployment procedures, and evidence of patch management tool configuration (update schedules, exemption processes).
IT team: supply monthly vulnerability scan reports (last 12 months) showing detected vulnerabilities, CVSS scores, remediation status, and closure dates.
IT team: deliver patch deployment logs or reports confirming patches applied to systems within defined timelines, including failed deployments and remediation steps.

Risk/Compliance team: provide documented risk acceptance records for any vulnerabilities not patched within policy timelines, signed by management, with business justification and compensating controls.
vendor_actions: Software vendors: provide documented patch release schedules, security bulletin timelines, and commit to notifying the organization of critical and high-severity patches within 48 hours of release.
Managed security service providers or patch management vendors: confirm they will apply security patches within the organization's defined timelines and provide monthly deployment reports.
Hardware and infrastructure vendors: confirm end-of-life dates for all products in use and provide notification of security patches at least 30 days in advance.
compliance_evidence: Approved vulnerability management and patch management policy document with defined SLAs by severity level and date of last review.
Monthly vulnerability scan reports (last 12 months) from automated scanning tool showing vulnerability counts, CVSS scores, asset coverage, and trend analysis.
Patch deployment reports showing percentage of systems patched within SLA timelines for the past 12 months, broken down by severity and system type.
Evidence of patch testing and rollback procedures (test environment configuration, test schedules, documented rollback playbooks).
Risk acceptance records (signed by management) for any open vulnerabilities not patched within policy timelines, with documented compensating controls.
Audit or penetration test report confirming absence of known unpatched critical and high-severity vulnerabilities.

regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions

regulations.name: Prohibition on Money Laundering Law
regulations.authority: Israel Money Laundering and Terror Financing Prohibition Authority (IMPA)
regulations.summary: Israeli law prohibiting money laundering and the financing of terrorism, requiring financial institutions, money service businesses, insurance companies, attorneys, and accountants to implement AML/CFT programs. Israel is a FATF member and aligns its regime with FATF recommendations.

key_requirements.text: Customer due diligence (CDD/KYC) for all customers prior to establishing a business relationship
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a KYC checklist requiring collection of full legal name, date of birth, address, identification document number, and beneficial ownership details before account opening. 2. Integrate identity verification through a third-party screening vendor against government ID databases or use manual document review with signed attestation. 3. Document all collected information in a centralized customer master file with timestamp and responsible staff member signature.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve KYC policy document with required data fields and verification methods. Compliance team: create customer intake form template and checklist. IT team: configure customer onboarding system to enforce mandatory field completion and block account activation until KYC approval.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Identity verification vendor: provide written SLA confirming verification response time, coverage of Israeli and international ID types, and ongoing screening database updates. Provide test environment access and audit trail reports for all verification requests submitted.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed KYC policy document; completed customer intake forms with identification copies and verification confirmations; system audit logs showing account activation dates aligned with KYC completion dates; vendor contract with SLA terms and verification sample reports.

key_requirements.text: Enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers, politically exposed persons (PEPs), and complex transactions
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a risk matrix assigning customers to risk tiers (standard, medium, high) based on country of residence, transaction volume, business type, and PEP status, documented in the AML policy. 2. For high-risk and PEP customers, require source of funds documentation, beneficial ownership verification beyond standard KYC, and quarterly transaction reviews with documented approval by the compliance officer. 3. Maintain a current PEP list sourced from IMPA guidance or third-party vendor and screen all customers against it during onboarding and periodically thereafter.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain customer risk assessment matrix and scoring rubric; establish PEP screening schedule (minimum annually). Legal team: draft enhanced due diligence (EDD) procedures and source of funds documentation templates. Finance team: provide transaction reports for high-risk customers quarterly for review and sign-off.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: PEP screening vendor: provide current, Israel-compliant PEP list with update frequency guarantee (minimum quarterly) and documented methodology for PEP identification. Provide written certification of data sources and regulatory alignment with IMPA standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed AML risk assessment policy with risk matrix; completed EDD forms for all high-risk and PEP customers with source of funds documentation; PEP screening results with dates and vendor list version used; compliance officer sign-offs on quarterly high-risk customer reviews; vendor PEP list update certificates.

key_requirements.text: File suspicious transaction reports (STRs) with IMPA without tipping off the customer
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a confidential STR reporting procedure requiring designated AML staff to submit suspicious transactions directly to IMPA via their secure portal or registered submission channel, with restricted access and no customer notification. 2. Create an internal suspicious activity intake form documenting transaction details, red flags observed, and supporting evidence; route through compliance officer for approval before submission. 3. Maintain a confidential STR log (separate from customer files) recording submission dates, IMPA reference numbers, and status, accessible only to authorized compliance personnel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft STR reporting procedure and intake form; establish secure submission protocol and IMPA contact procedures. IT team: set up restricted-access STR log system with role-based permissions; provide staff with secure IMPA portal credentials and authentication documentation. Legal team: prepare staff guidance on tipping-off prohibition and non-disclosure requirements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: IMPA or registered reporting intermediary: confirm receipt of STR submissions and provide reference numbers; supply secure submission channel documentation and confirm technical support contact for submission issues.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed STR reporting procedure document; completed STR intake forms with approvals by compliance officer; IMPA submission receipts with reference numbers and submission timestamps; confidential STR log with secure access audit trail; staff sign-off on tipping-off prohibition training documentation.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of all transactions and customer identification for at least 7 years
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a document retention policy specifying that all customer identification records, account opening files, and transaction records are archived for a minimum of 7 years from account closure or final transaction date. 2. Establish a centralized records repository (physical or digital) with indexing by customer ID and document type, and configure automated backup and disaster recovery. 3. Define a documented destruction procedure requiring approval from the compliance officer before any record disposal after the 7-year retention period.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve records retention and destruction policy with 7-year minimum retention timeline. IT team: implement data management system with automated retention date tracking, backup scheduling, and access logging; provide monthly retention status reports. Finance team: coordinate with IT for transaction record archival and provide data export schedules.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Document management or cloud storage vendor: provide written SLA confirming 7-year data availability guarantee, automated backup frequency (minimum daily), disaster recovery procedures, and secure destruction certification upon customer request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed records retention and destruction policy; IT system configuration documentation showing automated retention date enforcement; sample backup and disaster recovery test reports; vendor SLA document and destruction certificates; audit logs showing access to archived records.

key_requirements.text: Appoint an AML compliance officer and implement an internal AML program
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint an AML compliance officer in writing, specifying their title, reporting line (recommend direct to Board or Audit Committee), and explicit authority to access customer files, transaction data, and personnel. 2. Draft a comprehensive AML program document covering CDD, suspicious activity monitoring, STR procedures, record retention, staff training, and independent audit requirements; obtain Board approval and document sign-off. 3. Allocate dedicated budget and staff resources to the AML function and document in Board minutes; establish quarterly reporting cadence from the compliance officer to senior management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue formal appointment letter or Board resolution naming the AML compliance officer with title and authority. Legal team: draft and obtain Board approval for the AML program document; create organization chart showing compliance officer reporting structure. Finance team: establish AML function budget line and confirm allocation in annual financial plan. Board/Audit Committee: schedule quarterly compliance reporting meetings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External AML audit or consulting firm: provide engagement letter for annual independent AML program review and audit of compliance procedures; confirm expertise in Israeli regulatory requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Board resolution or HR appointment letter for AML compliance officer; approved AML program policy document with Board sign-off; AML function organization chart and reporting line documentation; quarterly compliance officer reports to Board/management with dates and attendance records; external audit engagement letter and scope of work.

key_requirements.text: Staff training on recognizing and reporting suspicious activity
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a mandatory AML training curriculum covering identification of suspicious activity indicators, reporting procedures, tipping-off prohibition, and Israeli regulatory requirements; deliver to all customer-facing and transaction-processing staff annually. 2. Document training attendance with sign-in sheets or learning management system records; assess comprehension through a short quiz or certification; maintain records for audit. 3. Provide refresher training within 30 days of any policy updates or regulatory guidance changes; communicate updates through email bulletins or staff meetings with confirmation of receipt.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Training team: develop AML training course materials (slides, written guide, or video); schedule and deliver annual training sessions; maintain attendance records and completion certificates. Compliance team: create quiz or assessment tool; review training effectiveness and update materials based on lessons learned or regulatory changes. IT team: upload training materials to learning management system if applicable; provide attendance reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External training provider (if outsourced): deliver customized AML training covering Israeli Money Laundering Law requirements and organizational policies; provide certificate templates and trainer credentials. Provide post-training assessment scores and recommend content refreshes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed training curriculum and course materials; attendance rosters or LMS completion reports for all staff; assessment quiz scores or certification documents; training acknowledgment forms signed by attendees; dated updates to training content following regulatory guidance or policy changes; refresher training records.

key_requirements.text: Comply with FATF Recommendations and UN Security Council resolutions on terrorism financing
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a dedicated sanctions screening process that cross-references all counterparties against OFAC SDN lists, UN Security Council consolidated sanctions lists, and Israeli Tax Authority terror designations on a monthly basis.

2. Integrate FATF Recommendation 1-10 controls into customer onboarding workflows, including beneficial ownership verification, PEP screening, and documentation of source of funds for high-risk customers.
3. Conduct annual risk assessments to identify sanctions exposure across products, geographies, and customer segments; document findings and remediation actions in an AML governance report.
internal_actions: Legal & Compliance: provide signed attestation letter confirming adoption of FATF 40 Recommendations and UN SC resolution mapping to internal policies.
Finance team: deliver monthly sanctions screening results report with hit/no-hit logs and customer disposition decisions.
IT team: furnish evidence of integration between CRM/banking system and external sanctions data feeds (vendor contracts, API logs, test results).
HR & Compliance: submit training completion records showing all customer-facing staff completed FATF terrorism financing awareness training within the last 12 months.
vendor_actions: Sanctions screening vendor: provide certified SLA confirming OFAC, UN SC, and Israeli regulatory list updates delivered within 24 hours of publication; submit quarterly coverage audit reports.

AML software provider: deliver technical documentation proving real-time transaction monitoring rules for FATF Recommendation 10 (customer due diligence) and Recommendation 16 (wire transfer information).
Compliance consultants: issue written confirmation that internal control framework has been benchmarked against FATF Mutual Evaluation Report standards for Israel.
compliance_evidence: Signed board-approved FATF Recommendations compliance policy document with explicit mapping to Recommendations 1-10 and 16.
Monthly sanctions screening reports (last 3 months) showing 100% customer/vendor coverage, screening date, lists queried, and remediation decisions.
UN Security Council Resolution tracking log documenting all applicable resolutions (e.g., 1373, 2231, 2396) with internal control alignment statement.
Annual AML/CFT risk assessment report with terrorism financing and sanctions risk findings, control gaps, and management sign-off.
Signed vendor service agreements with terrorism financing screening and FATF compliance provisions; SLA evidence showing list update timeliness.
Training completion certificates or LMS exports showing staff completion of FATF/UN financing designations curriculum.

regulations.penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities

regulations.name: Protection of Personal Information Act
regulations.authority: Information Regulator (South Africa)
regulations.summary: South Africa's comprehensive data protection law establishing eight lawful conditions for processing personal information. Closely modeled on the EU GDPR, POPIA applies to all public and private bodies that process personal information and creates the independent Information Regulator as enforcement authority.

key_requirements.text: Accountability: designate and register an Information Officer with the Information Regulator
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally appoint an individual as Information Officer with defined responsibilities in writing. 2. Submit the Information Officer's details (name, contact, organization) to the Information Regulator via the official registration portal. 3. Document the appointment decision and delegation in board minutes or equivalent governance record.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Information Officer appointment letter and board resolution authorizing the designation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable; this is an internal governance requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Information Officer appointment letter, board minutes/resolution, Information Regulator registration confirmation, and documented contact details on file.

key_requirements.text: Processing limitation: collect only for lawful, specific, explicitly defined, and legitimate purposes
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data mapping exercise to identify all collection points and document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, or public task) for each. 2. Draft and publish a Data Collection Notice specifying the purpose, categories of data, and legal basis for each collection activity. 3. Implement system controls to prevent collection of data beyond the declared scope.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide data collection audit and lawful basis justification matrix. IT team: confirm system controls restricting data input fields to declared purposes. Business owners: document specific purposes for each collection activity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide signed Data Processing Addendum confirming they will only process data for the purposes specified and will not use data for unrelated purposes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed data mapping register, published Data Collection Notice, lawful basis justification document, system configuration screenshots, and signed Data Processing Addenda with all vendors.

key_requirements.text: Purpose specification: retain information only as long as necessary
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish and document data retention periods for each category of personal information (e.g., customer records, employee files, transaction logs) based on legal, operational, and business requirements. 2. Implement automated deletion or anonymization routines in systems to enforce retention schedules. 3. Conduct quarterly audits to verify deletion of data exceeding retention periods.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide retention schedule with justifications. IT team: provide evidence of automated deletion/anonymization scripts, logs of executed deletions, and system-enforced retention controls. Records management: confirm archival or destruction procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide contractual commitment to delete or return data upon contract termination and evidence of automated purge routines for data they hold.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Data Retention Schedule, automated deletion logs, evidence of purge job executions, audit trail showing deleted records, and signed vendor deletion commitments.

key_requirements.text: Further processing limitation: must be compatible with original purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the original stated purpose for each data collection and establish a decision framework to evaluate compatibility before any new use. 2. Conduct a compatibility assessment for any proposed secondary use; if not compatible, obtain explicit fresh consent or identify an alternative lawful basis. 3. Maintain a register of all uses and compatibility assessments, updated whenever further processing is considered.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide compatibility assessment template and completed assessments for all current secondary uses. Business owners: document all known uses of personal information. IT/Data teams: confirm system logs showing what data is accessed for what purposes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide contractual assurance that they will not use personal information for purposes beyond those specified in their Data Processing Addendum without prior written authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented original purposes, completed compatibility assessments with legal reasoning, register of approved secondary uses, records of obtained consent for incompatible uses, and vendor contractual commitments.

key_requirements.text: Information quality: take steps to ensure personal information is complete, accurate, and up to date
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish data quality standards (accuracy, completeness, timeliness) and assign ownership of each data set to a named data steward. 2. Implement data validation rules at point of entry and periodic data quality audits (at least annually) to identify and correct errors or gaps. 3. Create a process for data subjects to request correction and log all correction requests and outcomes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data governance team: provide data quality policy, data steward assignments, and validation rule configurations. IT team: provide data quality audit reports, validation error logs, and system controls preventing incomplete data entry. Legal/Compliance: maintain log of correction requests and outcomes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data service providers: confirm their validation controls and provide evidence of data quality checks they perform; provide SLA for responding to correction requests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Quality Policy, data steward documentation, annual data quality audit reports with remediation evidence, validation rule screenshots, data subject correction request log, and vendor SLA agreements.

key_requirements.text: Security safeguards: implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct an Information Security Risk Assessment identifying threats to personal information confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and select proportionate technical and organizational controls. 2. Implement controls such as encryption in transit and at rest, access controls, audit logging, regular patching, employee training, and incident response procedures. 3. Document all controls in a Security Control Register and test them at least annually or following system changes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: provide Information Security Risk Assessment report, Security Control Register, system architecture diagrams showing encryption, firewall rules, and access control configurations. IT: provide evidence of penetration tests, vulnerability scans, patch management logs, and backup/disaster recovery test results. HR/Training: provide attendance records for security awareness training.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and infrastructure vendors: provide SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification, signed Security Addendum confirming encryption standards, access controls, and incident notification procedures. Provide evidence of their security controls and incident response times.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Information Security Risk Assessment, Security Control Register with test evidence, encryption certificates or configurations, access control matrix, security training records, audit logs, vendor SOC 2/ISO 27001 certificates, signed Security Addenda, and documented incident response plan.

key_requirements.text: Data subject participation: rights to access, correction, and deletion of personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal data subject request process with defined timelines (typically 30 days), assign ownership to a named officer, and document all access, correction, and deletion requests in a central register.

2. Implement technical controls enabling data subjects to submit requests via secure channels (email, web portal, or in-person); configure systems to retrieve, modify, or securely delete personal data upon verified request.
3. Create a standard response template confirming receipt, request status, action taken (access granted, correction made, deletion completed), and retention of audit trails for all transactions.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and sign off a data subject request policy and response template with timelines and approval workflows.
IT team: provide a data subject request register (spreadsheet or database) showing all requests received, processed dates, actions taken, and closure dates for the past 12 months.
IT team: confirm system capabilities to export, modify, or flag personal data for deletion and provide technical documentation of the request fulfillment workflow.
HR/Data steward: identify and document all systems and databases holding personal data subject to access/deletion rights.
vendor_actions: All data processors and cloud service providers: provide a written confirmation (clause in DPA or standalone letter) of their ability to retrieve, correct, or delete personal data upon organisation's instruction within agreed timeframes.
Portal/CRM vendors: supply technical documentation proving that data subject request forms are encrypted and audit logs are retained.
compliance_evidence: Signed data subject request policy and response template.
Data subject request register showing sample requests (access, correction, deletion) with dates, outcomes, and closure confirmation.
Email or portal evidence of at least 3–5 completed data subject requests with confirmation of action taken.
System technical specifications or screenshots showing request intake, data retrieval, and deletion/correction capabilities.
Signed Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with vendors confirming cooperation on data subject rights.

key_requirements.text: Notify the Information Regulator and affected data subjects of security compromises
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a security incident response plan that clearly defines thresholds for determining when a compromise has occurred, assigns incident classification responsibility to a senior IT/Security lead, and mandates notification within 30 days of discovery.

2. Create a notification protocol requiring the organisation to simultaneously contact the Information Regulator (via prescribed form), all materially affected data subjects (via email, SMS, or letter), and maintain a breach notification log with dates and parties notified.

3. Develop a breach communication template that discloses the nature of the compromise, categories of affected persons, likely consequences, remediation steps, and contact details for further information; route all notifications through Legal for compliance review before dispatch.
internal_actions: IT/Security team: provide the documented incident response plan, including thresholds for 'personal information compromise,' incident classification matrix, and escalation procedures.
IT/Security team: supply logs, alerts, or forensic reports from the past 24 months showing how security incidents were detected, classified, and time-stamped.
Legal team: draft and approve a breach notification template and a completed Information Regulator notification form (for a sample or historical breach, if applicable).
Communications/HR team: provide evidence of data subject breach notifications sent (email screenshots, SMS records, or postal delivery confirmations) with dates and recipient lists.
vendor_actions: All cloud service providers, hosting providers, and security monitoring vendors: supply written undertakings to notify the organisation of any detected security incidents affecting personal data within 24 hours, and to cooperate with breach investigations.
Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs) or SOC providers: confirm in writing their incident alerting SLA and provide sample incident reports or notification logs.
compliance_evidence: Signed incident response plan with clearly defined breach thresholds and notification timelines.
Breach notification log (spreadsheet or system record) showing incident date, discovery date, classification, and notification dates to Regulator and data subjects.
Sample breach notification communication sent to affected data subjects (email, letter, or SMS proof) disclosing incident nature, consequences, and remediation.
Completed and signed Information Regulator notification form (POPIA Form or similar) with submission date and acknowledgment from Regulator if available.
Signed SLAs or DPA clauses with vendors confirming 24-hour incident notification obligations.
Incident investigation report or forensic log showing root cause, scope of compromise, and remediation actions.

regulations.penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available

regulations.name: Financial Intelligence Centre Act
regulations.authority: Financial Intelligence Centre (FIC)
regulations.summary: South Africa's primary AML/CFT legislation requiring accountable institutions (banks, insurers, estate agents, legal practitioners, accountants, casinos) to implement customer due diligence and suspicious transaction reporting. The 2017 amendments introduced a risk-based approach to combat money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing.

key_requirements.text: Risk-based customer due diligence (CDD) including identification and verification of beneficial owners
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a risk classification matrix categorizing customers by jurisdiction, business type, and transaction patterns. 2. Implement a tiered CDD process requiring identity verification documents, beneficial ownership declarations, and source-of-funds evidence proportionate to risk rating. 3. Integrate CDD checks into vendor onboarding and periodic review cycles.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft CDD policy and beneficial ownership declaration template. Compliance Officer: establish risk classification criteria and CDD verification checklist. IT team: configure customer data fields to capture beneficial owner details and verification method used.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide certified copies of directors' identification, beneficial ownership declarations, and proof of registered address. Beneficial owners: sign beneficial ownership acknowledgment forms confirming their role and identity verification completion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed CDD matrices per vendor, beneficial ownership declaration forms with supporting ID copies, risk classification scorecard showing each vendor's categorization, and verification completion logs dated and initialed by reviewer.

key_requirements.text: Enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers, complex transactions, and PEPs
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create an enhanced due diligence (EDD) checklist for PEPs, high-risk jurisdictions, complex structures, and customers engaged in cash-intensive trades. 2. Require Compliance Officer or senior management sign-off on EDD findings before engagement approval. 3. Establish ongoing monitoring triggers (e.g., transaction frequency, value anomalies) requiring quarterly EDD refresh for flagged vendors.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance Officer: compile PEP screening list (OFAC, UN, South African government lists) and establish EDD trigger criteria. Legal team: draft enhanced verification procedures including source-of-wealth documentation requirements. Finance team: define transaction monitoring thresholds and escalation protocols.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: High-risk vendors: provide enhanced documentation including audited financial statements, beneficial ownership structure charts, business purpose statements, and senior management identification. PEPs: disclose political positions held and certify absence of sanctions designations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: EDD completion forms signed by Compliance Officer with date and approval, PEP screening reports and dates run, vendor risk reassessment records documenting decision rationale, and audit trail of monitoring alerts triggering EDD refresh.

key_requirements.text: File suspicious and unusual transaction reports (SUTRs) to the FIC
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish transaction monitoring procedures defining suspicious indicators (e.g., structuring, mismatched counterparties, unusual timing patterns). 2. Assign responsibility for SUTR preparation and filing to Compliance Officer, with documented assessment reasoning. 3. File SUTRs with FIC within 30 days using the prescribed reporting template and retain internal investigation logs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance Officer: create SUTR assessment template documenting red flags identified, timeline, and reporting decision. IT team: configure transaction logging to capture counterparty details, amounts, dates, and source. Finance/Operations team: document transaction context and business justification for each flagged transaction.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Payment processors and banking partners: provide transaction detail exports including timestamp, parties, amounts, and unusual activity flags. Third-party vendors: declare any sanctions, legal, or compliance issues affecting transaction legitimacy.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed SUTR forms with FIC filing confirmation receipts, internal investigation memos documenting suspicious indicators identified, transaction logs with annotated red flags, and filed copies showing submission dates within 30-day window.

key_requirements.text: File cash threshold reports (CTRs) for cash transactions exceeding ZAR 49,999
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement automated cash transaction monitoring flagging all cash receipts exceeding ZAR 49,999. 2. Assign Compliance Officer responsibility for verifying transaction legitimacy and filing CTRs within prescribed timeframe using FIC template. 3. Maintain a cash transaction register with daily reconciliation and documented filing evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: establish daily cash reconciliation procedures and transaction register. IT team: configure system alerts for cash transactions exceeding ZAR 49,999 threshold with automatic report generation. Compliance Officer: create CTR filing checklist and approval log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cash handling vendors and payment service providers: provide daily cash transaction summaries with timestamp, amount, customer identification, and deposit confirmation. Bank: furnish monthly cash deposit statements with transaction detail.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Daily cash transaction registers with entries exceeding ZAR 49,999 highlighted, submitted CTR forms with FIC receipt confirmations and filing dates, system-generated transaction alerts showing threshold breaches, and bank deposit confirmation statements.

key_requirements.text: Keep records of client identification and transactions for at least 5 years
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a document retention policy specifying 5-year minimum retention for all CDD records, transaction documentation, and correspondence. 2. Configure IT systems and physical file storage with secure access controls and deletion prevention. 3. Conduct annual audits confirming required records exist and are retrievable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft retention policy specifying document types, retention periods, and destruction procedures. IT team: implement system controls preventing deletion of records within 5-year window and configure backup/archival storage. HR team: document staff responsibilities and sign-off on retention procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Document storage and IT vendors: certify capability to store customer and transaction records for minimum 5 years with immutable audit trail. Cloud/archive providers: provide service-level agreements confirming data preservation and retrieval capabilities.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed document retention policy document, IT system configuration screenshots showing deletion prevention controls, annual audit reports confirming record availability, vendor service-level agreements with 5-year retention commitment, and sample retrieval test results demonstrating accessibility.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Compliance Officer and maintain an AML/CFT risk management and compliance program
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a named Compliance Officer with documented Board resolution and defined reporting line to senior management or Board committee. 2. Develop a written AML/CFT risk management and compliance program addressing customer due diligence, transaction monitoring, reporting, and staff training. 3. Allocate budget and resources for compliance function and conduct annual program effectiveness review.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Legal team: prepare and sign Board resolution appointing Compliance Officer with commencement date and authority scope. Compliance Officer: draft comprehensive AML/CFT program manual covering CDD, monitoring, reporting, sanctions, and training. HR team: establish compliance role job description and resource allocation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External compliance consultants: provide compliance program gap assessment and recommendations. Training vendors: deliver initial and annual AML/CFT training certification to staff with attendance records.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Board resolution appointing Compliance Officer with effective date, comprehensive AML/CFT program manual with version control and approval date, annual program review documentation with effectiveness findings, staff training completion records with certification dates, and Board meeting minutes discussing program oversight.

key_requirements.text: Ongoing staff training on AML/CFT obligations
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a mandatory annual AML/CFT training curriculum covering FICA obligations, suspicious transaction reporting, sanctions screening, and beneficial ownership identification.
2. Schedule and deliver role-specific training sessions (e.g., front-line staff, compliance officers, senior management) with documented attendance records and assessment scores.
3. Implement a refresher training cadence (minimum annually) and track completion rates by employee, with remedial training for non-completion within 30 days.
internal_actions: Compliance/Training team: develop and maintain a documented AML/CFT training curriculum with learning objectives, assessment criteria, and pass/fail thresholds.
HR team: provide employee roster, hire dates, and role classifications to schedule training by cohort and track attendance electronically.
Finance/Compliance team: create a training register (spreadsheet or LMS) recording name, date, topic, trainer, duration, and assessment score for all staff.
vendor_actions: External training provider (if outsourced): deliver certified AML/CFT training content aligned to South African FICA requirements and provide certificates of completion and trainer credentials.
LMS vendor (if applicable): provide user access logs, completion reports, and assessment scoring exports in machine-readable format (CSV/Excel) monthly.
compliance_evidence: Signed training policy document citing minimum frequency and mandatory participation.
Training curriculum document with FICA-specific content outline and learning objectives.
Attendance registers or LMS export showing all staff trained, dates, and assessment scores.
Training certificates or completion confirmations issued to participants.
Email communications or notices mandating training and deadlines.
Trainer qualifications or external provider contract and certifications.

regulations.penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC

regulations.name: Kenya Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC)
regulations.summary: Kenya's first comprehensive data protection law establishing principles for the processing of personal data, rights of data subjects, and obligations of data controllers and processors. Applies to any person who processes personal data about Kenyan residents.

key_requirements.text: Register as a data controller or processor with the ODPC if processing above prescribed thresholds
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data processing impact assessment to determine if your organisation meets ODPC registration thresholds (volume, sensitivity, or vulnerability of data subjects processed). 2. Complete the ODPC registration form with details of controller/processor status, processing activities, and data categories. 3. Submit registration to ODPC and maintain proof of registration and renewal certificates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written assessment of data processing volumes and scope; Finance team: confirm annual turnover and employee count to establish threshold applicability; Compliance team: prepare and submit ODPC registration application with supporting documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide written confirmation of their data processor status and processing scope so organisation can accurately declare them in ODPC registration.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ODPC registration certificate; data processing impact assessment report; registration renewal receipts; internal threshold determination memo dated and signed by Legal/Compliance leads.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally appoint a suitably qualified individual as Data Protection Officer (internal or external) with documented board or senior management approval. 2. Issue a written appointment letter specifying DPO roles, responsibilities, reporting line, and access to resources. 3. Publish DPO contact details on the organisation's website and communicate appointment to the ODPC if required by local guidance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue formal DPO appointment letter with job description and reporting structure; Board/Governance: approve DPO appointment with documented resolution; IT team: allocate budget and resources for DPO function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External DPO provider (if outsourced): provide signed DPO engagement agreement confirming qualifications, independence, and compliance with Kenya DPA Article 39 requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or management approval memo appointing DPO; signed DPO appointment letter; DPO CV or qualification certificate; published DPO contact details (website screenshot); ODPC notification letter (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Provide privacy notices disclosing processing purposes and data subject rights
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft privacy notices for each processing context (e.g. customer recruitment, employee onboarding, website visitors) that clearly disclose controller identity, processing purposes, lawful basis, data categories, retention periods, and data subject rights. 2. Ensure notices are provided in plain, intelligible language at or before data collection. 3. Maintain records of all active privacy notices with version control and implementation dates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft privacy notices aligned to Kenya DPA requirements and each processing scenario; Marketing/IT team: integrate privacy notices into data collection points (forms, websites, apps) with evidence of implementation; Records team: maintain master registry of all privacy notices with dates and versions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors processing data on your behalf: provide their privacy disclosures or attestation that they display your organisation's privacy notice to data subjects.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed-off privacy notice templates for each processing activity; screenshots or PDFs of privacy notices displayed on digital channels; privacy notice registry with version numbers and effective dates; evidence of testing/review by Legal team.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document a formal Data Subject Rights process covering receipt, acknowledgement, verification, and response to access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection requests within 30 days. 2. Designate an owner (typically DPO or Legal) to triage and respond to requests and maintain a register of all requests received and actions taken. 3. Implement technical controls (e.g. data export functionality, correction forms) to facilitate timely and accurate responses.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft Data Subject Rights procedure and response templates; IT team: configure systems to export, correct, or suppress data as requested; DPO/Compliance: create and maintain request register with dates, types, and outcomes; HR team: train staff on receiving and escalating rights requests.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Technology/service vendors: provide confirmation that their systems support data subject access, portability (data export in machine-readable format), deletion, and restriction capabilities; provide written commitment to support your responses within stated timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights procedure document (signed and dated); register or log of all subject requests received with responses and closure dates; templates used for access, rectification, and erasure responses; system screenshots showing export/correction/deletion functionality; staff training records.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent or establish lawful basis for processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all data processing activities and document the lawful basis for each (consent, contractual necessity, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests). 2. For processing based on consent, design clear, granular opt-in mechanisms (not pre-ticked boxes) and retain evidence of affirmative consent. 3. For other lawful bases, maintain a Lawful Basis Assessment document for each processing activity, approved by Legal, that explains why consent is not required.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: prepare Lawful Basis Assessment for all processing activities with sign-off; IT/Marketing team: audit and modify consent collection mechanisms to ensure affirmative, informed opt-in; Records team: maintain central register of lawful bases and consent evidence; Privacy by Design team: review new processing activities before launch.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide confirmation of their lawful basis for processing any personal data on your behalf; provide records of consent collected if processing is consent-based.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Lawful Basis Assessment document for each processing activity (signed by Legal); consent records or audit logs showing affirmative user consent with timestamps; consent form screenshots; preference centre configurations showing granular opt-in options.

key_requirements.text: Notify the ODPC and data subjects of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Data Breach Response Plan defining detection, triage, investigation, and notification workflows with roles, timelines (72-hour notification target), and escalation paths. 2. Configure monitoring and alerting systems (logs, intrusion detection, anomaly alerts) to detect breaches promptly and ensure rapid internal notification to Incident Response and Legal teams. 3. Prepare breach notification templates for ODPC and affected data subjects, and conduct breach response drills at least annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: implement breach detection logging and alerting; maintain security incident logs with timestamps; Incident Response team: activate breach response plan and document investigation findings; Legal/DPO: draft and send ODPC and data subject breach notifications within 72 hours; Compliance: maintain breach register with details and outcomes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide contractual commitment to report security incidents to your organisation within 24 hours; provide evidence of security monitoring and breach reporting procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Breach Response Plan (dated and approved by management); breach notification templates; ODPC notification letters with send receipts; data subject notification copies; breach investigation reports; security incident logs showing detection time and escalation; annual breach response drill records and findings; breach register documenting all incidents, notification dates, and remediation actions.

key_requirements.text: Conduct privacy impact assessments for high-risk processing
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all processing activities classified as high-risk (e.g. biometric data, automated decision-making, large-scale processing of sensitive personal data) and document them in a processing inventory.
2. Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for each high-risk activity, documenting purpose, necessity, safeguards, and residual risks.
3. Review DPIA findings quarterly or when processing activities materially change, and maintain an audit trail of all assessments and remediation actions taken.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft DPIA template aligned with Kenya DPA requirements and maintain a register of all completed assessments.
IT team: provide documentation of data flows, technical controls, and encryption mechanisms used in high-risk processing systems.
Data Protection Officer (if appointed): review and approve all DPIAs before processing commences and maintain signed sign-off records.
vendor_actions: Third-party processors and cloud providers: provide security documentation (SOC 2, ISO 27001 certificates, data location confirmations) to support DPIA risk analysis.
Vendors handling high-risk data: submit completed Data Processing Addendums (DPAs) that detail safeguards and sub-processor arrangements.
compliance_evidence: Signed and dated DPIA reports for all high-risk processing activities.
Processing inventory or register listing high-risk activities and corresponding DPIA completion dates.
Minutes or sign-off records from Data Protection Officer or Privacy Committee approving each DPIA.
Evidence of remediation actions taken (e.g. security patches, policy updates, vendor audit reports) in response to DPIA recommendations.

key_requirements.text: Restrict cross-border transfers unless adequate protection is ensured
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Audit all current and planned cross-border data transfers and document recipient countries, data types, and legal basis for each transfer.

2. For transfers outside Kenya, establish that the recipient country or organisation has adequate data protection standards (e.g. EU adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules, or Standard Contractual Clauses).

3. Execute Data Transfer Impact Assessments (DTIAs) for all jurisdictions with unclear or weak data protection, and implement supplementary safeguards (encryption, access restrictions, anonymisation) where adequacy gaps exist.
internal_actions: Legal team: maintain a cross-border transfer register documenting recipient country, data categories, transfer mechanism (e.g. SCCs, adequacy decision), and legal review sign-off.
Compliance team: conduct annual re-assessment of recipient countries' data protection laws and update transfer mechanisms if legal status changes.
IT team: implement technical controls (encryption keys held in Kenya, data localisation options) to support transfer restrictions.
vendor_actions: Data processors and cloud providers: confirm in writing their physical data storage location(s), confirm compliance with Kenya DPA, and sign Data Processing Agreements that prohibit unauthorised onward transfers.
Vendors in non-adequate countries: provide Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) and evidence of compliance with those clauses (e.g. auditor certification).
compliance_evidence: Signed cross-border transfer register or inventory with recipient country names and transfer mechanisms documented.
Adequacy assessments or DTIAs for each non-Kenya recipient, including legal analysis of recipient country data protection laws.
Executed Data Processing Agreements (or Transfer Impact Assessments) that explicitly restrict onward transfers and confirm data location.
Copies of Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules, or adequacy decision letters from relevant authorities.
Annual compliance review memos confirming recipient countries' continued adequacy or documenting new safeguards implemented.

regulations.penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation

regulations.name: Nigeria Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Nigeria Data Protection Commission (NDPC)
regulations.summary: Nigeria's comprehensive data protection law replacing the Nigeria Data Protection Regulation (NDPR) 2019. Creates the Nigeria Data Protection Commission with independent status and introduces significant penalties for non-compliance.

key_requirements.text: Establish a lawful basis for processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, public task, or legitimate interest) for each data processing activity. 2. IT team: tag all data flows in the organisation with the corresponding lawful basis category. 3. Conduct a lawful basis audit to identify any processing lacking documented justification and remediate gaps.
