key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide a Data Processing Lawful Basis Register identifying all data categories, processing purposes, and applicable lawful basis for each activity; IT team: supply system configuration screenshots and data flow diagrams showing lawful basis assignment.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor/processor: provide a signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) specifying the lawful basis under which they process data on behalf of the organisation and confirm no secondary use of data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Lawful Basis Register, dated DPA with each processor, internal audit report confirming lawful basis documentation, and sample processing activity records tagged with lawful basis.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: draft and publish a Data Subject Rights Policy detailing the procedure and timeline (not to exceed 30 days) for handling access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection requests. 2. IT team: configure a formal request intake mechanism (e.g., dedicated email, ticketing system) with automated acknowledgement and tracking. 3. Conduct quarterly testing by submitting sample requests to verify compliance with the policy timeline.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide final Data Subject Rights Policy document and decision-making templates for each right category; IT team: provide system configuration documentation, request handling SOP, and log exports showing request intake and closure timestamps for the past 12 months.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor/processor: provide written commitment to honour data subject rights requests within 30 days, including a sample Rights Request Process document and confirmation of technical ability to export, correct, or delete data upon instruction.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published Data Subject Rights Policy, sample fulfilled subject access requests with redaction logs, rights request intake system screenshots, written vendor commitment to rights processing, and audit trail showing request timelines.

key_requirements.text: Notify the NDPC and affected data subjects of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Security team: establish a Data Breach Response Plan detailing detection, assessment, and escalation procedures with named stakeholders and their roles. 2. Legal and Comms teams: create a breach notification template pre-approved by NDPC for data subject and regulator notifications within 72 hours. 3. Implement automated alerting on security tools to trigger immediate manual breach assessment upon detection of unauthorised access or data loss.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: provide Data Breach Response Plan, breach assessment checklist, and a list of all data systems with automated monitoring enabled; Legal team: provide NDPC-compliant breach notification template and proof of NDPC contact details on file; IT team: provide system logs and alert configurations showing detection mechanisms.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor/processor: provide a signed Service Level Agreement (SLA) committing to notify the organisation of any suspected breach within 24 hours and provide detailed incident report within 48 hours; vendor must also provide incident contact details and escalation procedure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Breach Response Plan with version control, NDPC pre-approval confirmation for notification template, sample breach notification records with timestamps of detection and notification, vendor SLA with breach notification clause, and proof of NDPC contact registration.

key_requirements.text: Data protection compliance audit annually for high-volume data processors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all high-volume data processors (those processing significant volumes or sensitive data categories) and document them in a Processor Register. 2. Contract with an independent auditor to conduct a NDPA compliance audit covering data protection practices, security controls, and rights processing; schedule annually. 3. Remediate audit findings within 90 days and retain audit reports and remediation evidence for regulator review.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: maintain and update a High-Volume Data Processor Register with processor names, data categories, and volumes; Finance team: budget for annual third-party audit and provide engagement letters; IT/Legal: provide auditor with access to systems, policies, and processing documentation for scope of audit.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: provide signed engagement letter with audit scope, timeline, and deliverables (final audit report with findings, evidence of audit, and remediation recommendations); high-volume processors: grant auditor access to their systems and documentation upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed engagement letter with auditor, completed annual audit report with auditor credentials, Processor Register, audit scope document, remediation tracking log with closure evidence, and management response letters to audit findings.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Board/leadership: appoint a qualified Data Protection Officer (DPO) with documented independence, expertise in data protection law, and sufficient organisational authority. 2. HR team: issue a formal DPO appointment letter specifying roles, responsibilities, reporting line (to board or senior leadership, not operational management), and protection from retaliation. 3. Register the DPO name, contact details, and appointment date with the NDPC within 30 days of appointment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed DPO appointment letter with role and responsibility statement, proof of qualifications (certification or relevant experience), and confirmation of reporting line to senior leadership; Finance team: confirm budget allocation for DPO role and training; IT team: provide DPO with access rights and system permissions as required.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: If using an external DPO service provider: provide signed DPO Services Agreement specifying scope, independence guarantees, response time commitments, and confidentiality obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment letter, DPO curriculum vitae or credentials documentation, NDPC DPO registration confirmation email, organisational chart showing reporting line, and evidence of DPO participation in board-level data protection discussions.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfer safeguards or NDPC authorization required
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: map all cross-border data transfers and identify the destination country's adequacy status under NDPA. 2. For non-adequate countries, either obtain explicit NDPC authorization before transfer or implement contractual safeguards (Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules). 3. Maintain a Cross-Border Transfer Register documenting transfer routes, data categories, recipient details, and the safeguard mechanism (authorization, DPA, or adequacy finding).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide Cross-Border Data Transfer Register, copies of all NDPC transfer authorization letters, and list of non-adequate country designations; Compliance team: maintain evidence of safeguard implementation (signed DPAs, BCR documentation, or NDPC approval); IT team: provide system configuration showing geo-location restrictions or encryption applied to cross-border transfers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Recipient/processor in non-adequate country: sign a Data Processing Agreement incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent NDPA-recognized safeguards; vendor must confirm country of data storage and any onward transfers, and provide proof of security certifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Cross-Border Data Transfer Register, NDPC authorization letters or decision, executed DPA with Standard Contractual Clauses, vendor security certifications (ISO 27001 or equivalent), and audit trail showing only authorised transfers occur.

key_requirements.text: Privacy notices in plain language at point of collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Audit all data collection touchpoints (web forms, apps, in-person, call centres) and document current notice language.
2. Draft plain-language privacy notices in English and relevant Nigerian languages, avoiding legal jargon and explaining data use, storage, and individual rights clearly.
3. Deploy notices at point of collection and maintain version control with dates; test readability with sample users and refine before deployment.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide template privacy notice in plain language approved by NDPA guidance; confirm all collection channels covered.
IT team: provide screenshots or technical specifications of all data collection interfaces (web, mobile, API); confirm notice display functionality.
Marketing/Customer Service team: provide list of all offline collection points (branches, call centres, events) where notices must be posted.
vendor_actions: Third-party platform vendors (CRM, survey, payment providers): supply confirmation that privacy notices can be embedded at their collection point or confirm NDPA-compliant notice template in their terms.
Translation vendors: provide certified translations of privacy notices into Hausa, Igbo, Yoruba as appropriate to service areas.
compliance_evidence: Signed-off privacy notice template(s) dated and versioned, with plain-language certification from Legal.
Screenshots or live-environment URLs showing privacy notice displayed at each collection point.
Records of user testing or readability assessment (e.g. Flesch-Kincaid score report) demonstrating plain language.
Audit trail or change log showing notice version history and deployment dates across all channels.

key_requirements.text: Children's data requires parental or guardian consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all services, products, or data collection activities that interact with children (under 18); document age thresholds and data types collected from each.
2. Design a parental/guardian consent mechanism (e.g. email verification, two-factor confirmation, or verified ID upload) and integrate into account creation or data collection workflows.
3. Create age-gating logic in systems to trigger parental consent flow automatically for users identified as or claimed to be under 18; audit consent records monthly.
internal_actions: Product/Business team: supply list of all services/features/data streams that may collect from children; specify minimum age policy per service.

IT team: provide system architecture and data flow diagrams showing where age verification and parental consent logic must be inserted; confirm database schema supports consent recording and parental contact fields.
Legal team: draft parental/guardian consent form and verification process (identity confirmation method, re-consent frequency); confirm NDPA and CRA (Child Rights Act) alignment.
Compliance team: establish audit schedule and metrics (e.g. monthly reports of consent capture rate, failed verification attempts, parental dispute resolution cases).
vendor_actions: Third-party verification/identity vendors: provide SLA confirming age verification or parental identity confirmation capability; supply technical integration specs and error handling.
Marketing/analytics vendors: confirm they have children's data handling policies and can filter out or embargo processing of children's data pending parental consent.
compliance_evidence: Signed-off parental/guardian consent form template and process documentation (including verification method and language).
System design document or requirements specification detailing age-gate logic, consent workflow, and data retention rules for children's records.
Sample audit report showing parental consent records captured, with timestamps and verification method used.
Screenshots of the age-gating and consent flow in production environment.
Legal opinion or letter confirming compliance with NDPA Section 82 and Child Rights Act 2003.

regulations.penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher

regulations.name: Ghana Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Data Protection Commission (DPC) of Ghana
regulations.summary: One of Africa's earliest comprehensive data protection laws, requiring organizations to register with the Data Protection Commission and comply with data protection principles. Establishes individual rights over personal data and imposes obligations on data controllers.

key_requirements.text: Register data processing systems with the Data Protection Commission
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compile a complete inventory of all data processing systems and activities across the organisation. 2. Complete the Data Protection Commission's registration application form with system descriptions, data types, and retention periods. 3. Submit the application to the DPC and maintain proof of registration.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: prepare data processing activity inventory and complete DPC registration form. IT team: provide technical documentation of all systems handling personal data.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: confirm whether they are registered as data processors with the DPC and provide registration certificates.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DPC registration certificate or acknowledgement letter, completed registration form, and signed record of submission date.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a data controller responsible for compliance
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally designate a senior individual (Data Controller) with clear accountability for data protection compliance. 2. Document the appointment in writing, specifying roles, responsibilities, and reporting structure. 3. Communicate the appointment to the Data Protection Commission and ensure the controller has sufficient authority and resources.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue formal appointment letter and update organisational chart. Legal team: draft Data Controller role description and compliance responsibilities document.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable; this is an internal governance requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter, role description document, board resolution or management memo confirming appointment, and DPC notification (if required by local procedure).

key_requirements.text: Comply with data protection principles: collection limitation, data quality, purpose specification, use limitation, security safeguards, openness
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and document a Data Protection Policy incorporating all six principles: collection limitation, data quality, purpose specification, use limitation, security safeguards, and openness. 2. Conduct a data audit to map current practices against each principle and identify gaps. 3. Implement corrective controls, train staff, and establish monitoring procedures to ensure ongoing compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft comprehensive Data Protection Policy aligned to GDPA principles. IT team: provide system audit report confirming security controls and data quality measures. HR team: schedule mandatory data protection training for all staff.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors and third-party vendors: provide written confirmation that their services comply with the six data protection principles, or sign a Data Processing Agreement incorporating these requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Data Protection Policy document, data audit report, staff training records and completion certificates, Data Processing Agreements with vendors, and periodic compliance monitoring reports.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access to personal information and correction of inaccuracies
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) procedure defining the process, timeframe (typically 30 days), and responsible team. 2. Implement a tracking system to log requests and ensure timely responses with accurate personal data and correction options. 3. Train the designated team on handling sensitive requests and maintaining audit trails.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft DSAR policy and response template. IT team: implement request tracking system and data retrieval process. Customer Service/HR team: assign responsibility for request receipt, verification, and response.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors and third-party service providers: commit to responding to data subject access requests within SLA timeframes and provide data in a portable, intelligible format upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented DSAR procedure, request intake logs and response records showing dates and actions taken, staff training records, and copies of responses provided to data subjects.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent of data subjects for processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design consent forms and mechanisms (digital or physical) that clearly describe what data is collected, why, and how it will be used—using plain language and avoiding pre-ticked boxes. 2. Implement a consent management system to record affirmative, freely given consent before processing begins. 3. Ensure consent records are retained and can be produced as evidence; allow data subjects to withdraw consent easily.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft consent templates and privacy notices for each processing activity. Marketing/Sales team: integrate consent capture into customer onboarding and digital touchpoints. IT team: deploy consent management platform or CRM functionality to record consent timestamps and versions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Marketing, email, and analytics vendors: confirm they will only process data on which valid consent has been recorded, and request copies of their consent documentation standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed or digitally recorded consent forms, consent management system audit logs, privacy notices provided to data subjects, and timestamped proof of consent prior to processing initiation.

key_requirements.text: Implement security measures to protect against unauthorized access and data breaches
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a security risk assessment to identify vulnerabilities in systems handling personal data, covering access controls, encryption, network security, and physical safeguards. 2. Implement technical and organisational controls (e.g. encryption at rest/in transit, role-based access, regular backups, audit logging) and document them in a Security Controls Inventory. 3. Establish an incident response plan, conduct regular security testing, and maintain breach notification procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: perform security audit, deploy encryption tools, configure access controls, and produce Controls Inventory and penetration test reports. Legal team: draft incident response and breach notification procedures. Security team: schedule annual security training and penetration testing.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers, hosting vendors, and third-party processors: provide ISO 27001 certificates or equivalent security attestations, SOC 2 reports, and signed Data Processing Agreements confirming security obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Security risk assessment report, Controls Inventory with implementation dates, encryption and access control configurations, incident response plan, breach notification logs (if applicable), penetration test results, SOC 2/ISO 27001 certificates from vendors, and staff security training records.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers only to countries with adequate protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data mapping exercise to identify all cross-border transfers, including cloud storage, SaaS platforms, and third-party processors, documenting destination countries and data categories.
2. Assess each destination country's data protection adequacy against Ghana DPA standards; document whether it has equivalent legal safeguards or is on the Commissioner's approved list.

3. For inadequate jurisdictions, implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) signed between your organisation and the recipient, and maintain a register of all active transfer mechanisms.
internal_actions: Legal team: maintain a data transfer register listing recipient organisations, countries, data categories, and adequacy determination (with supporting assessment documents).
IT team: document all SaaS and cloud vendor locations and confirm contractual data processing terms align with transfer restrictions.
Compliance team: establish a quarterly review process to validate that new vendors or platform updates do not introduce unapproved transfer routes.
vendor_actions: All vendors/processors: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) that explicitly restrict onward transfers to third parties and confirm sub-processor locations.
Cloud/SaaS vendors: supply their Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent adequacy commitments covering Ghana-to-[destination country] flows.
International vendors: certify their home country's data protection regime or commit to SCCs/BCRs before processing any personal data.
compliance_evidence: Data transfer register showing destination countries, data categories, recipient organisations, and adequacy assessment (with date and reviewer signature).
Signed Data Processing Agreements with each vendor, including clauses prohibiting unauthorised onward transfers.
Copies of Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) signed by your organisation and cross-border recipients.
Documentation of Ghana Data Protection Commissioner's approved adequacy list (if referenced) or legal opinion supporting adequacy determination.
Audit trail of quarterly transfer reviews with sign-off by compliance and legal leads.

regulations.penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices

regulations.name: Rwanda Law on Protection of Personal Data and Privacy
regulations.authority: Rwanda National Cyber Security Authority (NCSA)
regulations.summary: Rwanda's comprehensive law on the protection of personal data and privacy, aligning with international data protection standards. Establishes principles for data processing, individual rights, and obligations for data controllers and processors operating in Rwanda.

key_requirements.text: Process personal data only for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all processing activities in a Data Processing Register, specifying purpose, data categories, and legal basis for each. 2. Conduct internal audit to ensure active processing aligns with documented purposes and eliminate any secondary uses without explicit justification. 3. Update Privacy Notice and Data Processing Agreements to reflect only specified purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Data Processing Register with all active processing activities and their documented purposes. IT team: furnish system configuration documentation confirming data flows match registered purposes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: provide Data Processing Addendums (DPA) confirming they process data only for specified purposes defined in the service contract; supply documentation of their sub-processors and their processing purposes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Register dated and approved by Data Protection Officer or Legal; Privacy Notices referencing specific purposes; Data Processing Agreements with vendors signed within last 12 months; Internal audit report confirming alignment between system usage logs and documented purposes.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent of data subjects or rely on another lawful basis
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all personal data collections and processing activities to identify which rely on consent versus alternative lawful bases (contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest). 2. Implement a consent management system to capture, store, and audit explicit opt-in records with timestamps and proof of consent. 3. For non-consent bases, document the lawful basis assessment and obtain Legal sign-off on Legitimate Interest Assessments (LIA) where applicable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide Lawful Basis Assessment matrix for all processing activities and signed Legitimate Interest Assessments (LIAs). Compliance team: configure consent management system and export consent audit logs. IT team: disable any pre-ticked consent checkboxes and confirm affirmative consent mechanisms in place.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: provide confirmation of their lawful basis for processing customer data; supply executed Data Processing Addendum stating the lawful basis they rely upon; submit proof that their consent mechanisms (if applicable) use affirmative opt-in, not pre-ticked boxes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Lawful Basis Assessment matrix with documented rationale for each processing activity; Consent management system logs showing timestamp, purpose, and explicit user acceptance for consent-based processing; Signed LIAs for Legitimate Interest cases; Screenshots or technical evidence of opt-in mechanisms in customer-facing forms.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal Data Subject Rights Request procedure with defined timelines (typically 30 days) and assign a single point of contact to log, acknowledge, and fulfill requests. 2. Configure IT systems to enable secure data access exports, rectification workflows, erasure (with log retention where required), and restriction flags in customer records. 3. Document each request and response in a Rights Request Register with closure evidence (e.g., confirmation of erasure, corrected data screenshot).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve Data Subject Rights Policy and Request Template. IT team: implement data access export functionality, create rectification workflow, configure erasure/restriction features, and provide monthly Rights Request Register. HR/Customer Service: train staff on request acknowledgment and escalation procedures within 5 days of receipt.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: confirm they will respond to data subject requests routed through your organisation within agreed timeframes; provide signed commitment to support access, rectification, erasure, and restriction requests; supply template for request handling and escalation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Subject Rights Policy and publicly available request procedure; Data Subject Rights Request Register (minimum 12-month history) showing date received, request type, response date, and closure evidence; Samples of fulfilled requests with dated confirmations; Training records for staff handling requests; Screenshots or exports proving technical functionality for access, rectification, and erasure.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Technical and Organisational Measures (TOM) audit to map current security controls (encryption, access controls, audit logging, backup, MFA) against the Rwanda Data Protection Law requirements. 2. Implement missing controls: encrypt personal data at rest (AES-256 minimum) and in transit (TLS 1.2+), enforce multi-factor authentication for administrative access, and configure centralised audit logging. 3. Document all security measures in a Security Controls Matrix and obtain annual attestation from IT/Information Security leadership.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide network architecture diagram, encryption inventory, access control matrix, and audit log samples. Information Security team: conduct TOM gap assessment and provide remediation roadmap with timelines. Compliance team: maintain signed Security Controls Matrix updated annually.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors handling personal data: provide ISO 27001 certificate, SOC 2 Type II report, or detailed TOM documentation covering encryption, access controls, and audit logging; confirm incident response plan and backup/disaster recovery procedures in writing; supply evidence of annual security assessments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed TOM assessment report with gap analysis and remediation status; Security Controls Matrix listing all technical measures (encryption standards, MFA, logging, backup frequency); IT attestation letter confirming implementation of required controls; Encryption key management procedures; Sample audit logs showing access to personal data; Vendor security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) or equivalent TOM documentation.

key_requirements.text: Notify data subjects of data breaches that may cause significant harm
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define a Data Breach Response Plan specifying: detection mechanisms (monitoring, staff reporting), severity assessment criteria (likelihood of significant harm), notification timelines (immediately to DPA, typically 3 days to data subjects), and communication templates. 2. Establish a Breach Notification Register to log all suspected and confirmed breaches, including date discovered, affected records, cause, remediation, and notification evidence. 3. Configure automated security monitoring (log analysis, intrusion detection) and train staff to report suspicious activity within 24 hours.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve Breach Notification Plan and notification letter template. IT team: implement security monitoring tools, configure alerts for unauthorised access, and provide monthly incident logs. Compliance team: maintain Breach Notification Register with assessment and communication records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: confirm they will notify you of any suspected breach within 24 hours; provide signed commitment to investigate and provide details (affected records, cause, remediation) within 3 days; include breach notification obligations in the Data Processing Addendum.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Breach Response Plan with severity criteria and timelines; Breach Notification Register (12-month history) with each breach logged, severity assessed, and resolution documented; Sample breach notification letters sent to data subjects (with personal data redacted); Proof of DPA notification (if required); Security monitoring logs showing detection of sample incidents; Vendor breach notification commitments in signed DPA.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Privacy Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define high-risk processing criteria (e.g., large-scale processing, sensitive data, automated decision-making, vulnerable populations) and maintain a Risk Assessment Register identifying which activities trigger Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) requirements. 2. For each high-risk activity, conduct and document a formal PIA covering: processing purpose, data categories, recipients, risks to data subjects, and mitigation measures. 3. Obtain Data Protection Officer (or Legal/Compliance leadership) sign-off on each PIA and maintain a log of assessments with approval dates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/DPO team: develop PIA template aligned with Rwanda requirements and publish high-risk processing criteria matrix. Product/IT teams: identify high-risk processing activities and complete PIAs for each; provide documentation of mitigation measures implemented. Legal team: review and approve all PIAs before deployment of new processing.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Any vendor introducing new high-risk processing: complete a PIA (or equivalent risk assessment) and share with your organisation; confirm in writing that high-risk processing has been assessed for data protection risks; include PIA outcomes in the Data Processing Addendum.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed high-risk processing criteria matrix; Risk Assessment Register listing all identified high-risk activities and PIA status; Completed PIAs (minimum 1 per high-risk activity, minimum 12-month coverage) with documented risks and mitigation measures; Signed approval evidence (DPO/Legal sign-off) for each PIA; Evidence of mitigation implementation (e.g., encryption deployment, access restriction screenshots, consent mechanism updates).

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for large-scale processing
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Assess whether your organisation qualifies as 'large-scale processing' under Rwanda DPPPA (typically public bodies or private entities processing sensitive data of significant volume/scope).
2. Identify and formally appoint a named individual as Data Protection Officer with documented responsibility charter, reporting line to senior management, and requisite qualifications/experience.
3. Register the DPO role and contact details with the Rwanda National Data Protection Authority and communicate internally to all staff.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and execute a formal DPO appointment letter specifying role, responsibilities, independence safeguards, and term.
HR team: assign job code/cost centre for the DPO position and confirm no conflicts of interest with other roles.
Board/Management: issue resolution or executive memo confirming governance approval and DPO reporting line.
vendor_actions: If outsourcing DPO function: execute a DPO Services Agreement with external provider that guarantees independence, confidentiality, and compliance with RWANDA_DPPPA Article 41-44 requirements.
compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment letter with effective date and role description.
Proof of registration/notification to Rwanda National Data Protection Authority (official acknowledgement).
Board resolution or management authorisation document confirming DPO appointment.
DPO contact details published on organisation website or internal portal.
If externally engaged: executed Data Processing or DPO Services Agreement with independent third party.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers require adequate protection safeguards
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a mapping exercise to identify all cross-border data transfers (to countries outside Rwanda) and classify recipient country adequacy status under Rwanda DPPPA.
2. For transfers to non-adequate countries, implement one or more lawful safeguards: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), or regulatory adequacy determination.
3. Document transfer impact assessments and obtain sign-off from Legal and DPO before any new cross-border flow commences.
internal_actions: IT/Data team: provide complete inventory of systems, databases, and applications that transmit personal data outside Rwanda, including destination country, data categories, and frequency.
Legal team: draft cross-border transfer agreements (SCCs or equivalent); conduct Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) for each route; maintain register of approved transfers.
DPO/Compliance team: review each transfer against Rwanda DPPPA safeguard requirements and issue written approval or exception log.
vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: execute Standard Contractual Clauses (EU/Rwanda approved model or equivalent) with explicit data transfer terms, sub-processor restrictions, and data subject rights.
Vendors in non-adequate countries: provide written confirmation of safeguards applied (encryption, access controls, data minimisation) and agree to regulatory audit rights.
All recipients: sign Data Processing Agreement or Data Transfer Agreement including conflict resolution, liability, and assistance with regulator requests.
compliance_evidence: Completed cross-border data transfer register (recipient country, data category, legal basis, safeguard type).
Executed Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent lawful mechanism for each transfer route.
Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) or Supplementary Measures documentation for non-adequate countries.
DPO written approval or exception record for each cross-border flow.
Vendor Data Processing Agreements with transfer-specific clauses and sub-processor appendices.
Proof of vendor compliance audit or third-party attestation (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2) confirming safeguards in place.

regulations.penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders

regulations.name: Egypt Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Centre (PDPC) - Ministry of Communications and IT
regulations.summary: Egypt's first comprehensive personal data protection law establishing a legal framework for the collection, storage, processing, and transfer of personal data. Requires explicit consent as the primary basis for processing and introduces a licensing regime for data controllers.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit consent for processing personal data (consent is primary legal basis)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design consent forms in Arabic stating specific processing purposes, data categories, and recipient details. 2. Implement a system to capture affirmative consent before data collection (opt-in mechanism, not pre-ticked boxes). 3. Maintain timestamped records of each consent with associated purpose and scope.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft consent templates aligned with PDPL requirements; IT team: configure consent management system with audit logs; Marketing/Sales: provide inventory of all data collection touchpoints requiring consent capture.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party platforms (CRM, email, analytics): provide written confirmation of consent capture functionality and ability to generate proof of consent records upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or digital consent records with timestamps; consent management system configuration documentation; audit logs showing consent collection and storage; template review memo from Legal.

key_requirements.text: Provide privacy notices in Arabic covering processing purposes and data subject rights
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a master privacy notice in Arabic detailing processing purposes, legal basis, data categories, retention periods, and data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability). 2. Deploy the notice at all collection points (website, forms, app, in-store) before requesting personal data. 3. Maintain version control and evidence of deployment across all channels.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: author privacy notice in Arabic with PDPL-specific disclosures; Marketing/Web team: integrate notice into digital platforms with version timestamps; Compliance team: maintain master registry of notice versions and deployment locations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Website hosting provider: confirm notice is displayed and provide screenshot evidence; third-party app developers: embed notice with version identifier and deploy confirmation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed-off Arabic privacy notice document with date; screenshots of notice on website, app, and physical locations; deployment checklist with sign-off; version control log.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, and data portability
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data subject rights request procedure with a single point of contact and 30-day response timeline. 2. Create templates and workflows for handling access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests in a trackable system. 3. Document each request, action taken, and response provided with dates and evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document the rights request procedure in writing; IT team: deploy request tracking system with case numbering and escalation rules; HR/Admin: designate and train rights request handler(s) with contact information.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data management vendors: confirm ability to export data in portable, structured format (CSV/JSON); third-party processors: provide commitment to comply with erasure and rectification requests within 10 business days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed data subject rights procedure document; tracking system user guide and access logs; template request forms; sample case files showing requests, responses, and supporting actions; training records for designated handlers.

key_requirements.text: Notify the PDPC of data breaches in a timely manner
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define a breach notification procedure specifying detection, assessment, and reporting steps with clear timelines (notification to PDPC without undue delay, ideally within 72 hours of discovery). 2. Create a breach incident response team with defined roles and escalation matrix. 3. Document all breaches in a central log with discovery date, assessment findings, and PDPC notification proof.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: provide breach detection and response procedures with timelines; Legal team: draft PDPC notification template in Arabic; Incident Response team: maintain breach register with assessment findings and notification records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: contractually commit to notify organization of any suspected breach within 24 hours; third-party security auditors: provide breach assessment templates and guidance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response procedure document; PDPC notification letters (Arabic) with delivery proof; breach register log with timestamps and assessment details; incident response team roster with contact information; email correspondence or formal notices to PDPC.

key_requirements.text: Restrictions on cross-border data transfers - requires prior PDPC authorization
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all cross-border data transfers (to third countries) and map them against PDPC adequacy determinations and exemptions. 2. For non-adequate countries, apply for and obtain written PDPC authorization before initiating transfers. 3. Maintain current PDPC authorization documents and conduct annual review for new or modified transfers.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Data Governance team: complete data flow mapping showing destination countries for all personal data transfers; Legal team: submit PDPC authorization request (in Arabic) for each non-adequate jurisdiction with risk assessment; Compliance team: maintain PDPC decision register with decision dates and conditions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers (cloud, SaaS, outsourced processors): provide Data Processing Agreements explicitly stating data location; confirm no sub-processing without prior written authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data transfer mapping document (country-by-country list); PDPC authorization letters (signed and dated); Data Processing Agreements with location and transfer terms; internal memo documenting PDPC adequacy assessment methodology; annual transfer review records.

key_requirements.text: Obtain license from PDPC before processing sensitive or biometric data
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all processing activities involving sensitive data (health, biometrics, financial) or biometric processing and determine license requirement under PDPL. 2. Submit license application to PDPC with data protection impact assessment, security measures, and documented legitimate purpose. 3. Obtain and maintain valid PDPC license; prohibit processing until license is granted.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Governance team: inventory all sensitive/biometric data processing with purpose and scope; IT/Security team: prepare data protection impact assessment and technical security documentation; Legal team: draft PDPC license application (in Arabic) with compliance roadmap.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party biometric/health data processors: provide evidence of their own PDPC licensing or certification; confirm contractual obligation to assist organization in maintaining license compliance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: PDPC license decision letter (original, signed); data protection impact assessment document with approval memo; security controls documentation (encryption, access logs, audit trails); license application submission confirmation; inventory of licensed data processing activities; annual compliance attestation from responsible officer.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for processors licensed by the PDPC
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third-party processors currently licensed by the PDPC and maintain a register of their PDPC license status.
2. Formally appoint a Data Protection Officer for each licensed processor via written notice, documenting their name, contact details, and appointment date.
3. Publish or communicate the DPO contact information internally and ensure it is available for data subject inquiries.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide current list of all third-party data processors and verification of their PDPC licensing status.
HR team: coordinate appointment of DPO personnel and issue formal appointment letters with scope of responsibilities.
Compliance team: maintain a register of appointed DPOs, their contact details, and assignment to each licensed processor.
vendor_actions: All licensed processors: provide current PDPC license certificate or registration confirmation.
Licensed processors: acknowledge receipt of DPO appointment notice and confirm DPO contact details for escalations.
compliance_evidence: Register of licensed processors with PDPC license numbers and expiry dates.
DPO appointment letters signed by authorized personnel.
Written confirmation from each processor acknowledging their DPO and contact details.
Internal communications or policy documents listing DPO contact information.

key_requirements.text: Implement technical and organizational security measures proportionate to risk
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) to identify personal data processing activities and associated risks.
2. Design and document technical controls (encryption, access controls, audit logging) and organizational measures (policies, training, incident procedures) matched to the risk level identified.
3. Review and test implemented controls quarterly, updating measures if processing scope or risk profile changes.
internal_actions: IT team: provide inventory of technical systems handling personal data, current encryption standards, access control mechanisms, and audit log retention policies.
Security team: conduct risk assessment across all personal data processing and provide remediation roadmap for identified gaps.
Legal/Compliance team: document organizational security policies (access management, incident response, data retention, staff training) and evidence of staff completion.
Finance team: provide budget allocation and evidence of investment in security infrastructure and tools.
vendor_actions: All processors and service providers: submit details of technical and organizational security measures they employ, including encryption protocols, access controls, and incident response procedures.
Cloud/hosting providers: provide SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification, or equivalent security audit reports covering controls relevant to personal data processing.
Third-party vendors: confirm their data breach notification procedures and timeline for escalating incidents to the organization.
compliance_evidence: Data protection impact assessment (DPIA) document identifying processing activities, data categories, and risk ratings.
Security policies covering encryption standards, access management, authentication mechanisms, and audit logging.
System configuration documentation and screenshots showing encryption, role-based access controls, and logging settings.
Employee training records and completion certificates on data protection and security practices.
Vendor security questionnaire responses or third-party audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001).
Quarterly control testing reports or penetration test results demonstrating ongoing effectiveness.
Incident response plan and breach notification procedures with evidence of staff training.

regulations.penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment

regulations.name: Morocco Law on Protection of Personal Data (Law 09-08)
regulations.authority: Commission Nationale de controle de la protection des Donnees a caractere Personnel (CNDP)
regulations.summary: Morocco's foundational data protection law establishing principles for processing personal data, individual rights, and obligations for data controllers. Requires organizations to declare or obtain authorization for data processing activities from the CNDP.

key_requirements.text: Declare or obtain prior authorization from CNDP for data processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data processing audit to identify all activities and their legal basis. 2. Prepare a CNDP declaration form or authorization application detailing processing purposes, data categories, and retention periods. 3. Submit the completed application to the Commission Nationale de Contrôle de la Protection des Données (CNDP) and retain the acknowledgment or authorization certificate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: compile a processing inventory listing all data processing activities, purposes, and data categories; IT team: provide system architecture documentation showing data flows and storage locations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors processing personal data: provide written confirmation of their processing activities and alignment with CNDP requirements; third-party processors: sign Data Processing Agreements specifying their role and security obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CNDP authorization letter or declaration receipt; signed Data Processing Agreements with vendors; internal processing activity register; proof of CNDP submission.

key_requirements.text: Lawful basis for processing required - consent is primary
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the lawful basis for each processing activity (consent, legal obligation, contractual necessity, vital interest, public task, or legitimate interest). 2. Create or revise privacy notices and consent forms to clearly state the processing basis and obtain explicit consent where required. 3. Establish a consent management system (CMS) to record, track, and audit consent for each data subject.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: develop or update privacy policy and consent templates; IT team: implement consent management system with audit logs; Marketing/HR teams: provide details of current data processing purposes for each data type.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide written statements confirming their lawful basis for any personal data processing they perform on the organisation's behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Updated privacy notice and consent forms; signed consent records with timestamps; CMS configuration and sample audit logs; Data Processing Agreements referencing lawful basis.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, and objection to processing
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a formal data subject rights request procedure (access, correction, deletion, objection) with defined timelines (typically 30 days). 2. Deploy a ticketing system or registry to log, track, and document responses to each request. 3. Train IT and Legal teams on fulfilling requests and establish technical capabilities (e.g., data export, pseudonymization, deletion workflows) to respond accurately.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: document data retrieval, correction, and deletion capabilities in the current systems; Legal team: create a request handling SOP with response templates; HR team: identify staff responsible for processing requests.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and cloud providers: confirm in writing their ability to support data subject rights requests (export, deletion, correction) within agreed timelines and provide technical specifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data subject rights request procedure document; sample completed request tickets with response dates; CMS or ticketing system logs; signed vendor commitments on data rights support.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers restricted to countries with adequate protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all cross-border data transfers and verify that destination countries have been assessed as having adequate data protection by CNDP or equivalent standards. 2. For transfers to non-adequate countries, implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) with recipient organisations. 3. Maintain a register of all cross-border transfers documenting the country, adequacy status, and legal mechanism used.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide a map of all systems and cloud services showing data storage and processing locations; Legal team: verify adequacy status of each destination country and prepare SCCs or BCRs where needed; Finance team: identify all vendors and their server locations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and cloud providers: provide written confirmation of server locations, adequacy certifications (e.g., EU Standard Contractual Clauses if relevant), and commitment to restrict further transfers without explicit approval.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-border transfer register; CNDP adequacy determinations or international adequacy references; executed Standard Contractual Clauses or BCR documentation; vendor location and certification letters.

key_requirements.text: Special category data (health, biometric, religion, political opinion) requires CNDP prior authorization
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify and classify all special category data held (health, biometric, religion, political opinion, union membership, etc.). 2. Prepare a CNDP prior authorization application detailing the purpose, necessity, safeguards, and legal ground for processing special categories. 3. Obtain and retain the CNDP authorization certificate before commencing or continuing processing of these sensitive data.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: conduct a special category data audit across all business units; IT team: document systems storing special category data and security controls; Compliance team: prepare the CNDP authorization application with risk assessment.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors accessing special category data: provide written confirmation that they have received CNDP authorization or will not process special categories without the organisation's prior authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CNDP prior authorization letter for special categories; data inventory identifying special category data locations; vendor written confirmations; Data Processing Agreements restricting special category processing.

key_requirements.text: Security measures to protect personal data from unauthorized access or disclosure
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a security risk assessment covering encryption, access controls, authentication, audit logging, and incident response. 2. Implement and document technical controls: encryption at rest and in transit, role-based access control (RBAC), multi-factor authentication (MFA), and regular security testing. 3. Establish a security monitoring and incident response plan with regular log review and breach notification procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: perform vulnerability scans and penetration tests; provide encryption inventory, RBAC configuration, and access control logs; Security team: develop incident response plan and conduct staff security awareness training; Audit team: schedule quarterly security control reviews.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and cloud providers: provide security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II), detailed Data Processing Agreements with security obligations, and proof of encryption and access controls.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Security risk assessment report; encryption inventory and configuration; RBAC access control matrices; MFA implementation proof; security incident response plan; vendor security certifications; recent penetration test and vulnerability scan reports; system access logs (sample 30-day export).

key_requirements.text: Retention limitation: data must not be kept longer than necessary
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory audit identifying all personal data categories, their business purpose, and current retention duration across all systems and departments.
2. Document explicit retention schedules for each data category, aligned with legal obligations (tax, labour, contract law) and justified business needs, then communicate to all data handlers.
3. Implement automated deletion or anonymisation workflows in IT systems to enforce retention limits, with quarterly reconciliation reports to confirm adherence.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide written justification for each retention period linked to Moroccan tax code (minimum 6 years), labour law, and contractual requirements.
IT team: supply system audit logs showing current data volumes, last access dates, and backup/archive schedules for all personal data repositories.
Data Protection Officer (or appointed manager): produce a signed Data Retention Policy document specifying retention periods by data type and deletion procedures.
vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: submit Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming they will delete or return data upon contract termination and provide evidence of deletion upon request.
Third-party software vendors: provide contractual confirmation of data retention limits they enforce and commitment to support client-initiated deletion requests within 30 days.
Backup/archive service providers: document their retention policies and confirm they will destroy archived personal data at client instruction without indefinite archival.
compliance_evidence: Signed Data Retention Policy document dated and approved by management.
Data inventory register (spreadsheet or database) listing data categories, retention periods, legal basis, and deletion method.
Automated deletion job logs or scheduled task screenshots showing retention enforcement is active.
Vendor Data Processing Addenda with retention clauses highlighted and signed by both parties.
Quarterly deletion confirmation reports (e.g., 'X records deleted on [date]') for 12 months prior to audit.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders

regulations.name: UAE Federal Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: UAE Data Office
regulations.summary: The UAE's first federal personal data protection law (Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021) applying to all entities processing personal data of UAE residents outside of free zones. Establishes data protection principles, individual rights, and creates the UAE Data Office.

key_requirements.text: Lawful basis for processing: consent, contractual necessity, legitimate interests, or legal obligation
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data processing audit to identify all processing activities and document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, or legal obligation) for each. 2. Create a Data Processing Register listing each processing activity with its corresponding lawful basis and retain evidence (e.g., signed consent forms, contract clauses, or legal opinion). 3. Require all vendors handling personal data to sign a Data Processing Addendum confirming their lawful basis and restrictions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide a completed Data Processing Register with lawful basis justification for each activity; Compliance team: maintain evidence files (consent records, contracts, legal opinions) organized by processing purpose.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party processors: sign a Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming lawful basis, permitted use, and sub-processor restrictions; provide written attestation of lawful basis if acting as controller.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Register, executed Data Processing Addendums with all vendors, consent forms or records, contract extracts showing processing authority, legal opinions supporting legitimate interest or legal obligation claims.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, correction, erasure, restriction, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document a formal procedure for handling data subject access, correction, erasure, restriction, and objection requests, including response timelines (typically 30 days) and escalation paths. 2. Implement a request management system (log, track, fulfill) with evidence of response for each request type. 3. Train IT and customer service teams on identifying and processing each request category within regulatory timeframes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft and maintain a Data Subject Rights Procedure document; IT team: configure a ticketing or logging system to record all incoming requests and closure dates; HR team: deliver training records confirming staff understand request types and response deadlines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide written commitment to honor data subject rights requests within 30 days and cooperate with organization's fulfillment process; agree to assist with data correction, erasure, or restriction upon instruction.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Subject Rights Procedure, request log with dates received and responses issued, sample fulfilled requests (redacted), training sign-off sheets, vendor commitment letters.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) if processing personal data on a large scale
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Assess whether your organization processes personal data on a large scale (high volume, sensitive data, systematic monitoring) and determine DPO appointment necessity. 2. If required, appoint a DPO (internal or external) with expertise in data protection law and document the appointment in writing with role, responsibilities, and contact details. 3. Publish DPO contact information internally and notify the UAE Data Office of the appointment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Board: issue a formal letter of appointment or engagement for the DPO with clear mandate and reporting structure; Compliance team: maintain the appointment letter and DPO contact registry; Finance team: ensure DPO budget and resources are allocated.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: If engaging an external DPO, the service provider must provide: signed engagement letter, proof of data protection expertise (certifications or experience), written commitment to independence and confidentiality, and contact details for regulatory queries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment or engagement letter, published DPO contact information, notification acknowledgment from UAE Data Office (if applicable), evidence of DPO reporting line to board or management, external DPO service agreement.

key_requirements.text: Notify the UAE Data Office of personal data breaches within 72 hours
