key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident response plan defining breach detection, internal escalation, impact assessment, and notification workflows; designate a breach notification owner. 2. Configure IT monitoring and logging to detect unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or loss; establish a 72-hour notification trigger upon confirmed breach. 3. Submit a formal breach notification to the UAE Data Office within 72 hours containing incident details, affected data categories, scope, and remedial actions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: implement breach detection mechanisms (logs, alerts, forensic tools) and maintain incident records; Compliance/Legal team: draft a breach notification template and maintain a breach register; Management: confirm a named breach notification owner with authority to escalate.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party processors and service providers: sign an agreement requiring them to notify the organization of breaches within 24 hours; provide evidence of their incident response capabilities (e.g., security logs, incident response procedures).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Incident Response Plan, breach detection system documentation, breach notification letters sent to UAE Data Office (with timestamps), breach register, forensic reports, communications to affected data subjects, evidence of remedial actions taken.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers only to approved countries or with appropriate safeguards
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Maintain a registry of all countries or regions to which personal data is transferred and verify they are approved by UAE authorities or have equivalent data protection standards. 2. For transfers to non-approved countries, implement appropriate safeguards such as Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), or adequacy determinations; document the safeguard type and legal basis. 3. Require vendors receiving data transfers to sign a Data Transfer Agreement (DTA) explicitly confirming approved destination(s) and safeguards in place.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: create and maintain a Data Transfer Registry listing all destination countries, transfer mechanisms (consent, contract, SCC), and safeguard justification; IT team: provide documentation of data flow and destinations; Vendor Management team: ensure all DTAs are executed before transfers commence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors receiving cross-border transfers: execute a Data Transfer Agreement confirming destination countries, transfer mechanism (SCC, BCR, or adequacy decision), and commitment to maintain equivalent protections; provide sub-processor transfer documentation if applicable.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Transfer Registry with country approvals and safeguard documentation, executed Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules, Data Transfer Agreements with all recipients, legal opinions supporting transfer mechanisms, adequacy decision references (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Data minimization and purpose limitation principles apply to all processing
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document a Data Minimization and Purpose Limitation Policy defining which personal data is collected, the explicit purpose for collection, and the retention period; conduct a review to eliminate non-essential data collection. 2. Update all data collection forms, systems, and vendor instructions to collect only necessary data aligned with stated purposes. 3. Implement data retention schedules and automated purging or anonymization processes to delete data when retention periods expire.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft and approve Data Minimization and Purpose Limitation Policy; IT team: audit current data collection and implement field-level restrictions in systems; Finance/HR team: establish data retention schedules by data category and retention period.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party service providers and processors: acknowledge the Data Minimization Policy in their Data Processing Addendum; confirm they will not use personal data beyond the stated purpose or retain data longer than the organization's retention schedule.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Minimization and Purpose Limitation Policy, data inventory showing collected fields and purposes, updated data collection forms or system screenshots, retention schedules by data type, evidence of purging or anonymization (logs or reports), vendor acknowledgment in DPAs.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data security risk assessment identifying all systems, databases, and networks handling personal data, then document baseline technical controls (encryption, access controls, firewalls, intrusion detection).

2. Establish a Security Operations Center (SOC) function or assign responsibility for continuous monitoring, incident response, and vulnerability management, with documented procedures and staff training records.

3. Define and enforce organizational measures including data minimization policies, employee access provisioning/de-provisioning workflows, data retention schedules, and incident response playbooks with defined escalation paths.
internal_actions: IT team: provide network architecture diagram, firewall rules inventory, encryption key management procedures, and system access control matrix (who has access to what personal data systems).
Security team: deliver vulnerability assessment reports, penetration test results, and SOC monitoring logs for the past 12 months.
Legal/Compliance team: submit documented data security policy, incident response plan with timelines, and employee data handling agreements.
HR team: provide evidence of security awareness training completion records for all staff with personal data access.
vendor_actions: Third-party data processors/cloud providers: submit Service Organization Control (SOC 2 Type II) audit report or ISO 27001 certification covering the last 12 months.

All vendors: provide signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) confirming specific technical measures (encryption in transit/rest, access logging, audit trails) and commitment to sub-processor notification.

Hosting/infrastructure vendors: deliver detailed security documentation including incident response procedures, backup and recovery procedures, and data deletion certificates upon contract termination.
compliance_evidence: Data security risk assessment report with identified controls mapped to threats (dated within last 12 months).
ISO 27001 certification or equivalent SOC 2 report for the organization, or comprehensive security audit by independent third party.
Data security policy and information security policy (signed by management, dated, version-controlled).
Documented incident response plan with roles, responsibilities, and escalation matrix (approved by Data Protection Officer or Legal).
Employee training records and sign-off confirmations on data security obligations (roster with dates).
Network architecture diagram, data flow diagram showing encryption points, and access control matrix (current, signed by IT leadership).
Vendor SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certificates and signed Data Processing Agreements with specific technical and organizational measures clauses.
Vulnerability assessment and penetration test reports (independent assessment, dated, with remediation tracking).
Change management log and patch management records demonstrating regular system updates (sample from last 6 months).

regulations.penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
regulations.authority: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC)
regulations.summary: Canada's federal private-sector privacy law governing the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information in commercial activities. Based on 10 Fair Information Principles from the CSA Model Code. Mandatory breach reporting was added in 2018.

key_requirements.text: 10 Fair Information Principles: accountability, identifying purposes, consent, limiting collection, limiting use/disclosure/retention, accuracy, safeguards, openness, individual access, challenging compliance
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft and adopt a comprehensive Privacy Policy documenting all 10 Fair Information Principles with specific procedures for each (e.g., consent mechanisms, retention schedules, access request timelines). 2. Designate a Privacy Officer and establish a Privacy Governance Committee to oversee implementation and review compliance quarterly. 3. Conduct a privacy impact assessment (PIA) on all information systems and data flows to map adherence to each principle.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Privacy Policy and governance charter. IT team: deliver system inventory with data classification and flow diagrams. HR team: provide Privacy Officer appointment memo and committee roster.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: submit Data Processing Agreements (DPA) explicitly confirming their alignment with PIPEDA Fair Information Principles and their specific role in data handling.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Privacy Policy document; Privacy Impact Assessment report; Privacy Governance Committee meeting minutes (last 2 quarters); Data Flow Diagrams; Vendor DPAs with signed acknowledgment of PIPEDA principles.

key_requirements.text: Obtain meaningful consent before collecting, using, or disclosing personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and deploy a standardized consent mechanism (e.g., opt-in checkbox, signed form, or electronic declaration) that captures explicit agreement before any personal information collection or use. 2. Maintain a centralized consent registry documenting the date, type, scope, and method of consent for each individual. 3. Review and refresh consent annually, and implement logic to block processing when valid consent is absent.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide consent language template and scope matrix. IT team: configure consent management system with audit logging and reporting. Marketing/Operations team: implement consent capture at all customer touchpoints.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party processors: confirm in writing that they will not process personal information without documented evidence of organization's prior consent, and provide consent audit logs upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Consent management system screenshots showing consent capture and timestamp; Consent registry export (sample of 50+ records with dates and scope); Signed consent language templates; Vendor confirmations of consent verification practices.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory breach reporting to OPC for breaches posing real risk of significant harm
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Breach Response Protocol defining real risk assessment criteria (scope, sensitivity, likelihood of misuse) and a mandatory escalation path to the Privacy Officer within 24 hours of detection. 2. Document the risk assessment for each breach and submit a formal Breach Notification Report to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner (OPC) within 30 days when real risk threshold is met. 3. Maintain a centralized Breach Log recording all incidents, assessments, and OPC submissions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide Breach Response Protocol and real risk assessment framework. IT/Security team: deliver incident detection logs and forensic reports. Compliance team: maintain Breach Log with OPC submission evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: contractually commit to reporting security incidents to the organization within 24 hours and provide forensic data upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Breach Response Protocol document; Breach Log (minimum last 24 months) with real risk assessments; OPC Breach Notification Report(s) with proof of submission (e.g., OPC case reference numbers); Forensic incident reports for substantive breaches.

key_requirements.text: Notify affected individuals of breaches posing real risk of significant harm
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. For each breach meeting real risk threshold, draft a clear, timely notification letter in both official languages (where required) explaining the incident, data affected, and mitigation steps available. 2. Execute notification via direct contact (email, phone, or registered mail) to affected individuals and maintain a notification log with delivery proof. 3. Provide free credit monitoring or other remedial services as appropriate to the breach severity.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft notification template and approval authority matrix. Communications team: execute and log all individual notifications with delivery receipts. Finance team: arrange credit monitoring vendor and capture enrollment records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Credit monitoring vendor: provide proof of individual enrollment and service availability confirmation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Notification template and approved language (both EN/FR); Notification Log with recipient names, contact method, and delivery date/time; Email/SMS delivery receipts or postal acknowledgments; Credit monitoring enrollment confirmations; Sample notification letter.

key_requirements.text: Maintain breach records for at least 24 months
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Breach Record Retention Policy mandating storage of all breach documentation (incident reports, assessments, notifications, remediation logs) for a minimum of 24 months. 2. Implement a document management system with automated retention tags and deletion schedules to enforce compliance. 3. Conduct annual retention audits to verify all records are preserved and accessible for regulatory review.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure document management system with 24-month retention rules and audit trails. Compliance team: document retention policy and perform annual audit reports. Legal team: certify retention policy compliance.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Document management / archival vendor: provide signed Service Level Agreement (SLA) confirming 24-month minimum retention and provide access/export functionality for auditors.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Breach Record Retention Policy; Document management system configuration screenshots showing retention rules; Annual retention audit reports (last 2 years); Vendor SLA with retention commitments; Sample of retained breach records with metadata (creation date, retention tag).

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Privacy Officer and make their contact information available
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint a Privacy Officer via executive memo or board resolution, documenting their name, title, and contact information (email, phone, office address). 2. Publish the Privacy Officer contact details on the organization's website (Privacy/Contact page), in the Privacy Policy, and in employee handbooks. 3. Establish a dedicated privacy inbox and escalation procedure to ensure all privacy inquiries are logged, acknowledged within 5 business days, and routed to the Privacy Officer.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Board: issue formal Privacy Officer appointment memo with approval signature. Communications/Web team: publish Privacy Officer contact information on website and internal channels. Compliance team: establish privacy inquiry log with response tracking.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External privacy consultants (if applicable): acknowledge receipt of Privacy Officer designation and confirm contact protocol.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Privacy Officer appointment memo or board resolution; Website page screenshot showing Privacy Officer contact details; Privacy Policy with Privacy Officer contact; Employee handbook or intranet listing; Privacy inquiry log (sample last 12 months showing date, inquiry, and response); Email acknowledgment template confirming receipt of privacy inquiries.

key_requirements.text: Honor individual access requests within 30 days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a centralized request intake process with documented receipt timestamps and assign a single point of contact or team to manage all access requests.
2. Map all systems and databases where personal information is stored, and create a data inventory with retrieval timelines for each source.
3. Implement a tracking log with request date, deadline (30 days), retrieval completion date, and delivery method; automate deadline alerts at day 25.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve a standardized access request form and response template that includes confirmation of identity verification steps.
IT team: provide system inventory spreadsheet listing all databases/applications storing personal information with estimated retrieval times for each.
Compliance team: create and maintain a master access request register with status columns (received, in-progress, completed, denied with reason).
HR/Finance teams: confirm which employee records systems (payroll, benefits, performance) are included in scope and their data custodians.
vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide contractual commitment to retrieve and deliver individual's personal information within 15 business days upon request.
Document management vendors: confirm data extraction capability and turnaround time; provide written SLA for access request fulfillment.
Third-party service providers: sign data processing addendum acknowledging obligation to support access requests and provide individual's data within agreed timeframe.
compliance_evidence: Signed access request response template and intake form.
Completed access request register spanning past 24 months showing receipt date, 30-day deadline, completion date, and delivery confirmation.
Sample fulfilled access requests with dates and proof of delivery (email read receipts, signed acknowledgment forms).
Signed vendor agreements or SLAs explicitly committing to data retrieval timelines.
System inventory spreadsheet with data locations and retrieval time estimates.
Automated deadline reminder logs or calendar evidence showing day-25 alerts for pending requests.

regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders

regulations.name: Quebec Act to Modernize Privacy Laws (Law 25)
regulations.authority: Commission d'acces a l'information du Quebec (CAI)
regulations.summary: Quebec's sweeping modernization of its privacy law, often described as the strictest privacy law in Canada and closely aligned with the GDPR. Implemented in three phases (2022-2024), introduces mandatory PIAs, right to erasure, data portability, and GDPR-level penalties.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) before implementing technology involving personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a PIA template aligned with Quebec Law 25 requirements (data flow, risk assessment, mitigation controls). 2. Require IT and Legal teams to jointly complete PIAs before any technology deployment involving personal data collection, processing, or retention. 3. Document sign-off from Privacy Officer and business sponsor, with completion evidence filed before system launch.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide system architecture documentation, data flows, and technical specifications. Legal team: provide consent and regulatory assessment. Privacy Officer: provide PIA template and approval sign-off. Compliance team: establish a PIA register with completion dates and risk ratings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide detailed processing scope, technical security measures, data retention periods, and sub-processor lists relevant to any PIA requirement. Vendors must confirm alignment with Quebec Law 25 standards in their service proposals.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Privacy Impact Assessment documents (one per system/technology initiative), PIA register with timestamps, Privacy Officer approval memos, risk mitigation action logs, and system launch authorization tied to PIA completion.

key_requirements.text: Right to erasure (be forgotten) for individuals
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all systems and databases storing personal data, including retention rules and deletion workflows. 2. Implement a formal 'right to erasure' request process with defined timelines (typically 30 days) and create a tracking log for all erasure requests. 3. Verify complete deletion across all systems, backups, and vendor systems through IT audit reports.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide data inventory, deletion capabilities per system, and backup retention schedules. Legal team: draft erasure request procedure and response templates. HR team: define roles responsible for processing and fulfilling erasure requests. Compliance team: establish erasure request log with request date, subject, systems affected, and deletion completion evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: confirm capability and timeline to delete personal data upon client request; provide written commitment on backup retention periods and deletion methodology. Vendors must commit to providing deletion confirmation certificates.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data inventory document, signed erasure request procedure, sample completed erasure request forms with proof of deletion (IT audit reports, database deletion logs, vendor deletion certificates), and erasure request log showing 100% fulfillment rates.

key_requirements.text: Right to data portability in a commonly used technological format
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all personal data categories and specify export formats (CSV, JSON, XML) that are commonly used and machine-readable. 2. Build or configure automated data export functionality accessible via secure user portal or API. 3. Test export accuracy and completeness quarterly, with IT documenting supported formats and technical specifications.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: design and implement data export module; provide technical documentation of export formats, API specifications, and security controls. Data team: define data mapping for each export format. Compliance team: document supported formats and user instructions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: provide data portability export capability (or confirm data retrieval method) for any hosted personal data. Vendors must specify supported formats and turnaround time for fulfilling portability requests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Technical specification document describing export formats and API; sample data portability export files; user-facing instructions for requesting data portability; testing reports confirming export accuracy and completeness; vendor data portability commitments in service agreements.

key_requirements.text: Disclosure of automated decision-making when exclusively based on personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit all systems using automated decision-making (algorithms, AI, scoring models) to identify which ones rely exclusively on personal information. 2. Create disclosure notices (in plain language) for each automated decision, published in terms of service and made available at point of use. 3. Document the logic, significance, and consequences of each automated decision system in a central register.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide inventory of all automated decision-making systems, algorithms, and machine learning models in use. Data Science/Analytics team: explain the inputs, logic, and outputs of each automated system. Legal team: draft clear, plain-language disclosure notices. Compliance team: maintain a register of all automated decision systems with descriptions and disclosure dates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: disclose any automated decision-making systems they operate on your behalf; provide technical documentation of algorithm inputs and decision factors; confirm customer-facing disclosures are in place.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Automated decision-making system inventory/register; published disclosure notices (on website, in terms of service, or in user interfaces); plain-language descriptions of each algorithm and its consequences; vendor agreements specifying automated decision-making transparency obligations; screenshot evidence of live disclosures.

key_requirements.text: Explicit consent in clear and simple language; bundled consent not permitted
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit existing consent forms to eliminate bundled or pre-checked consents; redesign each consent request to cover a single, specific purpose with clear, non-technical language. 2. Implement separate opt-in mechanisms for each consent type (e.g., marketing, analytics, third-party sharing) with individual checkboxes and toggle switches. 3. Retain signed/timestamped proof of each specific consent in a consent management system.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide current consent forms and draft revised versions with unbundled, plain-language consent language. Marketing/Product team: identify all consent purposes in current use. IT team: implement consent management system with granular, separate consent captures. Compliance team: establish consent audit log and quarterly form review schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors: review and commit to respecting only unbundled, granular consents for data processing. Vendors must confirm they will not accept bundled or pre-checked consents and must return proof of specific consent per use case.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Updated consent forms (before/after versions); consent management system configuration showing separate consent options; sample captured consent records with timestamps and specific purposes; audit trail of consent choices per individual; internal memo confirming elimination of bundled consent practices; screenshots of live consent interfaces.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Privacy Officer and publish their contact information on the organization's website
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a Privacy Officer role (internal or external) with defined responsibilities and reporting line to senior leadership or board. 2. Publish the Privacy Officer's name, title, email, and phone number on the organization's website (typically in Privacy, Legal, or Contact sections). 3. Maintain an internal record confirming appointment, authority level, and update the website listing at least annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide employment contract or engagement letter for Privacy Officer role, including authority and responsibilities. Legal/Compliance team: confirm Privacy Officer role scope and escalation procedures. Communications/Web team: publish Privacy Officer contact information on website with version control.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: If using external Privacy Officer: vendor must provide proof of engagement (contract, credentials, availability commitment) and confirm direct contact availability to your organization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Privacy Officer appointment letter or employment contract; website screenshot showing published Privacy Officer contact details; internal organizational chart confirming reporting line; Privacy Officer credentials or certifications; annual website audit confirming listing accuracy; contact verification log showing Privacy Officer accessibility.

key_requirements.text: Notify the CAI and individuals of confidentiality incidents within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident detection and classification procedure that identifies confidentiality breaches within 24 hours of discovery.

2. Create a templated notification package for CAI (Commission d'accès à l'information du Québec) and affected individuals, including breach description, date, affected data categories, and remediation steps.
3. Implement a tracked escalation workflow with timestamps to ensure all notifications are sent within the 72-hour window, with proof-of-delivery records maintained.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft incident notification template compliant with CAI requirements and Quebec Act requirements.
IT/Security team: provide incident detection logs, breach discovery timestamps, and escalation records for the past 12 months.
HR/Communications team: define roles and sign-off authority for notification approvals to ensure timely dispatch.
vendor_actions: Service providers: provide contractual commitment to notify your organisation of any breaches within 24 hours of discovery.
Cloud/hosting vendors: furnish security incident response SLA documentation specifying notification timelines and escalation contacts.
compliance_evidence: Incident response policy document with 72-hour notification requirement and audit trail procedures.
CAI breach notification letters with timestamps and delivery confirmation receipts.
Individual notification emails/letters with certified delivery proof (registered mail, read receipts).
Internal incident register showing discovery date, notification date, and evidence of CAI and individual communications.
Screenshots or logs of incident ticketing system showing 72-hour compliance per incident.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border transfer safeguards assessed through Privacy Impact Assessments
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) for each cross-border data transfer jurisdiction, documenting adequacy of protection laws and safeguard mechanisms (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, binding corporate rules).
2. Maintain a cross-border transfer register listing all vendors, receiving countries, data categories, transfer mechanisms, and PIA sign-off dates.
3. Review and update PIAs annually and whenever transfer mechanisms or destination country laws change, maintaining dated versions and approval signatures.
internal_actions: Legal/Privacy team: conduct PIA template and complete assessments for each destination country, documenting legal adequacy analysis.
IT/Data Governance team: provide inventory of all cross-border transfers (cloud services, backup vendors, analytics platforms, HR systems) with destinations and data categories.
Compliance team: establish annual PIA review schedule and maintain centralized register with version control and approval evidence.
vendor_actions: All cross-border service providers: provide certified documentation of data storage location, sub-processor locations, and security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, regional equivalents).
Cloud vendors: furnish Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent transfer mechanism documentation signed by both parties.
Data processors: provide written confirmation of compliance with Quebec data protection principles and confirmation that no onward transfers occur without consent.
compliance_evidence: Signed Privacy Impact Assessments for each cross-border transfer, documenting legal framework adequacy analysis and safeguard evaluation.
Cross-border transfer register (spreadsheet or database) listing vendor name, country, data categories, transfer mechanism, and PIA reference.
Signed Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules agreements with all cross-border data recipients.
Vendor security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 reports, or equivalent) confirming data protection controls in receiving countries.
Annual PIA review records with dated sign-off showing updates triggered by regulatory changes in destination jurisdictions.

regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties

regulations.name: Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation
regulations.authority: Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC), Competition Bureau, OPC
regulations.summary: One of the world's strictest anti-spam laws, requiring express or implied consent before sending commercial electronic messages (CEMs), installing computer programs, or collecting email addresses through electronic means.

key_requirements.text: Express or implied consent required before sending commercial electronic messages (CEMs)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all consent collection methods (web forms, telephone recordings, written agreements) and classify each as express or implied consent. 2. Establish a consent capture system that records date, method, and recipient identifier before any CEM is sent. 3. Create a consent verification process requiring sign-off from Legal before launching any CEM campaign.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written consent classification matrix for all collection methods. IT team: provide technical specifications for consent database including timestamp, source, and recipient tracking. Marketing team: provide list of all active CEM campaigns with corresponding consent records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Email service provider: sign Data Processing Agreement confirming consent verification capability and provide API documentation for consent flag integration. Marketing automation vendor: provide written confirmation that consent checking is performed before message transmission.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent records database export (date, method, recipient, express/implied classification), consent classification policy document, sample CEM campaign launch approvals signed by Legal, screenshot of consent verification workflow in marketing system.

key_requirements.text: Identify the sender clearly in every message including name and contact information
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a mandatory sender identification template including legal business name, physical mailing address, and contact telephone or email. 2. Establish a message review checklist requiring verification that all CEMs contain complete sender details before deployment. 3. Implement automated template validation in email systems to prevent send if sender fields are empty.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: provide approved sender identification template and brand guidelines. IT team: configure email system validation rules to block messages missing sender information fields. Marketing team: provide sample of all CEM templates currently in use for audit.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Email service provider: provide written confirmation of message header validation rules and share technical log of blocked send attempts due to incomplete sender data. Marketing platform vendor: document how sender field requirements are enforced at template creation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved sender identification template document, email system validation rule configuration export, sample of 10 recent CEMs showing sender details, email service provider technical specification confirming sender validation enforcement.

key_requirements.text: Provide a functioning unsubscribe mechanism in every CEM
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Embed an unsubscribe link or reply-to address in every CEM template as a mandatory field that cannot be removed. 2. Direct unsubscribe requests to a monitored inbox or automated system that confirms receipt to the sender. 3. Test all unsubscribe mechanisms monthly to ensure functionality across email clients and devices.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide CEM template configuration showing unsubscribe mechanism as mandatory field, and monthly unsubscribe mechanism test results. Legal team: provide unsubscribe process documentation. Customer Service team: provide unsubscribe request log and confirmation sending procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Email service provider: provide technical documentation of unsubscribe link generation and confirmation that unsubscribe URLs are tested before send. Marketing automation vendor: provide API documentation proving unsubscribe mechanism is non-removable in template design.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CEM template configuration export showing unsubscribe field as mandatory, sample of 10 recent CEMs displaying functional unsubscribe mechanism, monthly test results report with dates and unsubscribe success verification, email service provider technical specification document.

key_requirements.text: Honor unsubscribe requests within 10 business days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the unsubscribe request process including receipt timestamp, processing steps, and completion confirmation. 2. Configure automation in email systems to suppress unsubscribed addresses within 5 business days (allowing 5-day buffer within the 10-day requirement). 3. Establish a weekly compliance report tracking all unsubscribe requests received and corresponding removal dates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure email system to auto-suppress addresses within 5 days and provide weekly suppression list reports with timestamps. Customer Service team: document unsubscribe request intake and log (date received, date processed, recipient identifier). Compliance team: create weekly audit report comparing request dates to suppression dates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Email service provider: provide written SLA confirming unsubscribe processing within 5 business days and supply automated weekly reports showing request received dates and suppression effective dates. Marketing automation vendor: document suppression list update frequency and provide API logs showing unsubscribe processing timestamps.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Weekly unsubscribe compliance reports showing request date and suppression completion date for all requests, email service provider SLA document, email system suppression list update logs with timestamps, sample unsubscribe request records with processing dates, automated suppression audit trail export.

key_requirements.text: Express consent required before installing computer programs on third-party devices
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Require all software vendors and internal development teams to obtain documented express written consent before any computer program installation on third-party devices, separate from general service consent. 2. Create a program installation consent form specifying the software name, function, and device impact, requiring explicit acknowledgment. 3. Maintain an audit log of all program installations with associated consent records linked by user identifier.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft express consent template for software installation specific to each program type (plugins, agents, mobile apps). IT team: provide inventory of all programs installed on user devices and configure installation logs capturing user, program, date, and consent reference. Security/Compliance team: establish monthly audit process verifying all installations have linked consent records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Software vendors: provide written confirmation that express consent is obtained before installation and supply installation logs showing user authentication and date-time stamps. Third-party device management providers: confirm that installation consent is captured and stored separately from general service agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Express software installation consent template document, signed consent records for each program (user name, program name, date, signature), installation audit log export showing program, user, date, and consent reference, vendor confirmation letters regarding consent practices, monthly compliance audit reports.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records proving consent for at least 3 years
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a centralized consent records repository with metadata tagging (consent date, type, recipient, method) and configure automatic retention policy for minimum 3 years. 2. Establish a records management schedule documenting retention periods for all consent types and schedule records for secure deletion after 3 years. 3. Conduct annual audit of consent records storage to verify completeness, accessibility, and retention compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provision secure storage system for consent records with immutable audit trails, configure 3-year retention policy, and provide quarterly storage capacity reports. Legal/Compliance team: document records management schedule and consent retention policy. Records Management team: perform annual audit confirming all consent records within 3-year window are retained and older records are securely deleted.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud storage/backup vendor: sign Data Processing Agreement confirming 3-year retention capability, immutable audit logging, and provide monthly retention compliance reports. Email service provider: confirm consent records are retained for 3 years and provide export functionality for audit purposes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed records management policy with 3-year retention schedule, centralized consent database configuration documentation showing retention settings, annual records audit report with record counts by age cohort, quarterly storage compliance reports, vendor Data Processing Agreements confirming retention terms, sample consent records export demonstrating complete metadata.

key_requirements.text: Implied consent expires after 2 years (for most business relationships)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a centralized consent tracking system (database or CRM) that records the date each implied consent relationship begins and automatically flags records at the 24-month mark for re-validation.

2. Create a documented procedure requiring the organization to either obtain fresh express consent or cease sending commercial messages 2 years after the last substantive business transaction or communication with each recipient.
3. Conduct quarterly audits of the consent database to identify and purge expired implied consent records, with monthly reports to compliance leadership documenting removals.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve the written 2-year implied consent expiry policy and retention schedule.
IT team: configure CRM/database with automated 24-month expiry alerts and provide monthly consent age reports with recipient counts by expiry date.
Marketing/Communications team: provide list of all current recipients claiming implied consent and document the business relationship date for each.
vendor_actions: Email service provider (ESP) or CRM vendor: confirm capability to tag, segment, and auto-suppress records based on consent expiry date; provide technical specification or configuration guide.

Third-party data provider (if used): certify in writing that all provided contact lists include documented relationship start dates and are screened to exclude contacts older than 2 years without fresh consent.
compliance_evidence: Signed and dated Implied Consent Expiry Policy document, approved by Legal and circulated to all teams.
Consent database schema or CRM configuration documentation showing the 24-month auto-flag and suppression mechanism.
Monthly or quarterly Consent Age Audit Reports showing date ranges, recipient counts, and records removed due to expiry.
Signed Service Level Agreement or technical addendum from ESP/CRM vendor confirming 2-year expiry automation.
Sample screenshots or logs from the CRM showing at least one expired consent record flagged and suppressed in the past 12 months.

regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals

regulations.name: OSFI Guideline B-13: Technology and Cyber Risk Management
regulations.authority: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI)
regulations.summary: OSFI's binding guideline setting out technology and cyber risk management expectations for all federally regulated financial institutions (FRFIs) in Canada including banks, insurance companies, and trust companies.

key_requirements.text: Board-level cyber risk oversight, accountability, and risk appetite definition
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Board Cyber Risk Committee or assign cyber oversight to existing Board committee with documented charter defining roles and responsibilities. 2. Define and document the organization's cyber risk appetite statement, including tolerance thresholds for incidents, availability targets, and data breach scenarios. 3. Require quarterly Board reporting on cyber risk posture, emerging threats, and compliance status with documented meeting minutes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretary: provide Board charter or committee charter establishing cyber risk governance; Risk/Compliance team: deliver cyber risk appetite statement signed by Board; Internal Audit: compile quarterly cyber risk dashboard reports presented to Board with attendance records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: No direct vendor actions required; however, third-party audit firms may be engaged to validate Board governance design through control testing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board charter or committee charter dated and signed; cyber risk appetite policy document with Board approval signature; quarterly Board meeting minutes documenting cyber risk discussions; Board-approved cyber risk register with risk ratings.

key_requirements.text: Technology and cyber risk identification, assessment, and management program
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct organization-wide technology and cyber risk assessment covering all systems, data flows, and third-party integrations, documented in a risk register with likelihood and impact ratings. 2. Design and implement a formal Technology and Cyber Risk Management Program with documented policies, procedures, roles, and escalation workflows approved by senior management. 3. Establish periodic review cycles (minimum semi-annual) with documented evidence of risk reassessment, remediation tracking, and status reporting to governance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: deliver comprehensive technology risk inventory including asset classifications, data sensitivity mappings, and system interdependencies; Risk team: produce cyber risk assessment report with heat maps and risk scoring; Compliance team: develop and maintain Technology and Cyber Risk Management Program documentation with version control and approval signatures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party technology providers: supply current System and Organization Controls (SOC 2 Type II) reports or ISO 27001 certificates; confirm in writing their adherence to data protection and incident reporting obligations aligned with organization's risk appetite.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Technology risk register with dated entries, risk ratings, and remediation plans; Technology and Cyber Risk Management Program policy document with management approval; risk assessment reports (minimum semi-annual) with sign-off dates; remediation tracking logs showing closure evidence.

key_requirements.text: Cyber resilience testing including penetration testing and cyber war-gaming exercises
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Commission annual penetration testing (external and internal scope) by qualified independent third party, with documented scope, methodology, findings report, and remediation action plan. 2. Conduct cyber tabletop exercises or war-gaming scenarios at least annually, simulating realistic attack vectors and response procedures, with documented exercise plan, participant list, and findings summary. 3. Document all testing results, remediation efforts, and evidence of control improvements in a resilience testing log reviewed by senior management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: define penetration testing scope and coordinate with external tester; prepare internal network documentation and access for testing; Risk/Security team: design and facilitate tabletop scenarios with cross-functional participants; Compliance team: maintain testing calendar, collect all reports, and track remediation completion.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Penetration testing vendor: deliver scope of work, Rules of Engagement signed by both parties, detailed findings report with CVSS ratings, and remediation recommendations; provide proof of tester credentials and liability insurance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual penetration test report dated and signed by vendor with scope and methodology documented; tabletop exercise plan and participant sign-in sheet; exercise findings and recommendations summary; remediation action plan with tracked completion dates; evidence of vulnerabilities remediated post-testing.

key_requirements.text: Third-party and supply chain technology risk management requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain a third-party technology vendor inventory with documented risk ratings, service dependencies, and data access classifications. 2. Establish third-party cyber risk management requirements in contracts, including incident notification timelines (24–48 hours), data protection standards, security certifications, and right to audit clauses. 3. Conduct initial and ongoing (minimum annual) third-party risk assessments using documented questionnaires, audit reports, and compliance certificates; track findings and remediation in a vendor risk log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: maintain vendor master list with contract review dates and cyber requirements clauses; Risk/Compliance team: develop third-party cyber risk assessment questionnaire and maintain completed assessments; Legal team: ensure all vendor contracts include cyber incident notification, data protection, and audit rights clauses; IT team: document system-to-vendor data flows and dependency maps.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All technology vendors: complete annual cyber risk assessment questionnaire with attestations; provide current SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or equivalent security certification; commit in writing to incident notification within 24–48 hours; execute Data Processing Agreements (DPA) or Security Addendums specifying data handling and breach notification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Third-party vendor inventory spreadsheet with risk ratings and assessment dates; executed vendor contracts with dated cyber risk clauses; completed vendor cyber risk questionnaires with dates and signed attestations; vendor security certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001) with current expiry dates; vendor risk assessment summary reports with documented follow-up actions.

key_requirements.text: Notify OSFI of technology or cyber incidents within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define 'technology or cyber incident' clearly in policy, including criteria that trigger the 72-hour OSFI notification requirement (e.g., material data loss, system unavailability >4 hours, compromise of customer data). 2. Establish an incident reporting workflow with documented escalation paths, a designated incident response coordinator, and a template for OSFI notification that captures incident timeline, impact, and response actions. 3. Create and maintain an incident log with time-stamped entries documenting discovery, assessment, OSFI notification timing, and incident closure.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: define incident classification criteria and severity thresholds that determine OSFI notification requirement; develop incident response procedure with escalation contact list; Chief Compliance Officer or designated officer: confirm incident notification authority and OSFI contact details. Legal team: draft OSFI notification template with required disclosures; Compliance team: maintain incident log with 72-hour notification tracking.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs) or incident response vendors: commit contractually to notify organization of detected security incidents within 2 hours; provide incident evidence, forensic reports, and remediation recommendations within agreed timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Incident definition and notification policy document with Board or management approval; completed incident log with dated entries showing discovery, initial assessment, OSFI notification date/time, and incident outcome; copies of incident notifications sent to OSFI (if any) with evidence of 72-hour threshold compliance; incident response procedures document with escalation flowchart.

key_requirements.text: Scenario-based stress testing for technology and cyber incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a scenario-based stress testing framework that includes 3–5 realistic technology and cyber incident scenarios (e.g., ransomware attack, data breach, system outage, supply chain compromise) with defined impact parameters and recovery objectives. 2. Execute stress tests at least annually, simulating business impact, recovery time, financial loss, and regulatory response; document assumptions, results, and recovery capability findings. 3. Track remediation of identified gaps in recovery plans, backup systems, and incident response capabilities; report results and improvements to Board-level governance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk/Security team: design scenario-based stress test framework with 3–5 realistic cyber incident scenarios and documented assumptions; Finance and Operations teams: participate in scenario testing and provide recovery time and financial impact estimates. IT team: validate backup and recovery procedures during stress tests, documenting actual recovery times achieved. Compliance team: compile stress test report with results, gap findings, and remediation tracking.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Disaster Recovery (DR) / Business Continuity (BC) service providers: validate recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) under stress test conditions; provide documented evidence of failover capability and recovery execution.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Scenario-based stress test framework document describing 3–5 scenarios with impact assumptions; annual stress test execution report with date, participants, scenario results, and actual recovery metrics; gap analysis identifying control weaknesses; remediation action plan with completion tracking; Board report summarizing stress test findings and recovery improvements implemented.

key_requirements.text: Data security classification and encryption standards implementation
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory across all systems and classify each dataset by sensitivity level (public, internal, confidential, restricted) based on regulatory, competitive, and personal information criteria.
2. Define encryption standards for each classification tier—specify algorithms (e.g., AES-256), key management protocols, and encryption scope (data at rest and in transit).
3. Deploy technical controls to enforce encryption policies and perform quarterly compliance audits to verify adherence across all storage and transmission channels.
internal_actions: IT team: provide data inventory spreadsheet with classification mappings, encryption policy document, and system configuration baselines.
Legal team: confirm regulatory and contractual data protection requirements that inform classification levels.
IT team: supply encryption deployment logs, key management system access records, and audit reports from encryption compliance scans.
vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide encryption deployment certificates, key escrow agreements, and attestations that encryption is applied per agreed standards.
Third-party processors: submit data processing addendums confirming encryption of personal information and encryption algorithm specifications.
Software vendors: deliver product security datasheets detailing native encryption capabilities and compatibility with organization's encryption standards.
compliance_evidence: Signed data classification policy and encryption standards document.
Current data inventory with classification tags and encryption status per dataset.
Encryption configuration screenshots or system logs showing active encryption on critical systems.
Third-party vendor encryption attestations and Data Processing Agreements with encryption clauses.
Quarterly encryption compliance audit reports with remediation records.

key_requirements.text: Annual board report on technology and cyber risk
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal annual reporting cycle requiring the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or equivalent to compile cyber and technology risk metrics (incidents, vulnerabilities, regulatory changes, system availability) for board review.

2. Create a board-level report template that summarizes risk landscape, remediation progress, emerging threats, technology investments, and risk tolerance alignment; present to the board at least once annually.
3. Document board discussions, decisions, and management action plans in board minutes; retain signed meeting records and follow-up accountability logs.
internal_actions: CISO/IT leadership: prepare annual technology and cyber risk report including incident statistics, vulnerability assessments, regulatory compliance status, and forward-looking risk scenarios.
Board secretary: schedule dedicated board meeting(s) for cyber risk review and ensure formal documentation in board minutes with attendance records.
Finance/Risk team: provide technology risk budget allocation, capital expenditure trends, and insurance coverage details relevant to cyber risk.
Compliance team: supply regulatory change summary and third-party audit findings (SOC 2, penetration test results) for the reporting period.
vendor_actions: External auditors: provide SOC 2 Type II reports or equivalent third-party cyber risk assessments covering the reporting year.
Penetration testing firms: deliver annual penetration test reports and remediation tracking to support cyber risk narrative.
Insurance broker: submit annual cyber insurance policy summary and claims history relevant to board risk awareness.
compliance_evidence: Board-approved annual technology and cyber risk report signed by CISO and board chair.
Board meeting minutes documenting cyber risk discussion, approvals, and assigned responsibilities with dated signatures.
Management action plan tied to board decisions, with documented progress tracking for prior-year items.
SOC 2 Type II report or equivalent third-party audit covering the reporting period.
Annual penetration test report and incident summary for the fiscal year.

regulations.penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs

regulations.name: Argentina Personal Data Protection Act (Ley 25.326)
regulations.authority: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica (AAIP)
regulations.summary: Argentina's foundational data protection law and one of the first in Latin America to receive EU adequacy recognition (2003). Establishes principles for processing personal data, rights of data subjects, and requires registration of databases.

key_requirements.text: Register personal information databases with the AAIP's Registro Nacional de Bases de Datos
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a complete inventory of all databases containing personal information, including data categories, volume, and processing purposes. 2. Prepare and submit the registration application to AAIP's Registro Nacional de Bases de Datos with required metadata and data controller details. 3. Maintain current registration status and update AAIP within 30 days of any material changes to database scope or processing activities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft database inventory spreadsheet listing all personal data repositories, processing purposes, and data retention schedules. Compliance team: prepare AAIP registration submission documentation with organizational contact details and data protection officer designation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide written confirmation of any personal data they process on your behalf, including database location, volume, and processing scope, to support registration completeness.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: AAIP registration certificate or acknowledgment letter; internal database inventory register with registration dates; evidence of AAIP submission and confirmation of current registration status.

key_requirements.text: Obtain informed consent for the processing of personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop written informed consent templates specific to each processing purpose (marketing, profiling, third-party sharing) that clearly explain data use in plain language. 2. Implement a consent capture mechanism (web form, checkbox, signed agreement) that records consent date, purpose, and data subject signature or explicit confirmation. 3. Establish a process to review and refresh consent annually or when processing purposes materially change.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create template consent forms for each data processing activity with clear language about purposes, recipients, and retention. IT team: configure consent management system or CRM to timestamp and store all consent records with audit trails.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Marketing vendors: provide proof that consent is obtained before sending communications and that opt-out mechanisms are implemented. Data processors: sign Data Processing Agreements confirming they process data only on documented instructions with valid consent evidenced.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed or electronically confirmed consent forms with dates and purposes; consent management system logs showing consent timestamp and version; annual consent refresh records; Data Processing Agreements with all vendors.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, suppression, and confidentiality
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal data subject rights request procedure (written form, email template, online portal) that specifies submission methods and 10-day response deadline. 2. Create internal workflows to handle access (data export), rectification (correction), suppression (deletion), and confidentiality requests with clear ownership and escalation paths. 3. Document all requests, responses, and actions taken; maintain records for audit purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Data Subject Rights Request Policy and response templates for access, rectification, suppression, and confidentiality claims. IT team: implement secure request portal or mailbox, establish data export procedures, and configure records retention for request documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customer service platforms: integrate rights request intake form and escalation workflow. Data processors: commit contractually to execute rectification or suppression instructions within 5 business days of notification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published Data Subject Rights Request procedure and templates; request tracking log with submission dates, request types, and response dates; sample fulfilled requests with data extracts or confirmation emails; signed Data Processing Agreements requiring processor cooperation.

key_requirements.text: Special protection for sensitive data (health, political opinions, religion, race, sexual orientation)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Classify all personal data fields to identify sensitive categories (health, political affiliation, religion, race, sexual orientation) and restrict collection to explicit legal basis or documented consent. 2. Implement enhanced access controls, encryption, and audit logging for sensitive data repositories separate from general personal data systems. 3. Conduct quarterly reviews of sensitive data handling to ensure it is minimized, segregated, and processed only for stated lawful purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Governance team: create sensitive data classification matrix and map all database fields. IT team: deploy encryption for sensitive data at rest and in transit; configure role-based access restrictions and logging. Compliance team: document legal basis (explicit consent, legal obligation, or vital interest) for each sensitive data category.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: provide encryption certificates and access control documentation confirming sensitive data is isolated and encrypted. Vendors processing sensitive data: sign enhanced Data Processing Agreements with specific safeguards and audit rights.
