key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data classification register identifying sensitive fields; encryption certificates and key management documentation; access control logs showing restricted user access to sensitive data; signed enhanced DPAs with sensitive data handlers; legal basis documentation for sensitive data collection.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border transfers only to countries with adequate protection levels or with AAIP authorization
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all jurisdictions where personal data is transferred, stored, or processed and verify adequacy of data protection standards (EU, USA under Privacy Shield/SCCs, or AAIP-approved countries). 2. For non-adequate jurisdictions, obtain AAIP written authorization or execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with third-country processors. 3. Maintain a register of all cross-border transfers with corresponding adequacy findings or AAIP approvals.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create cross-border transfer inventory by country and purpose; assess data protection adequacy for each jurisdiction and document findings. Compliance team: prepare AAIP authorization request or SCC documentation for transfers to non-adequate countries.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: International processors/cloud providers: provide country-by-country data location maps and confirmation of adequacy certifications (Privacy Shield, SCCs, GDPR adequacy). Vendors: execute AAIP-compliant Data Processing Agreements with SCC clauses for non-adequate jurisdictions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-border transfer register with destination countries and adequacy justifications; AAIP authorization letter for non-adequate transfers; executed Standard Contractual Clauses with international processors; data location attestations from cloud/hosting vendors.

key_requirements.text: Security measures proportionate to the nature and amount of data stored
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) or risk analysis proportionate to the volume, sensitivity, and processing scope of personal data held. 2. Implement security controls aligned to findings: encryption, access controls, audit logging, employee training, incident response, and backup/recovery procedures. 3. Document all security measures in a Security Controls Inventory and review effectiveness annually or after any security incident.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: perform DPIA or risk assessment; document findings with baseline security controls (encryption standards, MFA, firewall rules, intrusion detection). IT team: deploy and configure controls; maintain audit logs and patch management records. HR team: conduct annual data protection training and maintain training records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: provide SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification. Third-party processors: demonstrate equivalent security controls via audit reports, penetration test summaries, and incident response procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Protection Impact Assessment or risk analysis report; Security Controls Inventory documenting implemented measures; audit logs from systems processing personal data; employee training certificates; vendor security audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001); incident response plan and response logs.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on selling databases to unauthorized third parties
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
how_to_meet: 1. Document all legitimate purposes for which personal data may be shared (e.g., legal obligation, contractual performance, regulatory compliance) and formally authorize only those uses in writing.
2. Implement technical access controls (e.g., role-based permissions, API key restrictions) and audit logs to prevent unauthorized extraction or bulk transfer of personal data databases.
3. Establish a quarterly review process to verify that no data sales or transfers to third parties have occurred outside pre-approved, documented channels.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide written list of authorized data-sharing purposes and third parties; draft data sale prohibition clause for internal policies.
IT team: provide access control matrix, user permission reports, and 12-month database access/export audit logs filtered for bulk transfers.
Compliance team: maintain register of all third-party data disclosures with justification (legal basis, recipient, data scope, date).
vendor_actions: Data processors or hosting providers: sign Data Processing Addendum (DPA) explicitly prohibiting sale, re-use, or unauthorized transfer of personal data; provide annual attestation confirming no unauthorized disclosures occurred.
Any analytics, marketing, or business intelligence vendors: provide contractual commitment that they will not purchase, license, or resell client personal data databases.
compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all vendors containing data sale prohibition clauses.
Written organizational policy prohibiting sale of personal data databases to unauthorized parties, approved by management.
Audit logs (12 months) showing database access, export attempts, and transfers, with no unauthorized bulk exports detected.
Quarterly certification from Compliance/Legal confirming no unauthorized data sales occurred in the review period.
Vendor attestations or compliance certificates confirming non-sale and non-reuse of client personal data.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines)

regulations.name: Colombia Statutory Law 1581 on Personal Data Protection
regulations.authority: Superintendencia de Industria y Comercio (SIC)
regulations.summary: Colombia's comprehensive data protection law guaranteeing the fundamental constitutional right to habeas data. Requires prior express authorization (consent) as the primary lawful basis. The SIC supervises compliance.

key_requirements.text: Obtain prior express authorization (consent) from data subjects before processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design a consent collection mechanism (form, checkbox, or digital signature) that explicitly describes processing purposes, data categories, and recipient parties before any data collection. 2. Implement a consent management system to record the date, time, method, and content of each authorization. 3. Train staff to refuse data processing requests that lack documented prior consent.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve consent template language compliant with Colombian law; IT team: configure consent capture in data collection systems and export consent logs; Compliance team: create staff training materials on consent requirements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: confirm in writing that they will only process data upon receipt of documented subject consent and provide monthly consent audit reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent templates, timestamped consent database exports, training attendance records, and consent revocation logs.

key_requirements.text: Register databases with the National Registry of Databases (RNBD) administered by SIC
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compile a complete inventory of all databases containing personal data, including purpose, data categories, retention periods, and recipients. 2. Submit the RNBD registration application through the SIC online portal (www.sic.gov.co) with the required inventory documentation. 3. Maintain the RNBD registration and update it annually or whenever material changes occur (new databases, purpose changes, or processor additions).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide complete database inventory with technical specifications and data retention schedules; Legal team: prepare RNBD application form and supporting documentation; Compliance team: establish annual registration renewal calendar.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: disclose all databases they maintain on behalf of the organization so they can be included in the RNBD submission.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: SIC RNBD registration certificate, dated inventory documentation, and annual renewal confirmations.

key_requirements.text: Privacy notices informing data subjects of their rights and processing purposes
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a comprehensive privacy notice that includes organization name, lawful basis, processing purposes, data retention period, data subject rights (access, rectification, deletion, revocation), Data Protection Officer contact, and complaint procedures with the SIC. 2. Distribute the notice at the point of data collection in a clear, accessible format (printed, electronic, or verbal summary). 3. Maintain records of notice delivery and ensure notices are updated whenever processing purposes or recipient parties change.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create and approve privacy notice template in Spanish; Marketing/HR/IT teams: integrate notice into all data collection touchpoints (forms, applications, websites); Compliance team: establish version control and update notification procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: confirm receipt of the privacy notice content and agree to provide identical notices to data subjects when they collect data on the organization's behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed-off privacy notice templates in Spanish, screenshots or PDFs of notice placement at collection points, and distribution logs with timestamps.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, update, rectification, deletion, and revocation of authorization
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal data subject rights request procedure with a designated response team, including email address, phone number, and office location where requests are accepted. 2. Implement a ticketing system to log all requests (access, rectification, deletion, revocation) with receipt date, request type, and resolution timeline (maximum 10 business days under Colombian law). 3. Train all staff to route incoming rights requests to the designated team and never deny requests without documented legal justification.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document the formal rights request procedure and approval authority; IT team: configure ticketing system, build data export/deletion functionality, and create audit logs of all deletions; HR team: train staff on request intake and escalation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: commit to honoring all data subject rights requests forwarded by the organization within 5 business days and provide monthly reports of requests received.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published rights request procedure, ticketing system logs with request dates and resolutions, staff training records, and signed processor commitments.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (internal or external)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a named individual (internal full-time or external consultant) as Data Protection Officer (DPO) with authority to oversee compliance, investigate breaches, and manage data subject requests. 2. Issue a formal appointment letter or contract specifying DPO responsibilities, independence, reporting line to senior management or board, and contact details. 3. Publicize the DPO name and contact information to all staff and data subjects (on privacy notices, website, and internal policies).
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: hire or contract DPO and issue appointment letter with defined role, reporting line, budget, and independence guarantees; Legal team: document DPO responsibilities in the Data Protection Policy; Compliance team: create DPO contact directory and publish on company intranet and public website.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: acknowledge the DPO appointment in writing and agree to respond to DPO inquiries regarding processor activities within 5 business days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment letter or employment contract, published DPO contact information, and signed acknowledgments from all processors.

key_requirements.text: Sign data processor agreements with all third-party processors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third parties that process personal data on your behalf (IT vendors, payroll processors, cloud providers, marketing agencies) and classify them as data processors or independent controllers. 2. For each processor, negotiate and execute a written Data Processing Agreement (DPA) that includes processor obligations to process only on documented instructions, implement security controls, assist with data subject rights, notify of breaches, and permit audits. 3. Maintain a register of all signed DPAs and conduct annual reviews to confirm processor compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: provide vendor list; Legal team: draft DPA template and negotiate with each processor; IT team: collect processor security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2); Compliance team: maintain DPA register with signature dates and renewal reminders.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: execute signed DPA, provide current security certifications and audit reports, commit to breach notification within 24 hours, and confirm they do not sub-process without written authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Agreements with all vendors, DPA register indexed by processor, processor security certifications, and annual processor compliance confirmations.

key_requirements.text: Sensitive data (race, political opinion, health, sexual orientation) requires explicit consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Create a sensitive data inventory mapping all collection points for race, political opinion, health, and sexual orientation data, identifying which business processes require this data.

2. Design and deploy a consent collection mechanism (digital or paper form) that clearly describes the sensitive data categories, purpose, and retention period, capturing explicit affirmative consent before any processing.
3. Establish a consent audit trail system (database or log) recording each data subject's consent date, method, scope, and version of consent text, with annual review of consent validity.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft explicit consent template and consent withdrawal procedure; obtain board approval for sensitive data handling policy.
IT team: configure consent management system with audit logging; provide monthly consent acceptance reports by data category.
HR/Operations team: map all internal workflows that collect sensitive data and document legitimate business justifications.
vendor_actions: All third-party processors (e.g., HR systems, health insurance providers, survey platforms): provide Data Processing Addendum confirming they process sensitive data only on explicit documented instructions and maintain consent records per Law 1581.
Consent technology vendors: provide system documentation evidencing immutable consent logs, timestamp accuracy, and export capability for audit trails.
compliance_evidence: Signed Consent Template v[X] showing explicit language for each sensitive data category, purpose, and retention period.
Consent Management System audit logs showing timestamp, data subject ID, consent type, and affirmative action for a sample of records.
Data Processing Addendums or contractual amendments with all vendors handling sensitive data, bearing legal signatures and approval dates.
Sensitive Data Inventory spreadsheet mapping data sources, categories (e.g. health, sexual orientation), business justification, and consent mechanism.
Monthly Consent Metrics Report showing number of consents collected, withdrawn, and expired by category.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border transfers require guarantees of adequate protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data flow mapping exercise to identify all instances where personal data (sensitive or standard) is transferred outside Colombia, documenting destination country, recipient, and data categories.

2. Assess adequacy of legal frameworks or safeguards in destination countries (EU adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules, or local data protection laws) and document the guarantees.

3. Execute formal Data Transfer Agreements (or addenda to Data Processing Agreements) with all international recipients, explicitly stating data location, purpose, security obligations, and remedies for breach.
internal_actions: IT team: provide complete map of data flows (systems, countries, frequency, data categories) and document any cloud storage or backup locations outside Colombia.

Legal team: review destination country data protection laws and create a cross-border transfer risk assessment; draft or update Data Transfer Agreements with standard clauses appropriate to the jurisdiction.
Compliance/Privacy team: maintain a register of all approved cross-border transfer routes and conduct annual review of destination country legal changes.
vendor_actions: All international data processors, cloud providers, and service providers: execute a Data Transfer Agreement or contractual amendment explicitly incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent safeguards, and confirm the countries to which data will be transferred.

Sub-processors and affiliated entities receiving data outside Colombia: provide written confirmation of data location, security controls, and commitment to Law 1581 compliance; submit to annual compliance certification.
compliance_evidence: Data Flow Mapping Document identifying all cross-border transfers: destination country, recipient entity, data categories, frequency, legal basis (e.g., adequacy decision, SCC).
Cross-Border Transfer Risk Assessment signed by Legal and approved by Data Protection Officer, evaluating destination country legal frameworks.
Executed Data Transfer Agreements or contractual amendments with all international recipients, bearing signatures and referencing specific standard clauses (e.g., EU SCCs or equivalent).
Register of Approved Cross-Border Transfers maintained by Compliance team, updated annually with destination country law review outcomes.
Vendor Certification letters confirming commitment to Law 1581, data locations, security controls, and sub-processor notification obligations.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities

regulations.name: Chile Personal Data Protection Law (Ley 19.628 reformed by Ley 21.719)
regulations.authority: Agencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales (APDP - established under Ley 21.719)
regulations.summary: Chile's personal data protection law. A landmark reform (Ley 21.719, enacted December 2024) takes full effect in 2026-2027, introducing GDPR-level rights and creating a dedicated Agencia de Proteccion de Datos.

key_requirements.text: Lawful basis for processing - consent is primary lawful basis under current law
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all data processing activities and document the specific consent mechanism (written, electronic, or explicit opt-in) used for each. 2. Draft a Data Processing Register identifying which processing activities rely on consent versus other lawful bases. 3. Implement a consent management system to capture, store, and timestamp all consent records with proof of voluntary agreement.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide Data Processing Register template and consent form templates; IT team: configure consent management platform and establish audit logs for consent capture; Compliance team: define consent workflow approval process.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming their processing role and consent handling responsibilities; consent platform vendor: provide technical documentation on data encryption, retention, and audit trail capabilities.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent records with timestamps, Data Processing Register, DPA signed by vendors, consent management system audit logs, screenshots of consent capture interface, sample consent forms used in production.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, blocking, and transfer
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a formal Data Subject Request (DSR) handling procedure defining timelines, responsible teams, and escalation paths. 2. Implement technical mechanisms (portal, email inbox, or ticketing system) to receive, log, and respond to access, correction, deletion, blocking, and portability requests. 3. Establish SLAs requiring responses within 15 business days and maintain DSR registry with request type, date received, action taken, and closure date.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: build or configure DSR management system with automated acknowledgment and tracking; Legal team: draft DSR response templates for each right type; HR team: train relevant staff on DSR procedures; Compliance team: establish DSR registry and audit schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide written commitment to support DSR fulfillment within agreed timelines and supply sub-processor lists; technical vendors: confirm capability to export data in portable formats upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DSR procedure document, DSR registry with sample requests and responses, DSR management system screenshots, written DSR SLA commitments from vendors, training records for DSR handling staff, sample portability exports in standard formats.

key_requirements.text: Data accuracy principle: data must be exact, complete, and current
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data quality audit to identify completeness gaps and staleness in current datasets; classify data fields by accuracy requirements. 2. Implement data validation rules at point of entry (form validation, mandatory fields) and establish periodic data refresh cycles for critical fields. 3. Document data ownership and assign accountability for accuracy per data category (e.g., HR owns employee contact info, Finance owns billing addresses).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: implement field-level validation rules and document data schemas; Data Governance team: create Data Quality Standards document and data ownership matrix; Business teams: establish refresh schedules for their respective datasets; Audit team: design data accuracy testing procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data providers and external data vendors: provide accuracy guarantees and error correction SLAs in contracts; customer data sources (if applicable): confirm commitment to notify organization of data changes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Quality Standards document, data ownership matrix, validation rule documentation, data refresh schedule with evidence of execution, data accuracy audit reports, vendor accuracy SLAs, sample corrected records with dates.

key_requirements.text: Security obligations to protect personal data from unauthorized access
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a security risk assessment covering access controls, encryption, network segmentation, and incident response capabilities; document findings and remediation plans. 2. Implement technical controls: role-based access control (RBAC), encryption at rest and in transit, multi-factor authentication (MFA) for administrative access, and activity logging. 3. Establish an annual security testing program (penetration testing, vulnerability scanning) and maintain a security incidents log with remediation records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: conduct risk assessment, implement RBAC and MFA, deploy encryption solutions, configure audit logging, and schedule annual penetration tests; Compliance team: maintain security control inventory and incidents log; All teams: complete security awareness training annually.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and hosting providers: provide SOC 2 Type II certifications and/or annual security audit reports; third-party processors: commit to same security standards via DPA security schedules; security tool vendors: supply product security documentation and support incident response.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Security risk assessment report, RBAC policy and configuration screenshots, encryption implementation documentation, MFA deployment evidence, annual penetration test reports, vulnerability scan results and remediation logs, security training completion records, DPA security schedules signed by vendors, SOC 2 reports.

key_requirements.text: Sensitive data categories require express consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define and classify all sensitive data categories (health, biometric, criminal records, union membership, etc.) based on Chilean law definitions. 2. Implement a mandatory consent workflow requiring explicit written or electronic consent prior to collection and processing of any sensitive data. 3. Maintain a Sensitive Data Processing Register documenting each category, collection purpose, consent date, data subject, and retention period.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide sensitive data classification and consent form tailored to each category; IT team: flag sensitive data fields in databases and implement field-level access restrictions; Compliance team: establish and maintain Sensitive Data Processing Register with monthly reviews.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: acknowledge sensitive data handling in DPA and confirm they will process only with explicit consent; vendors accessing sensitive data: provide technical and organizational security measures documentation specific to sensitive data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Sensitive data classification document, sensitive data consent forms (signed), Sensitive Data Processing Register, database schema documentation showing sensitive data flagged, vendor DPA sensitive data schedules, access control logs for sensitive data systems.

key_requirements.text: Under Ley 21.719 (from 2026): GDPR-like rights including portability; mandatory DPO; PIAs; 72-hour breach notification
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) and document their role, independence, and contact details; establish DPO reporting structure and audit privileges. 2. Develop and conduct Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) for all new processing activities, high-risk projects, and system changes; document findings and controls. 3. Establish a security incident detection and response procedure with a 72-hour breach notification timeline; implement monitoring tools, incident log, and notification templates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Executive/Board: formally designate DPO with independence; DPO team: establish office and audit access; IT/Security team: implement incident detection tools and monitoring; Legal/Compliance team: develop PIA template and breach notification procedures; Incident Response team: draft notification templates and maintain incident log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors and sub-processors: commit to breach notification within 24 hours to the organization; DPO service provider (if outsourced): provide DPO service agreement defining response times and audit rights; monitoring/SIEM vendors: confirm 24/7 alert capability and incident reporting.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DPO appointment letter and organizational chart, DPO contact and independence documentation, PIA templates and sample completed PIAs, breach response procedure document, incident detection tool configuration, incident log with 72-hour notification evidence, breach notification templates, vendor breach notification SLAs, SIEM/monitoring tool audit logs.

regulations.penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations

regulations.name: Mexico Federal Law on Protection of Personal Data Held by Private Parties
regulations.authority: Instituto Nacional de Transparencia, Acceso a la Informacion y Proteccion de Datos (INAI)
regulations.summary: Mexico's federal data protection law for the private sector. Mexico is one of only three non-EU countries with limited EU adequacy recognition. Requires organizations to publish privacy notices (avisos de privacidad) and respond to ARCO rights requests.

key_requirements.text: Publish a Privacy Notice (aviso de privacidad) at or before the point of data collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a Privacy Notice (aviso de privacidad) in Spanish that discloses purposes, data categories, and rights, ensuring it is displayed visibly at collection points (online forms, physical locations, application processes). 2. Obtain Legal team approval and implement version control to track updates. 3. Maintain a record of all collection points where the notice is published and conduct quarterly audits to confirm continued visibility.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed-off Privacy Notice template in Spanish with all mandatory disclosures; Compliance team: document all data collection touchpoints (web forms, registration pages, paper forms, call centers) and maintain audit log of notice placement; Marketing/IT: confirm technical deployment of notice on all digital channels.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: provide written acknowledgment that Privacy Notice is displayed at their collection points on behalf of the organization; Website hosting provider: confirm notice is visible and not blocked by technical issues; Call center vendors: confirm scripting includes privacy notice disclosure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Privacy Notice document with INAI-compliant language; screenshots or photos of notice display at all collection points dated within last 12 months; audit report confirming placement at 100% of touchpoints; version control log showing all updates and Legal approval dates.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent for data processing; explicit consent for sensitive data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a consent management framework that captures affirmative (opt-in) consent for all data processing, with separate explicit consent checkboxes for sensitive data categories. 2. Implement a system to record consent timestamps, method (digital, signed form, verbal), and version of Privacy Notice accepted. 3. Establish a consent withdrawal mechanism and audit consent records quarterly to identify gaps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: deploy consent management platform with audit trail capabilities for all consent records; Legal team: define consent language differentiating standard vs. sensitive data; Compliance team: create consent audit procedures and sampling methodology; HR/Customer Service: train staff on obtaining and documenting consent.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Consent technology vendor: provide system that records timestamp, user identity, and version of terms consented to; third-party processors: provide proof they obtained separate explicit consent for sensitive data before processing began; email service providers: confirm consent records linked to each recipient's profile.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Consent management system configuration documentation; sample of 50+ signed/electronic consent records with timestamps and version numbers; consent withdrawal logs; training records for staff on consent procedures; vendor contracts requiring consent documentation; quarterly audit reports showing consent coverage by data category.

key_requirements.text: Honor ARCO rights - Access, Rectification, Cancellation, Opposition - within 20 business days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal ARCO request process: create a standardized intake form, assign a named Compliance Officer to manage requests, and implement a 20 business-day tracking log. 2. Design workflows for each right (Access retrieves all personal data held, Rectification corrects inaccuracies, Cancellation removes data upon justified request, Opposition halts processing) with documented decision criteria. 3. Maintain a register of all ARCO requests, responses, timelines, and outcomes; report metrics monthly to Legal.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft ARCO Request Form and decision criteria for each right type; Compliance Officer: establish and maintain ARCO request register with response deadlines and outcomes; IT team: create data retrieval and modification procedures for each data category; Database/Records team: document data retention schedules to support cancellation decisions; Internal Audit: test 10% of ARCO requests quarterly for timeliness and completeness.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: commit contractually to support ARCO requests within 10 business days of notification; cloud storage vendors: provide data export/deletion capabilities with audit trails; HR systems vendors: confirm ability to rectify or remove employee personal data on request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ARCO Request Form and process flowchart for each right; ARCO register showing at least 12 months of requests with submission date, request type, response date, and outcome; evidence of responses (letters, email confirmations) sent within 20 business days; sample of 5 rectification or cancellation records with supporting documentation; vendor SOWs confirming ARCO support SLAs.

key_requirements.text: Sensitive data (health, biometric, racial origin, beliefs) requires explicit consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify and classify all sensitive data categories handled (health, biometric, racial origin, political/religious beliefs, sexual orientation, financial information) within each business function. 2. Implement mandatory explicit consent workflows requiring clear affirmative action (checkbox + signature or verbal confirmation) before any sensitive data is collected or processed. 3. Segregate sensitive data in systems where technically feasible and conduct annual data audits to detect unlawful sensitive data collection.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Governance team: map all sensitive data flows and create a Sensitive Data Register; Legal team: draft explicit consent language specifically for each sensitive category; IT team: tag sensitive data fields in databases and implement access restrictions; Compliance team: design audit checklist for sensitive data collection points; HR team: review employee data collection forms to remove non-essential sensitive data.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Recruitment vendors: confirm explicit consent is obtained before processing candidate health or background information; health/wellness providers: submit contracts showing they process health data only with affirmative explicit consent; background check vendors: provide proof of explicit consent collection before processing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Sensitive Data Register documenting all categories and business justifications; sample of explicit consent records for each sensitive category with user signatures/acknowledgment; data classification report showing which systems hold sensitive data; vendor contracts explicitly requiring explicit consent for sensitive data; audit report showing 100% of sensitive data collection points have documented explicit consent.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate security measures based on data volume and sensitivity
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Data Security Risk Assessment identifying all systems holding personal data, volume of records, sensitivity levels, and current controls; rate risks as high/medium/low. 2. Design and implement security measures proportional to risk (encryption at rest/transit for high-sensitivity data, access controls, multi-factor authentication, regular backups, data minimization). 3. Document all security controls in a Security Control Matrix and perform quarterly vulnerability scans and annual penetration testing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: complete Data Security Risk Assessment covering all systems; implement encryption standards (AES-256 for data at rest, TLS 1.2+ for transit) and document in security architecture; deploy multi-factor authentication for all privileged access; create and maintain access control lists (ACLs); Database team: implement automated backups with encryption and test restore procedures monthly; Compliance team: maintain Security Control Matrix and track remediation of findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers: provide Service Organization Control (SOC 2 Type II) report or equivalent security certification; managed security service providers: conduct quarterly vulnerability scans and provide reports; backup/disaster recovery vendors: certify encryption standards and backup restoration SLAs; payment processors: confirm PCI DSS compliance if processing payment data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed Data Security Risk Assessment with risk ratings and mitigation plans; Security Control Matrix mapping risks to implemented controls; encryption configuration documentation and certificates; access control lists showing role-based permissions; quarterly vulnerability scan reports with remediation tracking; annual penetration testing report; vendor SOC 2 Type II reports or security certifications; backup restore test logs (monthly); incident response plan referencing security controls.

key_requirements.text: Notify INAI and affected individuals of data security breaches
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Data Breach Response Plan defining breach detection triggers, internal escalation (notify Legal, Compliance, IT Security within 24 hours), and notification timelines (INAI within 5 business days, affected individuals without undue delay). 2. Create a Breach Notification Form template and maintain a Breach Register with date, scope, cause, and remediation actions. 3. Conduct tabletop exercises annually to test notification procedures and maintain documented evidence of all breaches and notifications sent.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Data Breach Response Plan and Breach Notification templates in Spanish; IT Security: implement breach detection mechanisms (SIEM alerts, intrusion detection) and establish 24-hour incident response protocol; Compliance Officer: maintain Breach Register and track notifications to INAI and individuals; Communications team: draft breach notification letters including remedy details; Internal Audit: conduct annual breach response tabletop exercise and document findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed security/SIEM vendors: commit to alerting within 4 hours of potential breach detection; third-party data processors: contractually obligate to notify the organization within 24 hours of discovering a breach; legal counsel: provide templates for INAI notification letters.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Breach Response Plan approved by Legal; Breach Register showing all incidents in last 3 years (date, description, scope, remediation); copies of INAI notifications sent (INAI complaint number or receipt confirmation); sample breach notification letters sent to affected individuals with remedy information; email delivery receipts or certified mail confirmations; vendor incident response contracts with notification SLAs; tabletop exercise report documenting response timeline and gaps identified.

key_requirements.text: Sign data processing agreements with third-party processors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Audit all third-party vendors and service providers who access, store, or process personal data and classify by risk level.
2. Draft or obtain standardized Data Processing Agreements (DPA) that specify processor obligations, data security requirements, and liability terms compliant with LFPDPPP.
3. Execute signed DPAs with all identified processors before data transfer; maintain a registry of all active agreements with execution dates and scope.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft template Data Processing Agreement incorporating LFPDPPP Article 37 obligations (confidentiality, security, audit rights, data subject rights support).
Compliance team: create and maintain a Vendor Processing Register listing all third parties, data categories processed, and DPA signature dates.
IT team: document all systems and data flows involving external processors to identify all parties requiring DPAs.
vendor_actions: All vendors/processors: sign executed Data Processing Agreement specifying data handling scope, security standards, permitted sub-processors, and audit/inspection rights.
Vendors: provide written commitment to implement administrative, physical, and technical security measures equivalent to the controller's standards.
Vendors: confirm in writing their compliance with LFPDPPP obligations and their liability for breaches.
compliance_evidence: Executed Data Processing Agreements with original signatures or digital certificates for all active third-party processors.
Vendor Processing Registry showing vendor name, data categories, processing activities, DPA execution date, and renewal status.
Evidence of vendor security assessments or questionnaires completed prior to DPA execution.
Audit trail or internal memo documenting the process used to identify all processors requiring agreements.

key_requirements.text: Designate a data protection department or person responsible for compliance
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Designate a named individual or department as Data Protection Officer/Responsible Party with explicit authority, reporting line, and documented role scope.
2. Issue an internal appointment memo defining the role's responsibilities including policy development, breach response, data subject request handling, and regulatory liaison.
3. Ensure the designated person/department has adequate budget, staff, and access to systems needed to fulfill compliance obligations across the organization.
internal_actions: HR team: issue formal appointment letter or employment contract amendment documenting the Data Protection Officer/Responsible Party role, reporting structure, and compliance authority.
Finance team: allocate annual budget line for data protection function including staffing, training, audit, and technology costs.
Executive/Board: document board resolution or management decision formally appointing the individual or department and granting compliance authority.
IT team: provision access permissions enabling the designated person/team to audit systems, access logs, and retrieve personal data inventories.
vendor_actions: External Legal/Compliance Counsel (if retained): provide advisory agreement confirming availability for data protection compliance consultations and breach response support.
DPO service provider (if outsourced): sign Service Agreement defining scope of compliance support, response times, and escalation procedures.
compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter or employment contract specifying the Data Protection Officer/Responsible Party name, appointment date, and role responsibilities.
Board resolution or management memorandum formally establishing the data protection function and granting compliance authority.
Organizational chart showing the Data Protection Officer/Department and their reporting line to executive/board level.
Job description or role specification document defining responsibilities for policy development, breach management, audit, and data subject inquiries.
Evidence of budget allocation and staffing dedicated to the data protection function (payroll records, budget approvals, or resource allocation documents).

regulations.penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations

regulations.name: Ecuador Organic Law on Personal Data Protection
regulations.authority: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales
regulations.summary: Ecuador's comprehensive data protection law closely modeled on the GDPR, establishing rights for data subjects and obligations for controllers and processors. Applies to any organization processing personal data of individuals located in Ecuador.

key_requirements.text: Lawful basis for processing required - consent, contractual necessity, legitimate interests, legal obligation
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data mapping exercise to identify all processing activities and document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, or legitimate interest) for each. 2. Create a Data Processing Register with entries linked to specific lawful basis justifications. 3. Update vendor contracts and internal processing agreements to explicitly reference the lawful basis before any processing begins.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: deliver completed Data Processing Register with lawful basis annotations and justifications for each activity. IT team: provide system configuration documentation showing where consent flags or contract references are stored. Contracts team: supply signed processing agreements with lawful basis clauses.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide written confirmation of the lawful basis applicable to their processing activity and sign Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) that reference the specific legal ground. Vendors handling sensitive data: deliver attestations confirming they process only on instruction and under identified lawful basis.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Register, executed Data Processing Agreements with lawful basis statements, consent logs (if consent is the basis), legal memoranda justifying legitimate interest assessments, and vendor DPA confirmations filed in the compliance repository.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, deletion, portability, objection, restriction, and right not to be subject to automated decisions
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement or configure a subject rights request management system (ticketing or case management tool) to capture, log, and track all data subject requests (access, deletion, rectification, portability, objection). 2. Establish a 30-day response SLA and assign ownership to a single team (e.g., Privacy or Legal) to ensure timely fulfilment. 3. Disable automated decision-making for high-risk classifications or implement transparency and review mechanisms where automated decisions are unavoidable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: deploy or configure a documented request management system with audit logs and response templates. Legal team: draft response templates for each right type and establish the 30-day SLA with escalation procedures. Data Governance team: identify all automated decision systems and document the review/override processes or justify why automated decisions are necessary.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: provide written commitment to facilitate data subject rights requests (access, deletion, etc.) upon instruction within specified timelines. System vendors: confirm in writing that their platforms log and can generate data exports in portable formats (e.g., CSV, JSON) for portability requests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Request management system screenshots with dated request entries and fulfilment records, response templates with timestamps, a log of all rights requests received in the past 12 months with closure dates, vendor commitments to rights fulfilment, and documentation of automated decision systems with override procedures.

key_requirements.text: Notify the Superintendencia of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data breach notification procedure that mandates detection, internal assessment, and escalation to a designated Breach Response Team within 24 hours of discovery. 2. Create a breach notification template pre-populated with organisational details and a standard submission format for Superintendencia reporting. 3. Document and test the 72-hour notification timeline at least annually through a tabletop or simulated breach scenario.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: provide security event logs, intrusion detection outputs, and documented evidence of breach detection timestamp. Legal team: prepare breach notification letter and Superintendencia submission form. Risk/Incident team: maintain a Breach Register with date detected, date notified, and notification reference numbers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All service providers handling personal data: contractually commit to notify the organisation of any suspected breaches within 24 hours of discovery and provide evidence logs (access records, suspicious activity alerts) supporting the breach scope.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Incident response procedure document with 72-hour timeline, Superintendencia notification letter with submission date/receipt confirmation, Breach Register entries with detection and notification timestamps, vendor notification clauses in contracts, and tabletop exercise records demonstrating the procedure's functionality.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Delegate (DPO equivalent) for large-scale processing
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify whether the organisation qualifies as engaging in large-scale processing (systematic monitoring, processing of special category data, or extensive automated processing) based on activity scope. 2. If qualified, formally appoint a named Data Protection Delegate (or equivalent officer) and document their role, responsibilities, and reporting line to executive leadership. 3. Ensure the Delegate has access to all processing records, audit trails, and a direct reporting mechanism to the board or compliance committee.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Board: issue a formal appointment letter for the Data Protection Delegate with defined responsibilities and independence protections. Legal team: document the criteria assessment confirming large-scale processing status and supporting rationale. Executive team: establish governance structure showing the Delegate's reporting line and board access.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not typically applicable; however, if vendors are also large-scale processors, request their confirmation of DPO/Delegate appointment or written acknowledgement of your organisation's Delegate as the point of contact.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Formal appointment letter with job description, board resolution or governance memo confirming the Delegate's independence and reporting structure, large-scale processing assessment document, and evidence of the Delegate's access to processing records (e.g., system access logs or delegation of authority memos).

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define risk criteria (e.g., processing of sensitive data, large-scale automated decisions, high impact on individuals) and maintain a high-risk processing inventory. 2. For each high-risk activity, conduct a documented Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) including description, necessity justification, risk analysis, mitigation measures, and residual risk statement. 3. Review DPIAs annually or when processing scope materially changes, and document any remediation actions in a tracked action log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Governance team: create and maintain a high-risk processing inventory with risk scoring methodology. Privacy/Legal team: complete DPIA templates for each identified high-risk activity with technical and organisational risk mitigations. IT team: provide system design documentation, data flows, and access control specifications to support the DPIA analysis.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors handling high-risk data categories: provide risk assessments or security documentation (e.g., SOC 2 reports, encryption specifications) to inform the DPIA. Vendors offering automated decision or profiling services: supply technical documentation explaining the decision logic and any safeguards or human review mechanisms.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed DPIA templates (one per high-risk activity) with risk matrices and mitigation sign-offs, high-risk processing inventory spreadsheet with scoring rationale, documented remediation action log with completion dates, and vendor risk documentation incorporated into the DPIAs.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfer restrictions with adequate protection requirement
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Maintain a cross-border data transfer inventory documenting all transfers (to which countries, data categories, and transfer mechanism). 2. For transfers outside Ecuador, verify and document an adequate protection mechanism: adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), or explicit consent from data subjects. 3. Conduct annual reviews of transfer destinations to confirm that recipient countries' data protection laws remain adequate or that contractual safeguards remain enforceable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create and maintain a Cross-Border Transfer Register with destination countries, data categories, and transfer mechanism for each flow. Compliance team: obtain and file evidence of adequacy determinations or execute SCCs/BCRs with all recipient entities. IT team: provide data flow diagrams and system architecture documentation showing transfer points and recipient systems.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party recipients of personal data outside Ecuador: execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent data transfer agreements. Cloud and SaaS providers: confirm the location(s) where data is stored and processed, and provide a Data Processing Agreement that includes cross-border transfer safeguards (e.g., encryption in transit, SCC incorporation).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-Border Transfer Register with destination countries and transfer mechanisms, executed Standard Contractual Clauses or adequacy determination documentation, Data Processing Agreements that reference transfer safeguards, annual transfer review records, and technical documentation confirming encryption or other protective measures for data in transit.

key_requirements.text: Privacy by design and privacy by default principles
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for all new systems, services, or processing activities before deployment, documenting privacy controls at the architecture and design phase.
2. Establish default privacy settings in all data processing systems (e.g., minimal data collection, pseudonymization, access restrictions) and document these as baseline configurations.
3. Create and maintain a Privacy by Design Policy that mandates privacy review checkpoints in the software development lifecycle (SDLC) and data handling procedures.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft and approve Privacy by Design Policy and DPIA template aligned with Ecuadorian LOPDP requirements.
IT team: provide system architecture documentation, data flow diagrams, and evidence of default privacy settings in production systems (e.g., database encryption configs, user access matrices).
Product/Development team: supply SDLC governance records showing privacy review sign-offs before deployment of new systems or data processing features.
Data Protection Officer (if appointed): maintain DPIA register with completed assessments for all major processing activities.
vendor_actions: Processors/Cloud providers: supply Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) confirming they implement privacy by design principles and provide technical specifications of default security and privacy controls.
Third-party software vendors: deliver documentation of built-in privacy defaults (e.g., data minimization features, encryption at rest/transit, audit logging).
System integrators: provide system design documentation and risk assessment reports confirming privacy controls embedded before go-live.
compliance_evidence: Completed and approved Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for major data processing activities, dated and signed by DPO/Legal.
Privacy by Design Policy document with version control and board/management sign-off.
System architecture and data flow documentation annotated with privacy controls (encryption, access controls, data minimization).
SDLC checklist or governance log entries showing privacy review approvals before each major system release.
Signed Data Processing Agreements with vendors explicitly confirming privacy by design implementation.
Screenshots or configuration exports showing default privacy settings in production systems (e.g., minimal permissions, encryption enabled by default).

regulations.penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations

regulations.name: Uruguay Law on Personal Data Protection and Habeas Data Action
regulations.authority: Unidad Reguladora y de Control de Datos Personales (URCDP)
regulations.summary: Uruguay's data protection law, the first in Latin America to receive EU adequacy recognition (2012). Establishes principles for data processing, individual rights including a constitutional habeas data action, and requires database registration.

key_requirements.text: Register databases containing personal data with the URCDP
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a complete inventory of all systems and databases storing personal data, categorizing by data type and processing purpose. 2. Prepare and submit the official URCDP registration form (Formulario de Registro de Bases de Datos) with detailed descriptions of each database, data categories, and retention periods. 3. Maintain the registration certificate and update it annually or when material changes occur to database contents or purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: prepare URCDP registration submission document with database inventory and processing descriptions. IT team: provide complete list of all systems storing personal data with technical specifications and data retention policies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: confirm in writing which databases they process personal data in and provide technical documentation of their systems if they host or maintain data on the organization's behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: URCDP registration certificate, signed and dated database inventory spreadsheet, annual registration renewal confirmations, correspondence with URCDP confirming registered status.

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, suppression, and the constitutional habeas data action
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft and approve a formal Data Subject Rights Policy documenting procedures for access requests, rectification, suppression, and habeas data actions with defined timelines (typically 5 business days per Uruguayan law). 2. Establish documented workflows with assigned responsibility for receiving, processing, and responding to requests, including template responses and escalation procedures. 3. Train relevant personnel (customer service, legal, IT) on request handling and ensure audit logs capture all requests and resolutions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Data Subject Rights Policy and response templates for access/rectification/suppression/habeas data requests. HR team: conduct mandatory training and maintain training records. IT team: configure audit logging for all data subject request handling and provide monthly reports of requests received and resolved.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: provide written commitment to honor data subject requests within legal timelines and confirm they will not process suppression or rectification requests until receiving explicit instruction from the organization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Subject Rights Policy document, training attendance records with dates, sample request intake forms and response letters, audit logs showing request dates and resolution timelines, internal procedure documentation.

key_requirements.text: Informed consent for collecting and processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and deploy explicit consent mechanisms (opt-in forms, checkboxes, or agreements) that clearly describe the purpose, scope, and duration of personal data processing before collection occurs. 2. Document and store proof of consent for each data subject (signed forms, timestamp-verified digital confirmations, or recorded acknowledgments). 3. Establish a consent management system (manual or automated) to track consent status per data subject and purpose, enabling revocation on request.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft plain-language consent forms for each processing purpose and define consent retention periods. Marketing/Sales team: provide list of all data collection touchpoints (web forms, sign-up flows, contracts) requiring consent language. IT team: implement consent capture mechanism (form builder, consent platform, or CRM integration) and create monthly consent audit report.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers processing personal data: confirm they will only process data covered by valid consent and will honor revocation requests. Web/app vendors: implement consent banner or form and provide data showing consent was captured before processing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current consent forms (print and digital versions) with effective dates, sample signed/recorded consent confirmations, consent management system user access logs, monthly consent audit reports showing total consents by purpose.

key_requirements.text: Sensitive data protection requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all sensitive data categories (health, genetic, biometric, racial, religious, union, political, sexual orientation data) held by the organization and document legal basis for processing each (explicit consent, legal obligation, vital interest, etc.). 2. Implement enhanced safeguards including access restrictions (role-based), encryption at rest and in transit, and pseudonymization where feasible. 3. Establish a sensitive data register separate from general databases, with defined retention limits and quarterly compliance audits.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create Sensitive Data Register documenting which data categories are held, legal processing basis, and retention periods. IT team: implement encryption for sensitive data fields, configure role-based access controls, and provide quarterly access reports. Compliance team: conduct quarterly audits verifying that sensitive data access logs show only authorized personnel accessed sensitive fields.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: sign a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) or addendum explicitly restricting access to sensitive data, confirming encryption standards (minimum AES-256), and committing to sub-processor confidentiality clauses.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Sensitive Data Register with legal basis justifications, encryption configuration documentation, role-based access control matrix, sub-processor agreements, quarterly access audit reports with timestamp logs.

key_requirements.text: International data transfers only to countries with adequate protection or URCDP authorization
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all international data transfers and verify that recipient countries have been assessed by URCDP as having adequate data protection standards, or obtain URCDP authorization in advance. 2. For transfers to non-adequate countries, execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) with all receiving entities, countersigned and filed. 3. Document transfer justifications and maintain a transfers register with country risk assessments and authorization evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: complete International Data Transfer Risk Assessment documenting recipient countries and adequacy status; draft or obtain SCCs/BCRs for all non-adequate country transfers. Compliance team: maintain an International Transfers Register with authorizations and contract execution dates. IT team: flag any systems or backups that replicate data internationally and confirm controls are in place.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and international service providers: provide confirmed country locations where data is stored/processed; sign Data Processing Agreements including SCCs if transfers occur to non-adequate jurisdictions; confirm they will not sub-transfer without explicit written approval.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed International Transfer Risk Assessment, country adequacy determination (URCDP official list reference or formal assessment), executed and countersigned SCCs or BCR documentation, signed vendor DPAs with transfer clauses, International Transfers Register with approval dates.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a representative contact for data subjects
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
