key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint a named individual or dedicated contact role (Data Protection Officer equivalent or Privacy Contact) responsible for receiving and coordinating responses to data subject inquiries and URCDP communications. 2. Publish the representative's contact details (email, phone, mailing address) prominently on the organization's website, privacy notice, and all data collection forms. 3. Ensure the representative receives appropriate training, has documented delegated authority, and maintains a log of all inquiries received and responses sent.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue formal appointment letter naming the Data Protection Representative, defining delegated authorities and responsibilities. Legal team: draft the representative's job description and escalation matrix. IT/Communications team: publish representative contact details on company website, email signature, and update all privacy documentation. Representative: maintain a central log (email folder or spreadsheet) of all data subject inquiries with dates received and resolution status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: No specific external vendor actions required; however, if a third-party (law firm, consultant) is appointed as representative, obtain a signed Service Agreement confirming their availability, response timeline commitments, and confidentiality obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter with representative name and contact details, published privacy notice showing representative contact information, training completion certificate for appointed representative, inquiry log showing incoming requests and response dates, internal delegation document.

key_requirements.text: Security measures appropriate to the nature and volume of data stored
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory and risk assessment to classify data by sensitivity level (public, internal, confidential, restricted) and volume.
2. Document and implement technical controls (encryption at rest/transit, access controls, firewalls, intrusion detection) proportionate to each data classification.
3. Establish a baseline security standard document and review it annually or when data scope changes significantly.
internal_actions: IT team: provide current network architecture diagram, encryption inventory (algorithms, key management procedures), and access control configurations (IAM/RBAC logs).
IT team: deliver security audit reports, vulnerability assessments, and penetration test results from the past 12 months.
Legal/Compliance team: compile the data classification policy and risk assessment matrix mapping data types to required controls.
vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification, and a detailed security addendum specifying encryption methods, backup frequency, and incident response procedures.
Third-party processors: submit completed Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming technical and organizational measures meet Uruguay Law requirements.
All vendors: sign Security Requirements Attestation confirming they meet minimum encryption, access logging, and availability standards.
compliance_evidence: Data classification policy and inventory spreadsheet with assigned security levels.
Risk assessment report documenting data volumes, sensitivity, and mapped security controls.
Current Information Security Policy or Security Baseline Standard document.
Vendor SOC 2 Type II/ISO 27001 certificates and executed Data Processing Addendums.
Network security diagrams, encryption implementation logs, and access control audit trails (6–12 months).
Annual security audit or penetration test report showing controls are functioning.

regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages

regulations.name: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act - FTC Safeguards Rule
regulations.authority: Federal Trade Commission (FTC), federal banking regulators (OCC, FDIC, Federal Reserve)
regulations.summary: Federal law requiring financial institutions to protect the privacy and security of customers' non-public personal financial information. The FTC's updated 2023 Safeguards Rule strengthened security requirements for non-banking financial institutions including auto dealers, mortgage brokers, payday lenders, tax preparers, and financial advisors.

key_requirements.text: Designate a qualified individual to oversee the information security program
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or equivalent officer in writing, documenting their authority and responsibility for the information security program. 2. Establish a charter defining their scope, including oversight of risk assessment, safeguards implementation, and breach response. 3. Ensure the designee reports directly to senior management and board-level committees with quarterly compliance status updates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/HR: provide signed appointment letter and job description. IT: provide organization chart showing reporting lines. Executive team: confirm board awareness and oversight delegation in meeting minutes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable—this is an internal governance requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter, CISO charter document, board resolution or meeting minutes confirming designation, organizational charts, and evidence of executive-level reporting (e.g., board meeting attendance logs).

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual risk assessments identifying reasonably foreseeable threats
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Commission an independent risk assessment annually, identifying threats to customer information confidentiality, integrity, and availability across all systems and processes. 2. Document findings in a formal risk assessment report with likelihood and impact ratings, vulnerability inventory, and prioritized remediation roadmap. 3. Present results to senior management and board; track remediation status quarterly.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT: provide system inventory, network diagrams, and data flow documentation. Legal: compile list of regulated data types and sensitivity classifications. Finance: identify business-critical systems and dependencies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party assessor: deliver signed risk assessment report with methodology, findings, and recommendations. Cloud/hosting providers: provide SOC 2 Type II reports and vulnerability assessment results.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual risk assessment report signed by assessor, executive summary presented to board, risk register with remediation tracking, evidence of corrective actions taken (patches, configuration changes, system upgrades).

key_requirements.text: Implement access controls, multi-factor authentication, and encryption for customer information
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Deploy role-based access controls (RBAC) restricting customer information access to authorized personnel only; document access policies and enforce the principle of least privilege. 2. Mandate multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all administrative accounts and remote access; configure MFA logs for audit. 3. Encrypt customer information in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256 or equivalent); maintain encryption key management procedures and key rotation schedules.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT: provide access control policy, current access matrix by role/system, MFA enrollment records, and encryption configuration documentation. Security: deliver encryption key management plan and rotation logs. Audit: compile access review reports (quarterly) and MFA bypass request log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/SaaS providers: submit encryption specification sheets, MFA capability attestations, and key management responsibility matrix. Third-party processors: provide encryption in-transit and at-rest certifications per contract requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Access control policy signed by CISO, active directory group memberships or IAM system exports, MFA enrollment and usage logs, encryption configuration screenshots, TLS certificate details, key rotation audit logs, and third-party attestations (SOC 2, BAA signed copies).

key_requirements.text: Develop and test an incident response plan
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a comprehensive incident response plan defining roles, communication procedures, containment/eradication/recovery steps, and notification timelines for breaches affecting customer information. 2. Test the plan annually through tabletop exercises or simulated breach scenarios; document test results, gaps identified, and corrective actions. 3. Update the plan based on test findings and changes to systems, threats, or regulatory requirements; track version history and approval by senior management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance: draft incident response plan template and notification protocols. IT: define technical recovery procedures and system restoration timelines. HR/Communications: develop communication templates for employees and customers. Security: schedule and execute annual testing, document results in test report.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response service provider (if used): submit engagement terms, response SLAs, and evidence of testing capabilities. Forensic firm: provide retainer agreement and incident response playbook alignment.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response plan with effective date, annual test report(s) with execution date and participant names, gap remediation log, plan update log showing version history and approval dates, and evidence of senior management sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Notify the FTC within 30 days of a data breach affecting 500+ customers
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a breach detection and reporting procedure identifying when notification to FTC is triggered (500+ customers affected); calculate customer count accurately using documented data inventory. 2. Notify the FTC in writing within 30 days of discovery through the FTC's Safeguards Rule breach notification portal, including breach scope, date discovered, and remediation steps. 3. Maintain records of all breach notifications with proof of delivery and FTC acknowledgment for audit purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security: develop breach discovery and escalation procedure; define 'affected customer' criteria and counting methodology. Legal: prepare breach notification letter template and submit procedures. Compliance: maintain centralized breach notification log with dates, customer counts, and FTC confirmation receipts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Forensic/legal counsel: provide sample breach notification letter and FTC submission templates. Law enforcement/credit bureaus (if applicable): request notification confirmation letters.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: FTC breach notification submission with date and proof of delivery, written acknowledgment from FTC, breach investigation report showing customer count calculation, internal breach notification log, and evidence of remediation actions taken.

key_requirements.text: Oversee third-party service providers with security requirements in contracts
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a vendor management program requiring all third-party service providers accessing customer information to execute written security agreements (Data Processing Agreement, Business Associate Agreement, or Service Organization Controls addendum). 2. Document vendor security requirements in contracts: encryption, access controls, incident reporting, audit rights, and compliance with Safeguards Rule standards. 3. Conduct annual vendor risk assessments, review SOC 2 Type II or equivalent audit reports, and maintain evidence of vendor compliance through signed attestations or audit reports.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement: maintain vendor register identifying those with access to customer information and their security agreement status. Legal: draft standard security contract addendum and DPA templates. Compliance: schedule and document annual vendor risk assessments, collect and file SOC 2 reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: sign Data Processing Agreement or Business Associate Agreement. Key vendors: submit SOC 2 Type II audit report annually or provide signed security attestation. Vendors: acknowledge specific Safeguards Rule requirements in writing (encryption, access controls, breach notification).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed vendor contracts or security addenda, executed Data Processing Agreements, vendor register with security status, SOC 2 Type II or equivalent audit reports, annual vendor risk assessment documentation, evidence of vendor attestations or certifications, and breach notification agreements signed by vendors.

key_requirements.text: Board-level annual report on the status of the information security program
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a Chief Information Security Officer or senior security lead to compile annual findings on control implementation, incidents, and remediation status. 2. Schedule a formal board meeting each fiscal year to present the security program status report, including risk ratings, control assessments, and budget requirements. 3. Document board acknowledgment and approval via signed minutes or board resolution.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/CISO team: prepare written annual security program status report including control inventory, incident summary, remediation tracking, and budget justification. Legal team: ensure board meeting minutes formally record security program discussion and any board decisions or directives.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party security assessors or auditors: provide written annual audit or assessment report confirming control testing results and risk findings to support board reporting.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed board meeting minutes or resolution; dated annual security program status report presented to board; CISO attestation letter; third-party audit reports or control assessment summaries; calendar invites and attendance records for board security briefings.

key_requirements.text: Monitor and test effectiveness of security controls annually
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop an annual control testing plan that identifies all material security controls and specifies testing methodology (e.g., automated scans, manual reviews, penetration testing). 2. Execute testing activities by Q4 of each calendar year and document results, including pass/fail status, findings, and root causes. 3. Track remediation of failed or deficient controls and validate closure within 90 days, with evidence retained in a control testing register.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: create and execute annual control testing plan with test cases, documented evidence of execution (logs, test reports, screenshots), and maintain a control register with test dates and results. Risk/Compliance team: perform effectiveness assessment and document control ratings (effective, ineffective, or needs improvement) with supporting rationale.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors or penetration testing firms: provide written test reports with specific control testing procedures performed, findings, and ratings of control effectiveness; supply detailed test methodology and scope documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual control testing plan document; test execution logs and reports; control assessment matrix showing each control tested and effectiveness rating; remediation ticket records with closure dates; independent audit reports confirming control testing was performed; management summary of control testing results with sign-off by CISO or Chief Risk Officer.

regulations.penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors

regulations.name: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act
regulations.authority: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law placing parents in control over the online collection of personal information from children under 13. Applies to operators of commercial websites, online services, and apps directed at children or with actual knowledge they are collecting information from children under 13.

key_requirements.text: Post a clear and comprehensive privacy policy describing information collection practices
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a COPPA-specific privacy policy that explicitly describes what information is collected from children under 13, how it is used, and who has access to it. 2. Post the policy prominently on your website homepage and in-app in plain, easy-to-understand language. 3. Have Legal review and certify that the policy addresses all COPPA requirements before publication.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide executed privacy policy document with sign-off; Marketing team: confirm placement and visibility on all digital properties; Compliance team: create and maintain a policy change log with review dates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide written confirmation that they have reviewed and will comply with the privacy policy as posted; any analytics or ad tech partners: submit attestation that they will not collect additional data beyond what the policy permits.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed privacy policy document dated and approved by Legal; screenshots of policy placement on website and mobile app; policy change log with review dates; vendor attestations of compliance acknowledgement.

key_requirements.text: Obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting personal information from children under 13
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a documented parental consent mechanism (email verification, credit card verification, or third-party verification service) that confirms the adult is the parent/guardian before any data collection. 2. Create and retain a signed parental consent record for each child under 13, including the parent's name, email, date of consent, and scope of data to be collected. 3. Test the consent flow quarterly to ensure it functions as intended and document results.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide technical specifications and audit logs of the consent capture system; Legal team: draft the parental consent form and approval workflow; Compliance team: maintain a database of all parental consents with timestamps and confirmation method; QA team: document quarterly consent mechanism testing results.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Identity verification vendor (if used): provide Service Organization Control (SOC 2) report and written confirmation of age/parental verification accuracy rates; consent management platform: submit API documentation and audit trail export capabilities.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Parental consent database export with timestamps and verification method for a sample of child accounts; executed parental consent forms; screenshots of consent flow in application; SOC 2 report from verification vendor; quarterly testing reports with pass/fail results.

key_requirements.text: Allow parents to review, correct, and delete their child's personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Build a parent portal or request mechanism (email, web form, or app feature) that allows parents to submit verified requests to view, correct, or delete their child's data within 30 days. 2. Create a documented process for data retrieval, correction, and deletion, including verification that the requester is the parent/guardian. 3. Maintain a log of all parental access requests, actions taken, and completion dates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: develop and document the parent portal with access controls and provide code review evidence; Database team: provide data extraction and deletion procedures; Customer Service team: create and train staff on the request handling workflow; Compliance team: establish and maintain a request tracking spreadsheet with request date, action taken, and completion date.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Any data processor or backup service provider: provide written commitment to support data deletion/correction within the stated timeframe and confirm no backups will be retained after deletion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Parent request tracking log with sample requests showing dates submitted, actions taken, and completion dates; parent portal screenshots and user guide; documented data retrieval and deletion procedures; signed data processor agreements confirming deletion timelines; sample corrected/deleted data confirmation emails to parents.

key_requirements.text: Not condition a child's participation on providing more information than necessary
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data minimization review: map all data fields collected during child registration and identify which are truly necessary for service delivery; remove optional or convenience fields. 2. Implement field-level access controls so that only necessary data fields are presented during signup and collect remaining data only if functionally required. 3. Document and communicate the data minimization decision in writing to product and engineering teams.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Product team: provide a list of all data fields currently collected from children with justification for each; IT team: modify signup forms to remove non-essential fields and provide screenshots of revised forms; Legal team: review and approve the data minimization scope; Compliance team: create a Data Necessity Matrix signed by Product and Legal.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers (CRM, marketing, analytics): confirm in writing which child data elements they receive and certify that no additional data beyond what is transmitted will be used or shared.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Necessity Matrix document signed by Product and Legal; before/after screenshots of signup forms; email communications to teams confirming data minimization requirements; vendor attestations limiting use to transmitted fields only.

key_requirements.text: Maintain reasonable security procedures to protect children's personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a security risk assessment focused on child data, including encryption in transit and at rest, access controls, and vulnerability scanning, then document findings and remediation. 2. Implement and enforce multi-factor authentication for all personnel accessing child data, encrypt all child data at rest with industry-standard algorithms, and enable TLS 1.2+ for all data transmissions. 3. Establish a vulnerability disclosure policy and perform annual penetration testing on systems handling child data.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: provide security risk assessment report, encryption implementation details, and MFA deployment confirmation; IT team: configure and maintain audit logs of all child data access; Compliance team: document the vulnerability disclosure policy and maintain a log of any identified/remediated vulnerabilities; QA/Security team: provide annual penetration test results.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All service providers handling child data: submit a SOC 2 Type II report (or equivalent) covering security controls; provide written confirmation of encryption methods, data retention periods, and incident notification procedures within 24 hours of breach discovery.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Security risk assessment report with remediation sign-off; encryption specification document; MFA deployment screenshot or confirmation; TLS configuration documentation; SOC 2 Type II reports from vendors; vulnerability disclosure policy document; annual penetration test report; access audit logs for a 12-month period.

key_requirements.text: Retain personal information only as long as reasonably necessary
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data retention schedule that specifies the maximum retention period for each category of child personal information based on business necessity (e.g., 30 days, 1 year). 2. Implement automated deletion logic in your database system to purge data upon expiration of the retention period, and configure audit logging to record all deletions. 3. Document and communicate the retention schedule to all teams that handle child data and review it annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve a Data Retention Schedule by data type and use case; IT/Database team: implement automated deletion procedures and provide technical specifications; Compliance team: maintain a record of the approved retention schedule and any amendments; IT team: configure audit logs to record deletion events with timestamp and volume.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors and backup service providers: confirm in writing their data retention limits and provide evidence that they will delete child data in accordance with your retention schedule; confirm no indefinite backups or archives will retain child data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Retention Schedule document with approval dates; database deletion procedure documentation; sample automated deletion log excerpts showing timestamp and data volume deleted; vendor confirmation letters committing to deletion timelines; annual retention schedule review meeting notes or email.

key_requirements.text: Verify age before collecting data - COPPA 2.0 expands protections to under 16
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Deploy an age-gating mechanism at service entry that blocks or redirects users under 13 (or under 16 if applicable per your service scope) before any personal information collection occurs.
2. For users claiming to be 13–15, implement either parental consent workflows or use a verifiable parental consent service provider; document all consent requests and responses.
3. Conduct quarterly audits of age-verification logs to detect bypass attempts, failed verifications, or data collection from unverified minors; retain audit reports for 24 months.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide written age-gating policy, definition of 'personal information' scope under COPPA 2.0, and decision matrix for parental consent workflows.

IT team: provide system architecture documentation for age-verification module, access logs showing verification check results, and database schemas confirming no personal data is stored before age confirmation.
Compliance team: deliver audit checklists, sample audit reports from last 12 months, and incident log of any age-gating failures or workarounds.
vendor_actions: Age-verification service provider: supply SLA guaranteeing verification accuracy ≥98%, SOC 2 Type II report, and certification that their service does not retain user data beyond verification decision.
Parental consent service provider (if used): provide written guarantee of COPPA compliance, verification method documentation, and proof of parental identity verification standard used.
Analytics/tracking vendors: sign addendum confirming they will not receive, process, or log any data from users whose age has not been verified as 13+ (or 16+).
compliance_evidence: Age-gating policy document with effective date and sign-off by Legal and Privacy Officer.
System screenshots or recorded demonstration of age-verification flow blocking data collection before confirmation.
Parental consent templates, tracking spreadsheets, and sample signed consent forms (redacted for PII).
Audit reports from past 12 months showing verification attempt counts, success rates, and zero instances of unverified minor data collection.
Vendor SLAs, SOC 2 reports, and signed COPPA compliance addenda with all third-party services.
Access logs or database transaction logs showing timestamp and result of every age-verification check for past 90 days (sample set acceptable).

regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common

regulations.name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act
regulations.authority: CISA, Office of Management and Budget (OMB), NIST, Agency Inspector Generals
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law requiring all federal agencies and their contractors to develop, document, and implement information security programs protecting government information and systems. Mandates use of the NIST Risk Management Framework.

key_requirements.text: Develop an agency-wide information security program using the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or equivalent role with documented authority and responsibility for agency-wide security governance.
2. Develop and formally approve an Information Security Program Charter that maps all agency systems to the six RMF phases: Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor.
3. Create an RMF Implementation Roadmap with timelines, resource allocation, and accountability assignments across all business units and IT functions.
internal_actions: Executive Leadership: sign the Information Security Program Charter and commit annual budget for RMF execution.
IT/Security team: provide inventory of all agency information systems with proposed RMF phase assignments and completion timeline.
Legal/Compliance: deliver risk tolerance statement and executive sponsorship memo confirming alignment with federal compliance obligations.
vendor_actions: Cloud/Infrastructure vendors: provide NIST RMF alignment documentation and confirm their participation in agency-wide control implementation and monitoring.
Third-party assessors: submit proposal demonstrating NIST RMF and federal compliance assessment methodology and provide references from similar government clients.
compliance_evidence: Signed Information Security Program Charter with CISO signature and executive approval date.
RMF Implementation Roadmap document identifying all systems, phases, milestones, and responsible parties.
CISO appointment letter or role description confirming authority and reporting line.
Budget allocation memo or fiscal year funding approval for RMF program execution.

key_requirements.text: Categorize systems as low, moderate, or high impact per FIPS 199
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Convene a cross-functional System Categorization Team (IT, business owners, legal, security) to evaluate each system against FIPS 199 impact categories (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
2. Complete a FIPS 199 System and Information Impact Analysis worksheet for each system, documenting mission criticality, data classification, and potential harm from compromise.
3. Formally approve and document the impact level assignment (Low/Moderate/High) for each system in a System Categorization Register, with dates and approver signatures.
internal_actions: Business Unit Owners: provide system descriptions, data flow diagrams, user count, and mission impact assessment for each application.
IT Security team: compile FIPS 199 Impact Analysis worksheets for all systems and maintain the System Categorization Register.
Legal/Compliance: review confidentiality and legal risk factors and sign off on categorization decisions.
vendor_actions: SaaS/Cloud vendors: provide system architecture documentation and confirm their system's role in processing agency data at each confidentiality/integrity/availability level.
Third-party assessors: validate categorization methodology and confirm alignment with FIPS 199 and agency mission.
compliance_evidence: Completed FIPS 199 System and Information Impact Analysis worksheets for all systems.
System Categorization Register listing each system name, impact level (Low/Moderate/High), and approval date/signature.
Data flow diagrams showing information flows between systems and external parties.
Signed categorization review and approval memo from Chief Information Officer or CISO.

key_requirements.text: Select and implement security controls from NIST SP 800-53
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Map each categorized system to the applicable NIST SP 800-53 control baseline (Low, Moderate, or High per FIPS 199 result) and document required control families.
2. Conduct a gap analysis comparing current system controls against the baseline requirements and identify missing or non-compliant controls.

3. Develop and approve a Control Implementation Plan with remediation timelines, assigned owners, and resource requirements; implement controls and document configuration in the System Security Plan (SSP).
internal_actions: IT/Security team: provide current system technical configuration, access controls, encryption status, and audit logging capabilities; create gap analysis report.
System Owners: allocate resources and sign Control Implementation Plan accepting remediation timelines and ownership of control evidence.
IT Operations: configure missing controls (firewalls, patch management, encryption, logging) and provide evidence of implementation (screenshots, configuration files, test results).
vendor_actions: SaaS/Cloud vendors: provide Control Implementation Summary describing which NIST SP 800-53 controls are met by their service; supply configuration guides for agency-managed controls.
Security tool vendors: deliver product documentation, deployment guides, and evidence of compliance with selected controls (authentication, encryption, monitoring).
compliance_evidence: NIST SP 800-53 Control Baseline Selection document identifying required control family for each system by impact level.
Gap Analysis Report comparing current state to baseline controls and listing each non-compliant or missing control.
Signed Control Implementation Plan with timelines, resource allocation, and control owner assignments.
System Security Plan (SSP) documenting selected controls, implementation approach, and configuration details for each control.
Control implementation evidence (configuration files, screenshots, test reports, access lists, encryption certificates).

key_requirements.text: Conduct independent security assessments and obtain Authorization to Operate (ATO)
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
how_to_meet: 1. Engage an independent, qualified third-party assessor (federal contractor with required credentials) to conduct a Security Control Assessment (SCA) per NIST SP 800-53A; assessor evaluates each control via interview, testing, and document review.
2. Remediate all control deficiencies identified in the SCA Report and re-test or re-validate corrected controls with the assessor.

3. Submit the final System Security Plan, SCA Report, and Authorization Request package to the Authorizing Official (AO); AO issues a signed Authorization to Operate (ATO) memo or accepts risk in writing and authorizes system operation.
internal_actions: IT/Security team: prepare comprehensive control evidence binders for assessor review (policies, procedures, logs, configuration screenshots, test results, access matrices).
System Owner: coordinate with assessor scheduling, facilitate control interviews, and provide subject-matter expert availability.
IT Operations: remediate any deficiencies identified in SCA and coordinate re-testing; provide updated evidence to assessor.
Executive/AO: review final SCA Report and SSP, and sign the Authorization to Operate memo with explicit risk acceptance statement.
vendor_actions: Third-party Independent Assessor: perform NIST SP 800-53A assessment, provide detailed SCA Report with findings, and confirm assessor credentials (CISSP, CCSK, or equivalent federal compliance background).
Cloud/SaaS vendors: provide assessor direct access to systems and controls; supply audit logs, configuration evidence, and attestation letters of compliance.
compliance_evidence: Contract or Statement of Work with independent third-party assessor confirming assessment scope and NIST SP 800-53A methodology.
Security Control Assessment (SCA) Report documenting assessment methodology, each control tested, findings, and remediation recommendations.
Remediation evidence for all deficiencies (updated policies, configuration changes, test results, access control modifications).
Signed Authorization to Operate (ATO) memo from Authorizing Official with authorization period and explicit risk acceptance language.
System Security Plan (SSP) final version approved by AO and dated.

key_requirements.text: Continuously monitor all security controls via Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Implement automated security monitoring tools (e.g., SIEM, vulnerability scanners, configuration management database, log aggregators) that continuously collect and analyze control evidence from all systems in scope.

2. Establish a monthly or quarterly Continuous Monitoring Plan with defined metrics (patch compliance, access review results, vulnerability scan findings, control test results) and assign monitoring responsibilities to IT Security and System Owners.
3. Generate and review continuous monitoring dashboards and reports monthly; escalate anomalies or control failures to the CISO and document remediation actions in a Control Deviation Log.
internal_actions: IT Security team: deploy SIEM, vulnerability scanner, and automated control monitoring tools; configure dashboards and alert thresholds for control failures.
IT Operations: configure systems to export logs and metrics to monitoring platform; respond to control failure alerts and remediate deficiencies within SLA (e.g., critical patches within 15 days).
System Owners: review monthly continuous monitoring reports, investigate control deviations, and sign off on remediation completion.
CISO: review monthly Control Deviation Log and approve remediation plans; escalate persistent deficiencies to executive leadership.
vendor_actions: SIEM/Monitoring tool vendors: provide integrations for all agency systems and confirm real-time log ingestion and alerting capabilities.
Cloud/SaaS vendors: enable API access for automated control monitoring; provide audit logs and configuration snapshots in required formats.
Managed Security Service Providers (if used): deliver weekly control status reports and escalation procedures for anomalies.
compliance_evidence: Approved Continuous Monitoring Plan document describing monitoring frequency, metrics, tools, and responsibilities.
Continuous Monitoring Dashboard or reports (monthly/quarterly) showing patch compliance, vulnerability counts, access review completion, and control test results.
Control Deviation Log documenting each control failure, remediation action, completion date, and approver signature.
SIEM/monitoring tool logs or reports confirming continuous collection of control evidence.
System configuration snapshots or baseline comparisons showing detection of unauthorized changes.
Monthly or quarterly executive summary report to CISO and leadership on control status.

key_requirements.text: Report cybersecurity metrics annually to OMB and DHS
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Collect cybersecurity metrics from all systems via continuous monitoring tools and final Security Control Assessment reports; compile metrics per OMB and DHS reporting templates (e.g., number of systems authorized, control compliance percentage, vulnerability remediation rates).
2. Validate metrics accuracy with IT Security and System Owners; document data sources (SIEM logs, assessment reports, patch management system) to support audit trail.

3. Submit annual cybersecurity metrics report to OMB (via FISMA reporting portal or consolidated submission) and to DHS-designated reporting authority by established deadline; retain submission confirmation and signed attestation memo from CISO.
internal_actions: IT Security team: extract and compile cybersecurity metrics from monitoring tools and assessment database; validate accuracy and completeness.
Compliance/Legal: confirm reporting requirements (OMB deadlines, DHS portal, reporting format) and prepare submission package.
CISO: sign attestation letter confirming accuracy of reported metrics and reviewing agency's cybersecurity posture; submit to OMB and DHS on deadline.
vendor_actions: Third-party assessors: provide final SCA data and control compliance percentages for inclusion in annual metrics report.
Monitoring vendors: export metrics in OMB/DHS-required format (e.g., SIEM reports on vulnerability remediation and patch compliance rates).
compliance_evidence: OMB FISMA reporting template or DHS cybersecurity metrics form completed with all required fields (systems count, control compliance %, vulnerabilities, remediation rates, ATO status).
Supporting documentation from monitoring tools and assessments backing each reported metric.
Data validation spreadsheet or memo confirming accuracy review by IT Security and System Owners.
Signed CISO attestation letter confirming report accuracy and submission to OMB/DHS.
OMB or DHS submission confirmation receipt (e-mail or portal acknowledgment) with submission date and agency identifier.

key_requirements.text: Contractor systems holding federal data must comply with FISMA requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all contractors and systems that process, store, or transmit federal data through a data inventory audit.
2. Establish contractual clauses requiring FISMA compliance, NIST SP 800-53 controls implementation, and right-to-audit provisions.
3. Conduct annual compliance assessments of contractor systems using the same security control framework as internal federal systems.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain master service agreements (MSAs) with FISMA compliance, audit rights, and incident reporting clauses.
IT team: maintain the federal data systems inventory identifying all contractor-operated systems and their risk categorization (Low/Moderate/High).
Compliance team: schedule and document annual contractor security assessments with remediation tracking.
vendor_actions: Contractors: provide System Security Plan (SSP) documenting NIST SP 800-53 control implementation specific to federal data handling.
Contractors: furnish annual independent security assessment report (FedRAMP authorization, SOC 2 Type II, or equivalent third-party audit).
Contractors: sign addendum confirming right-to-audit, incident disclosure within 24 hours, and data handling compliance with FISMA requirements.
compliance_evidence: Signed contractor agreements with FISMA compliance and audit clauses.
Contractor System Security Plans (SSPs) with control narratives mapped to NIST SP 800-53.
Annual contractor security assessment reports dated within the past 12 months.
Incident response logs documenting contractor breach disclosures.
Data inventory spreadsheet listing all contractor systems handling federal information.

key_requirements.text: Inspector General conducts annual independent evaluation of agency security programs
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Establish an annual IG evaluation schedule aligned with agency fiscal or calendar year, documented in writing to the Inspector General.
2. Prepare a comprehensive security posture report covering all systems, control implementation status, vulnerabilities, and remediation plans for IG review.
3. Conduct independent third-party assessment of the agency security program using NIST standards and provide findings to the IG.
internal_actions: Compliance team: coordinate IG evaluation schedule and prepare evaluation charter defining scope, objectives, and timeline with Inspector General office.
Security team: compile annual security program assessment report including control maturity ratings, identified deficiencies, and corrective action plans.
IT team: provide system inventory, vulnerability scans, patch management logs, and access control documentation for IG review.
vendor_actions: Third-party assessor: conduct independent security evaluation and issue audit report with findings, ratings, and recommendations per NIST standards.
Assessor: deliver detailed control testing results and evidence of compliance aligned to IG evaluation criteria.
compliance_evidence: Letter of engagement or memorandum with Inspector General confirming annual evaluation schedule.
Annual security program assessment report signed by agency CISO dated within the past 12 months.
Third-party independent evaluation report with control assessment findings and risk ratings.
Corrective action plan (CAP) document addressing IG findings with assigned owners, due dates, and completion evidence.
IG letter or management decision acknowledging receipt and acceptance of annual evaluation results.

regulations.penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA

regulations.name: SEC Cybersecurity Risk Management and Incident Disclosure Rules
regulations.authority: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
regulations.summary: SEC rules requiring public companies to disclose material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days on Form 8-K, and to provide annual disclosures (Form 10-K) about cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and board governance.

key_requirements.text: Disclose material cybersecurity incidents on Form 8-K within 4 business days of determining materiality
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; D&O liability; securities fraud claims
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Materiality assessment records; 8-K filings; incident classification documentation
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a materiality assessment process that defines quantitative and qualitative thresholds (data volume, customer impact, regulatory scope, financial exposure) for cybersecurity incidents. 2. Create an incident response team with clear escalation procedures to Legal and Investor Relations, ensuring materiality determination occurs within 2 business days of incident discovery. 3. Draft and file Form 8-K with SEC within 4 business days of materiality determination, including incident summary, response actions, and known impacts.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide materiality assessment framework and decision log documenting when and why each incident was deemed material or immaterial. IT Security team: provide incident discovery and initial assessment reports with timestamps. Investor Relations: provide draft and filed Form 8-K copies with SEC submission confirmations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cybersecurity incident response vendors: provide written acknowledgment of 4-business-day notification requirement and commit to rapid preliminary forensic findings delivery within 48 hours of engagement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: SEC EDGAR Form 8-K filings with exhibit showing incident disclosure, internal materiality assessment policy document, incident response playbook with escalation timelines, email chains demonstrating materiality determination dates, and SEC filing confirmation receipts.

key_requirements.text: Annually disclose cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and governance in Form 10-K
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; restatement risk; investor claims
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Annual 10-K cybersecurity disclosure; board oversight evidence; risk management programme description
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct an annual cybersecurity risk management audit spanning strategy, governance structure, risk identification processes, and incident history; document findings in a formal report. 2. Prepare a cybersecurity risk disclosure statement addressing overall strategy, material risks, and management accountability; align with 10-K Item 1A and Item 1C requirements. 3. Have General Counsel and Chief Information Security Officer certify and submit the disclosure to SEC as part of the 10-K annual report filing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/Security team: provide annual cybersecurity risk assessment report, incident statistics, vulnerability management metrics, and security program maturity assessment. Legal team: draft 10-K cybersecurity disclosure sections and coordinate with external auditors on disclosures. Finance team: quantify cybersecurity program budget and any material costs associated with incidents or remediation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors: provide written comfort letter confirming cybersecurity risk disclosures are complete and not misleading. Third-party penetration testing or assessment vendors: provide annual security assessment reports and certification letters supporting risk management disclosures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: SEC EDGAR Form 10-K filing with completed Item 1A (Risk Factors) and Item 1C (Cybersecurity) sections, annual internal cybersecurity audit report, CISO annual risk summary memo, management certification of disclosures, and external auditor comfort letters.

key_requirements.text: Disclose the board of directors' oversight of cybersecurity risks and management's role in assessing and managing those risks
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; shareholder litigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board committee charters; meeting minutes with cybersecurity agenda items; management reporting structure
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the board's cybersecurity oversight structure in board charters and committee charters, specifying which committee owns cybersecurity oversight and what management roles report to the board. 2. Prepare a formal governance matrix showing board committee responsibilities, management roles (e.g., CISO, CTO), escalation triggers, and quarterly reporting cadence to the board. 3. Compile meeting minutes, board dashboards, and CISO reports to the board/Audit Committee demonstrating regular oversight activities and risk discussions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretarial/Corporate Governance team: provide board and committee charter documents, governance policy on cybersecurity oversight, and board meeting minutes from the past 12 months showing cybersecurity discussions. CISO/Security team: provide quarterly board reports, risk dashboards, and incident summaries presented to directors. General Counsel: confirm management reporting lines and escalation protocols to the board.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External corporate governance consultants (if engaged): provide gap analysis and recommendations on board cybersecurity oversight alignment with SEC disclosure rules.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board and committee charters with cybersecurity oversight provisions, signed governance matrix defining roles (Board, Audit Committee, CISO, CRO), 12 months of board/committee meeting minutes referencing cybersecurity discussions, quarterly CISO reports to board with date stamps, and internal governance policy document.

regulations.penalties: SEC enforcement actions; material misstatements in public filings subject to securities fraud liability; penalties up to $5 million per violation

regulations.name: Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification 2.0
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)
regulations.summary: DoD's cybersecurity certification program requiring defense contractors and subcontractors handling Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) to achieve and maintain certified cybersecurity postures.

key_requirements.text: Level 1 (Foundational): 17 basic practices per FAR 52.204-21; annual self-assessment and senior official affirmation
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Document all 17 basic practices from FAR 52.204-21 in a centralized cybersecurity baseline policy document, assigning ownership to IT and security leads.
2. Conduct an annual self-assessment by completing the CMMC Level 1 self-assessment questionnaire, documenting evidence for each of the 17 practices.
3. Obtain written affirmation from a senior official (C-suite or equivalent) confirming organizational compliance, signed and dated annually.
internal_actions: IT team: compile evidence repository (policies, logs, screenshots) supporting all 17 basic practices; provide self-assessment completion with narrative explanations.
Legal/Compliance team: prepare senior official affirmation letter template and track annual sign-off dates.
Security team: document practice implementation dates and any remediation actions taken during the year.
vendor_actions: Subcontractors handling FCI/CUI: provide written confirmation of their own Level 1 compliance status and self-assessment results upon request.
compliance_evidence: Signed annual compliance affirmation letter from senior official with date.
Completed CMMC Level 1 self-assessment questionnaire with supporting evidence attachments.
Master cybersecurity policy document cross-referenced to all 17 FAR 52.204-21 practices.
Evidence inventory (access logs, security training records, incident reports, system configuration screenshots) tied to each practice.

key_requirements.text: Level 2 (Advanced): 110 practices per NIST SP 800-171; triennial C3PAO third-party assessment for critical programs
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Map all 110 NIST SP 800-171 practices to your systems, policies, and operational procedures; establish a remediation timeline for any gaps.
2. Engage a qualified C3PAO (Certified Third-Party Assessment Organization) to conduct a triennial (every three-year) independent assessment and issue a formal CMMC certification report.
3. Maintain the C3PAO certification and submit proof of current certification status to relevant DoD contracting officers and include in proposal documentation.
internal_actions: IT team: provide comprehensive inventory of systems, network architecture diagrams, access control matrices, and encryption implementations aligned to 800-171 controls.
Compliance team: create a control matrix mapping all 110 practices to organizational policies, procedures, and technical controls; maintain remediation tracking.
Security team: prepare annual audit logs, incident response records, vulnerability assessments, and security training completion evidence for C3PAO review.
Finance/Contracts: budget and schedule C3PAO assessment every three years; track certification expiration dates.
vendor_actions: C3PAO: execute assessment agreement and conduct on-site review; deliver formal CMMC Level 2 certification report and scorecard.
Subcontractors: provide evidence of their own Level 2 certification (copy of C3PAO report) or commit to achieving it before contract start.
compliance_evidence: Signed C3PAO certification report with assigned maturity level and detailed scorecard.
Control matrix document mapping all 110 NIST SP 800-171 practices to organizational evidence.
C3PAO assessment summary and any identified gaps with corresponding remediation actions.
Proof of current certification status (certificate with expiration date) for contract proposals.
Subcontractor C3PAO certification copies or compliance commitment letters on file.

key_requirements.text: Level 3 (Expert): 24 additional practices per NIST SP 800-172; government-led assessment by DCSA
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Beyond the 110 Level 2 practices, implement the 24 advanced practices from NIST SP 800-172 (e.g., advanced threat detection, continuous monitoring, resilience).
2. Notify DCSA (Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency) of your Level 3 candidacy and coordinate a government-led assessment.
3. Complete the DCSA assessment and receive formal Level 3 certification, which is the highest maturity level.
internal_actions: IT/Security team: design and deploy advanced monitoring systems (SIEM, EDR, threat intelligence feeds) and resilience measures per 800-172 requirements.
CTO/CISO: document advanced practices including advanced persistent threat (APT) detection, automated incident response, and supply chain risk management in policies.
Compliance team: prepare comprehensive evidence dossier with all 24 advanced practices and their implementation status.
Executive team: commit organizational resources and leadership engagement for government assessment process.
vendor_actions: DCSA: schedule and conduct government-led assessment; provide detailed findings and certification determination.
Critical subcontractors: commit to achieving Level 2 minimum; may be required to achieve Level 3 for sensitive programs.
compliance_evidence: Signed DCSA Level 3 certification document and formal authorization letter.
Documentation of all 24 NIST SP 800-172 advanced practices with technical implementation details (configuration files, monitoring dashboards, threat logs).
DCSA assessment report and compliance findings.
Evidence of advanced monitoring systems (SIEM logs, EDR alerts, threat intelligence integration examples) covering 90+ days of operations.

key_requirements.text: Plans of Action and Milestones (POA&Ms) allowed for Level 1 and 2 assessment gaps
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Identify and document any practices not yet fully implemented during self-assessment or C3PAO assessment, assigning risk ratings and remediation timelines.
2. For each gap, develop a detailed Plan of Action and Milestone (POA&M) that specifies the corrective action, responsible owner, required resources, and completion date.
3. Submit POA&Ms as part of the assessment report or gap remediation plan; track and report progress monthly to leadership and (if requested) to the contracting officer.
internal_actions: Compliance/Security team: conduct gap analysis and create POA&M document for each identified deficiency, including risk assessment and mitigation strategy.
IT team: provide cost and resource estimates for each remediation action; confirm feasibility of target completion dates.
Project Management/Finance: allocate budget and schedule resources; establish monthly progress review meetings.
Leadership: review and approve all POA&Ms before submission to customers or assessors.
vendor_actions: C3PAO (if applicable): accept POA&Ms as part of assessment findings and confirm acceptable remediation timelines per CMMC rules.
Subcontractors: submit their own POA&Ms if they have gaps and commit to closure dates aligned with prime contractor schedule.
compliance_evidence: Formal POA&M document(s) listing all gaps, assigned risk levels, corrective actions, owners, and target closure dates.
Risk assessment and justification for each gap remediation timeline.
Monthly progress status reports showing implementation updates, milestone completion, and evidence of closure.
C3PAO acknowledgment of POA&M acceptability in assessment report.
Final evidence (logs, policies, test results) demonstrating closure of each POA&M item.

key_requirements.text: Affirmation by a senior official confirming compliance annually
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Designate a senior official (CEO, President, or equivalent C-suite officer) authorized to affirm compliance on behalf of the organization.
2. Prepare an affirmation statement that explicitly confirms the organization's compliance with the applicable CMMC level, signed and dated by the senior official each year.
3. File the signed affirmation with compliance records and provide a copy to contracting officers and customers upon request.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft affirmation statement template referencing the specific CMMC level and 17 (Level 1) or 110+ (Level 2/3) practices; route to senior official for signature.
Compliance team: maintain a signed affirmation letter on file with date of signature; send reminder notices 30 days before annual renewal.
IT team: ensure self-assessment evidence is complete and available to support the senior official's affirmation.
vendor_actions: Subcontractors: provide their own signed senior official affirmation letter annually, confirming their CMMC compliance status.
compliance_evidence: Original or certified copy of signed annual affirmation letter from senior official with date and title.
Affirmation statement document explicitly referencing CMMC level and foundational practices affirmed.
Dated compliance self-assessment or C3PAO report supporting the affirmation.
File tracking log showing annual renewal cycle and senior official succession (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Flow-down requirements: prime contractors must impose CMMC on subcontractors handling FCI/CUI
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Review all prime contracts with DoD and identify which subcontractors handle Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI).
2. Amend subcontracts and supply chain agreements to explicitly require the subcontractor to achieve and maintain the same (or higher) CMMC level as the prime contractor.
3. Establish a flow-down governance process: track subcontractor CMMC certification status, conduct periodic audits, and address non-compliance promptly.
internal_actions: Contracts/Procurement team: insert CMMC flow-down clauses into all relevant subcontracts specifying required maturity level, certification deadline, and annual proof of compliance.
Compliance team: maintain a subcontractor CMMC tracking spreadsheet with certification status, expiration dates, and audit results; escalate expired or lapsed certifications.
IT/Security team: integrate subcontractor security requirements into supplier risk assessment process; conduct or review subcontractor assessments annually.
Legal team: prepare template subcontract language and review for consistency and enforceability.
vendor_actions: All subcontractors handling FCI/CUI: obtain CMMC certification at the required level (typically Level 2 for NIST 800-171 contractors); provide copy of C3PAO certification report or self-assessment.
Subcontractors: commit to annual compliance affirmation and notify prime contractor of any material security incidents or loss of certification within 48 hours.
Subcontractors: submit to periodic audits or reassessment by prime contractor or C3PAO as contract requires.
compliance_evidence: Executed subcontracts or amendments containing explicit CMMC flow-down requirements and maturity level specifications.
Subcontractor CMMC tracking register showing certification status, C3PAO report copies, and expiration dates.
Copies of all subcontractor C3PAO certification reports or self-assessment documentation on file.
Evidence of annual subcontractor compliance audits or reviews (assessment reports, compliance affirmations).
Incident reports or escalation logs documenting any subcontractor non-compliance and remediation actions taken.
Flow-down requirement template language included in all new subcontracts.

regulations.penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors

regulations.name: New York DFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) — 2023 Amendment
regulations.authority: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)
regulations.summary: The New York Department of Financial Services cybersecurity regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) imposes prescriptive security requirements on all DFS-licensed entities. Originally effective March 2017, a major amendment effective November 1, 2023 substantially expanded requirements: mandatory multi-factor authentication for all systems, 24-hour incident notification to NYDFS, enhanced CISO requirements, annual penetration testing, vulnerability management, and board-level cybersecurity accountability.

key_requirements.text: Establish and maintain a cybersecurity programme based on an annual risk assessment
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; consent orders; fines; licence actions
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Annual risk assessment report; cybersecurity programme documentation; board approval
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Commission an annual third-party risk assessment covering all material assets, data flows, and threat vectors relevant to your financial services operations. 2. Document findings in a formal Risk Assessment Report with likelihood and impact ratings for each identified risk. 3. Map identified risks to specific cybersecurity controls within your documented programme and review alignment annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: confirm scope of NPI and regulated data holdings; IT team: provide asset inventory, network diagrams, and data flow documentation; Risk/Compliance team: compile annual risk register and control mapping spreadsheet.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party assessor: deliver signed Risk Assessment Report with executive summary, detailed findings, and remediation roadmap; attestation of assessment methodology used.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual Risk Assessment Report; documented risk register with control mappings; evidence of board or committee review and sign-off; prior-year assessment comparison showing updates.

key_requirements.text: Designate a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) responsible for cybersecurity programme
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; programme deficiency finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: CISO appointment; annual CISO board report; role responsibilities
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a named CISO (internal or contracted) with explicit authority over cybersecurity decisions and budget authority for security investments. 2. Document the appointment in board minutes or equivalent governance record, including role title, reporting line, and delegated responsibilities. 3. Establish a formal cybersecurity steering committee or equivalent with CISO as chair, meeting at least quarterly.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide employment contract or engagement letter for named CISO showing start date and reporting structure; Legal/Board: provide board minutes or signed resolution appointing CISO; Finance: confirm budget authority and allocation to security function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: If CISO is contracted: provide signed service agreement or engagement letter explicitly naming the individual, scope of authority, and cybersecurity programme responsibilities.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or minutes naming CISO with effective date; CISO employment contract or engagement letter; organisational chart showing CISO reporting line; steering committee meeting minutes and attendee list.

key_requirements.text: Implement multi-factor authentication for remote access and privileged account access
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; consent orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: MFA deployment records; privileged access inventory; authentication policy
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Deploy multi-factor authentication (MFA) enforcement at directory/identity layer (e.g. Active Directory, Azure AD) for all remote access and all privileged account access, requiring at minimum a second factor (authenticator app, hardware token, or SMS). 2. Configure conditional access policies or equivalent controls to block non-MFA access and log all authentication attempts. 3.
