Test and document MFA enforcement across remote access tools (VPN, RDP, SSH) and for all accounts with elevated permissions (admin, DBA, security roles).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide MFA deployment plan, configuration screenshots, and active directory group membership reports for privileged accounts; Security team: provide MFA testing results and exception register (if any); Audit team: confirm MFA coverage across all remote and privileged access vectors.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: MFA vendor or identity provider: provide deployment certificate, configuration guide, and attestation of MFA enforcement status; VPN/remote access vendor: confirm MFA integration and provide logs showing MFA challenge rates.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Screenshots of MFA policy configuration in directory service; MFA deployment checklist signed by IT and security leads; authentication logs showing MFA successful/failed attempts over 30-day period; list of all privileged accounts with MFA enforcement confirmation; penetration test report confirming inability to bypass MFA.

key_requirements.text: Notify DFS within 72 hours of a cybersecurity event that has a reasonable likelihood of materially affecting operations or involving NPI
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident log; DFS notification receipts; incident classification methodology
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define 'cybersecurity event' in your incident response plan to align with DFS definition (unauthorised access, malware, denial of service, or other event affecting confidentiality, integrity, or availability of NPI or systems). 2. Establish a 72-hour incident notification workflow: incident detection → initial triage → escalation to CISO and legal team → draft notification to DFS within 72 hours of discovery. 3. Assign a dedicated incident response coordinator to track incident timeline, coordinate internal investigation, and manage DFS communication.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft DFS notification template and approval workflow; Compliance team: establish incident log template with discovery date, event type, and NPI impact assessment fields; IT/Security team: configure alerting rules and incident intake process; Executive team: confirm CISO and General Counsel are named escalation contacts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed security service provider or SOC (if used): confirm incident alert procedure, SLA for severity classification, and commitment to notify organisation within specified timeframe; forensics vendor: if engaged post-incident, confirm capability to deliver forensic report within timeframe needed for DFS notification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented incident response plan with 72-hour notification procedure; incident log showing discovery date, assessment date, and DFS notification date for any reported events; DFS notification letter(s) sent within 72 hours; email or meeting records showing CISO escalation; evidence of internal investigation and remediation steps taken.

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual penetration testing and bi-annual vulnerability assessments
key_requirements.consequence: DFS examination findings; enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Penetration test reports; vulnerability scan results; remediation tracking
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage a qualified independent penetration testing firm to conduct annual full-scope penetration testing covering all external-facing systems, internal networks, and web applications used in financial services operations. 2. Conduct bi-annual vulnerability assessments using automated scanning tools (or third-party vendor) covering all assets, with results reviewed and prioritised by risk rating. 3. Document all findings, remediation actions, and re-test results; remediate critical and high-severity findings within defined SLAs (typically 30–90 days).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: identify all systems in scope and provide network/application inventory; IT team: provide access and change windows for testing; Risk/Compliance team: maintain remediation tracking log and re-test status for all findings; Management: approve testing schedule and remediation budget.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Penetration testing firm: deliver signed pentest report with scope, methodology, detailed findings with CVSS ratings, and executive summary; attestation of tester qualifications (e.g., OSCP, CEH); Vulnerability scanning vendor: provide weekly/monthly scan reports with vulnerability inventory, severity distribution, and trend analysis; re-test confirmation for closed findings.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual penetration test report from independent assessor with findings and remediation recommendations; bi-annual vulnerability assessment reports (e.g., Nessus or Qualys scans) with timestamps and findings counts; remediation tracking spreadsheet showing finding ID, discovery date, target remediation date, closure date, and evidence of closure; management sign-off on remediation plan.

regulations.penalties: Up to $250,000 per violation; up to $2,500 per day for extended violations; up to $1 million for serious or systemic violations (2023 amendments)

regulations.name: Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act
regulations.authority: Private right of action - enforced by courts (no state agency)
regulations.summary: The nation's most powerful biometric privacy law, prohibiting private entities from collecting, storing, or profiting from biometric identifiers (fingerprints, retinal scans, facial geometry, voiceprints, handprints) without prior informed written consent.

key_requirements.text: Obtain written informed consent before collecting any biometric identifier or biometric information
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a biometric consent form that clearly describes the specific biometric identifiers to be collected, the purpose, and retention period. 2. Implement a documented sign-off process requiring individuals to execute written consent before any biometric data collection begins. 3. Maintain signed consent records indexed by individual and collection date for audit purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide final consent template and approval; IT team: configure system to block biometric collection until consent is recorded; Compliance team: design and document consent workflow with approval chains.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Biometric vendors: provide written confirmation that their collection interface enforces consent capture before data processing; confirm consent records are retained and made available for audit.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms (sample set from each collection point); system logs showing consent timestamp before first biometric capture; policy document defining consent requirements and approval process.

key_requirements.text: Publish a publicly available written retention and destruction policy
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a written retention and destruction policy specifying retention periods, destruction methods, and responsible parties. 2. Publish the policy on the company website and/or employee handbook in plain language accessible to all individuals whose data is collected. 3. Document the publication date and maintain version control with all policy updates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft retention and destruction policy with specific timelines and methods; Communications team: publish to website and confirm accessibility; Compliance team: maintain policy registry with publication dates and version history.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Website vendor or content manager: confirm policy publication and provide screenshot evidence; biometric service providers: acknowledge receipt and understanding of the retention and destruction policy.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published retention and destruction policy (dated web page or handbook excerpt); version control log showing publication date; screenshot or URL confirming public accessibility; vendor acknowledgment letter.

key_requirements.text: Destroy biometric data when the original purpose expires or within 3 years of collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement automated or manual tracking to record biometric data collection dates and link them to documented purpose expiration or the 3-year threshold. 2. Establish a quarterly data inventory and lifecycle review process to identify records due for destruction. 3. Execute and document destruction activities (deletion logs, certificate of destruction, or vendor destruction confirmation) before the deadline.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide data inventory reports showing collection dates, purpose codes, and destruction dates; Database/Security team: generate deletion logs and technical destruction confirmation; Compliance team: maintain destruction schedule and sign-off records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Biometric data processors: provide quarterly reports of data in custody with collection dates; furnish certified destruction logs or certificates of destruction upon deletion; confirm destruction method (secure deletion standard used).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data inventory spreadsheet with collection dates and destruction deadlines; quarterly destruction schedules with sign-offs; vendor destruction certificates or deletion logs; audit trail showing data removal from production systems.

key_requirements.text: No sale or other profit from biometric data under any circumstances
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a binding contractual policy prohibiting monetization or sale of biometric data in all vendor agreements and internal use guidelines. 2. Conduct a data flow audit to verify no biometric data is sold, licensed, or exchanged for value. 3. Include explicit audit rights and compliance certifications in vendor contracts to confirm no revenue-generating use occurs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft policy and amendment clauses prohibiting all sale/monetization of biometric data; Finance team: audit all vendor payments and revenue streams to confirm no biometric-related sales; Compliance team: document all data flows and third-party access.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All biometric vendors and data processors: provide signed certification that biometric data will not be sold or monetized; contractual prohibition on sale with audit rights; annual attestation of compliance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed vendor contracts with explicit prohibitions on biometric data sales; Finance audit report confirming no revenue from biometric data; vendor compliance certifications; internal policy document on non-monetization of biometric data.

key_requirements.text: No disclosure of biometric data without consent or court order
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all authorized recipients of biometric data and require explicit consent or court order documentation before any disclosure. 2. Implement access controls and logging to track all requests for and disclosures of biometric data. 3. Create a disclosure log recording date, recipient, purpose, and legal basis (consent or court order) for each disclosure event.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: maintain registry of authorized recipients and consent approvals; IT team: configure access controls and audit logging for biometric data queries; Compliance team: operate and review disclosure log monthly.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Biometric vendors: provide monthly access and disclosure logs showing who accessed data and why; confirm written policy prohibiting unauthorized disclosure; provide audit trail of all data transfers.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Disclosure log (sample entries) showing recipient, date, purpose, and legal basis; access control configuration documentation; vendor disclosure audit logs; consent forms or court order copies for each disclosed record.

key_requirements.text: Provide individuals with the right to know what biometric data is being collected and why
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a biometric data subject rights procedure enabling individuals to request what biometric data is held, why it is being collected, and the retention schedule. 2. Establish a response timeline (recommend 30 days) and assign responsibility for fulfilling requests. 3. Maintain records of all requests and responses to demonstrate compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft data subject rights request procedure and response template; IT team: develop query process to retrieve individual biometric records; Compliance team: establish request intake process and response tracking log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Biometric vendors: commit to supporting data subject access requests within 15–20 days; provide individual-level data retrieval capability; confirm cooperation with individual transparency requests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented data subject rights procedure (posted on website or in privacy notice); sample completed request forms and responses; response tracking log with request dates and fulfillment timelines; vendor commitment letter to support transparency requests.

regulations.penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million

regulations.name: Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Virginia Attorney General
regulations.summary: Virginia's comprehensive privacy law applying to controllers processing personal data of 100,000+ Virginia consumers annually. Modeled after the GDPR, establishes consumer rights and controller obligations with AG-only enforcement.

key_requirements.text: Data minimization - limit collection to what is adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data inventory audit mapping all personal data collected against specific business purposes and retention periods. 2. Document justification for each data element as adequate, relevant, and necessary using a Data Collection Assessment Form. 3. Implement automated controls to prevent collection of non-essential fields at the point of capture.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written purpose documentation for each data collection point. IT team: supply current data schema and collection logic documentation. Business owners: complete Data Collection Assessment Forms justifying each personal data element.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide attestation that any data shared with them aligns to agreed purposes and provide their data minimization procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Collection Assessment Forms, data inventory spreadsheet with purpose justification, system configuration screenshots showing collection controls, vendor attestation letters.

key_requirements.text: Purpose limitation - process data only for disclosed and compatible purposes
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a comprehensive Data Processing Policy that maps each data collection to its disclosed purposes and explicitly prohibits secondary uses without reconsent. 2. Embed purpose checks into data access approval workflows requiring justification before processing. 3. Train all data handlers on the policy and document attendance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Data Processing Policy with purpose matrices for each business unit. Compliance team: design approval workflow checklist for data access requests. HR team: schedule and track mandatory training completion with sign-off records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All processors: sign Data Processing Addendum (DPA) explicitly limiting their use to agreed purposes only and confirming no secondary processing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Policy, purpose matrices by business unit, completed training sign-off sheets, sample approved/denied data access request records, signed DPAs with all vendors.

key_requirements.text: Consumer rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, opt-out of targeted advertising/sale/profiling
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop technical mechanisms for access (export), correction, deletion, and portability (standardized format export) accessible via self-service portal or documented manual process. 2. Implement opt-out controls for targeted advertising, sales, and profiling with verification of preference enforcement in downstream systems. 3. Document all request handling procedures and test end-to-end fulfillment quarterly.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: design and deploy consumer rights portal with access, correction, deletion, and portability modules; provide test case documentation. Privacy team: create Consumer Rights Request Log template with request type, timestamp, and fulfillment proof. QA team: execute quarterly testing of all five rights mechanisms with results documented.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors and advertising partners: provide written confirmation of technical capability to honor opt-out signals and deletion requests, including API integration documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Consumer rights portal screenshots with functional demonstrations, Consumer Rights Request Log entries showing timely fulfillment, quarterly QA test reports, signed vendor confirmations of opt-out capability.

key_requirements.text: Data protection assessments for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify high-risk processing activities (e.g., profiling, automated decision-making, sensitive data handling) and document in a Risk Assessment Inventory. 2. Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for each high-risk activity, recording risk description, mitigation measures, and residual risk. 3. Review and update assessments annually or when processing activities change materially.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Privacy team: create and maintain Risk Assessment Inventory with categorization of processing activities. Data handlers and IT team: complete DPIA templates for assigned activities including mitigation controls. Compliance team: maintain version control and approval records for all DPIAs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors handling high-risk data: provide completed DPIAs or risk assessments for their processing activities; confirm alignment with organisation's risk tolerance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Risk Assessment Inventory, completed DPIAs with approval sign-offs, mitigation control implementation evidence, annual review updates with change logs.

key_requirements.text: Controller-processor agreements required
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third-party processors of personal data and maintain a current Processor Registry. 2. Develop a template Data Processing Addendum (DPA) defining processor obligations, sub-processor rules, data security requirements, and deletion/return procedures. 3. Execute DPAs with all existing processors before processing begins; obtain renewals at contract anniversary.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft DPA template compliant with Virginia CDPA. Vendor Management team: compile current Processor Registry and flag gaps. Procurement team: embed DPA as mandatory component of processor contract renewal workflow.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All processors: execute signed DPA within 30 days of request; provide list of sub-processors and confirm sub-processor agreements are in place.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current Processor Registry with execution dates, executed DPAs for all processors, sub-processor notifications received, contract amendments documenting DPA incorporation.

key_requirements.text: Respond to consumer requests within 45 days (with 45-day extension if needed)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented Consumer Request Handling Procedure specifying intake, routing, fulfillment, and response steps with clear 45-day deadline and extension protocol. 2. Implement a Consumer Request Tracking Log (spreadsheet or system) recording request receipt date, type, fulfillment date, and extension justification if applicable. 3. Designate a request coordinator and set calendar reminders for approaching deadlines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft Consumer Request Handling Procedure and disseminate to all relevant departments. IT team: provide Consumer Request Tracking Log template or configure intake system with deadline alerts. Legal team: define extension criteria and approval process.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Processors: commit in DPA to provide requested data exports or deletion confirmations within 10 days to allow parent organisation to meet the 45-day deadline.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Consumer Request Handling Procedure, populated Consumer Request Tracking Log with dates and proof of response, examples of on-time responses, extension justification records, signed vendor commitments to meet support deadlines.

key_requirements.text: Opt-in consent required for processing sensitive data categories
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all sensitive data categories processed by the organisation (precise personal information, genetic data, health data, etc.) and document which business functions collect each category.
2. Implement a consent capture mechanism (consent banner, form, or API) that records affirmative opt-in prior to any sensitive data processing, with timestamped proof of consent.
3. Establish a consent management system (CMS) or database that maintains audit trails linking each data subject's consent status to processing activities.
internal_actions: Legal team: provide written classification of all sensitive data categories your organisation processes and the business justification for each.
IT team: configure consent management platform settings and provide system access logs showing consent capture, storage, and retrieval for audit purposes.
Marketing/Product team: supply documentation of all user-facing consent flows (screenshots, wireframes, or functional specifications) where sensitive data opt-in occurs.
vendor_actions: CMS vendor: provide technical specification sheet confirming timestamped consent recording, audit trail retention (minimum 3 years), and API documentation for consent queries.

Analytics/marketing platform vendors: confirm in writing their acceptance of opt-in consent signals before processing sensitive data categories and their ability to honour revocation requests within 45 days.
compliance_evidence: Signed consent management system procurement contract or SaaS agreement.
Data Processing Inventory document mapping sensitive categories to opt-in consent requirements.
Sample timestamped consent records and audit trail exports from CMS showing at least 10 representative data subjects.
Consent flow screenshots or recorded user journey demonstrating clear affirmative action prior to sensitive data processing.
Internal policy or Standard Operating Procedure document detailing sensitive data handling and consent prerequisites.

key_requirements.text: Recognize universal opt-out mechanisms (GPC) from 2024
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Integrate Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal detection into all data collection touchpoints (website, mobile app, APIs) so that GPC headers are recognised and logged.
2. Configure your consent management system and marketing automation tools to treat inbound GPC signals as a universal opt-out instruction effective immediately upon receipt.
3. Implement a weekly automated audit to verify that no sensitive data processing, sale, or sharing occurs for visitors/users whose GPC signal was detected in the past 7 days.
internal_actions: IT/Engineering team: integrate GPC signal detection library (e.g., standard header check: Sec-GPC: 1) and provide code repository commit logs, testing reports, and documentation of GPC handling logic.
Compliance/Legal team: issue written instruction to all data processing teams (marketing, analytics, sales) confirming that GPC = do-not-sell and do-not-share directive.
Data Governance team: document in Data Processing Register which systems have GPC detection enabled and provide monthly logs of GPC signal detections and corresponding opt-out actions taken.
vendor_actions: Web analytics vendor (e.g., Google Analytics, Mixpanel): confirm in writing that they honour GPC signals and provide audit logs of GPC detections in your account.
Marketing/CDP vendor: provide attestation that their platform respects GPC signals for data sharing and sale, and confirm their default setting when GPC is detected.
Cookie consent management vendor: supply configuration export showing GPC as a recognized signal triggering automatic preference opt-out.
compliance_evidence: Technical specification or architecture document describing GPC signal detection implementation and integration points.
Code repository commit logs or pull request history showing GPC signal handling.
Automated test results (unit or integration tests) demonstrating correct response to GPC header in requests.
Sample web server or CDN access logs showing Sec-GPC: 1 header detection and corresponding no-processing actions.
Signed vendor attestations confirming GPC support and audit logs from third-party platforms showing GPC signal recognition.
Monthly GPC audit report (automated export) listing number of GPC signals detected and confirmation of zero processing/sale/sharing for those users.

regulations.penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action)

regulations.name: Colorado Privacy Act
regulations.authority: Colorado Attorney General and District Attorneys
regulations.summary: Colorado's comprehensive privacy law requiring organizations to recognize universal opt-out mechanisms like Global Privacy Control (GPC). Applies to organizations controlling or processing personal data of 100,000+ Colorado consumers annually.

key_requirements.text: Consumer rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, opt-out of targeted advertising/sale/profiling
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Build API endpoints or web portal enabling consumers to submit access, correction, deletion, and portability requests with identity verification. 2. Establish 45-day fulfillment SLA and create audit logs documenting each request and response. 3. Implement opt-out functionality in marketing platform to suppress targeted advertising, sale, and profiling for verified consumers.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide architecture design and API specifications for consumer request portal; Legal team: draft consumer rights request templates and fulfillment procedures; Marketing team: document current targeted advertising, sale, and profiling practices to identify systems requiring opt-out capability.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Marketing vendors: provide opt-out API documentation and confirmation of ability to honor opt-out signals; Analytics vendors: confirm deletion and portability capabilities for consumer data sets.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Consumer request portal user documentation, API audit logs showing requests received and fulfilled within 45 days, opt-out confirmation records, data portability export samples.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory recognition of universal opt-out mechanisms (GPC) since July 2024
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit current website and application code to identify all tracking mechanisms and assess GPC signal detection capability. 2. Implement browser-side GPC signal recognition and integrate with existing opt-out workflow by end of 2024. 3. Document and test GPC signal processing across all digital properties and third-party integrations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: conduct GPC capability audit and deploy signal detection code; Web Development team: integrate GPC processing into existing consent management system; Compliance team: create GPC signal log and verification testing documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Tag management vendors: confirm GPC support and signal pass-through; Analytics platforms: request GPC compliance certification and configuration documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: GPC signal detection code repository, testing reports showing GPC signals are captured and honored, configuration screenshots from consent management system, vendor compliance attestations.

key_requirements.text: Process opt-out signals from authorized agents without verification of the agent
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a whitelist of authorized agent categories (power of attorney, guardians, designated representatives) and document acceptance criteria without requiring secondary identity verification of the agent themselves. 2. Build automated workflow to accept and process opt-out signals from authorized agents using only consumer identity confirmation. 3. Log all agent-submitted requests with authorization category and fulfillment status.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: define authorized agent types and create processing framework; IT team: develop agent submission portal with consumer identity verification only; Compliance team: establish audit logs and monthly review procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Consent management vendors: confirm ability to process authorized agent opt-out signals without agent verification; Customer service platform: update scripts to facilitate authorized agent submissions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Authorized agent acceptance policy document, automated workflow configuration, audit logs showing agent-submitted requests processed without agent verification, sample processed requests.

key_requirements.text: Data protection assessments required for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify high-risk processing activities (profiling, targeted advertising, cross-context behavioral advertising, sale of personal data) and classify each using CPA risk criteria. 2. Commission or conduct data protection impact assessments (DPIA) for each high-risk activity documenting risks, mitigation measures, and safeguards. 3. Review and update assessments annually or when processing purposes change materially.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Protection Officer or Privacy team: create high-risk activity inventory with justification; Legal team: define DPIA template and approval process; IT team: document technical safeguards implemented for each high-risk activity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide documentation of high-risk processing activities they perform on behalf of the organization and their existing risk mitigations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: High-risk processing activity register, completed DPIAs with dated approvals, mitigation control matrices, annual review records, vendor risk assessment summaries.

key_requirements.text: Heightened consent requirements for sensitive data categories
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define sensitive data categories under CPA (health, financial, biometric, precise geolocation, race/ethnicity, union membership, etc.) and map to your current data collection inventory. 2. Implement affirmative, explicit opt-in mechanisms (checkbox with clear language) for each sensitive category, separate from general consent. 3. Document consent choices in audit-ready logs and establish denial-of-service protocols if consumers withhold consent for non-essential processing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Privacy team: create sensitive data categories and processing purpose mapping; Legal team: draft category-specific consent language; IT team: implement granular consent recording in data management systems.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Consent management platform: request sensitive category tagging functionality and consent signal logging; Data processors: confirm ability to honor category-level opt-in decisions and restrict processing accordingly.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Sensitive data category classification document, consent form/UX screenshots with category-specific opt-in fields, consent preference database logs, data access logs demonstrating category-level restrictions applied.

key_requirements.text: Controller-processor data processing agreements required
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third parties that process personal data on your behalf (processors, service providers, sub-processors) and classify as controller or processor. 2. Execute or amend Data Processing Agreements (DPA) with all processors incorporating CPA-required provisions (data protection standards, audit rights, sub-processor obligations, consumer rights support). 3. Maintain DPA register with execution dates and annual compliance attestations from processors.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: review existing vendor contracts and create standardized CPA-compliant DPA template; Vendor Management team: inventory all current processors and identify gaps; Compliance team: maintain DPA register and track execution status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All data processors: execute or amend DPA to include CPA obligations; Processors: provide annual data protection compliance attestation or SOC 2 report; Sub-processors: provide signed commitment to comply with processor DPA terms.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Executed DPAs with all processors showing CPA-required language, DPA register with dates and processor names, vendor attestations or audits, evidence of processor sub-processor notification and approval.

key_requirements.text: Respond to consumer requests within 45 days (with 45-day extension available)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented consumer request intake process with timestamped entry point (email, web form, or ticketing system) that logs all incoming data subject access, deletion, and opt-out requests.

2. Configure automated workflow routing to assign requests to the responsible data handling team and set calendar reminders for day 30 (initial response) and day 45 (final deadline or extension notification).

3. Maintain a request register capturing request ID, submission date, consumer identifier, request type, resolution date, and extension justification (if applicable) to enable audit trail and deadline compliance monitoring.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve the consumer request response template and policy defining circumstances for invoking the 45-day extension.
IT team: configure request logging and workflow management system with automated deadline alerts; provide monthly reports of request volumes, response times, and extension usage.
Compliance team: establish standard operating procedures for acknowledging requests within 10 business days and define data retrieval timelines by data source.
vendor_actions: Third-party data processors and service providers: provide signed Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming obligation to support consumer requests and their maximum turnaround time for returning data or confirming deletion.
Cloud and SaaS vendors: confirm in writing their capability to provide or delete customer personal data within 15 business days and document any dependencies or limitations.
compliance_evidence: Consumer request register (spreadsheet or system export) showing all requests from the past 24 months with submission date, request type, response date, and status.
Sample completed consumer responses (minimum 3 per request type) demonstrating timely acknowledgment and substantive reply within the 45-day window.
Workflow screenshots or system configuration showing automated deadline tracking and escalation rules.
Signed DPAs with all third-party vendors containing consumer request support obligations.
Monthly compliance metrics report confirming 100% of requests responded to within 45 days or documented extension notifications sent by day 45.

regulations.penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action)

regulations.name: Australian Privacy Act 1988 with Australian Privacy Principles and NDB Scheme
regulations.authority: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC)
regulations.summary: Australia's primary privacy law applying to Australian Government agencies and most private-sector organizations with annual turnover over AUD 3 million. The 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) govern all aspects of personal information handling. The Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) Scheme requires notification of eligible breaches.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals of what personal information is collected and why at or before the time of collection (APP 5)
key_requirements.consequence: OAIC investigation; civil penalties up to A$50M for serious or repeated breaches
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy policy; collection notice templates; delivery confirmation records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop collection notices (privacy statements, website banners, form disclaimers) that clearly state the categories of personal information collected, collection method, and primary purpose. 2. Embed these notices at the point of collection across all channels (online forms, paper forms, phone calls, in-person interactions). 3. Maintain a collection notice register documenting each notice, its content, channels, and deployment date.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve privacy collection notices for each data collection point; Communications team: deploy notices on website, forms, and customer touchpoints; Compliance team: maintain register of all notices with effective dates and version control.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers (e.g. CRM, survey platforms, payment processors): confirm in writing they will display organisation's privacy notice or provide notice on behalf of the organisation; confirm collection notice requirements in Data Processing Agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed-off collection notice templates, screenshots of deployed notices on website/forms/systems, privacy notice register with dates and versions, Data Processing Agreements with vendors confirming notice obligations, audit log of notice deployments.

key_requirements.text: Use and disclose personal information only for the primary purpose of collection or a reasonably expected secondary purpose (APP 6)
key_requirements.consequence: OAIC investigation; civil penalties up to A$50M
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data use register; secondary purpose justification records; consent records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the primary purpose of collection for each personal information category in a data purpose register. 2. Where secondary use is intended, conduct a 'reasonableness test' (would the individual reasonably expect this use?) and obtain explicit consent if the secondary purpose is not reasonably related. 3. Train staff on authorised uses and implement system controls (role-based access, data use policies in employment contracts) to restrict access and use to documented purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create and maintain data purpose register linking each personal information type to primary and approved secondary purposes; HR team: include data use restrictions in employment contracts and confidentiality agreements; IT team: configure role-based access controls to limit staff access to data relevant to their documented purpose.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors processing personal information: provide signed Data Processing Agreements specifying the permitted purposes for processing, confirmation they will not use data for their own purposes, and contractual clauses restricting secondary disclosure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data purpose register with documented primary and secondary purposes, signed Data Processing Agreements with purpose limitations, role-based access control configuration documentation, staff training records on data use policy, sample employment/confidentiality agreements with use restrictions.

key_requirements.text: Take reasonable steps to protect personal information from misuse, interference, loss, and unauthorised access, disclosure, or modification (APP 11)
key_requirements.consequence: Civil penalties up to A$50M; OAIC enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security risk assessment; technical and organisational controls evidence; incident history
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data security risk assessment identifying personal information assets, storage locations, access points, and threats; document the assessment and agree on mitigations with IT and management. 2. Implement a baseline of technical and administrative controls: encryption of personal information in transit and at rest, access controls (authentication, authorisation, role-based access), audit logging, regular backups, and incident response procedures. 3. Document all controls in a security control register and perform annual or event-triggered reviews to ensure controls remain appropriate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: perform data security risk assessment and provide documented findings; implement encryption standards for data at rest and in transit, multi-factor authentication, role-based access, and centralised audit logging; schedule penetration testing annually. Security team: maintain security control register and incident response procedures. Compliance team: verify control implementation and document evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and cloud service providers: provide ISO 27001 or SOC 2 Type II certification, or detailed security questionnaire responses; confirm encryption methods, access controls, audit logging, backup procedures, and subcontractor security requirements in Data Processing Agreements; commit to annual security assessments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data security risk assessment report with identified risks and agreed mitigations, encryption certificates/configuration documentation, multi-factor authentication configuration records, access control matrix and role definitions, audit logs showing authentication and data access events, backup and disaster recovery test reports, vendor security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), completed vendor security questionnaires, Data Processing Agreements with security clauses.

key_requirements.text: Notify OAIC and affected individuals of eligible data breaches as soon as practicable (Notifiable Data Breaches scheme)
key_requirements.consequence: Civil penalties for failure to notify; OAIC enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach assessment log; NDB assessment records; OAIC notification receipts
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data breach response procedure defining: what constitutes an 'eligible data breach' (unauthorised access/disclosure/loss of personal information likely to result in serious harm), roles and responsibilities for detection/assessment/notification, and escalation triggers to OAIC and affected individuals. 2. Implement technical and procedural detection mechanisms: security monitoring alerts, incident response team contact protocols, and breach assessment decision criteria. 3. Upon breach confirmation, notify OAIC and affected individuals 'as soon as practicable' (without unreasonable delay) using the prescribed notification template, documenting the breach date, nature, affected individuals, harm risk, and mitigation steps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft data breach response procedure defining 'eligible data breach' criteria, notification timelines, OAIC reporting templates, and individual notification content; provide staff decision trees for breach assessment. IT team: configure security monitoring and alerting systems, establish incident response team with emergency contact protocols, maintain breach incident logs. Compliance team: maintain breach register and track notification evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and service providers: contractually commit to immediately notify the organisation of any suspected or confirmed data breaches involving personal information; provide breach notification procedures and escalation contacts in Data Processing Agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed data breach response procedure with decision criteria and notification templates, security monitoring configuration and alert logs showing breach detection, breach incident register with dates and assessments, OAIC breach notification submission confirmations (or draft if not yet required), individual notification letters (email, SMS, postal) with dates sent, proof of delivery, affected individual communications, breach assessment documentation showing 'serious harm' evaluation, incident investigation report, remediation steps taken.

regulations.penalties: Up to AUD 50 million per violation for serious or repeated breaches; individual penalties also apply under 2024 amendments

milestones.label: Privacy Act Review reforms tabled in Parliament
Anticipated commencement of reformed Privacy Act

regulations.name: Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (SOCI)
regulations.authority: Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) / Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre (CISC)
regulations.summary: Australian law imposing security obligations on owners and operators of critical infrastructure across 11 sectors: electricity, gas, water and sewerage, ports, data storage and processing, financial market infrastructure, food and grocery, healthcare and medical, space technology, broadcasting, and transport.

key_requirements.text: Register critical infrastructure assets with the Register of Critical Infrastructure Assets
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a comprehensive asset inventory identifying all critical infrastructure systems and their dependencies. 2. Complete the Register of Critical Infrastructure Assets submission form with asset classifications, ownership details, and system descriptions. 3. Maintain a live register internally and update registrations within 30 days of material changes to asset status or ownership.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/Operations team: provide asset inventory spreadsheet with system names, CRITIS classifications, and jurisdictional impacts. Legal team: confirm ownership structure and beneficial interest declarations. IT team: document critical system locations and cross-border data flows.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting/Cloud providers: provide written confirmation of infrastructure location and data residency. Third-party operators: supply system architecture diagrams showing their components within the critical infrastructure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed Register of Critical Infrastructure Assets submission with ASD acknowledgement. Internal asset registry with audit trail of changes. Ownership certification letter signed by company secretary or director. Screenshots of registered asset status in the official register.

key_requirements.text: Develop and maintain a Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP) reviewed annually by the board
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a written Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP) document addressing threat identification, risk assessment, mitigation strategies, and governance accountability. 2. Establish an annual review cycle with documented board approval, including risk appetite statements and resource allocation for critical controls. 3. Maintain CIRMP version control with change logs and board meeting minutes recording approval.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk/Compliance team: draft CIRMP document including threat landscape analysis and control framework. Board secretariat: schedule annual CIRMP review, prepare board papers with risk heat maps. IT/Security team: provide baseline assessment of existing controls and identified gaps. Finance team: budget allocation evidence for critical infrastructure security initiatives.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Risk consultants/auditors: provide independent risk assessment report or third-party validation of CIRMP effectiveness. Managed security service providers: supply annual security posture assessments aligned to CIRMP objectives.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed CIRMP document with board approval resolution and date. Board meeting minutes recording CIRMP discussion and approval. Annual CIRMP review checklist with sign-off by board chair and CEO. Risk register with control mapping to CIRMP framework and evidence of risk treatment decisions.

key_requirements.text: Report cybersecurity incidents to ASD within 12 hours for critical infrastructure systems
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a cybersecurity incident response procedure defining what constitutes a reportable incident, assessment criteria, and escalation to ASD, with a 12-hour clock starting from detection or reasonable belief of compromise. 2. Configure security monitoring and alerting to flag potential critical incidents with timestamps and create an incident log template capturing mandatory reporting fields. 3. Designate an incident response lead with ASD contact authority and conduct quarterly tabletop exercises simulating critical cybersecurity incidents to validate the 12-hour reporting timeline.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: provide incident response procedure document, monitoring alert rules, and incident log template. CISO/Security Lead: confirm ASD emergency contact details and authority delegation. Legal/Compliance team: prepare incident notification template with required disclosure elements. Incident response team: maintain timestamped incident register with detection-to-report intervals.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: SOC/Managed security providers: commit to immediate incident flagging and 24/7 availability for incident response consultation. Forensic/IR firms: provide pre-engagement letters confirming availability within 2 hours and capability to support ASD reporting.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response procedure dated and approved by CISO/CEO. Timestamped ASD incident report(s) submitted within 12 hours with incident summary, impact assessment, and timeline. Incident logs showing detection timestamp, assessment period, and notification time to ASD. Email confirmation or ticket reference from ASD acknowledging receipt. Records of incident response drills with attendee sign-in and timeline validation.

key_requirements.text: Report other serious security incidents within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define 'serious security incident' in policy (e.g., data exfiltration, ransomware, credential compromise) and document the 72-hour reporting clock starting from discovery or reasonable belief of the incident. 2. Integrate serious incidents into the incident management system with mandatory fields for notification status, recipient details, and reporting timestamp. 3. Assign accountability for 72-hour reporting to a named incident coordinator and document all serious incidents in a compliance register with evidence of timely notification.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: provide serious incident definition matrix and 72-hour reporting procedure. Compliance team: create and maintain serious incident notification register with recipient names and submission timestamps. IT/forensics team: document investigation timelines and evidence of incident discovery dates. Legal team: prepare serious incident notification template covering regulatory/stakeholder disclosure.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response/forensics vendors: contractually commit to reporting timeline support and provide incident assessment reports within 48 hours. Insurance brokers: confirm cyber liability policy notification requirements and timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented serious incident definition and reporting policy approved by management. Serious incident notification register with timestamped entries, recipient confirmation emails, and investigation start dates. Copies of notifications sent to stakeholders within 72 hours. Incident summary reports documenting discovery date and notification date. Audit trail from IT systems showing incident detection timestamps.

key_requirements.text: Disclose offshore ownership interests and notify of changes to ownership above thresholds
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a beneficial ownership review identifying all entities with direct or indirect ownership interests above statutory thresholds (typically 20-25%), including offshore structures and nominee arrangements. 2. Prepare and lodge an offshore ownership disclosure statement with ASIC or relevant regulator, with certified schedules of ownership hierarchy and percentage holdings. 3. Establish a quarterly compliance checklist to identify and notify regulators of any changes to offshore ownership above thresholds within mandatory timeframes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Company Secretariat team: produce beneficial ownership register with full ownership hierarchy, including offshore entities and nominee details. Corporate Finance team: provide cap table and shareholder register extracts. Compliance team: create offshore ownership disclosure form and establish quarterly change notification calendar. External company secretaries: supply certified corporate structure documents.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Corporate trust/custodian services: provide written confirmations of beneficial ownership positions and nominee relationships. Offshore legal counsel: certify foreign entity ownership structures and jurisdiction of registration.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed offshore ownership disclosure statement filed with regulator including certification by director/company secretary. Certified cap table and shareholder registry showing ownership percentages and offshore holder details. Statutory declaration or director's certificate confirming accuracy of beneficial ownership information. Regulatory filing receipts and acknowledgements. Change notification letters (if applicable) with regulator evidence of receipt.

key_requirements.text: Enhanced cybersecurity obligations for designated Systems of National Significance
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Confirm designation status as a System of National Significance (SNS) through ASD notification and cross-reference against the published SNS criteria and list. 2. Implement enhanced baseline security controls aligned to ASD's Essential Eight strategies (e.g., application whitelisting, patching, MFA, incident logging) with documented compliance mappings. 3. Conduct annual independent security assessments against SNS-specific obligations and maintain improvement plans with board oversight.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/Security team: request ASD SNS confirmation letter and map organisational controls to Essential Eight framework. IT team: provide patch management logs, MFA deployment reports, and application whitelisting configurations. Compliance team: develop SNS compliance checklist and maintain control evidence repository. Internal audit: schedule annual SNS control assessment.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Accredited security assessors: conduct SNS-scoped security audit and produce independent assessment report against Essential Eight and SOCI requirements. Cloud/infrastructure providers: certify Essential Eight control implementation within hosted environments (logging, patching, backups).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ASD notification letter confirming SNS designation. Essential Eight implementation roadmap with control status (implemented/in-progress/planned). Annual independent SNS security assessment report from accredited auditor. Control evidence matrix (screenshots, logs, configurations) mapped to Essential Eight controls. Board minutes recording SNS obligations review and remediation decisions. Patch management and incident log audit trails.

key_requirements.text: Government has step-in rights to direct response to major national cyber incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal incident response escalation procedure that explicitly grants the Australian Government (via ASD) authority to direct response actions during declared major national cyber incidents.
2. Document the conditions under which government step-in rights are activated, including notification triggers, communication channels, and command structure handover protocols.
3. Conduct annual tabletop exercises simulating government-directed incident response to validate procedures and ensure personnel understand their roles under government direction.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve incident response escalation procedure documenting government step-in rights and decision-making authority transfer.
IT Security team: create incident classification matrix defining what constitutes a 'major national cyber incident' requiring government activation.
Incident Response team: establish 24/7 contact registry for ASD liaison officers and define notification escalation paths.
vendor_actions: Third-party incident response providers: confirm contractual acceptance of government step-in rights and agree to comply with ASD-directed response actions.
Managed security service providers: commit to immediate incident information sharing and operational direction acceptance from ASD during national incidents.
compliance_evidence: Signed Incident Response Escalation Procedure document explicitly authorising government step-in rights.
Major Incident Classification Matrix showing criteria and approval authority.
Tabletop exercise reports with attendee sign-in and scenario outcomes demonstrating government direction capability.
Executed vendor addenda or letters of commitment confirming step-in rights acceptance.
ASD liaison contact agreements and 24/7 escalation contact list.

key_requirements.text: Notify ASD of system vulnerabilities that could result in significant harm
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vulnerability identification and assessment process that evaluates each discovered vulnerability against the 'significant harm' threshold defined by ASD guidelines.

2. Create a formal notification procedure to report vulnerabilities meeting the threshold to ASD within the required timeframe, including vulnerability details, affected systems, and remediation status.
3. Maintain a register of all reported vulnerabilities with ASD confirmation receipts and track remediation milestones to demonstrate ongoing compliance.
internal_actions: IT Security team: define 'significant harm' criteria aligned with ASD guidance and create a vulnerability triage checklist for daily assessment.
Legal/Compliance team: establish notification templates and approval workflow for ASD reporting, including sign-off authority and retention requirements.
IT Operations team: implement automated vulnerability scanning and logging to provide audit trail of vulnerability discovery and assessment decisions.
vendor_actions: Software vendors and application providers: commit to disclosing vulnerabilities in their products affecting the organisation within 48 hours of discovery.
Vulnerability disclosure/bug bounty platforms: provide APIs or reporting logs confirming submission of vulnerabilities to ASD and timestamp evidence.
compliance_evidence: ASD Vulnerability Reporting Procedure document with 'significant harm' threshold definition and approval workflow.
Vulnerability register showing discovery date, assessment outcome, ASD notification date, and ASD acknowledgement receipt.
ASD-issued incident/vulnerability acknowledgement letters or ticket numbers for reported vulnerabilities.
Vulnerability scanning logs and triage decision records demonstrating systematic assessment process.
Signed vendor agreements committing to timely vulnerability disclosure.

regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches

regulations.name: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006
regulations.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
regulations.summary: Australia's AML/CTF regime applying to reporting entities providing designated services including banks, credit unions, money remitters, casinos, bullion dealers, and digital currency exchanges. Major 2024 reforms extended obligations to Tranche 2 entities including lawyers, accountants, and real estate agents.

key_requirements.text: Enroll or register with AUSTRAC as a reporting entity
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify your entity type and applicable AML/CTF obligations under the Act. 2. Complete AUSTRAC's online registration form with accurate business, beneficial ownership, and compliance officer details. 3. Submit registration and retain the AUSTRAC registration certificate and confirmation letter.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: confirm entity classification and AML/CTF reporting obligations; Compliance team: designate AML/CTF compliance officer and document their contact details; Finance team: provide business structure and beneficial ownership information.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable for this requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: AUSTRAC registration certificate, AUSTRAC registration confirmation letter, registration application form with timestamps, internal memo designating AML/CTF compliance officer.

key_requirements.text: Develop and maintain an AML/CTF program including risk assessment and customer due diligence procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a documented AML/CTF risk assessment covering customer types, products, services, and geographic exposure. 2. Develop a written AML/CTF Program document detailing risk mitigation, CDD procedures, transaction monitoring, and reporting protocols. 3. Establish governance with board/senior management approval and annual review cycles.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft risk assessment report and AML/CTF Program document with sign-off; Legal team: review for regulatory alignment; Board/Senior Management: formally approve program in meeting minutes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party risk assessors (if engaged): provide preliminary risk assessment findings and audit report; Compliance consultants: deliver risk assessment methodology and program framework documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed AML/CTF Program document, dated risk assessment report, board resolution approving the program, annual review sign-offs, risk assessment update records.

key_requirements.text: Know Your Customer (KYC) / Customer Identification Program (CIP) for all customers
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define CIP/KYC procedures specifying identity verification methods (government ID, address verification, beneficial ownership confirmation) by customer risk tier. 2. Implement automated or manual verification workflows integrated with customer onboarding systems. 3. Maintain a centralized record of all CIP/KYC documentation with dates verified and verification method used.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure CIP/KYC workflows in core systems and provide audit logs of verifications performed; Compliance team: document CIP/KYC procedures, risk rating criteria, and exemption rules; Finance team: provide customer onboarding data sample.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Identity verification vendors: provide audit reports of verification accuracy, false negative/positive rates, and API/integration test results; Document vendors: certify quality of identity document scanning and storage.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CIP/KYC procedure manual, sample customer files showing ID copies and verification dates, system configuration documentation, transaction logs showing verification completion, vendor audit reports.

key_requirements.text: Transaction monitoring program to identify suspicious activity
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define transaction monitoring rules and thresholds targeting high-risk typologies (structuring, unusual beneficiaries, high-risk jurisdictions) documented in a monitoring policy. 2. Deploy transaction monitoring software with automated alert generation or establish manual review procedures; configure rule testing and tuning. 3. Document all alerts generated, investigations conducted, and outcome decisions (reported/dismissed) in a monitoring log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide transaction monitoring system configuration, rule documentation, alert logs, and sample investigation case files; Compliance team: draft monitoring policy and procedures; Finance team: provide transaction data for rule validation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Transaction monitoring software vendor: deliver system documentation, rule library, alert reporting capabilities, audit trail export, and performance metrics (hit rate, false positive ratio).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Transaction monitoring policy document, system configuration screenshots, sample alert logs with investigation notes, monitoring rules documentation, vendor system audit report.

key_requirements.text: File Suspicious Matter Reports (SMRs) to AUSTRAC within 24 hours of suspicion
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented SMR procedure defining suspicion triggers, assessment process, and approval by AML/CTF compliance officer before filing. 2. When suspicion arises, investigate the transaction/activity, document findings, and obtain compliance officer sign-off within 24 hours. 3. File SMR via AUSTRAC's online portal and retain confirmation number, SMR copy, and supporting investigation records.
