key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: develop SMR assessment template and suspicion trigger checklist; Legal team: review legal privilege and safe harbour provisions; Finance/Operations teams: provide transaction and customer context upon request.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable for this requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: SMR procedure document, signed SMR assessment forms with dates and compliance officer approval, AUSTRAC SMR filing confirmation (email/receipt), investigation working papers, internal SMR log tracking all filings.

key_requirements.text: File Threshold Transaction Reports (TTRs) for cash transactions of AUD 10,000+
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Configure transaction systems to identify and flag all cash transactions of AUD 10,000 or more (or equivalent foreign currency). 2. Implement automated TTR batch filing or manual reconciliation process to ensure all reportable transactions are captured and filed to AUSTRAC monthly. 3. Maintain a TTR register recording transaction details, filing date, AUSTRAC confirmation reference, and any corrections filed.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure transaction reporting system thresholds, export TTR data feeds, and provide system testing evidence; Finance team: validate accuracy of transaction amounts, customer identification, and filing frequency; Compliance team: design TTR filing procedure and register template.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Bank/Payment processor: provide transaction feeds in agreed format (CSV/XML) with field mapping; Transaction reporting software vendor: deliver TTR file formatting, batch processing capabilities, and AUSTRAC submission confirmation receipts.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: TTR register/log with monthly filing dates and AUSTRAC reference numbers, sample TTR data export files, system configuration evidence, AUSTRAC filing confirmations, reconciliation reports matching internal transactions to filed TTRs.

key_requirements.text: File International Funds Transfer Instructions (IFTIs) for all international transfers
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Configure your payments system to capture and validate all International Funds Transfer Instructions (IFTIs) with originator and beneficiary details before processing. 2. Establish a dedicated IFTI filing process that submits required fields (payer name, address, account number, beneficiary details, amount, currency, purpose) to AUSTRAC within the prescribed timeframe. 3. Maintain a reconciliation log matching filed IFTIs to actual transactions processed and retain all supporting documentation for 7 years.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide current international transfer workflow documentation and volume estimates; IT team: configure payment system to capture IFTI fields and generate audit trails; Legal team: document IFTI filing procedures and AUSTRAC submission schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Payment processor or bank: confirm IFTI data field compatibility, provide transaction-level export formats that include originator and beneficiary details, and supply a service level agreement confirming IFTI filing timelines and data retention periods.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed IFTI filing procedures document; sample filed IFTIs with AUSTRAC submission receipts or confirmation logs; transaction reconciliation spreadsheet linking filed IFTIs to cleared transfers; payment system configuration screenshots showing mandatory IFTI field capture; AUSTRAC filing portal audit logs for the past 12 months.

key_requirements.text: Tranche 2 entities (lawyers, accountants, real estate) must enroll from 2026
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify whether your organisation falls within Tranche 2 scope (legal practices, accounting firms, real estate agents, or trustees) and confirm the enrolment deadline is 1 January 2026. 2. Register with AUSTRAC's AML/CTF Registry before the deadline, submit the prescribed enrolment form with nominated AML/CTF Compliance Officer details and entity governance structure. 3. Document your enrolment confirmation and monitor AUSTRAC communications for any transitional obligations between now and 2026.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Governance team: confirm entity classification against Tranche 2 definitions and document scope decision; HR team: formally appoint or designate an AML/CTF Compliance Officer with written job description; Finance/IT team: prepare entity registration data (ABN, business address, key personnel details) ready for AUSTRAC submission.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: AUSTRAC: provide enrolment form template, confirmation of registration acceptance, and any sector-specific guidance for Tranche 2 entities; external legal advisor (if applicable): provide written opinion on Tranche 2 classification status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Written entity classification assessment against Tranche 2 criteria; AUSTRAC enrolment confirmation email or registry extract showing entity registration; Compliance Officer appointment letter with signed acceptance; completed enrolment form with all supporting entity documentation; calendar reminder or project plan documenting the 2026 enrolment deadline and responsible parties.

regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020)

regulations.name: Cyber Security Act 2024
regulations.authority: Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) / Department of Home Affairs
regulations.summary: Australia's first dedicated cybersecurity act, part of the 2023-2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy. Establishes mandatory ransomware payment reporting, minimum security standards for smart connected devices (IoT), and a new Cyber Incident Review Board.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory reporting of ransomware payments to ASD within 72 hours (immunity from regulatory use of the report)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a ransomware detection and response playbook that triggers immediate escalation to designated incident commander upon payment confirmation. 2. Create a reporting template and submit notification to ASD Cyber Security Incident Response Team within 72 hours of confirmed payment, including payment amount, threat actor identifier, and incident timeline. 3. Retain the ASD acknowledgment letter confirming immunity protection for the report.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve ransomware incident disclosure form and immunity letter template. IT Security team: provide incident timeline, payment proof, forensic findings, and threat actor attribution within 24 hours of payment confirmation. Finance team: document payment method, amount, and authorization records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response provider (if engaged): deliver forensic report confirming payment details and threat actor identification within 48 hours to support ASD submission.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ASD acknowledgment letter of immunity, signed incident report submitted to ASD, payment confirmation records, forensic report with timestamp, incident response playbook version control log showing 72-hour escalation procedure.

key_requirements.text: Minimum security standards for smart connected devices (IoT) sold in Australia - aligned with ETSI EN 303 645
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a technical audit of all smart connected devices (IoT) against ETSI EN 303 645 standard requirements (e.g. password strength, secure update mechanism, vulnerability disclosure). 2. Obtain third-party certification or internal validation report confirming alignment with each mandatory security requirement. 3. Document exceptions and remediation plans for any non-compliant devices and implement corrective actions before market release.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Product Engineering team: complete ETSI EN 303 645 gap assessment against each device model and provide remediation roadmap. Quality Assurance team: execute security test cases covering all EN 303 645 control objectives and sign off results. Legal/Compliance team: maintain register of certified devices and support periods.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hardware/component suppliers: provide Security Datasheet or Certificate of Compliance confirming ETSI EN 303 645 alignment for embedded chipsets and firmware. Third-party testing lab: deliver formal certification report or detailed compliance assessment against EN 303 645 clause-by-clause.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ETSI EN 303 645 compliance matrix (control-by-control assessment), third-party certification or accredited lab test report, device security documentation, firmware release notes confirming secure update capability, vulnerability disclosure policy document.

key_requirements.text: Suppliers of smart devices must publish statements of compliance and support periods
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Publish a publicly accessible compliance statement on corporate website and product pages confirming adherence to ETSI EN 303 645 for each smart device model. 2. Clearly state the security support period (including security patch and firmware update availability end-date) for each product version. 3. Update statements annually and maintain archive of historical statements to demonstrate continuous compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Product Management team: define and document security support lifecycle (minimum duration and end-of-life policy) for each device SKU. Marketing/Communications team: draft and publish compliance statements on website with legal review. Compliance team: implement annual statement review and refresh calendar with notification schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party compliance verification provider (if applicable): validate accuracy of published compliance and support period statements before publication.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published compliance statement URL and cached webpage, device support period matrix with versioning dates, annual statement refresh audit log, website maintenance records confirming statement visibility, signed statement attestation by authorized officer.

key_requirements.text: Cyber Incident Review Board reviews significant cyber incidents to identify systemic improvements
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Cyber Incident Review Board (CIRB) with representatives from IT Security, Legal, Operations, and senior management; define charter, meeting frequency, and escalation triggers for 'significant' incidents. 2. For each significant incident, convene the board within 5 working days to review root cause, systemic vulnerabilities, and recommend enterprise-wide improvements. 3. Document findings and track remediation actions in a centralized incident review register with board sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: define 'significant incident' criteria (e.g. data exposure, >4-hour outage, external compromise) and provide incident summaries to CIRB. Legal team: record board meeting minutes and member attendance. Operations team: assign owners to remediation recommendations and provide quarterly completion status updates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response or forensic provider (if engaged): submit detailed incident investigation report to support board review within 10 days of incident confirmation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CIRB charter and governance document, meeting minutes with attendance and signed-off findings for each significant incident reviewed, incident review register with root cause and systemic improvement recommendations, remediation action tracking log with completion evidence.

key_requirements.text: Limited-use obligations: government assistance data can only be used to manage the incident, not for prosecution
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data governance protocol that restricts use of government-provided incident assistance data (logs, threat intelligence, forensic findings) to active incident containment and remediation only. 2. Insert contractual language into incident response agreements and internal SOPs explicitly prohibiting use of government data for criminal prosecution, regulatory penalty, or non-incident purposes. 3. Implement access controls and audit logging for any government-provided data and conduct annual compliance certification.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve Limited-Use Data Agreement and incident data handling SOP with restricted-use clauses. IT Security team: establish separate data repository with access logs for government-provided incident data and ensure purge schedule post-remediation. Compliance team: conduct annual attestation confirming no prohibited use of government data.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response provider: sign Limited-Use Data Addendum acknowledging restrictions on use of government assistance data and confirming data handling practices.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Limited-Use Data Agreement, incident data handling SOP with restricted-use provisions, access control logs for government-provided data repository, annual compliance attestation letter, data retention and purge schedule documentation.

key_requirements.text: Cyber security obligations for critical infrastructure interconnected with SOCI Act requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all critical infrastructure assets in scope under Security of Critical Infrastructure Act (SOCI) and cross-reference cyber security obligations under the Cyber Security Act 2024. 2. Develop an integrated control framework that satisfies both SOCI and CSA 2024 requirements (e.g. asset management, incident reporting, vulnerability management). 3. Document the mapping of controls to both legislative frameworks and conduct annual gap assessments.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Infrastructure/Operations team: provide inventory of critical infrastructure assets and interdependencies with external systems. IT Security team: complete SOCI/CSA 2024 control mapping and identify overlapping obligations. Compliance team: establish dual-framework compliance calendar and audit schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party critical infrastructure providers: furnish asset ownership documentation, security control attestations, and confirmation of any shared SOCI/CSA 2024 obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Critical infrastructure asset register with SOCI/CSA 2024 scope designation, integrated control framework documentation, control-to-requirement traceability matrix for both acts, annual compliance gap assessment report, audit findings for SOCI and CSA 2024 combined.

regulations.penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices

regulations.name: International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: The most important international treaty on maritime safety, prescribing minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of merchant ships. Covers fire safety, life-saving appliances, radio communications, navigational safety, carriage of cargoes and dangerous goods, ISM Code, and ISPS Code.

key_requirements.text: Construction standards: watertight integrity, stability, subdivision, and fire safety systems
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage a Classification Society (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) to survey and certify watertight integrity, stability calculations, and subdivision compliance before vessel operation. 2. Establish a preventive maintenance schedule with documented inspections of watertight doors, bulkheads, and fire safety system components. 3. Maintain design documentation (stability books, general arrangements, fire safety plans) onboard and in company records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Engineering team: provide ship design specifications, stability calculations, and maintenance logs for all watertight and fire safety systems. Fleet Management: confirm Classification Society engagement letters and survey schedules.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Classification Society: issue Passenger Ship Safety Certificate, Load Line Certificate, and International Tonnage Certificate with survey reports confirming watertight integrity and subdivision standards. Fire safety equipment suppliers: provide certification of approved equipment installations and testing records.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Classification Society certificates (valid, unrevoked), approved ship design plans, stability booklet, Construction and Equipment Certificate, maintenance records with dated inspections, fire safety system test reports.

key_requirements.text: Life-saving appliances: sufficient lifeboats, liferafts, EPIRBs, immersion suits, and fire extinguishers
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a capacity calculation based on vessel tonnage and crew to determine exact lifeboat, liferaft, immersion suit, and fire extinguisher quantities; document in the Ship's Inventory Plan. 2. Arrange annual servicing and certification of all life-saving appliances by an approved service station; maintain service tags and certificates onboard. 3. Schedule muster drills (fire, abandon ship) at least monthly and document attendance, deficiencies, and corrective actions in the Safety Log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: maintain Ship's Inventory Plan with current quantities and locations of all life-saving appliances. Safety Officer: provide muster drill records, deficiency reports, and corrective action logs for the past 24 months.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Approved life-saving appliance service station: issue annual certification and service tags for lifeboats, liferafts, EPIRBs, immersion suits, and fire extinguishers; provide spare parts and maintenance schedules. EPIRB supplier: confirm battery expiry dates and registration status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Ship's Inventory Plan, approved service certificates (dated within 12 months), EPIRB registration confirmation, lifeboat/liferaft inspection tags, immersion suit inventory records, fire extinguisher inspection labels, muster drill documentation with attendance sheets.

key_requirements.text: Radio communications: GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) equipment installation
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage a GMDSS-approved service provider to install, test, and commission all mandatory GMDSS equipment (VHF, MF/HF radio, INMARSAT-C EPIRB, etc.) in accordance with IMO Performance Standards. 2. Conduct annual radio surveys and maintenance with a certified technician; retain all test reports and calibration certificates. 3. Ensure all seafarers holding watch on the bridge hold valid GMDSS Radio Operator's Certificates (ROC) or GMDSS Radio Operator's Certificate Restricted (GMDSS-R).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Engineering: provide list of installed GMDSS equipment with model numbers and installation dates. HR/Crew Management: provide copies of valid GMDSS certificates for all bridge officers and radio operators on rotation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: GMDSS equipment service provider: issue Recommendations on Harmonization of SOLAS regulations compliance certificate and annual test/maintenance reports. Radio survey authority: provide radio survey report confirming GMDSS equipment functionality and compliance with IMO Performance Standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: GMDSS Radio Installation Certificate, annual radio survey reports (dated within 12 months), GMDSS equipment test logs, crew GMDSS certificates (ROC/GMDSS-R), maintenance and spare parts inventory for emergency systems.

key_requirements.text: Navigation: mandatory ECDIS, AIS, voyage data recorder (VDR), and ARPA radar on applicable vessels
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Verify installation of ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System), AIS (Automatic Identification System), VDR (Voyage Data Recorder), and ARPA radar on all applicable vessels per ship type and tonnage; document in Equipment Inventory. 2. Establish a schedule for annual certification, software updates, and chart corrections for ECDIS; maintain records of all updates applied. 3. Conduct quarterly bridge team drills to test VDR functionality, ECDIS failover procedures, and AIS transmission; document results.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Navigation Department: provide ECDIS software version list, chart update logs, and failover test records. Fleet Operations: submit AIS and VDR commissioning certificates and annual inspection reports. Bridge Officers: confirm participation in quarterly navigation system drills.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: ECDIS/AIS/VDR equipment manufacturers or service providers: issue Type Approval Certificates and confirm software version compliance with IMO Standards. Chart data provider: supply certified chart updates and issue compliance statements for SOLAS carriage requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Type Approval Certificates for ECDIS, AIS, VDR, and ARPA radar; annual certification reports from Classification Society or approved testing body; ECDIS software version logs and chart update records; VDR functional test reports; drill documentation with crew sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Chapter IX - ISM Code: mandatory Safety Management System for company and ships
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop or update the company's Safety Management System (SMS) documentation including the Policy, Objectives, Procedures, and Implementation Manuals aligned with ISM Code Part A; submit to Flag State Administration for approval. 2. Designate a Designated Person Ashore (DPA) with direct access to company management and assign a Ship Security Officer (SSO) onboard; document their roles, responsibilities, and qualifications. 3. Conduct annual internal audits and management reviews of the SMS; establish a non-conformity log and corrective action tracking system.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Quality/Compliance team: provide draft SMS documentation, DPA designation letter, SSO appointment records, and internal audit schedules. Management: sign off on SMS policy and assign DPA. HR: maintain crew training records for ISM Code awareness and familiarization.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag State Administration: issue ISM Code Document of Compliance (DoC) and International Safety Management (ISM) Certificate for each vessel. Classification Society: provide SMS approval assessment reports and annual verification reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ISM Code Document of Compliance (valid, unrevoked), ISM Certificate for each ship, approved SMS manual (all parts), DPA appointment letter with qualifications, internal audit reports (annual), management review meeting minutes, non-conformity and corrective action logs.

key_requirements.text: Chapter XI-2 - ISPS Code: Ship Security Plan and continuous Synopsis Record
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a Ship Security Plan (SSP) approved by the company and Flag State that addresses threats, vulnerabilities, and protective measures; ensure it is specific to each vessel and its trading patterns. 2. Appoint a Company Security Officer (CSO) and Ship Security Officer (SSO) with appropriate training and qualifications; maintain records of appointment and training completion. 3. Maintain a Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) onboard with entries documenting all security-relevant changes, incidents, and drills; ensure regular updates and crew sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/Compliance team: develop Ship Security Plan, designate CSO and SSO, and maintain crew training records for ISPS Code familiarity. Operations: provide vessel-specific trading route and call port information for SSP risk assessment. Record-keeping: establish CSR management system with version control and audit trail.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag State Administration: approve Ship Security Plan and issue International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC). Port State authorities (or contracted verification body): conduct International Ship and Port Facility Security Code (ISPS Code) verifications if required for high-risk ports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Ship Security Plan (Flag State stamp), International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC), valid and current CSR with dated entries, CSO/SSO appointment letters and training certificates, drills documentation (fire, security, emergency response), ISPS Code compliance audit reports from Flag State or Classification Society.

key_requirements.text: Five-year SOLAS certificates renewed via annual, intermediate, and renewal surveys
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a survey scheduling calendar tracking annual, intermediate, and renewal survey dates for each vessel or asset subject to SOLAS. 2. Assign responsibility to a designated compliance officer to coordinate with Classification Society surveyors and ensure surveys are completed within prescribed intervals. 3. Maintain a centralized register documenting survey completion dates, issued certificates, and any deficiencies identified during inspections.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain five-year survey schedule with vessel identifiers and survey due dates. IT team: provide access to document management system for storing and retrieving survey records. Finance team: budget and approve Classification Society survey fees annually.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Classification Society: provide written confirmation of annual survey dates, submit signed Interim Safety Certificates upon completion of intermediate surveys, and issue renewed SOLAS Safety Certificates within 30 days of renewal survey completion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed SOLAS Safety Certificates (initial and renewal), Interim Safety Certificates for each intermediate survey, Classification Society inspection reports detailing survey scope and findings, survey scheduling calendar with timestamps, and audit trail of certificate issuance dates.

key_requirements.text: Port State Control: non-compliant ships may be detained until deficiencies are corrected
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct internal gap assessments against SOLAS requirements to identify and document deficiencies before port inspections occur. 2. Establish a corrective action tracking system that prioritizes critical safety deficiencies and records remediation timelines and evidence. 3. Brief all vessel operators and port operations staff on Port State Control detention risks and ensure procedures exist to halt operations if a vessel is detained until deficiencies are corrected.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: maintain deficiency register with classification (critical/major/minor) and planned correction dates. Operations team: develop incident response protocol for vessel detention scenarios. Legal team: review insurance policies to confirm coverage for detention-related costs and liabilities.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Port State Control authorities: provide written inspection reports detailing any deficiencies found; Classification Society: confirm completion of remedial work and issue clearance documentation before vessel departure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Port State Control inspection reports with deficiency lists and detention notices (if issued), photographic or video evidence of deficiency correction, signed Classification Society clearance certificates, corrective action completion logs with dates and responsible party signatures, and insurance claim files documenting detention costs if applicable.

regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law

regulations.name: International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL)
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: The primary international convention preventing pollution from ships, covering six annexes (oil, noxious liquids, harmful packaged substances, sewage, garbage, air pollution). Key requirements include the 2020 global 0.5% sulfur cap on marine fuels and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) ratings from 2023.

key_requirements.text: Annex I: maintain Oil Record Book; prohibit oil discharges except compliant bilge water via OCM
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish Oil Record Book procedures requiring daily entries of all oil transfers, machinery operations, and bilge water handling. 2. Install and maintain Oil Content Monitor (OCM) on all vessels with calibration certificates renewed annually. 3. Audit Oil Record Book entries quarterly and retain records for three years.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: provide Oil Record Book templates and monthly completion audits. IT team: implement electronic Oil Record Book system with immutable audit trails. Legal team: document discharge authorization protocols for compliant bilge water only.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: OCM equipment supplier: provide Type Approval Certificate and annual calibration service agreements. Vessel maintenance contractor: supply OCM commissioning reports and proof of Type Approval installation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Oil Record Book entries (digital or paper), OCM Type Approval Certificates, calibration reports dated within last 12 months, quarterly audit reports, three-year record retention inventory.

key_requirements.text: Annex II: chemical tankers must comply with IBC Code; pre-washing procedures before port discharge
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Verify all chemical tankers hold current International Code for the Construction and Equipment of Ships Carrying Dangerous Chemicals in Bulk (IBC Code) compliance documentation. 2. Document pre-washing procedures in the Ship's Procedures Manual with intervals and solvents specified by cargo compatibility. 3. Obtain and verify pre-washing certificates from port facilities before discharge authorization.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: create pre-washing procedure checklist for each cargo type. Compliance team: maintain register of IBC Code certificates for each tanker with renewal dates. Legal team: draft discharge authorization policy tied to pre-washing certificate receipt.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Port authority/discharge facility: provide pre-washing service agreements and certification templates. IBC Code auditor: supply initial and triennial compliance audit reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current IBC Code certificates for each tanker, signed Ship's Procedures Manual with pre-washing procedures, pre-washing certificates from port facilities, discharge authorization records, triennial audit reports.

key_requirements.text: Annex V: Garbage Management Plan; prohibited from discharging plastics and food waste in special areas
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and implement a Garbage Management Plan detailing segregation, storage, and disposal procedures for all waste categories (food waste, plastics, paper, metals). 2. Issue crew training certificates annually covering prohibition on plastic and food waste discharge in Special Areas (Mediterranean, Black Sea, Baltic, North Sea, Antarctic). 3. Maintain Garbage Record Book with daily entries and quarterly audits of Special Area compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: create Garbage Management Plan template and waste segregation diagrams for each vessel. Training team: develop and track mandatory crew certification on waste handling and Special Area restrictions. Compliance team: maintain Garbage Record Book repository with exception reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Port waste reception facility: provide receiving certificates and manifest documentation for all offloaded waste. Crew training provider: issue certificates of completion for annual MARPOL Annex V training.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Garbage Management Plan for each vessel, crew training certificates (names, dates, signatures), Garbage Record Book entries for 12 months, waste reception facility manifests, quarterly compliance audit reports highlighting zero plastic/food waste discharges in Special Areas.

key_requirements.text: Annex VI: global sulfur cap 0.5% (0.1% in Emission Control Areas - North Sea, Baltic, North America, US Caribbean)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Source compliant fuel with sulfur content ≤0.5% globally and ≤0.1% in designated ECAs (North Sea, Baltic, North America, US Caribbean); maintain fuel supplier declarations and bunker delivery notes. 2. Implement fuel sampling and testing regime with quarterly third-party lab analysis confirming sulfur limits. 3. Maintain fuel compliance register documenting all bunkering operations with geographic location and applicable limit.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: establish fuel supplier contracts requiring sulfur content declarations and ISO 8217 compliance. Operations team: create fuel sampling and testing schedule with lab submission protocols. Compliance team: maintain fuel register by vessel and port with sulfur limits applied per ECA status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Fuel supplier: provide ISO 8217 certificates and sulfur content declarations with every delivery. Testing laboratory: supply quarterly fuel analysis reports (sulfur content, density, viscosity) with accreditation stamp.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Fuel supplier ISO 8217 certificates, bunker delivery notes with sulfur declarations, quarterly lab test reports confirming ≤0.5% global and ≤0.1% ECA compliance, fuel register entries with ECA boundaries and applicable limits, exception reports for any out-of-spec deliveries.

key_requirements.text: Annex VI: NOx Tier III standards for new ships in ECAs
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all vessels constructed after 1 January 2016 operating in ECAs and verify NOx Tier III engine certification on the International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP Certificate). 2. If Tier III engines not installed, implement Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) systems with Type Approval and periodic effectiveness testing. 3. Schedule annual IAPP Certificate renewals and maintain NOx compliance documentation for port state control inspection.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet team: audit all vessels for build date and ECA routes; create NOx Tier III compliance matrix. Operations team: schedule SCR system maintenance and performance testing protocols if Tier III engines absent. Compliance team: calendar IAPP Certificate renewal dates and maintain certified compliance file per vessel.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Engine manufacturer or retrofit contractor: provide NOx Tier III engine Type Approval Certificate or SCR system Type Approval Certificate with installation completion reports. IAPP Certificate issuing authority: supply current certificates with NOx Tier III or approved alternative notation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Vessel build specifications confirming post-2016 construction date, IAPP Certificate with Tier III notation or SCR Type Approval Certificate, SCR system performance test reports (annual), maintenance logbook entries for SCR systems, port state control inspection pass records.

key_requirements.text: Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) rating A-E from 2023
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and implement Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) for each vessel detailing energy baseline, efficiency measures, and data collection methodology; obtain company-level approval. 2. Collect and analyze operational data (fuel consumption, distance, time at sea) monthly to calculate Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) using IMO formula; assign annual A-E rating from 2023 onwards. 3. Submit CII rating to flag state administration annually and maintain records for three years demonstrating compliance with applicable reference line.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: develop SEEMP template and establish monthly data collection procedures (fuel logs, voyage hours, cargo tonnage-miles). IT team: implement data aggregation system for CII calculation; ensure immutable audit trail of submissions. Compliance team: maintain CII rating register by vessel and reference line; track corrective action plans for D/E-rated ships.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag state administration: confirm SEEMP approval letter and provide CII rating submission portal access. Data verifier (Class society or independent): provide annual CII calculation verification reports and rating assessment.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved SEEMP document (version-controlled), monthly operational data logs (fuel consumption, distance, ballast condition, cargo weight), annual CII calculation worksheets with IMO formula applied, CII A-E rating certificate from flag state, three-year data retention register, corrective action plans for D/E ratings.

key_requirements.text: EEDI/EEXI requirements for new and existing ships to improve energy efficiency
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct baseline energy efficiency audit on all vessels in fleet, calculating current EEDI (new ships) or EEXI (existing ships) values against IMO thresholds. 2. Establish Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) for each vessel documenting operational measures, monitoring protocols, and corrective actions. 3. Appoint qualified personnel to oversee EEDI/EEXI compliance and maintain mandatory Ship Energy Efficiency Notation (SEEN) issued by Classification Society.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Management team: compile vessel technical specifications, engine power ratings, and deadweight tonnage for each ship. Compliance Officer: draft and maintain SEEMP documentation templates and revision records. Finance: budget for required vessel retrofit assessments or propulsion efficiency upgrades if baseline EEDI/EEXI exceeds limits.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Classification Society: conduct EEDI/EEXI calculations, issue Ship Energy Efficiency Notation (SEEN) certificates, and provide annual verification reports. Engine/Equipment Supplier: provide certified technical data sheets confirming engine efficiency ratings and compliance with IMO Tier standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Valid Ship Energy Efficiency Notation (SEEN) certificate from recognized Classification Society. Signed SEEMP for each vessel with annual updates and monitoring records. EEDI/EEXI calculation reports with baseline vs. required values. Engine technical data sheets and Tier compliance certificates. Port State Control inspection records confirming EEDI/EEXI compliance status.

key_requirements.text: IMO 2030/2050 GHG Strategy: 50% total GHG reduction by 2050 vs 2008
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish baseline GHG emissions inventory for the fleet using 2008 as reference year, measuring CO₂ equivalent from fuel consumption across all vessels. 2. Develop a corporate GHG reduction roadmap with interim targets for 2030 and 2050, incorporating fuel-switching strategies (LNG, biofuels, zero-carbon fuels), operational efficiency improvements, and technological upgrades. 3. Implement annual GHG reporting and verification process aligned with IMO Data Collection System (DCS) and EU MRV/FuelEU requirements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Operations: provide 2008 baseline fuel consumption records and current annual bunker reports for all vessels (minimum 5-year historical data). Sustainability/Environmental team: develop GHG reduction strategy document with 2030/2050 milestones and responsible parties. Finance: allocate capital expenditure budget for low-carbon fuel infrastructure and vessel retrofitting. IT: establish GHG emissions tracking system with monthly data ingestion from vessel monitoring systems.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Fuel Suppliers: certify carbon intensity (CI) values for each fuel grade supplied and provide sustainability declarations for alternative/zero-carbon fuels. Classification Society or IMO DCS Approved Organization: conduct independent verification of GHG emissions data and provide annual attestation report. Technology Providers: deliver emissions monitoring software or hardware integrations for real-time fuel consumption and GHG calculation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: IMO Data Collection System (DCS) annual submission confirming reported GHG emissions and fuel consumption. Corporate GHG reduction roadmap signed by senior management with 2030 and 2050 targets. Third-party verified baseline emissions report (2008 reference year). Annual GHG inventory with reconciliation to DCS or EU MRV reporting. Bunker delivery notes (BDN) and fuel specifications confirming carbon intensity. Signed fuel supply contracts specifying sustainability requirements and low-carbon fuel sourcing commitments.

regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day)

regulations.name: International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO) - implemented via SOLAS Chapter XI-2
regulations.summary: International code for the security of ships and port facilities adopted after 9/11, applying to international cargo ships of 500 GT or more, all passenger ships, and port facilities serving international ships. IMO MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 (2017) integrates cybersecurity into ISPS requirements.

key_requirements.text: Ship Security Assessment (SSA) identifying vulnerabilities and security risks
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage a qualified maritime security assessor to conduct a documented vulnerability and risk assessment covering vessel design, cargo handling, crew access, and IT systems. 2. Document all identified threats, vulnerabilities, and residual risks in a formal Ship Security Assessment report. 3. Review and update the assessment every 2 years or following significant operational changes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/Operations team: provide vessel layout diagrams, access control procedures, crew roster policies, and current incident logs. Legal team: confirm flag state assessment requirements and regulatory deadlines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Maritime Security Consultants: deliver a written Ship Security Assessment report detailing vulnerabilities by category (physical, procedural, cyber) and risk ratings. Training provider: confirm assessor qualifications and IMO compliance certifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Ship Security Assessment report with assessor credentials, dated vulnerability matrix, management sign-off, and evidence of assessment update cycle compliance.

key_requirements.text: Implement a Ship Security Plan (SSP) addressing three security levels
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a formal Ship Security Plan document defining procedures for all three ISPS security levels (Normal, Enhanced, Restricted Area). 2. Embed specific control measures, escalation procedures, crew briefing protocols, and equipment requirements for each level. 3. Obtain flag state approval and distribute the signed plan to all crew and relevant shore-based personnel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/Operations team: draft SSP procedures and internal protocols for each security level, including access control, cargo inspection, and visitor management. HR team: confirm crew training and briefing schedules. IT team: document system lockdown procedures for Enhanced and Restricted levels.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag state maritime authority: provide written approval or acknowledgement of the Ship Security Plan. Security equipment vendors: confirm system compatibility with SSP procedures (e.g., SSAS integration, biometric access control).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Flag state-approved Ship Security Plan document (dated and signed), internal procedures matrix for each security level, crew acknowledgement records, and records of plan distribution to shore staff.

key_requirements.text: Designate a Ship Security Officer (SSO) and a Company Security Officer (CSO)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally designate a Ship Security Officer (SSO) with documented training, competency certification, and clear reporting lines to Company Security Officer (CSO) and Master. 2. Designate a Company Security Officer (CSO) with equivalent training, audit authority, and direct access to senior management. 3. Publish organizational charts and role descriptions confirming SSO and CSO responsibilities, including independence and escalation authority.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue written appointment letters for SSO and CSO with defined responsibilities, authority, and reporting lines. Training/Compliance team: maintain records of IMO ISPS training certificates and competency assessments. Executive team: confirm CSO reporting relationship (typically to Managing Director or Chief Risk Officer).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training providers: issue formal ISPS Code Part A certification documents for both SSO and CSO. Third-party auditors: confirm SSO/CSO independence and effectiveness during security assessments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed SSO and CSO appointment letters, IMO training certificates, organizational charts showing reporting lines, role descriptions, and auditor confirmations of independence and authority.

key_requirements.text: Install a Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) transmitting covert distress alert
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Procure and install a certified Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) capable of transmitting a covert distress alert to flag state maritime authorities and Recognized Organization surveyors. 2. Integrate SSAS with vessel communication systems and establish redundant alert pathways. 3. Conduct monthly testing of the system and maintain test logs; train SSO and designated crew on activation procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Operations team: install SSAS hardware, configure alert transmission protocols, and document system specifications. Security team: establish monthly test schedule and maintain test logs with dates, times, and activation responses. Training team: provide documented SSAS activation training to SSO and designated officers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: SSAS equipment manufacturer: provide Type Approval Certificate, installation certification, and written technical support for system maintenance. Communication service provider: confirm alert transmission routing to flag state, RO, and Rescue Coordination Centre.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: SSAS Type Approval Certificate, installation certification, monthly test logs (minimum 12 months), crew training records, and vendor-signed System Maintenance Agreement.

key_requirements.text: Maintain an International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC) - issued by flag state
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Submit a formal security compliance dossier (including SSA, SSP, SSO/CSO credentials, and SSAS certification) to the flag state maritime authority. 2. Schedule and complete a flag state or Recognized Organization security audit. 3. Obtain and maintain the signed International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC), valid for 5 years, and display it on board.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: compile complete security file (SSA, SSP, SSO credentials, maintenance records) and submit to flag state authority. Document team: maintain copy of ISSC on bridge and in administrative records. Operations team: ensure ISSC remains current and schedule renewal audit 6 months before expiry.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag state maritime authority: issue the signed International Ship Security Certificate following successful audit. Recognized Organization surveyor (if applicable): conduct security audit and recommend approval to flag state.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed and stamped International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC), flag state audit report, certificate expiry date log, and renewal application submission proof.

key_requirements.text: Port facilities must have a Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP) and Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a comprehensive Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP) documenting security measures for all three ISPS levels, covering access control, cargo screening, vessel interface, and emergency procedures. 2. Formally designate a Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) with documented training, clear authority, and direct reporting to facility management. 3. Obtain port authority and flag state approval of the PFSP and PFSO credentials.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Port Management team: draft PFSP with site-specific procedures, entry/exit protocols, and cargo handling security requirements. HR team: issue PFSO appointment letter with defined authority and independence. Facilities team: implement access control systems, CCTV, and perimeter security aligned to PFSP. Training team: maintain ISPS training records for PFSO and security personnel.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Port authority: review and approve Port Facility Security Plan. Flag state maritime authority: acknowledge PFSP approval and PFSO designation. Access control/surveillance vendors: provide equipment compatibility confirmation with PFSP requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Port authority-approved Port Facility Security Plan (dated and signed), PFSO appointment letter and ISPS training certificate, organizational chart confirming reporting lines, access control logs, and port authority audit records.

key_requirements.text: Port and ship security drills and exercises annually
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Schedule and conduct a minimum of one full-scale port security drill and one ship security drill annually, with documented participation from all relevant departments and external stakeholders.
2. Document all drill activities, including date, time, participants, scenarios tested, and identified gaps in a Drill Register.
3. Review drill findings and implement corrective actions within 30 days; retain records for at least five years.
internal_actions: Security team: develop and maintain the annual drill schedule with assigned drill coordinators and defined scenarios.
Operations team: provide participant attendance logs and real-time observations during each drill.
Compliance team: compile post-drill reports documenting findings, non-conformances, and corrective action closure evidence.
vendor_actions: Port authority: confirm scheduled participation dates and provide port-side drill coordination requirements.
Ship operators or terminal service providers: sign commitment letter confirming annual drill participation and timelines.
compliance_evidence: Annual Drill Register showing dates, participants, scenarios, and outcomes for preceding 5 years.
Signed drill participation confirmations from all stakeholders.
Post-drill reports with identified gaps and documented corrective actions with closure dates.
Photographic or video evidence of drill execution (optional but strengthens audit trail).

key_requirements.text: Cyber risk incorporated into Ship Security Plans per IMO 2017 guidance
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Incorporate cyber risk assessment into the Ship Security Plan by identifying vessel IT systems, networks, and data assets subject to security threats.
2. Define cyber-specific security measures aligned with IMO 2017 Cyber Risk Management guidance, including access controls, system monitoring, and incident response protocols for cyber events.
3. Assign cyber security responsibility to the Ship Security Officer or a designated cyber officer and establish annual review cycles to update the plan against emerging threats.
internal_actions: IT team: provide inventory of all vessel systems (ECDIS, engine control systems, bridge networks, communication systems) and current vulnerability assessments.
Compliance/Security team: draft Cyber Risk Annex to Ship Security Plan incorporating IMO 2017 guidance and company cyber policies.
Legal team: confirm contractual cyber liability and incident notification requirements with insurers and flag state administrators.
vendor_actions: Ship operators or vessel managers: submit updated Ship Security Plan with cyber risk section and confirm assignment of cyber security responsibility.
Cyber security service provider: deliver annual cyber threat landscape assessment specific to maritime vessel types in the fleet.
Vessel systems suppliers (OEMs): provide security advisories, patch release schedules, and end-of-life dates for critical onboard systems.
compliance_evidence: Ship Security Plan document with dedicated Cyber Risk Management section referencing IMO 2017 guidance (document control date and version).
Cyber risk register identifying vessel IT assets, threat scenarios, and mitigation controls.
Written assignment of cyber security responsibility with named officer and role description.
Annual cyber risk review meeting minutes with attendees, findings, and plan update dates.
Evidence of cyber incident response drills or tabletop exercises conducted within the past 12 months.

regulations.penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law

regulations.name: Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006) — ILO Convention C186
regulations.authority: International Labour Organization (ILO)
regulations.summary: The Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006) is an International Labour Organization (ILO) Convention (C186), often called the 'Seafarers' Bill of Rights.' Administered by the ILO — not the IMO — it consolidates 68 previous ILO maritime standards into a single instrument covering seafarers' employment conditions, working hours, accommodation, health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security. Port state control inspections are coordinated with IMO conventions. Entered into force 20 August 2013; ratified by states representing over 96% of gross tonnage.

key_requirements.text: Seafarer Employment Agreement (SEA) must be provided to every seafarer before joining the ship
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR team: develop a standardised Seafarer Employment Agreement (SEA) template compliant with MLC 2006 Regulation 2.1, including wages, hours of work, leave entitlements, and dispute resolution procedures. 2. Establish a document management process requiring SEA signature by seafarer minimum 7 days before embarkation, with retention of signed copies on file. 3. Conduct quarterly audit of all active seafarers to confirm SEA presence in personnel records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed SEA register (spreadsheet or database) showing seafarer name, vessel, SEA execution date, and filing location; Legal team: confirm template MLC 2006 compliance review documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Crewing agency: deliver signed copy of each SEA within 3 business days of seafarer hire; Manning agency: confirm in writing that all supplied seafarers hold executed SEAs before vessel departure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Executed SEA documents (original or certified copy) for each active seafarer; HR audit report listing all seafarers and corresponding SEA dates; signed undertaking from crewing/manning vendors confirming SEA compliance protocol.

key_requirements.text: Minimum age: 16 years (18 for hazardous work and night work)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR team: implement mandatory age verification in recruitment: request certified copy of birth certificate or passport for all candidates, reject any applicant under 16 years. 2. For seafarers assigned to hazardous work (deck machinery, cargo handling) or night shifts, enforce minimum 18-year age gate in assignment system. 3. Conduct semi-annual age verification audit across all active crew to confirm ongoing compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: maintain age verification register (name, DOB, hire date, age at hire, work category assigned) and provide evidence of rejections for under-age applicants; IT team: configure crew management system to flag or block assignment of under-18 seafarers to hazardous/night duties.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Recruitment agency: confirm in writing that all candidates supplied meet minimum age requirement and provide age verification copies; Crewing partner: certify no under-16 or inappropriate-age seafarers in supplied crew lists.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Age verification register with DOB and hire date for all seafarers; sample of certified birth certificates or passport copies; crew assignment logs showing age-appropriate task allocation; HR semi-annual audit confirmation; vendor written certification of age compliance.

key_requirements.text: Minimum wages per ILO/MLC guidelines (USD 673/month basic rate as of 2024)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Finance/Payroll team: establish monthly minimum wage floor at USD 673 (or current ILO MLC 2024 rate) in payroll system; configure automated monthly compliance check to flag any seafarer below threshold. 2. Conduct wage audit upon SEA execution and at each contract renewal to confirm rates meet or exceed MLC minimums, excluding overtime. 3. Document all wage adjustments annually to reflect ILO index changes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide monthly payroll reports (last 12 months) showing each seafarer's basic wage, deductions, and net pay; Payroll system: furnish automated wage compliance audit results and any flagged below-threshold payments; Accounting: supply invoice/remittance records evidencing wage payment.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Payroll service provider: confirm in writing that payroll system enforces USD 673 minimum wage floor and provide configuration audit; Bank/remittance partner: deliver monthly payment confirmation statements showing wage transfers to seafarers.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Payroll master data export showing basic wage rates for all active seafarers; monthly payroll audit reports (automated compliance check results); wage payment bank statements or remittance confirmations; ILO MLC 2024 wage index acknowledgement; vendor payroll configuration audit report.

key_requirements.text: Maximum work hours: 14 hours in any 24-hour period and 72 hours in any 7-day period
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Fleet Operations team: deploy automated time-tracking system (electronic logbook or crew management system) recording daily work hours per seafarer; configure alert for any shift exceeding 14 hours in 24-hour window or cumulative >72 hours in 7-day period. 2. Implement weekly work-hour reconciliation process: review flagged records and approve exceptions only with documented operational justification (emergency, bad weather). 3. Conduct monthly compliance review and report violations to Master and HR.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: export time-tracking logs monthly showing hours worked per seafarer per 24-hour and 7-day rolling windows; IT team: configure system alerts for >14h/24h and >72h/7d violations and provide alert trigger logs; Management: document operational justifications for any approved exceptions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Time-tracking software provider: confirm system enforces 14-hour/24-hour and 72-hour/7-day work-hour limits and provide technical specifications; Crew management system vendor: deliver monthly compliance report functionality and certify alert accuracy.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Monthly work-hour audit reports with flagged violations and corrective actions; time-tracking system configuration documentation showing alert thresholds; operational justification records for approved exceptions; vendor system specification confirming enforcement capability; signed monthly compliance certificate from Fleet Operations.

key_requirements.text: Minimum rest: 10 hours in any 24-hour period and 77 hours in any 7-day period
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Fleet Operations team: configure crew management system to calculate and track rest hours per seafarer in 24-hour (minimum 10 hours) and 7-day (minimum 77 hours) rolling windows; set automated alerts for shortfalls. 2. Review and approve rest schedules weekly; document any variance (e.g., operational emergency requiring reduced rest) with justification and corrective compensation plan. 3. Conduct monthly rest-hour compliance audit and report to management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: provide monthly rest-hour compliance reports showing each seafarer's rolling 24-hour and 7-day rest totals, flagged shortfalls, and documented exceptions; Crew scheduling: export master schedule showing rest period allocations; Management: document justifications and recovery rest compensation for any approved rest deviations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Crew management system vendor: confirm rest-hour calculation logic and alert thresholds in writing, provide monthly compliance report export; Staffing partner: certify no crew supplied for assignments preventing 10-hour/24-hour or 77-hour/7-day rest achievement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Monthly rest-hour audit reports with 24-hour and 7-day rolling calculations per seafarer; crew schedule extracts showing allocated rest periods; records of operational justifications for rest shortfalls; compensation/recovery rest documentation; vendor system specification and monthly compliance report.

key_requirements.text: Annual paid leave of at least 2.5 calendar days per month of service
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR/Payroll team: establish annual paid leave entitlement policy calculating 2.5 calendar days per month of service (30 days/year for full-year service); input into payroll system to auto-calculate accrual and track usage. 2. At contract end or annually, issue leave statement to seafarer showing accrual, used days, and any balance owed as monetary compensation. 3. Maintain leave register tracking approved leave dates and ensure leave is granted within 12 months of accrual (or paid out if contract ends).
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide annual leave policy document confirming 2.5 days/month calculation and payout procedure; Payroll team: export leave accrual register for all active seafarers (accrual date, balance, dates used, outstanding); Finance: document leave payout transactions or leave booking confirmations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Payroll service provider: confirm leave accrual calculation (2.5 days/month) embedded in system and provide configuration audit; HR management system vendor: deliver leave balance reports by seafarer showing accrual vs. usage.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Leave policy document with 2.5-day/month entitlement and payout terms; payroll leave accrual register (12-month history) for all seafarers; sample leave statements issued to seafarers; leave payout transaction records (bank transfers or cash receipts); vendor payroll system configuration audit confirming calculation rule.
