key_requirements.text: Medical care on board and ashore; medical examination certificates
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a medical examination protocol aligned with ILO Convention C186 Part 1, Section A, requiring seafarers to hold valid medical certificates issued by approved medical practitioners before joining the vessel. 2. Designate a responsible officer to maintain a register of all crew medical certificates, conduct periodic reviews, and ensure ashore medical care arrangements are documented with contracted healthcare providers. 3. Implement a pre-joining checklist that verifies each seafarer's medical certificate validity and maintains records of any onboard medical treatments provided.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Crew Management: provide crew roster with medical certificate expiry dates, names of approved medical examiners used, and records of any medical incidents treated onboard. Legal: confirm medical care ashore contracts with port state healthcare facilities and insurance coverage. Finance: provide invoices and payment records for medical examinations and ashore medical services.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Approved Medical Examiners: submit copies of issued medical certificates for each crew member examined, including examination date, validity period, and any limitations noted. Ashore Healthcare Providers: provide service agreements detailing emergency and routine medical care availability at key ports, response times, and billing procedures. Occupational Health Clinic: confirm accreditation status and compliance with ILO medical examination standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Valid medical examination certificates for 100% of current crew; Medical Register (log of all certificates held, issue/expiry dates, examiner details); Contracts with ashore medical providers showing service scope and emergency availability; Insurance certificates confirming medical liability coverage; Records of any onboard medical treatments with crew sign-off; Approved Medical Examiner accreditation certificates.

key_requirements.text: Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC certificate) and Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC) for ships 500 GT+
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a comprehensive maritime labour compliance audit of each vessel 500 GT and above against all MLC 2006 requirements (accommodation, working hours, crew agreements, medical care, etc.), documented in a detailed DMLC Part I checklist. 2. Obtain the Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC) from the flag state authority by submitting the completed DMLC Part I and passing an official inspection; maintain the original certificate onboard and a certified copy in the compliance file. 3. Complete and maintain the Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC) Part II annually, signed by the company and vessel master, detailing any changes to labour conditions and corrective actions taken.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance Officer: coordinate MLC audit against all 5 Title requirements and produce completed DMLC Part I template with supporting evidence. Fleet Management: maintain a register of all vessels 500 GT+, their flag states, MLC certificate expiry dates, and next inspection due dates. Legal: prepare crew agreements template compliant with MLC Title 2 and ensure all signed agreements are retained. Document Control: establish a central repository for all MLC certificates, DMLC documents, audit reports, and inspection correspondence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag State Authority: provide the Maritime Labour Certificate upon successful inspection, including issuance date and expiry (5-year validity). Classification Society (if authorized by flag state): submit pre-inspection checklist completion and arrange official MLC survey; provide inspection report and deficiency log with corrective action deadlines. Ship Management Company (if outsourced): submit annual DMLC Part II declarations confirming ongoing compliance and any labour condition changes during the year.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Original Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC) onboard and filed centrally with expiry date clearly recorded; Completed DMLC Part I (initial audit report) with date and authorization signature; Annual DMLC Part II declarations signed by company and master; Flag state inspection report showing 'approved' status; Classification Society inspection certificates (if applicable); Register of all vessels 500 GT+ with MLC compliance status; Copies of all crew agreements signed under MLC Title 2 requirements; Evidence of corrective actions addressing any deficiencies raised in inspections.

regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation

regulations.name: International Safety Management (ISM) Code
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO) - mandatory via SOLAS Chapter IX
regulations.summary: An international standard for the safe management and operation of ships and for pollution prevention. Requires shipping companies to establish a Safety Management System (SMS), appoint a Designated Person Ashore (DPA), and demonstrate compliance through Document of Compliance (DOC) and Safety Management Certificate (SMC).

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain a Safety Management System (SMS) covering safety policy, procedures, and emergency preparedness
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a comprehensive Safety Management System document covering safety policy, objectives, and procedures applicable to all vessel operations. 2. Establish an SMS manual detailing roles, responsibilities, and implementation procedures for all company personnel and crew. 3. Conduct annual SMS effectiveness reviews and document corrective actions taken.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed SMS policy document and evidence of board approval. HR team: provide training records showing all personnel have completed SMS induction. Safety team: provide SMS manual, effectiveness review reports, and corrective action logs for the past 24 months.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Class society or certifying body: provide ISM Certificate of Compliance and most recent audit report. Maritime consultants (if used): provide SMS documentation audit results and gap analysis.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ISM Certificate of Compliance, signed SMS manual with version control, board approval minutes, personnel training records, SMS effectiveness review reports, corrective action closure documentation, and annual audit reports from the class society.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Designated Person Ashore (DPA) ensuring safe ship operations and providing a link between the company and those on board
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint a senior-level individual as Designated Person Ashore (DPA) with clear written appointment letter detailing authority and responsibilities. 2. Document the DPA's access channels, escalation procedures, and communication protocols with vessel masters and crew. 3. Ensure DPA appointment details are notified to the flag state and included in the ISM Certificate documentation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed DPA appointment letter with job description, authority limits, and emergency contact information. Legal team: provide documentation of DPA notification to flag state authority. Finance team: confirm DPA compensation and budget allocation for DPA function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag state maritime authority: confirm receipt of DPA appointment notification. Class society: confirm DPA details are recorded in ISM audit file.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPA appointment letter, flag state notification acknowledgement, DPA job description, emergency contact procedures, communication logs between DPA and vessels over audit period, and evidence of DPA involvement in incident investigations.

key_requirements.text: Define and document responsibilities and authorities under the SMS
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create an organisational chart with defined SMS responsibilities and decision authorities for all levels (shore management, DPA, masters, crew). 2. Document specific responsibilities for each role including incident reporting, maintenance oversight, emergency response, and safety communication. 3. Publish the responsibility matrix in the SMS manual and conduct induction training for all personnel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide current organisational chart and role descriptions for all SMS-related positions. Legal team: provide documented responsibility matrix cross-referenced to SMS procedures. Management team: provide evidence of role-specific training and competency assessments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Class society: confirm that responsibility matrix aligns with ISM Code requirements during certification audit.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Organisational chart with SMS roles highlighted, responsibility matrix document (showing who, what, when, where for each SMS function), role-specific job descriptions, training attendance records, and class society audit findings on adequacy of defined responsibilities.

key_requirements.text: Procedures for hazard identification, risk assessment, and accident investigation
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and document standardised procedures for hazard identification and risk assessment applicable to all vessel types and operations (e.g. deck operations, engine room work, cargo handling). 2. Establish a formal accident and near-miss investigation procedure with a template requiring root cause analysis, contributing factors, and corrective actions. 3. Maintain a hazard register and accident investigation log with evidence of implementation and effectiveness monitoring.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Safety team: provide hazard identification procedure document, risk assessment templates, and current hazard register. Operations team: provide accident investigation procedure and sample investigation reports from past 24 months. IT team: provide access to incident tracking system or log database.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Risk assessment consultants (if external): provide methodology documents and validated templates. Class society: confirm hazard identification and investigation procedures meet ISM requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Hazard identification procedure document, risk assessment methodology with scoring criteria, completed hazard register with dates and responsible parties, accident investigation procedure, at least three sample investigation reports with root cause analysis and corrective actions, and evidence that recommendations have been implemented and verified.

key_requirements.text: Planned Maintenance System (PMS) for hull, machinery, and safety equipment
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a Planned Maintenance System (PMS) covering hull, machinery, safety systems, and emergency equipment with scheduled maintenance intervals based on manufacturer specifications and flag state requirements. 2. Record all maintenance activities in a centralised log with completion dates, personnel responsible, and any defects identified. 3. Conduct quarterly reviews of PMS compliance and document remedial actions for overdue or incomplete maintenance items.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations/Technical team: provide PMS procedure document, maintenance schedule for each vessel class, and maintenance history logs (past 24 months). IT team: provide access to PMS software or database with search and export capabilities. Finance team: provide maintenance cost records and budget forecasts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Shipyard/maintenance contractors: provide certificates of completion for all major maintenance work performed, including scope of work and dates. Equipment manufacturers: provide recommended maintenance intervals and specifications for critical safety systems.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: PMS procedure document with maintenance intervals, current maintenance schedule for each vessel, maintenance completion logs showing dates and signatures, quarterly PMS compliance reports, equipment manufacturer specifications, certificates from contractors for completed work, and photographic or technical evidence of completed maintenance.

key_requirements.text: Emergency response procedures including contingency plans
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop comprehensive emergency response procedures covering fire, collision, grounding, medical emergency, pollution, and crew abandonment with clear escalation and communication protocols. 2. Document contingency plans for each vessel type identifying emergency contacts, resource locations, and decision authorities. 3. Conduct documented emergency drills at least twice yearly with crew participation, recording outcomes and any procedure improvements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Safety/Operations team: provide emergency response procedure manual and vessel-specific contingency plans. HR team: provide evidence of emergency drill schedules and participation records. IT team: provide drill observation logs and photographic/video evidence of drills.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Salvage/emergency response contractors: provide standby agreements and evidence of capability and readiness. Flag state or coastal authorities: provide confirmation of emergency plan submission and approval (where required).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Emergency response procedure manual (fire, medical, pollution, abandonment), vessel-specific contingency plans with emergency contact lists, emergency drill schedules and attendance records for past 24 months, signed drill observation reports showing drill objectives and outcomes, documented corrective actions from drills, and flag state/port state approval of emergency plans.

key_requirements.text: Document of Compliance (DOC) for the company and Safety Management Certificate (SMC) for each ship, renewed every 5 years
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Engage a Recognized Organization (RO) or Flag State maritime authority to conduct initial SMS audit and issue the Document of Compliance (DOC) to the company.
2. For each vessel, schedule and complete the Safety Management Certificate (SMC) survey with the RO, ensuring all SMS requirements are met on board.
3. Establish a renewal schedule 6 months before expiry of DOC and SMC, with audits scheduled to maintain continuous certification without gaps.
internal_actions: Compliance Officer: compile SMS manual, crew training records, and vessel maintenance logs for RO review.
Fleet Management: maintain a certification register with issue and expiry dates for all vessels; provide proof of crew competency and medical certificates.
Finance: retain evidence of fees paid to RO and processing correspondence.
vendor_actions: Recognized Organization: issue signed Document of Compliance valid for 5 years and Safety Management Certificate for each ship; provide audit reports detailing findings and corrective actions.
Flag State (if applicable): verify and endorse DOC and SMC certifications in their registry.
compliance_evidence: Original or certified copy of Document of Compliance (DOC) with issue and expiry dates.
Original or certified copy of Safety Management Certificate (SMC) for each vessel.
RO audit reports (initial and renewal audits) signed and dated.
Company SMS manual approved by RO.
Crew competency and medical certificates on file.
Certification register maintained on shore showing all active certificates and renewal schedules.

key_requirements.text: Internal audits and management review of the SMS annually
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Appoint an internal audit team independent of operational management; schedule and conduct comprehensive SMS audits covering all vessels and shore-based functions annually.
2. Document audit findings, non-conformities, and observations in a formal audit report; assign corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) with target closure dates.
3. Conduct an annual management review meeting with senior shore management and vessel representatives to assess SMS effectiveness, review audit findings, and approve policy updates.
internal_actions: Compliance/Quality team: develop annual audit plan covering all ISM Code elements; conduct audits and compile audit checklists and working papers.
Fleet and Shore Operations: provide records of SMS implementation, incident reports, near-misses, and crew training attendance.
Management team: schedule and chair annual management review meeting; document meeting minutes, decisions, and resource allocation for SMS improvements.
vendor_actions: Recognized Organization (if contracted): provide independent audit support or witness audits if required by DOC conditions; confirm audit plan compliance.
Vessels: provide crew cooperation, access to records, and documented responses to audit requests during audit visits.
compliance_evidence: Annual internal audit plan approved and dated.
Completed audit checklists and audit reports for each audit conducted, signed by audit team lead.
Register of non-conformities and observations with corrective action plans and closure evidence.
Minutes of annual management review meeting signed by attendees, including decision log and resource commitments.
Evidence of CAPA closure (e.g., photographs, certificates, training records, procedure updates).
SMS effectiveness evaluation summary included in management review documentation.

regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence

regulations.name: International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: International convention preventing the spread of harmful aquatic organisms, bacteria, and pathogens transported in ships' ballast water. Ships must implement approved ballast water management measures using either ballast water exchange (D-1 Standard) or a type-approved Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS).

key_requirements.text: Develop and implement a Ballast Water Management Plan (BWMP) approved by the flag administration
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a Ballast Water Management Officer and establish the ship's operational structure for ballast water handling. 2. Draft the BWMP document referencing the ship's design, equipment, and routes, then submit to the flag administration for written approval. 3. Maintain the approved BWMP on board and ensure all crew receive documented training on its procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Management team: compile ship design specifications, cargo/route profiles, and crew roster; Legal/Compliance team: prepare submission package for flag administration with company letterhead and designated officer credentials.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag administration: issue written BWMP approval letter; Classification society (if applicable): review and certify BWMP compliance with IMO standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed BWMP approval letter from flag administration, crew training attendance records, and copies of the approved BWMP document on file.

key_requirements.text: Maintain a Ballast Water Record Book (BWRB) documenting all ballast water operations
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a standardized Ballast Water Record Book format compliant with IMO Annex I, either paper or electronic, and assign responsibility to the Ballast Water Management Officer. 2. Create a procedural checklist requiring entries for every ballast water operation: intake location, date/time, volume, discharge details, and treatment applied. 3. Implement monthly audits and annual reviews of all BWRB entries to verify completeness and accuracy.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Operations team: provide ship-specific ballast tank capacity data and operational schedules; IT team: if electronic, configure database with mandatory field validation and date-stamping; Compliance team: establish internal audit templates and reviewer assignments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Software provider (if applicable): deliver BWRB module with IMO-compliant data fields and audit trail functionality; Classification society: confirm BWRB format meets regulatory expectations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed and signed Ballast Water Record Books for audit period, electronic system audit logs showing all entries with timestamps, and internal audit reports documenting review findings.

key_requirements.text: Install an IMO type-approved Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS) meeting D-2 Performance Standard by the IOPP renewal survey date
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify the ship's next IOPP renewal survey date and confirm the installation deadline with the flag administration and Classification society. 2. Obtain IMO type-approval certificate for the selected BWTS and contract the installation with a certified shipyard, scheduling work before the survey deadline. 3. Conduct factory acceptance testing (FAT) and sea trials post-installation to verify D-2 compliance, then prepare technical documentation for the Classification society surveyor.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Technical team: document current ballast system configuration and prepare ship for retrofit; Finance team: allocate budget and approve shipyard contracts; Compliance team: track IMO type-approval certificates and IOPP renewal schedules.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: BWTS manufacturer: provide IMO type-approval certificate, installation manual, and operator training; Shipyard: deliver completion certificate and factory acceptance test results; Classification society: schedule and conduct IOPP renewal survey with BWTS verification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: IMO type-approval certificate for the BWTS, shipyard completion certificate, FAT/sea trial reports with D-2 compliance test results, and surveyor approval note in the renewed IOPP Certificate.

key_requirements.text: D-2 Standard: less than 10 viable organisms per cubic meter (more than 50 microns); less than 10 viable organisms per ml (10-50 microns)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a baseline compliance test protocol requiring annual sampling of treated ballast water by a certified third-party laboratory using IMO-approved methodology. 2. Define corrective action thresholds: if results exceed 10 viable organisms/m³ (>50 µm) or 10 viable organisms/ml (10–50 µm), trigger BWTS maintenance and re-testing within 30 days. 3. Maintain a compliance dashboard tracking all test results and corrective actions for trending and regulatory reporting.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Technical team: define sampling procedures and establish maintenance schedules; Compliance team: create result tracking spreadsheet and corrective action log; Quality Assurance: review lab reports and certify data accuracy.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Certified laboratory: provide ISO 17025-accredited testing reports with viability counts per IMO Res. MEPC.338(76); BWTS service provider: deliver maintenance records and performance verification if D-2 levels are not met.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual laboratory test reports with certified organism counts, maintenance work orders and completion records, internal compliance dashboards, and records of any corrective actions taken.

key_requirements.text: Alternative: ballast water exchange in open ocean at least 200 nautical miles from land and 200m depth (D-1 Standard)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. If electing D-1 Standard as an alternative, establish a written policy requiring ballast water exchange to occur at least 200 nautical miles from nearest land and in water depth of 200 meters or greater. 2. Configure the ship's navigation system and bridge procedures to cross-check exchange location criteria using GPS and electronic charts before each operation. 3. Document every D-1 exchange in the Ballast Water Record Book with verified coordinates, depth soundings, and GPS timestamps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Bridge Operations team: update standing orders and bridge checklists to include location/depth verification steps; IT team: configure chart plotter alerts for 200 nm / 200 m thresholds; Compliance team: create D-1 exemption justification document if alternative to BWTS is chosen.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Chart provider: confirm electronic chart coverage and depth data accuracy; Flag administration (if applicable): issue written approval if D-1 is claimed as alternative to BWTS installation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Bridge standing orders and procedures manual with D-1 criteria, electronic chart system screenshots showing threshold alerts, Ballast Water Record Book entries with GPS coordinates and depth readings, and flag administration approval letter (if D-1 is formal exemption).

key_requirements.text: IMO Ballast Water Management Convention (BWM) Certificate issued by flag state
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Ensure all preceding requirements (BWMP approval, BWTS installation or D-1 procedures, and BWRB documentation) are completed and verified by the Classification society. 2. Schedule and conduct the BWM Certificate survey with the flag state's authorized surveyor, presenting all supporting documentation. 3. Upon issuance, maintain the original BWM Certificate on board and store certified copies in the company's document management system.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: compile complete certificate application package including approved BWMP, BWTS documentation (or D-1 approval), and BWRB samples; Document Control: establish filing system for the issued Certificate and expiry tracking.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Flag administration: conduct BWM Certificate survey and issue the Certificate valid for 5 years; Classification society: provide attendance certificate confirming surveyor verification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Issued BWM Certificate (original and certified copies), surveyor's attending surveyor certificate, complete application submission file, and internal certificate register tracking issue and expiry dates.

key_requirements.text: Port State Control may inspect Ballast Water Record Book and sample ballast water
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a procedure for maintaining the Ballast Water Record Book (BWRB) in both physical and electronic formats, with entries completed within 24 hours of each ballast water operation.
2. Designate a trained ballast water management officer responsible for BWRB accuracy and retention for a minimum of three years.
3. Create a sampling protocol and secure sample storage procedure (with temperature/seal controls) to enable Port State Control inspections on demand.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve the Ballast Water Management Policy and BWRB completion procedure.
Operations team: provide crew training completion certificates and ballast water operation checklists.
IT team: configure ballast water operation logging system with tamper-evident audit trails and backup mechanisms.
Fleet Management: establish sample custody chain documentation templates and storage facility specifications.
vendor_actions: Ballast water treatment system supplier: provide technical documentation confirming sample-taking compatibility and certification of any onboard sampling equipment.
Record-keeping software vendor: supply proof of BWRB template compliance with IMO requirements and audit trail capabilities.
Port facility operators: confirm availability of ballast water sampling facilities and testing laboratory accreditation at nominated ports.
compliance_evidence: Completed and signed Ballast Water Record Books for the last 36 months (all vessels).
Port State Control inspection reports showing BWRB review and ballast water samples taken.
Crew training certificates for ballast water management (initial and refresher).
Sample custody chain logs with timestamps, temperatures, and inspector signatures.
Ballast water treatment system type approval certificate or ballast water management plan approval letter from Flag State.
Laboratory test reports for ballast water samples (where taken).

regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties

regulations.name: Bank of Israel Directive 357 — Technology and Information Systems Risk Management
regulations.authority: Bank of Israel — Banking Supervision Department
regulations.summary: Bank of Israel Directive 357 on Technology and Information Systems Risk Management establishes binding cybersecurity and IT governance requirements for all Israeli banking corporations. It mandates risk management frameworks covering IT systems, cyber defense, business continuity, and outsourcing of technology services including cloud.

key_requirements.text: Establish a board-approved technology and cyber risk management policy
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Draft a comprehensive technology and cyber risk management policy document covering risk identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring frameworks aligned with Bank of Israel expectations.
2. Submit the policy to the Board of Directors for formal review and approval, documenting the approval date and board resolution number.
3. Distribute the approved policy to all relevant departments and establish a review cycle (minimum annually) with documented updates and re-approvals.
internal_actions: Board Secretariat: provide signed board resolution and meeting minutes approving the policy.
Legal team: confirm policy aligns with Bank of Israel Directive 357 requirements and other applicable banking regulations.
HR team: maintain records of staff acknowledgment of policy receipt and compliance obligations.
vendor_actions: Third-party risk management consultants (if engaged): provide attestation that policy framework meets NIST or equivalent industry-standard maturity levels.
compliance_evidence: Signed board resolution with date and approval confirmation.
Final approved policy document with version control and effective date.
Distribution log showing dissemination to all business units and staff acknowledgment records.
Annual review and re-approval documentation.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a CISO with direct board reporting line and defined authorities
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Formally designate a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) role with a written charter defining reporting line directly to the Board or Board Risk Committee, not through operational management.
2. Document the CISO's delegated authorities in writing, including authority to enforce security policies, access control decisions, and incident response escalation.
3. Communicate the CISO appointment to the Bank of Israel Supervisor and include the CISO in all board-level risk discussions with meeting attendance records.
internal_actions: Board Secretariat: issue appointment letter and charter documenting direct board reporting line and decision-making authorities.
HR team: establish CISO job description, compensation structure, and performance evaluation criteria independent of operational business targets.
Legal team: draft conflict-of-interest and independence policies confirming CISO cannot be overridden by operational leaders without board escalation.
vendor_actions: External governance consultants (if applicable): provide advisory confirmation that CISO role structure meets Bank of Israel independence and authority requirements.
compliance_evidence: Signed CISO appointment letter and role charter with effective date.
Board meeting minutes documenting CISO appointment and confirmation of independent reporting line.
CISO job description clearly stating reporting to Board/Risk Committee.
Bank of Israel Supervisor notification letter acknowledging CISO appointment.

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual cyber risk assessment aligned with NIST or equivalent frameworks
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Engage an internal or external risk assessment team to conduct a comprehensive cyber risk assessment using NIST Cybersecurity Framework or ISO 27001 as the reference model.
2. Document all identified risks with likelihood, impact, and current control effectiveness ratings; map gaps to required controls.
3. Present findings and remediation roadmap to the Board annually, with documented approval of risk acceptance decisions and mitigation priorities.
internal_actions: IT Security team: coordinate risk assessment scope, provide system inventories, control documentation, and evidence of control execution.
Risk Management team: facilitate risk assessment process, document risk ratings, and maintain risk register with tracking of remediation status.
Finance team: provide budget allocation for risk mitigation initiatives identified in assessment.
vendor_actions: External audit/assessment firm (if not conducted internally): deliver formal cyber risk assessment report using NIST or ISO 27001 framework, including executive summary and detailed findings with remediation timelines.
compliance_evidence: Annual cyber risk assessment report with assessment date and framework used (NIST/ISO reference).
Risk register documenting identified risks, ratings, and control gaps.
Board meeting minutes approving risk assessment results and remediation roadmap.
Documented risk acceptance decisions for residual risks signed by Board and CISO.

key_requirements.text: Implement multi-layered defense-in-depth controls for critical banking systems
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Map all critical banking systems and define a layered security architecture including network segmentation, application controls, data encryption (in-transit and at-rest), and access controls.
2. Implement controls across network, host, application, and data layers; document control specifications and validation evidence.
3. Establish a control testing schedule (minimum semi-annually) with independent validation and document any deviations or compensating controls.
internal_actions: IT Security/Architecture team: map critical systems, design defense-in-depth architecture, and maintain control specifications documentation.
IT Operations team: implement and maintain network segmentation, firewalls, intrusion detection/prevention, endpoint protection, and patch management processes.
Database/Application teams: enforce encryption standards (TLS 1.2+, AES-256), implement application-level access controls, and maintain encryption key management procedures.
Internal Audit team: conduct independent control testing and report findings to management.
vendor_actions: Security hardware/software vendors: provide product security documentation, vulnerability disclosure policies, and support for configuration hardening.
Third-party security assessors (if engaged): deliver control validation reports confirming adequate implementation of defense-in-depth layers.
compliance_evidence: System architecture diagram showing critical systems and layered defense controls.
Control implementation documentation for each layer (network, host, application, data).
Encryption audit reports confirming in-transit and at-rest encryption deployment.
Semi-annual independent control testing reports with evidence of validation.
Configuration baselines and deviation register with documented compensating controls.

key_requirements.text: Report material cyber incidents to the Bank of Israel Supervisor within 24 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a written incident classification policy defining 'material cyber incidents' based on impact (data breach scope, system availability, regulatory impact) and a 24-hour reporting procedure.
2. Designate an incident response lead responsible for threat assessment and regulatory notification; ensure they have direct communication channels with the CISO and legal counsel.
3. Create a Bank of Israel Supervisor contact protocol and maintain a log of all incidents reported, including timestamp, notification recipient, and confirmation of receipt.
internal_actions: CISO/Incident Response team: develop incident classification matrix and 24-hour escalation procedure; maintain incident reporting log with timestamps and evidence of submission.
Legal team: draft incident notification templates compliant with Bank of Israel requirements and review regulatory notification language.
IT Operations: establish secure communication channels (dedicated email, phone contacts) for reporting to Bank of Israel Supervisor and confirm contact details annually.
vendor_actions: External incident response firms (if engaged): provide 24-hour incident triage and assessment capability; confirm availability in service level agreement.
compliance_evidence: Incident classification policy document with materiality thresholds.
Incident reporting procedure and 24-hour escalation flow chart.
Bank of Israel Supervisor contact list with confirmed authorized recipients.
Incident report log showing all reported incidents with dates, descriptions, notification timestamps, and Bank of Israel acknowledgment receipts.
Sample incident notification submissions to Bank of Israel with proof of delivery.

key_requirements.text: Maintain a tested cyber incident response and business continuity plan
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
how_to_meet: 1. Develop a formal Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) and Business Continuity Plan (BCP) covering critical banking functions, recovery time objectives (RTOs), and recovery point objectives (RPOs).
2. Conduct tabletop exercises (minimum annually) and full technical tests (minimum semi-annually) of response procedures and recovery capabilities; document results and remediation actions.
3. Maintain current contact lists, alternate processing sites, backup systems, and communication protocols; review and update plans following each test or material incident.
internal_actions: CISO/Incident Response team: develop CIRP with roles, responsibilities, escalation procedures, and recovery steps; schedule and execute tabletop exercises annually with documented results.
Business Continuity/IT Operations: develop BCP with RTO/RPO targets for critical systems, maintain backup systems, and execute semi-annual failover tests with results documentation.
Communications team: prepare incident communication templates (internal, external, regulatory) and confirm contact lists.
Finance team: allocate budget for backup infrastructure, disaster recovery sites, and plan testing costs.
vendor_actions: Disaster recovery/backup service providers: provide service level agreements confirming RTO/RPO commitments, execute bi-annual recovery tests, and deliver test result reports.
Alternate processing site operators (if third-party): confirm facility availability, capacity, and readiness for rapid activation with signed agreements.
compliance_evidence: Current Cyber Incident Response Plan document with version date and board approval.
Current Business Continuity Plan with documented RTO/RPO targets for critical systems.
Annual tabletop exercise scenarios, attendance records, and documented findings/recommendations.
Semi-annual backup/recovery test results with system recovery times, data integrity confirmation, and sign-off from IT leadership.
Plan update log showing revisions following tests or incidents, with effective dates.
Contact lists (internal, external, Bank of Israel) with last verification date.

key_requirements.text: Manage technology outsourcing risk including cloud providers with enhanced due diligence
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct formal risk assessment of all cloud and outsourced technology providers, documenting data sensitivity, availability criticality, and inherent risks.
2. Establish vendor evaluation criteria aligned with BoI 357 requirements (security controls, certifications, audit rights, incident response SLAs) and score each provider.
3. Execute enhanced due diligence agreements requiring annual third-party security audits, penetration testing results, and business continuity plans.
internal_actions: Risk Management team: develop and maintain vendor risk register with risk ratings, assessment dates, and remediation tracking.
Legal team: create standardized vendor service level agreements (SLAs) and data processing addendums (DPAs) referencing BoI 357 compliance obligations.
IT team: document inventory of all cloud and outsourced systems, including data flows, access points, and criticality classifications.
vendor_actions: Cloud providers: furnish SOC 2 Type II audit reports, ISO 27001 or equivalent security certifications, and incident notification procedures with 24-hour breach disclosure commitment.
Outsourcing vendors: provide detailed security questionnaire responses, supply chain sub-processor lists, disaster recovery and backup test results, and signed data protection agreements.
All vendors: commit to annual security assessments, grant audit rights for on-site inspections, and provide evidence of mandatory employee cybersecurity training.
compliance_evidence: Completed vendor risk assessment matrices with documented scoring and approval sign-offs.
Executed service level agreements and data processing addendums referencing BoI 357 control requirements.
Vendor audit reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, penetration test results) with dates within 12 months.
Vendor security questionnaire responses and sub-processor disclosure documentation.
Audit access logs and inspection reports from internal reviews of vendor control environments.
Incident notification records demonstrating vendor compliance with breach disclosure timeframes.

key_requirements.text: Employee cybersecurity awareness training and phishing simulation exercises
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
how_to_meet: 1. Develop mandatory annual cybersecurity awareness training curriculum covering phishing recognition, password hygiene, incident reporting, and data handling specific to banking operations.
2. Implement platform-based phishing simulation exercises quarterly, with immediate post-click micro-training for users who fall for simulated phishing attempts.
3. Track training completion rates and phishing simulation failure rates by department; escalate departments failing to meet 90% pass threshold to management.
internal_actions: HR/Learning & Development: procure or build training delivery platform, schedule mandatory sessions annually, maintain attendance and completion certificates for all staff.
Security team: design phishing simulation campaigns, select third-party simulation tool, configure click reporting and analytics dashboards, and document lessons learned from results.
Compliance team: establish metrics (training completion % by role, phishing click rates, time-to-report incidents) and prepare quarterly reports to senior management and board.
vendor_actions: Training platform provider: deliver annual cybersecurity awareness modules tailored to banking/BoI 357 context, tracking per-user completion and assessment scores.

Phishing simulation vendor: execute quarterly campaigns with configurable email templates, provide detailed analytics on click rates and reporting behavior, and enable automated post-click training delivery.
compliance_evidence: Training completion certificates or logs showing 100% of staff (by role) completed annual awareness training with dates and assessment pass scores.
Phishing simulation campaign reports (quarterly) showing participation rates, click rates by department, and trend analysis over 12+ months.
Training curriculum content and phishing scenario templates reviewed and approved by Compliance and Information Security teams.
Management reports documenting low-performers, remedial actions taken, and exceptions approval for non-compliant staff.
Signed acknowledgement forms from employees confirming receipt of mandatory security awareness and phishing reporting procedures.

regulations.penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations

regulations.name: Israeli Capital Market, Insurance and Savings Authority Cyber Risk Management Circular
regulations.authority: Capital Market, Insurance and Savings Authority (CMISA)
regulations.summary: The CMISA Cyber Risk Management Circular mandates that all entities regulated by the Israeli Capital Market Authority — insurance companies, pension funds, provident funds, and investment managers — implement a comprehensive cyber risk management program proportionate to their size and systemic importance.

key_requirements.text: Board-level accountability for cyber risk governance and annual cyber risk reporting
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Board Cyber Risk Committee with defined charter, meeting cadence (minimum quarterly), and explicit accountability for cyber governance oversight. 2. Require the CISO or designated cyber officer to present annual cyber risk assessment and status report to the Board, including risk metrics, incidents, and remediation progress. 3. Document Board approvals of cyber strategy, risk appetite, and material cyber incidents in Board minutes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft Board Cyber Risk Committee charter and governance policy. Board Secretariat: maintain signed Board minutes documenting cyber risk discussions and approvals. CISO: prepare annual cyber risk executive summary for Board review.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External audit/advisory firm: provide annual independent cyber risk assessment report to present to Board if outsourced.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board Cyber Risk Committee charter, signed Board minutes (minimum quarterly) documenting cyber governance decisions, annual cyber risk report presented to Board with date stamps and attendance records.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a CISO or designate a senior responsible officer for cyber
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Issue a formal appointment letter or Board resolution naming the CISO or equivalent senior officer (e.g., Chief Information Security Officer, VP Cyber Risk) with explicit cyber risk accountability and reporting line to Board or Audit Committee. 2. Define the role's scope, authority, budget, and staffing in a written job description or charter. 3. Ensure the appointed officer has direct access to Board/Audit Committee without business unit interference.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide appointment letter or employment contract for CISO with defined cyber responsibilities and reporting structure. Board/Audit Committee: issue formal Board resolution or memo confirming CISO appointment and authority. Finance team: confirm dedicated cyber budget allocation to CISO.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable for this control.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Board resolution or appointment letter naming CISO with effective date, CISO job description specifying cyber governance accountability, organizational chart showing direct reporting line, and evidence of CISO participation in Board/Audit Committee meetings.

key_requirements.text: Implement a cyber risk management framework covering identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery (NIST CSF-aligned)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop or adopt a documented cyber risk management framework (aligned with NIST Cybersecurity Framework) covering five functions: Identify (asset and threat inventory), Protect (safeguards), Detect (monitoring), Respond (incident procedures), and Recover (continuity). 2. Map the framework to your organisation's critical processes, systems, and data classifications, with assigned owners and timelines. 3. Document how the framework is reviewed and updated annually, with evidence of Board or management approval.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/IT team: develop comprehensive cyber risk framework document detailing all five NIST functions with internal controls, ownership, and metrics. Risk/Compliance team: create risk register mapping cyber risks to controls and business processes. Architecture/IT team: conduct baseline assessment of current state against framework and document gaps.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cybersecurity consultant or audit firm: provide external validation or gap assessment of framework design against NIST CSF best practices (optional but strengthens evidence).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed cyber risk management framework document (minimum 15–20 pages), NIST CSF mapping spreadsheet, annual framework review and approval memo, gap assessment report, and evidence of stakeholder sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual penetration testing and vulnerability assessments
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage an accredited third-party penetration testing firm to conduct a full-scope annual penetration test covering external and internal attack surfaces, with scope approved by CISO. 2. Conduct vulnerability assessments (automated scans plus manual review) at least quarterly on critical assets; document findings in a prioritized remediation plan. 3. Track remediation of identified vulnerabilities with evidence of closure and retesting within agreed timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: compile scope document (systems, networks, applications in-scope for annual pentest), maintain records of all vulnerability scans, and track remediation tickets with closure evidence. CISO: approve pentest scope and review final pentest report.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Penetration testing firm: deliver signed Statement of Work, annual pentest report with findings, risk ratings, and proof of testing (signed test sign-off). Vulnerability scanning vendor: provide quarterly scan reports or continuous scanning evidence.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual pentest report (from accredited firm) with test scope, methodology, and date, vulnerability assessment reports (quarterly), remediation tracking log with ticket numbers and closure dates, and evidence of retesting for critical findings.

key_requirements.text: Report significant cyber incidents to CMISA within 12 hours of detection
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define 'significant cyber incident' (e.g., confirmed breach, data loss, system unavailability >4 hours, ransomware) in a formal Incident Response Plan with escalation triggers and notification roles. 2. Establish a 12-hour incident detection-to-reporting process: assign a duty officer to validate incident severity, document detection time, and trigger mandatory CMISA notification via designated secure channel. 3. Maintain an incident log with detection timestamp, classification, notification time to CMISA, and post-incident review outcome.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/Legal team: document Incident Response Plan defining 'significant' incident criteria, CMISA contact details, and 12-hour reporting clock (starting from detection, not discovery). IT/Security team: maintain incident tracking system with timestamped detection and reporting records, duty officer schedule, and incident log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable; CMISA reporting is the organisation's direct responsibility. (Vendors may assist via breach notification contracts, but reporting to CMISA is mandatory.)
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Incident Response Plan with incident classification thresholds, incident log entries showing detection timestamp and CMISA notification timestamp within 12 hours, CMISA submission receipts (email confirmations or reference numbers), and post-incident review summaries.

key_requirements.text: Maintain cyber insurance coverage proportionate to risk exposure
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a cyber risk exposure assessment (quantify potential loss scenarios for data breach, ransomware, system failure) to determine adequate insurance coverage limits. 2. Procure cyber liability insurance from a licensed provider with minimum coverage aligned to assessed exposure (typically €5M–€50M+); ensure policy covers breach response, regulatory fines, and business interruption. 3. Review and renew cyber insurance annually, updating coverage limits as business and threat landscape evolve; maintain proof of active coverage.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk/Finance team: perform cyber risk quantification (e.g., asset value, business impact analysis) to determine insurance coverage recommendations. Procurement/Legal team: obtain and review cyber insurance policy documentation; confirm coverage limits, exclusions, and claims process. Finance team: maintain proof of premium payment and active policy status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Insurance broker or insurer: provide cyber liability insurance policy document, coverage schedule, exclusions, and renewal quotations annually. Insurer: deliver Certificate of Insurance or policy endorsement confirming active coverage.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cyber risk exposure assessment document, signed cyber liability insurance policy with coverage schedule and limits, Certificate of Insurance (showing insured entity, coverage amount, and effective dates), annual renewal evidence, and premium payment records.

key_requirements.text: Establish third-party cyber risk management including InsurTech vendors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a formal inventory of all third-party vendors and InsurTech service providers with access to systems or data, documenting their criticality classification.
2. Develop and implement a vendor cyber risk assessment framework including mandatory security questionnaires, audit rights, and incident notification clauses in contracts.
3. Establish ongoing monitoring procedures with quarterly compliance reviews and annual security assessments for all critical vendors.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft standardized vendor cyber security addendum and data processing agreements with mandatory incident reporting SLAs.
IT team: create and maintain a third-party access register with system names, data classifications, and connection methods.
Risk/Compliance team: design vendor cyber risk scoring matrix and schedule quarterly vendor risk review meetings with documented outcomes.
vendor_actions: All vendors: provide completed NIST Cybersecurity Framework or ISO 27001 assessment questionnaires signed by vendor management.
InsurTech vendors: submit SOC 2 Type II audit reports covering the preceding 12 months or equivalent security certification.
All critical vendors: sign amended service level agreements explicitly including cyber incident notification within 24 hours and breach liability clauses.
compliance_evidence: Signed vendor cyber risk addenda to all service agreements.
Completed vendor risk assessment spreadsheet with criticality ratings and review dates.
SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificates from all critical vendors.
Vendor incident notification logs and response records from the past 12 months.
Meeting minutes from quarterly vendor cyber risk review sessions.

key_requirements.text: Business continuity and disaster recovery planning with annual testing
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Develop a comprehensive Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) that covers all critical systems, data, and services with defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO).
2. Conduct mandatory annual tabletop and technical failover tests for both BCP and DRP, documenting results and remediation actions.
3. Maintain an updated recovery procedures manual with clear roles, escalation paths, and contact information accessible to authorized personnel 24/7.
internal_actions: IT team: document system criticality rankings, prepare DRP technical runbooks with step-by-step failover procedures and RTO/RPO targets.
Business continuity coordinator: develop BCP with department-level recovery teams, responsibilities, and communication trees.
IT team: schedule and execute annual combined BCP/DRP test, recording test logs, outcomes, and time-to-recovery metrics.
Compliance team: maintain test evidence repository including pre-test checklists, post-test reports, and sign-offs from all participating teams.
vendor_actions: Cloud/Infrastructure vendors: provide documented service availability SLAs with guaranteed RTO/RPO commitments and failover procedures.
Backup and disaster recovery vendors: certify annual restore testing results and provide test certificates showing successful data recovery within stated RPO windows.
All critical service vendors: confirm their own business continuity procedures and incident notification protocols during recovery scenarios.
compliance_evidence: Signed and approved Business Continuity Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan documents with version control and last review date.
Annual BCP/DRP test execution reports including test date, scope, participants, pass/fail results, and time-to-recovery metrics.
Detailed remediation action log from each test with owner assignments and completion evidence.
Backup vendor restoration test certificates confirming successful recovery within stated RPO targets.
Updated recovery procedures manual with distribution list and acknowledgment signatures from recovery team members.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance

regulations.name: Israel Securities Authority Cyber Disclosure and Risk Management Directive for Public Companies
regulations.authority: Israel Securities Authority (ISA)
regulations.summary: The Israel Securities Authority directive requires all companies publicly listed on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange (TASE) to disclose material cybersecurity risks and incidents in periodic reports, appoint a board-level cyber committee, and establish documented cyber risk management processes.

key_requirements.text: Disclose material cybersecurity risks in annual and periodic reports to ISA
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team identifies material cybersecurity risks using a risk assessment framework aligned with ISA materiality thresholds. 2. Compile risk disclosures into a dedicated cybersecurity risks section of annual and periodic reports. 3. CFO and General Counsel sign off on risk statements before submission to ISA.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written risk materiality assessment and list of identified risks; IT team: supply vulnerability scan reports and threat landscape analysis; Compliance team: maintain disclosure sign-off records and ISA submission confirmations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Security assessment vendors: provide detailed risk reports identifying threats with business impact; External audit firm: confirm risk disclosure adequacy in audit working papers.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual report with dedicated cybersecurity risk section (signed by CFO/General Counsel); ISA submission receipts; materiality assessment memo; IT vulnerability reports dated within 12 months prior to reporting period.

key_requirements.text: Immediately report material cybersecurity events affecting company operations or data to ISA
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident classification matrix defining 'material' based on data volume, operational impact, and ISA guidance. 2. Create an incident escalation procedure requiring General Counsel or Chief Information Security Officer to notify ISA within 24–48 hours of confirmed material events. 3. Maintain a signed incident response plan approved by board audit committee and Legal.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: document incident detection, triage, and root-cause findings; Legal team: record materiality decisions and ISA notification timestamps; Compliance team: maintain incident log with event dates, impact summaries, and notification evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed Security Service Provider (MSSP) or incident responders: provide incident forensic reports, timeline documents, and confirmation of findings within 48 hours of engagement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident response plan; incident classification matrix approved by audit committee; dated ISA notifications (email or portal submission records); forensic reports from IR vendor; internal incident log entries with timestamps and materiality classifications.

key_requirements.text: Board of directors must establish a cyber committee or assign cyber oversight to existing committee
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Board passes a resolution establishing a dedicated cyber/risk committee or formally delegates cyber oversight to an existing audit or risk committee, documented in board minutes. 2. Define committee charter specifying cyber responsibilities, meeting frequency (at least quarterly), and reporting lines to the full board. 3. Appoint committee members with relevant expertise (CISO, IT audit, risk, or external cybersecurity expert).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretary: draft and file board resolution establishing committee; Legal/Compliance: prepare committee charter and governance documentation; HR team: maintain records of committee member qualifications and appointment letters.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External board advisor or independent cybersecurity consultant (if appointed as committee member): provide CV, expertise summary, and signed engagement letter.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution (dated and signed); committee charter document (approved and dated); board meeting minutes confirming cyber committee establishment; committee member appointment letters with qualifications; quarterly committee meeting minutes.

key_requirements.text: Management must conduct annual review of cyber risk posture and report findings to board
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. CISO or Chief Risk Officer conducts a formal annual cyber risk assessment covering threat landscape, vulnerabilities, controls effectiveness, and business impact scenarios. 2. Prepare a written cyber risk posture report with findings, risk ratings, and remediation recommendations. 3. Present findings to the board cyber committee or audit committee and document board discussion and any approved remediation plans.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/IT Security team: conduct risk assessment using a recognized framework (e.g., NIST, ISO 27005); Compliance team: compile assessment documentation and maintain risk register; Board Secretary: record cyber risk discussion in board/committee minutes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party risk assessment firm or penetration testing vendor: deliver assessment report with risk findings, severity ratings, and executive summary.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Dated annual cyber risk assessment report (signed by CISO/CRO); risk register with risk ratings and control status; board or committee meeting minutes documenting risk review and approved remediation actions; management response plan with timelines.

key_requirements.text: Describe the company cyber risk management framework and key controls in annual reports
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the company's cyber risk management framework (governance structure, roles, risk appetite, and policy hierarchy) in a formal framework document. 2. Inventory key controls (access management, encryption, incident response, business continuity, vendor management, employee training) with descriptions and responsible owners. 3. Include framework and control descriptions in the annual report's management discussion section or dedicated cybersecurity governance disclosure.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/IT Security: document cyber framework, control catalogue, and control owners; Compliance team: map controls to business processes and ISA expectations; Legal team: draft disclosure language for annual report.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External audit or cyber consulting firm: validate framework documentation and confirm alignment with industry standards (NIST, ISO 27001).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cyber Risk Management Framework document (board-approved); controls inventory matrix with owner assignments; annual report section describing framework and key controls; audit firm validation letter; Board approval of framework disclosure.
