key_requirements.text: Disclose third-party cyber incidents where the vendor holds material company data
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish vendor data inventory and materiality classification (e.g., vendors handling financial data, customer PII, operational systems). 2. Require vendors to notify the company within 24–48 hours of confirmed security incidents affecting company data; include this in vendor security agreements. 3. Legal and Compliance assess vendor incident impact and determine materiality; if material, report to ISA following the incident disclosure procedure (ISA_CYBER_IL-R002).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement/Legal team: maintain vendor register with data classification and materiality tier; IT team: monitor vendor security advisories and incident notifications; Compliance team: log vendor incidents and impact assessments; Board Secretary: escalate material vendor incidents to audit committee.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All material data vendors: sign Data Security Amendment or Vendor Security Agreement committing to incident notification within 24–48 hours and providing forensic details; provide incident notification contacts and escalation procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Vendor data inventory and materiality classification document; signed Data Security Agreements with all material vendors; vendor incident notification log (if any incidents occurred); impact assessment memos for vendor incidents; ISA notifications for material vendor incidents; vendor forensic reports provided to company.

key_requirements.text: CISO role recommended; cyber expertise on board or access to external advisors required
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Appoint or designate a CISO (Chief Information Security Officer) with defined authority, reporting line to CEO or board audit committee, and documented cyber security responsibilities.

2. Establish board-level cyber oversight through either: direct board member with demonstrable cyber expertise, or a formal relationship with an external cyber advisor (consultant, firm, or retainer agreement).

3. Document the governance structure in board minutes, CISO job description, and board charter amendments; schedule quarterly cyber risk reviews with board participation or external advisor attendance.
internal_actions: HR/Legal: provide CISO appointment letter, job description, and reporting structure diagram showing board/audit committee escalation path.
Board/Governance: provide board charter or governance policy amendment establishing cyber oversight responsibility.
Legal: provide board minutes from meetings where cyber governance and CISO appointment were approved and documented.
vendor_actions: External Cyber Advisor (if selected): provide signed engagement letter outlining scope of advisory services, reporting frequency (minimum quarterly), and accessibility to board or audit committee.
External Cyber Advisor: provide curriculum vitae or credentials summary demonstrating cyber security expertise relevant to public company risk landscape.
compliance_evidence: Signed CISO appointment letter and current job description with cyber security mandate.
Board resolutions or minutes approving CISO role and cyber governance framework, dated within last 12 months.
If external advisor model: signed engagement letter with scope, duration, and board reporting obligations.
Evidence of board cyber risk discussion (meeting minutes or advisor attendance logs) from at least one meeting in current fiscal year.
Organizational chart or governance diagram showing CISO reporting line and board/audit committee oversight structure.

regulations.penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure

regulations.name: Israeli Computer Law (Hok HaMahshevim) 1995
regulations.authority: Israeli Ministry of Justice / Israel Police Lahav 433 Cybercrime Unit
regulations.summary: Israel foundational computer crime law prohibiting unauthorized access to computer systems, data theft, and computer fraud. Establishes criminal liability for hacking, malware distribution, and cybersabotage. Subsequent amendments aligned Israel with the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on unauthorized intrusion into computer systems (criminal offence)
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish role-based access control (RBAC) with principle of least privilege across all systems. 2. Deploy network segmentation and intrusion detection systems (IDS) to monitor for unauthorized access attempts. 3. Conduct quarterly penetration testing and document remediation of identified vulnerabilities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide current access control matrix, user provisioning/deprovisioning procedures, and IDS alert logs for past 12 months. Security team: deliver penetration test reports and remediation tracking records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting/cloud providers: furnish System and Organization Controls (SOC 2 Type II) reports covering access controls and authentication mechanisms. Security vendors: provide documentation of IDS/firewall rule configurations deployed in production.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: RBAC policy document with approval sign-off, SOC 2 Type II audit report, IDS configuration screenshots, penetration test reports with executive summaries, access logs showing blocked unauthorized connection attempts, and documented remediation tickets.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on disruption, damage, or destruction of computer data or systems
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement automated backup and disaster recovery procedures with verified restoration testing at least semi-annually. 2. Deploy file integrity monitoring (FIM) tools to detect unauthorized changes to critical data and system files. 3. Establish data loss prevention (DLP) controls and document all data modification events in immutable audit logs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide backup/recovery runbooks, FIM tool configuration, DLP policy ruleset, and audit logs covering all data modifications in past 12 months. Database team: deliver schema and data integrity validation reports and backup restoration test results.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Backup service providers: submit backup frequency, encryption method, and tested recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO) documentation. DLP/monitoring vendors: furnish tool configuration screenshots and incident response procedures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Backup policy with approved schedule, FIM configuration document, successful backup restoration test records with timestamps, DLP rule documentation, immutable audit log exports showing all write/delete operations, and incident response playbook for data integrity breaches.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on fraudulent use of computers to obtain money, goods, or services
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement transaction monitoring and fraud detection controls within payment and financial systems with real-time alerting. 2. Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all financial transaction systems and maintain detailed audit trails of all financial activities. 3. Conduct annual financial controls assessment and document compensating controls for any identified gaps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide list of financial systems, transaction thresholds, and approval matrices. IT team: deliver MFA implementation status across all financial applications and transaction audit logs for past 24 months. Compliance team: submit annual financial controls self-assessment and risk register.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Payment processors: provide fraud detection methodology, false-positive rates, and compliance certifications (PCI-DSS). Financial software vendors: furnish audit trail specifications, transaction logging capabilities, and MFA integration documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Transaction monitoring policy, MFA deployment certificate or configuration audit, transaction audit logs showing authorization chain, fraud detection rule documentation, financial controls assessment report, and documented instances of prevented fraudulent transactions.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on creating, distributing, or possessing malicious software with criminal intent
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) and antivirus solutions on all endpoints with real-time threat scanning and automatic quarantine capabilities. 2. Establish a software development security standard prohibiting malicious code and require code review with static application security testing (SAST) before production deployment. 3. Maintain a list of approved/blocked software and conduct monthly vulnerability scans on all systems.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide EDR/antivirus deployment inventory, quarantine logs, and threat intelligence feeds configured. Development team: deliver secure coding policy, code review checklist, SAST tool configuration, and log of all scanned code repositories. Security team: submit approved software whitelist/blacklist and monthly vulnerability scan reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: EDR/antivirus vendors: furnish malware definition update schedule, detection rate documentation, and quarantine log export capabilities. SAST tool vendors: provide scan result reports and false-positive management procedures. Vulnerability scanner vendors: deliver monthly scan result summaries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Secure coding policy signed by development leadership, SAST scan reports with remediation tracking, EDR/antivirus deployment certificate, quarantine log exports showing detected/blocked malware, code review audit trail, approved software inventory with justifications, and monthly vulnerability assessment reports.

key_requirements.text: Organizations must implement reasonable access controls to avoid liability for inadequate protection
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document a comprehensive Information Security Policy covering access controls, authentication standards, data protection, and incident response; ensure board-level approval and annual review. 2. Implement technical controls: encryption for data at rest and in transit, strong password policies, session timeouts, and audit logging across all systems. 3. Conduct annual risk assessments, maintain a risk register, and document risk acceptance decisions with business owner sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: CISO/Security team: deliver comprehensive Information Security Policy, risk assessment report, and risk register with mitigation plans. IT team: provide encryption inventory, authentication standard documentation, and control configuration baselines. Compliance team: submit board meeting minutes approving security policies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: furnish encryption specifications, compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), and control matrices aligned with policy requirements. All technology vendors: provide security datasheets confirming alignment with organizational access control and encryption standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Information Security Policy with board approval and annual review evidence, risk assessment report with risk register, encryption inventory and certificates, authentication configuration documentation, audit log samples, incident response plan with testing records, vendor compliance certificates, and risk acceptance memo signed by business leadership.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory reporting of unauthorized access affecting critical infrastructure to INCD
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify critical infrastructure systems within scope of INCD reporting requirements and establish clear definitions of 'unauthorized access' triggering mandatory disclosure. 2. Implement 24/7 incident detection and response procedures with documented escalation paths to legal/compliance for triggering incidents; create incident classification and assessment templates. 3. Establish direct reporting channel to INCD (Israel National Cyber Directorate) with pre-approved contact and message templates; maintain incident reporting log with timestamps and regulatory submission evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/Incident Response team: identify critical infrastructure systems in scope, define unauthorized access criteria, and deliver incident response playbook with INCD escalation procedures. Legal team: clarify INCD notification timeline and documentation requirements. IT team: provide system criticality assessment and baseline security event definitions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed Security Service Providers (MSSP): confirm incident detection thresholds, 24/7 availability, and commitment to immediately notify organization of critical security events. Vendors managing critical infrastructure: certify notification capabilities and support for regulatory breach reporting.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Critical infrastructure inventory with INCD scope determination, incident classification policy, incident response procedure document with INCD escalation paths, incident tickets/logs showing detection timestamps and assessment conclusions, INCD submission confirmations (with timestamps and reference numbers), legal review documentation of reporting timeline compliance, and evidence of any incidents reported within regulatory timeframe.

key_requirements.text: Israel is a signatory to the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime — cross-border cooperation
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Map all vendors and third-party service providers handling data or systems, and confirm their jurisdictions and data residency locations.
2. Document organisational procedures for responding to cross-border law enforcement requests (mutual legal assistance treaties, mutual legal assistance requests) and assign a legal point of contact.
3. Include Budapest Convention compliance obligations in vendor contracts, requiring vendors to maintain records and cooperate with lawful cross-border investigations.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain a cross-border data request response procedure, including escalation paths and timelines for cooperation with Israeli authorities and foreign law enforcement.
IT team: document data flows mapping where customer/operational data is stored, processed, or transmitted across borders.
Compliance team: maintain a current vendor and third-party register identifying each party's jurisdiction and applicable mutual legal assistance obligations.
vendor_actions: All vendors: sign amended data processing agreements or addenda explicitly acknowledging Budapest Convention obligations and commitment to respond to lawful cross-border investigation requests within specified timelines.
All vendors: provide written confirmation of their jurisdiction(s) and any mutual legal assistance treaty relationships they maintain with Israel or relevant third countries.
Cloud/hosting vendors: provide contractual commitments to preserve logs, metadata, and transaction records in compliance with international cybercrime cooperation standards for minimum 90 days.
compliance_evidence: Signed vendor contracts or addenda containing Budapest Convention compliance clauses and cross-border cooperation commitments.
Data location and flow mapping document showing jurisdictional boundaries and cross-border data transfers.
Organisational cross-border legal request response procedure (version-controlled, dated, and approved by Legal and Compliance).
Vendor register listing all third parties, their jurisdictions, and mutual legal assistance treaty status.
Copies of vendor written confirmations of jurisdiction and treaty status.
Evidence of legal team training or briefing on Budapest Convention obligations and cross-border request handling.

regulations.penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines

regulations.name: Singapore Personal Data Protection Act 2012
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC)
regulations.summary: Singapore's primary data protection law governing the collection, use, disclosure, and care of personal data. Major 2020 amendments introduced mandatory breach notification within 3 days for significant breaches, data portability, enhanced consent exceptions, and increased financial penalties up to 10% of annual turnover.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent and notify individuals of purposes before or at the time of collecting personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Financial penalty up to S$1M per contravention
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consent records; consent capture UI evidence; purpose notification records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a privacy notice template that clearly states collection purposes, data categories, and retention periods before any collection begins. 2. Implement a consent capture mechanism (signed form, checkbox with timestamp, or opt-in email) that records individual acknowledgement at point of collection. 3. Maintain a consent registry linking each individual's data type to their dated consent record and stated purpose.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide final privacy notice template and consent form approved for use. IT team: configure consent capture system with audit logs and retention settings. HR team: document the consent process for internal staff data collection.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: provide written confirmation of the specific purposes for which they will process data and commit to process only on documented instructions. Third-party integrations: supply privacy notices they will display to end-users on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed privacy notices or timestamped consent records for sample of individuals, consent registry or database export, approved privacy notice template, email confirmations of processor instruction documents.

key_requirements.text: Protect personal data with reasonable security arrangements against unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, or disposal
key_requirements.consequence: Financial penalty up to S$1M; public reprimand by PDPC
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security policy; risk assessment; encryption evidence; access control logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data mapping exercise to identify all systems, databases, and locations where personal data resides, then classify by sensitivity level. 2. Implement technical controls including encryption at rest and in transit, access controls with role-based permissions, multi-factor authentication, and regular patching. 3. Document and test incident response and data recovery procedures annually to ensure availability.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide data inventory spreadsheet, network architecture diagram, list of encryption standards in use, access control matrix, patch management logs for past 12 months, and disaster recovery test results. Security team: supply information security policy, penetration test reports, and vulnerability scan results. Finance team: confirm budget allocation for security tools and staff.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and hosting vendors: supply SOC 2 Type II reports, encryption specifications, backup and recovery procedures, and security incident notification procedures. Software vendors: confirm patch release cycles and provide vulnerability disclosure agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data inventory spreadsheet, encryption audit report, access control configuration screenshots, multi-factor authentication implementation proof, security policy document, recent penetration test and vulnerability scan reports, SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certificate (if applicable), disaster recovery test logs.

key_requirements.text: Notify PDPC within 3 days and affected individuals as soon as practicable of a data breach resulting in significant harm
key_requirements.consequence: Financial penalty; PDPC enforcement action
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach assessment records; PDPC notification receipts; individual notification evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data breach response plan defining significant harm criteria (e.g., identity theft risk, financial loss, reputational damage), roles, escalation procedures, and communication templates. 2. Configure automated monitoring and alerting for intrusion detection, access anomalies, and integrity checks; ensure logs are retained for forensic analysis. 3. Create notification templates for PDPC and affected individuals and conduct an annual tabletop drill to test detection and notification timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft breach notification templates for PDPC and individuals, document significant harm assessment criteria. IT team: configure security monitoring, provide intrusion detection logs and access logs from past 12 months, test alert systems. Incident response lead: develop and maintain breach response playbook with roles assigned and contact lists.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed security service providers: commit to detecting and alerting on breach indicators within defined timeframe. Cloud providers: provide logs and forensic data access procedures; commit to notifying organisation of suspected breaches within 24 hours.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented breach response plan with significant harm criteria, notification templates (PDPC and individual), monitoring system configuration and alert test results, sample intrusion detection and access logs, contact list and roles matrix, breach tabletop drill report, PDPC notification letter (if breach occurred during audit period).

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) and make their business contact details publicly available
key_requirements.consequence: PDPC advisory; financial penalty
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DPO appointment record; website/intranet contact details; DPO responsibilities document
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint a Data Protection Officer (internal or external) and assign clear accountability for PDPA compliance monitoring and individual inquiries. 2. Document the DPO's role, responsibilities, and independence in a policy or appointment letter; ensure they report to senior management. 3. Publish the DPO's email address and phone number on the company website, privacy policy, and internally accessible directory.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed DPO appointment letter or employment contract. Legal team: draft Data Protection Officer policy defining role, responsibilities, reporting line, and independence. Communications team: confirm DPO contact details are published on website footer, privacy policy, and internal intranet.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External DPO service provider (if applicable): provide service agreement specifying scope, availability, reporting structure, and indemnification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPO appointment letter or contract, Data Protection Officer role policy document, website footer or privacy policy page showing published contact details (screenshot), internal directory or organisational chart showing reporting line to senior management, DPO service agreement (if external).

regulations.penalties: Up to SGD 1 million or 10% of annual Singapore turnover (whichever is higher) for significant breaches

regulations.name: Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI) — 2022 Amendment
regulations.authority: Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC)
regulations.summary: Japan's comprehensive personal information protection law, substantially amended in April 2022 to strengthen individual rights, introduce opt-out requirements for third-party transfers, mandatory breach reporting, and extraterritorial application. APPI establishes a balanced framework aligning with GDPR while reflecting Japanese business practices.

key_requirements.text: Specify and disclose the purpose of use at or before the time of personal information collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a Purpose of Use statement for each data collection point (web forms, apps, in-store kiosks) specifying the exact business purpose, retention period, and any third-party sharing. 2. Deploy the statement visibly at collection time; for online channels, display it before data entry; for offline, include it on forms or signage. 3. Maintain version control and approval records showing Legal review and sign-off of all Purpose statements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain Purpose of Use statements for each collection channel and data category. Compliance team: create a Purpose Statement registry documenting collection points, statement text, deployment date, and responsible owner.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Marketing/Analytics vendors: provide written confirmation of data use purposes and retention policies; confirm no use beyond stated purposes or retain data only per contract terms.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Purpose of Use statements (in Japanese and English where applicable), screenshots of statements deployed on digital channels, Purpose Statement registry with approval dates, vendor data use confirmations in writing.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit opt-in consent before third-party disclosure of personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a third-party disclosure consent form that names each recipient (or recipient category), purpose, and data categories; require explicit checkbox or signature before any disclosure. 2. For existing disclosures, obtain retroactive written consent or suspend disclosure until consent is obtained. 3. Maintain a Disclosure Log recording consent date, recipient name, data categories, and purpose for each third party.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft third-party disclosure consent template compliant with APPI wording requirements. Compliance team: audit current vendor relationships and create a Third-Party Disclosure Log with consent dates and evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party service providers and partners: sign a Data Processing or Data Sharing Addendum confirming they will not re-disclose data without explicit written consent and will inform the organisation of any sub-processors.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms (one per third party or recipient category), Third-Party Disclosure Log with timestamps and recipient details, Data Processing Addendums or Data Sharing Agreements, audit trail of consent collection and storage.

key_requirements.text: Notify PPC and affected individuals when a breach meets the threshold (500+ records or sensitive data)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Breach Threshold Matrix defining what constitutes a reportable breach (500+ records, or any breach involving sensitive data such as health, biometric, or financial information). 2. Create an Incident Response Plan with escalation procedures: notify Legal/Compliance immediately, assess scope, prepare PPC and affected-individual notification within legally required timeframes. 3. Document the breach assessment, notification decisions, and communications in an Incident Log with dates and recipients.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide incident logs, forensic reports, and data access records confirming breach scope. Legal team: draft PPC and individual notification letters (in Japanese), assess legal timelines, and maintain signed records of all notifications sent.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide incident response procedures, commit to reporting security events within 24 hours, and supply forensic evidence (logs, screenshots, access records) upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Breach Threshold Matrix document, Incident Response Plan (signed by senior management), PPC notification letters with proof of delivery, individual breach notification letters, Incident Log entries with assessment date, scope, and notification timeline, forensic reports from vendors.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, and third-party disclosure suspension
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Data Subject Rights Request Process: create intake forms (online and offline) for access, correction, deletion, and disclosure suspension requests; set a 30-day response deadline. 2. Train staff and designate a Data Rights Coordinator to log requests, retrieve data, and document responses; implement a tracking spreadsheet. 3. Create audit records for each request showing receipt date, data provided or action taken, and response date.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Compliance team: establish Data Subject Rights Request intake procedures and assign a coordinator. IT team: provide data retrieval processes and confirm ability to delete or correct records; supply request fulfilment logs. Legal team: draft response templates for each right type.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party data processors: provide written confirmation that they will process deletion, correction, and suspension requests upon organisation instruction within 10 business days; supply vendor procedures for handling data subject requests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights Request intake forms and tracking log, response letters to requestors (showing date and action taken), IT deletion or correction records with timestamps, vendor Data Processing Addendums confirming request handling obligations, training records for staff.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers require consent or use of contractual safeguards with equivalent protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Cross-Border Data Transfer Inventory: document all transfers (to servers, cloud providers, vendors located outside Japan), recipient country, data categories, and legal basis (consent or contractual safeguards). 2. For each transfer, either obtain explicit consent from the data subject OR execute a Standard Contractual Clause (SCC) or Binding Corporate Rule (BCR) that ensures recipient countries have equivalent data protection. 3. Maintain signed transfer agreements and consent records with periodic compliance reviews.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Compliance team: audit all data flows and systems to identify cross-border transfers; document recipient countries and data categories. Legal team: draft or adopt Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for each non-Japanese recipient; maintain a Cross-Border Transfer Registry.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors receiving data outside Japan: sign a Data Processing Addendum incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses (Module Two or appropriate module); confirm data location, sub-processors, and security measures; commit to data localisation requirements if applicable.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-Border Data Transfer Inventory (listing recipient countries, data types, legal basis), signed Standard Contractual Clauses for each vendor, individual consent forms where consent is the legal basis, Data Processing Addendums with transfer clauses, periodic transfer compliance audit reports.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate security management measures proportionate to risk
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Data Security Risk Assessment to classify data by sensitivity (public, internal, confidential, restricted) and determine proportionate controls (encryption, access controls, backup, incident response). 2. Document security measures in a Security Baseline document addressing: encryption (in transit and at rest), access control policies, employee training, vulnerability management, and incident response. 3. Perform annual security audits and penetration testing; document findings and remediation in a Security Control Log.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: conduct and document a Data Security Risk Assessment, implement encryption on databases and file transfers, enable multi-factor authentication, maintain access logs. Security team: develop Security Baseline, conduct annual penetration testing, and maintain a vulnerability tracking system.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors handling personal data: provide annual SOC 2 Type II reports (or ISO 27001 certification), attest to encryption implementation, confirm multi-factor authentication, and commit to vulnerability disclosure and remediation SLAs.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Security Risk Assessment report, Security Baseline document (approved by CISO/IT Director), encryption configuration records, access control policies and logs, annual penetration test reports with remediation tracking, vendor SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certificates, security training records for employees, incident response plan with tabletop exercise results.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of third-party transfer and receipt of personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data transfer register documenting each instance of personal data shared with third parties, including recipient identity, data categories, transfer date, and legal basis.
2. Implement automated logging of data exports and API transfers to third-party systems, with timestamps and data volumes captured in audit trails.
3. Conduct quarterly reconciliation of transfer records against actual third-party service agreements and data processing purposes.
internal_actions: IT team: export system access logs and data transfer event logs for the past 24 months with sender, recipient, data classification, and timestamp fields.
Legal team: compile executed Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or vendor contracts for all third parties who have received personal data.
Compliance team: prepare a master data transfer register showing date, recipient name, data categories, and purpose for each transfer instance.
vendor_actions: All third-party recipients: provide signed receipt and acknowledgment forms confirming receipt of personal data, including data categories and volumes received.
Data processors: submit attestation letters confirming the dates and scope of personal data they received from your organisation.
Business partners: return completed data inventory questionnaires listing all personal data transfers received in the past 24 months.
compliance_evidence: Signed data transfer register or ledger with sequential entries, recipient details, and authorisation signatures.
System audit logs (unmodified exports) showing data export events with timestamps, user IDs, recipient systems, and data volumes.
Executed Data Processing Agreements or transfer agreements with each third-party recipient, signed and dated.
Third-party signed receipts or data receipt acknowledgment forms.
Quarterly reconciliation reports cross-referencing transfer logs against service agreements.

key_requirements.text: Foreign businesses that handle personal information of Japanese individuals are subject to APPI
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct an audit to identify all foreign entities (subsidiaries, parent companies, service providers) that handle personal information of Japanese residents, regardless of physical location.
2. Apply APPI requirements—including user rights, security safeguards, and transfer restrictions—to all such foreign entities through binding Data Processing Agreements and policies.
3. Document the legal jurisdictional analysis confirming APPI applicability for each foreign entity and the compliance framework imposed on them.
internal_actions: Data Governance team: prepare a map of all foreign subsidiaries, parent companies, and offshore service providers that process personal data of Japanese individuals, with business rationale and data flows.
Legal team: draft jurisdiction analysis memoranda for each foreign entity, citing APPI Article 2(1) and confirming applicability.
Compliance team: create a consolidated policy document extending APPI requirements (consent, security, breach notification, cross-border transfer rules) to all foreign entities.
vendor_actions: Foreign service providers: sign supplemental Data Processing Agreements explicitly incorporating APPI compliance obligations, including data security standards, user rights, and notification timelines.
Foreign subsidiaries and affiliates: certify in writing their APPI compliance status and commit to implementing specified security controls and user consent mechanisms.
Overseas cloud and SaaS providers: provide Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent legal mechanisms that permit lawful transfer of Japanese personal data under APPI constraints.
compliance_evidence: Completed data flow mapping document showing all foreign entities processing Japanese resident data, with business purposes and data categories.
Legal jurisdictional analysis memoranda for each foreign entity, with APPI applicability determination signed by legal counsel.
Executed Data Processing Agreements with all foreign entities, incorporating APPI Article 27 (security), Article 23 (user rights), and Article 28 (transfer restrictions).
Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent international transfer agreements signed by foreign recipients.
Policy document formally extending APPI requirements to foreign entities, approved by board or executive committee.

regulations.penalties: Up to JPY 100 million corporate fine; up to 1 year imprisonment for individuals for unlawful use

regulations.name: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023
regulations.authority: Data Protection Board of India
regulations.summary: India's landmark data protection legislation enacted in August 2023. Applies a consent-based framework for processing personal data of Indian residents. Establishes the Data Protection Board as the enforcement authority, introduces significant financial penalties, and creates obligations for Data Fiduciaries (controllers) including Significant Data Fiduciaries. Implementing rules are being developed.

key_requirements.text: Obtain free, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent before processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft consent templates that clearly separate consent requests by processing purpose (marketing, service delivery, analytics) and obtain explicit opt-in for each. 2. Implement a consent management system (CMS) to capture, timestamp, and store consent records with proof of user acceptance. 3. Audit all data collection touchpoints to ensure no pre-ticked boxes or forced consent mechanisms exist.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide drafted consent forms for all processing activities; IT team: configure CMS and provide access audit reports; Marketing/Product teams: identify all data collection touchpoints and current consent mechanisms.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors processing personal data: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPA) confirming they process data only on documented instructions and obtain no independent consent.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent records with timestamps, consent management system audit logs, Data Processing Agreements with all vendors, screenshots of consent UI showing no pre-selections.

key_requirements.text: Provide clear notice of purpose, data collected, and individual rights before seeking consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a Privacy Notice document that lists the specific purposes of processing, categories of data collected, retention periods, and data principal rights (access, correction, erasure, grievance). 2. Integrate the notice into all collection forms, app privacy sections, and website footers in plain, understandable language. 3. Maintain version control and deploy notice updates 30 days before material changes take effect.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft comprehensive Privacy Notice and maintain version log; IT team: deploy notice to all digital touchpoints and provide screenshots of deployment; Compliance team: create communication log for notice updates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party platforms and service providers: confirm receipt of Privacy Notice requirements and provide written acknowledgment of their obligation to display notices where they collect data on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Current Privacy Notice document with effective date, screenshots of notice deployment across all channels, version history with dates, vendor acknowledgment letters.

key_requirements.text: Honour data principal rights: access, correction, erasure, and grievance redressal
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented Data Subject Rights Procedure specifying timelines (typically 30 days) and process for handling access, correction, erasure, and portability requests. 2. Implement a ticketing system to log all requests, assign ownership, track completion, and maintain audit trails. 3. Conduct quarterly audits to verify requests are processed within SLA and individuals receive required responses in the stated format.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: finalize Data Subject Rights Procedure and SLA documentation; IT team: configure request tracking system and provide access logs; Operations team: assign staff to manage requests and provide completion reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors storing or processing personal data: provide contractual commitment to support access and erasure requests within 10 business days and confirm technical capability to retrieve or delete data upon instruction.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights Procedure document, request tracking system logs (sample of 10 recent requests), signed vendor commitments, completion certificates for fulfilled requests with response dates.

key_requirements.text: Report personal data breaches to the Data Protection Board and affected individuals
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Breach Response Plan documenting detection, investigation, and notification procedures; define breach as unauthorized access, loss, or unauthorized disclosure of personal data. 2. Implement monitoring controls (log aggregation, intrusion detection, access reviews) to detect breaches within 72 hours and maintain incident logs. 3. Prepare notification templates for the Data Protection Board and affected individuals, and maintain a Breach Register with details of each incident, affected data principals, and remediation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: deploy breach detection tools and provide incident logs; Legal team: prepare notification templates and board communication drafts; IT team: configure alerting and provide evidence of monitoring coverage; Compliance team: maintain Breach Register with breach assessments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and cloud providers: commit contractually to notify you of any suspected breach within 24 hours and provide forensic evidence, access logs, and remediation plans.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Breach Response Plan document, monitoring tool configuration and alert logs, Breach Register with incident summaries, Data Protection Board notification copies, breach notification letters sent to affected individuals with dates.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for Significant Data Fiduciaries
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Assess whether your organization qualifies as a Significant Data Fiduciary (SDFs process large volumes of sensitive data or profiling activities); if yes, formally appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) with independence and adequate resources. 2. Document the DPO appointment in writing, assign reporting lines directly to senior management or the board, and ensure the DPO has authority to audit, train, and oversee compliance. 3. Publish the DPO contact details on your website and inform the Data Protection Board of the appointment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: prepare DPO job description and appointment letter; Finance team: allocate budget for DPO team and tools; Board/CEO: approve DPO appointment and assign reporting structure; IT/Legal: provide DPO with systems access and authority.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: No specific vendor actions required; however, external DPO providers must provide signed Service Level Agreements (SLA) confirming independence, availability, and compliance with DPDPA requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DPO appointment letter with board approval, DPO job description defining authority and independence, organizational chart showing reporting line to board/CEO, website page listing DPO contact details, Data Protection Board notification (if required).

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for Significant Data Fiduciaries
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. For each Significant Data Processing activity, conduct a formal Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documenting the purpose, data categories, processing methods, identified risks (unauthorized access, profiling, tracking), and mitigation controls. 2. Prioritize assessments for high-risk activities (biometric processing, automated decision-making, data sharing with third parties) and update DPIAs annually or when processing changes materially. 3. Review and approve all DPIAs through the DPO or compliance officer to ensure identified risks are adequately controlled.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/DPO team: develop DPIA template and conduct assessments for all material processing activities; IT/Security team: provide technical details of data flows, storage, encryption, and access controls; Product/Legal teams: document processing purpose and retention periods; Board: review and approve high-risk DPIAs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide risk assessments for their processing activities and confirm technical and organizational safeguards in place; cloud providers: submit security certifications (ISO 27001) and data handling documentation to support your DPIA.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed DPIA documents for all material processing activities (minimum 3), DPIA approval sign-offs, vendor risk assessments and certifications, evidence of annual DPIA updates, Data Protection Board submission (if required for high-risk processing).

key_requirements.text: Data localisation requirements for certain categories — to be defined by government
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Monitor official gazette notifications and MeitY/DPA guidance to identify which data categories require localisation within Indian borders.
2. Conduct a data flow audit to map all personal data currently stored, processed, or transmitted across geographies and classify by category.
3. Implement technical controls (encryption, access restrictions, data residency rules in cloud contracts) to ensure localised data does not leave Indian territory without authorisation.
internal_actions: IT team: provide current data inventory and storage location map (on-premise, cloud regions, third-party hosts); document any cross-border data flows.
Legal team: maintain a register tracking all government notifications defining localisation-restricted categories and update annually.
Cloud/Infrastructure team: provide evidence of data centre locations, contractual residency clauses, and backup/DR site locations.
vendor_actions: Cloud service providers: sign amended Data Processing Agreements (DPA) confirming data residency in India-only data centres for identified categories.
Backup and disaster recovery vendors: provide written certification of storage locations and confirm no replication outside Indian territory.
Third-party processors: submit attestation confirming compliance with localisation requirements and audit rights for location verification.
compliance_evidence: Signed amendments to vendor contracts specifying India-only data residency with audit rights.
Data flow diagram and inventory spreadsheet mapping all personal data categories to storage/processing locations.
Government notification tracker document with dates and affected data categories.
Audit reports from external auditors confirming data localisation compliance (e.g. SOC 2 Type II with residency attestation, or third-party verification of data centre locations).

key_requirements.text: Restrictions on processing children's data (under 18) without parental consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Define 'child' (under 18) in internal data processing policies and establish a parental consent workflow requiring verifiable parental permission before collecting or processing any child personal data.

2. Implement age-verification controls at point of collection (e.g. date-of-birth field, email domain checks for educational institutions) and flag records flagged as under-18 in your data management system.
3. Create a parental consent registry documenting guardian identity, consent date, scope of processing, and retain records for the duration of data retention plus 3 years.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve a 'Children's Data Processing Policy' defining restricted processing categories, consent requirements, and retention limits.
Product/UX team: design and document age-verification mechanisms and provide screenshots of parental consent capture flows.
IT team: configure database flags or fields to tag child records; export sample parental consent records and demonstrate search/audit capabilities.
Data Protection Officer (DPO) / Compliance team: maintain a parental consent registry with audit trail showing consent capture, verification, and withdrawal requests.
vendor_actions: Marketing/analytics vendors: provide written confirmation that child data will not be used for profiling, targeted advertising, or sold to third parties without documented parental consent.
Email/communication service providers: confirm they will honour opt-out or parental withdrawal requests within 24 hours and provide logs of such actions.
Any third-party processor handling child data: sign a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with explicit clauses limiting processing to stated purposes and prohibiting secondary use.
compliance_evidence: Signed and approved Children's Data Processing Policy document.
Screenshots and functional test results of age-verification and parental consent capture mechanisms.
Sample parental consent forms (digital or hardcopy) with guardian signatures, child name, DOB, and dated consent approval.
Parental consent registry export showing at least 10–20 child records with consent dates and scope; audit log showing any consent withdrawals or updates.
Signed Data Processing Agreements with third-party vendors containing child-data restrictions.
Internal audit or DPO report confirming age-verification controls are functioning and parental consent is being collected before processing.

regulations.penalties: Up to INR 250 crore (approx USD 30 million) per violation; higher for Significant Data Fiduciaries

milestones.label: Rules notified by Ministry of IT

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) — 2023 Amendment
regulations.authority: Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC)
regulations.summary: South Korea's comprehensive data protection law enforced by the Personal Information Protection Commission. The 2023 amendment introduced extraterritorial jurisdiction for foreign businesses processing Korean residents' data, mobile-aligned data portability rights, strengthened breach notification, and pseudonymisation provisions aligned with GDPR principles.

key_requirements.text: Collect personal information only for a specified and legitimate purpose with individual consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the specified purpose for each data collection point (e.g. customer onboarding, billing, marketing) in a Purpose Register. 2. Obtain explicit written consent from data subjects before collection, with consent records retained for audit. 3. Train staff on approved purposes and prohibit use outside documented scope.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Purpose Register listing all collection activities and their lawful bases. HR team: confirm completion of consent and purpose training for all personnel handling personal data.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide signed Data Processing Addendum (DPA) confirming their permitted use of personal data is limited to stated purposes only.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or logs, Purpose Register with date-stamped entries, training completion certificates, signed DPAs with vendors, audit trail of consent collection.

key_requirements.text: Limit collection to minimum personal information necessary for the purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data minimization audit: map each collection point and identify which fields are strictly necessary for the stated purpose. 2. Remove or mark as optional all non-essential fields in forms and systems. 3. Document the minimization rationale for each retained field in a Data Inventory.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide current database schema and collection forms showing all fields collected. Legal team: sign off on the Data Inventory identifying which fields are necessary versus unnecessary.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Technology vendors: provide confirmation that systems can support optional field collection and that non-essential data can be excluded from data flows.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Inventory with necessity justifications, before-and-after screenshots of forms, system configuration records, vendor confirmation letters.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals and PIPC of personal data breaches without delay
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Breach Response Plan with defined notification timelines (notify PIPC and affected individuals without delay, typically within 72 hours). 2. Create an incident logging system and assign incident response lead with escalation authority. 3. Draft pre-approved breach notification templates in Korean for regulators and data subjects.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: finalize Breach Response Plan and notification templates in Korean. IT team: configure incident logging system with automatic alert triggers. Security team: conduct tabletop breach simulation and document response times.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign commitment to notify your organization of any breach affecting your data within 24 hours, with audit trail obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Breach Response Plan, incident logs with timestamps, notification templates (Korean versions), PIPC notification receipts, evidence of timely notifications sent, breach simulation report.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, and processing restriction
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Deploy or configure a Data Subject Rights portal or ticketing system enabling individuals to submit access, correction, deletion, and restriction requests with audit trails. 2. Establish a Service Level Agreement (SLA) for responding to requests (typically 30 days under PIPA). 3. Document and train staff on the verification procedure to confirm requester identity before fulfilling requests.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: implement or configure Data Subject Rights portal with request logging and status tracking. Legal team: publish SLA and response procedures. HR team: deliver training to all staff processing rights requests.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Technology service providers: provide technical capability to support data access exports, correction workflows, and deletion/anonymization functions; sign confirmation of compliance with response timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights portal screenshots, SLA document with sign-off, request log with timestamps and resolution records, staff training records, vendor technical capability statements.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Privacy Protection Officer (CPO) responsible for data management
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally appoint or hire a Chief Privacy Officer (CPO) and issue a signed appointment letter defining responsibilities for data governance, breach response, and PIPC liaison. 2. Register the CPO's name and contact details with PIPC as required. 3. Assign the CPO explicit authority to audit data handling, approve new processing activities, and escalate incidents.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide signed CPO appointment letter and organizational chart showing reporting line. Legal team: draft CPO Role and Responsibilities document. Finance team: confirm budget allocation for CPO function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External CPO consultants (if outsourced): provide signed Engagement Agreement confirming authority to represent the organization to PIPC and conduct audits of vendors.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed CPO appointment letter, PIPC registration confirmation, CPO Role and Responsibilities document, org chart, signed consultant engagement agreement (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Implement technical and physical security measures appropriate to risk
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Risk Assessment identifying data sensitivity (personal ID, financial, health, etc.) and map appropriate technical controls (encryption at rest/in transit, access controls, network segmentation). 2. Implement controls: e.g. AES-256 encryption for databases, TLS for transmission, multi-factor authentication, role-based access controls. 3. Conduct annual penetration testing and vulnerability scans; document remediation of findings.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: provide Risk Assessment report and matrix of controls mapped to data sensitivity. IT team: provide system hardening documentation, encryption certificates, access control matrices, firewall rules. Facilities team: document physical security measures (locks, cameras, badge access).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: provide SOC 2 Type II audit report, encryption certifications, and signed commitment to maintain specified security standards. Third-party tools: provide security test results and vulnerability disclosure policies.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Risk Assessment, system architecture diagrams with security controls labeled, encryption configuration exports, penetration test reports with remediation logs, SOC 2 certifications, access control audit logs, physical security photos/inventory.

key_requirements.text: Obtain separate consent and notify PIPC before transferring personal data abroad
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data transfer impact assessment identifying all cross-border flows, recipient countries, and legal bases for each transfer. 2. Obtain explicit written consent from data subjects prior to any international transfer, documenting consent date, scope, and recipient jurisdiction. 3. File a Data Transfer Notification with the Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) at least 10 business days before the first transfer, including recipient country adequacy assessment and safeguard mechanisms.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: prepare data transfer impact assessment and PIPC notification documentation. Compliance team: design and implement consent collection mechanism with timestamp records. IT team: configure data transfer logging to capture destination IP, recipient entity, and transfer timestamp.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party recipient: provide signed Data Processing Agreement confirming adherence to Korean PIPA standards, receipt country privacy laws, and commitment to equivalent data protection measures. Cloud provider or outsourcer: submit written certification of their jurisdiction's data protection adequacy or approved contractual safeguards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed individual consent forms with dates and scopes; PIPC submission confirmation receipt; Data Processing Agreement with non-Korean recipients; transfer impact assessment report; audit logs showing all cross-border data movements with recipient identification and timestamps.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of processing activities and disposal of personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Processing Activity Register documenting purpose, category, retention period, and security measures for each dataset, updated quarterly. 2. Implement automated data lifecycle management with scheduled disposal triggers, generating disposal certificates upon completion. 3. Maintain centralized disposal records including date, method, data category, and authorized approver signature for regulatory inspection.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: deploy data classification and lifecycle management tools; export monthly processing activity logs and quarterly disposal execution reports. Legal team: define retention schedules and disposal procedures in writing. Records management: establish secure archive for disposal certificates and maintain disposal register with supporting evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Managed service provider or data processor: provide monthly attestation of data processing activities performed on your behalf, including volume, categories, and retention compliance. Disposal service contractor: furnish signed disposal certificates with method description, witness confirmation, and timestamp for each disposal engagement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Processing Activity Register covering all datasets; documented retention schedule by data category; monthly disposal certificates signed by authorized personnel; audit trail logs showing data deletion timestamps and methods; third-party processor attestation statements; disposal reconciliation reports matching register entries to evidence.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to KRW 30 billion or 3% of annual global revenue; up to 2 years imprisonment for intentional violations

regulations.name: Thailand Personal Data Protection Act 2019
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Committee (PDPC)
regulations.summary: Thailand's GDPR-inspired data protection law, effective for all sectors from June 2022 after COVID-related delays. Requires consent-based processing, data subject rights, DPO appointment for certain controllers, breach notification within 72 hours, and cross-border transfer protections.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit consent from data subjects before processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a consent form template that clearly states the purpose and scope of data processing, and obtain signed or digital acceptance before any personal data collection begins. 2. Implement a consent management system to record when, how, and from whom consent was obtained, with timestamps and audit trails. 3. Establish a process to refresh consent annually or when processing purposes materially change.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide draft consent templates and consent refresh schedule. IT team: configure consent management system with audit logging capabilities. Compliance team: document consent workflows for each data collection channel (web forms, in-person, phone).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) confirming they process data only on documented instructions and with explicit consent; provide evidence of their consent management practices if they directly collect data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or digital consent records with timestamps, consent management system audit logs showing all collection events, DPAs with third parties, and documented consent refresh records for the past 12 months.

key_requirements.text: Inform data subjects of the purpose, type, and use of data collection at the time of collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a standardized Privacy Notice in Thai and English that lists the data controller, purpose, data types, recipients, and retention period, and display it at the point of collection or in the collection mechanism. 2. Document all data collection touchpoints (website forms, registration kiosks, customer service calls) and map each to a specific Privacy Notice version. 3. Maintain a central register of all active Privacy Notices with version numbers, approval dates, and deployment locations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft comprehensive Privacy Notices for each data collection scenario. Communications team: ensure notices are prominently displayed and accessible in plain language. Compliance team: maintain a register of all notices and their deployment status with evidence of placement.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data collectors: provide copies of any Privacy Notices they use on your behalf or confirm they will use the organization's approved notice; sign agreements to use only approved notices.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Privacy Notice templates with version control and deployment dates, screenshots or logs of notice placement on websites and applications, records of notices provided during in-person or telephone collection, and vendor agreements confirming use of approved notices.

key_requirements.text: Notify the PDPC and data subjects of data breaches within 72 hours of becoming aware
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
