key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Data Breach Response Plan that defines roles, escalation timelines, and notification procedures; designate a breach response lead and supporting team. 2. Implement automated logging and monitoring tools to detect unauthorized access or data anomalies, with alerts triggered within 24 hours of detection. 3. Create a breach notification template in Thai and English, and test the notification process annually to ensure notifications to the PDPC and affected subjects can be sent within 72 hours.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: deploy data loss prevention and access monitoring tools with alerting; provide monthly logs of detected incidents and response times. Security team: conduct quarterly breach simulation drills and document response timelines. Legal team: maintain breach register and notification templates. Compliance team: oversee breach reporting and maintain evidence of all notifications sent.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and managed service vendors: provide Service Level Agreements (SLAs) committing to incident notification within 24 hours and availability of forensic logs; sign contractual clauses requiring them to notify the organization of any suspected breaches.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Data Breach Response Plan with defined roles and 72-hour timelines, monitoring tool configuration and sample alert logs, breach register showing date of discovery, notification date to PDPC, and evidence of communications sent to affected subjects within 72 hours (email records, SMS logs, or formal letters), and breach drill test reports.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, portability, rectification, erasure, and restriction
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a Data Subject Rights Request procedure that specifies how requests for access, portability, rectification, erasure, or restriction are received, logged, and fulfilled within 30 days (or legally required timeframe). 2. Implement a request management system with tracking that records receipt date, request type, and fulfillment date for each subject request. 3. Train relevant teams (customer service, IT, legal) on how to handle each right category, including technical steps for data export and deletion.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document the Data Subject Rights Request procedure with decision trees for each right type and exemptions. IT team: develop capabilities for data export, secure deletion, and redaction; provide training on technical fulfillment steps. Customer Service team: document request intake procedures and provide monthly reports of all requests received and their status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide contractual commitments to assist in fulfilling subject rights requests within agreed timeframes (typically 10 business days); confirm technical capabilities for data export and deletion on request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Approved Data Subject Rights Request procedure, signed vendor agreements with fulfillment timeframes, request management system logs showing all requests received, fulfillment dates, and evidence of responses (export files provided, rectifications made, deletion confirmations), and sample compliance letters sent to data subjects.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer if processing large-scale or sensitive data
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Assess whether your organization meets the PDPA threshold for DPO appointment: typically when processing large-scale sensitive personal data (health, biometric, financial) or performing regular systematic monitoring. 2. If required, recruit or designate an individual with data protection expertise as DPO, ensure they report directly to the board or a C-level executive, and provide them with adequate budget and authority. 3. Register the DPO with the PDPC as required and establish an annual role review process to confirm independence and competence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: post and fill DPO role with required qualifications; provide employment contract and reporting structure. Finance team: allocate budget for DPO salary, training, and tools. Board/Executive team: document DPO appointment decision and confirm independence. Compliance team: maintain DPO competency records and conduct annual review.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External DPO service provider (if outsourced): provide Service Agreement confirming reporting independence, availability, and professional indemnity insurance; sign terms ensuring they advise the organization on PDPA compliance and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or executive decision confirming DPO appointment, DPO employment contract or external service agreement, DPO registration confirmation from PDPC (if required), DPO competency records and training logs, and annual DPO role review documentation.

key_requirements.text: Ensure cross-border data transfers are to adequate countries or use contractual protections
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all cross-border data transfers and identify destination countries; verify whether each destination country has been deemed adequate by Thailand or is an approved organization (e.g., certified under international frameworks). 2. For transfers to non-adequate countries, execute a Standard Contractual Clause (SCC) or Binding Corporate Rule (BCR) that includes mandatory data protection safeguards equivalent to PDPA. 3. Document all transfer mechanisms in a Transfer Register with destination, transfer mechanism, data types, and approval date.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: conduct cross-border transfer impact assessment and identify all destination countries; draft and execute SCCs or BCRs. IT team: map data flows and system configurations supporting cross-border transfers; provide documentation of technical safeguards (encryption, access controls). Compliance team: maintain Transfer Register with current approval status and evidence of contractual protections.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and international vendors: sign Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or SCCs that govern cross-border transfers; provide a Data Transfer Impact Assessment and confirm use of encryption in transit and at rest. Confirm they do not transfer data to non-approved third countries without explicit authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed cross-border transfer impact assessments, Transfer Register documenting all transfers with destination countries and mechanism (SCC/BCR/adequacy), signed SCCs or BCRs for each non-adequate country transfer, vendor DPAs with cross-border transfer clauses, and technical safeguards documentation (encryption certificates, access control logs).

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of processing activities (ROPA)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a central repository (spreadsheet, database, or DPIA tool) documenting all personal data processing activities across the organisation, including purposes, data categories, recipients, and retention periods.
2. Assign responsibility to Data Protection Officer or Privacy Lead to maintain and update ROPA records at least quarterly or whenever processing activities change.
3. Ensure ROPA covers both internal processing and third-party processor arrangements, with clear identification of controller vs. processor roles.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft ROPA template aligned with PDPA 2019 Schedule 5 requirements.
IT team: provide inventory of all systems and databases handling personal data, including data flows and access controls.
Department heads: complete ROPA questionnaires detailing their processing activities, purposes, and data categories.
Finance team: identify all contracts with data processors and confirm processor liability clauses.
vendor_actions: Data processors: provide written confirmation of processing scope, duration, nature, and purpose per PDPA Article 24 processor agreements.
Vendors: supply documentation of sub-processor arrangements and any data transfers outside Thailand.
Service providers: confirm data retention periods and deletion procedures in writing.
compliance_evidence: Completed and signed ROPA covering all personal data processing activities.
Data inventory spreadsheet or tool export showing processing purposes, data categories, recipients, and retention schedules.
Signed Data Processing Agreements (DPA) with all third-party processors naming controller and processor.
ROPA update log with dates and names of authorised personnel performing reviews.
Minutes from quarterly ROPA review meetings demonstrating ongoing maintenance.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Define and document criteria for 'high-risk' processing activities (e.g., large-scale collection, automated decision-making, use of sensitive data, overseas transfers) aligned with PDPA Article 26.
2. Conduct DPIA for each identified high-risk activity using a structured template, documenting the risk assessment, mitigation measures, and residual risks.
3. Obtain approval from Data Protection Officer or compliance authority before proceeding with high-risk processing, and retain DPIA reports for audit.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: establish DPIA policy and decision tree for identifying high-risk processing.
IT team: provide technical specifications, security controls, and data flow diagrams for systems handling sensitive or high-volume personal data.
Business/Operations teams: complete DPIA questionnaires describing processing scope, lawful basis, data subjects affected, and intended uses.
Risk Management team: assess likelihood and impact of data breaches, unauthorised access, or data subject rights violations for each activity.
vendor_actions: Data processors and vendors: provide security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) and technical/organisational security measures documentation.
Cloud/hosting providers: confirm encryption, access controls, audit logs, and incident response procedures in a security questionnaire.
Third parties: disclose any sub-processors, data locations, and cross-border transfer mechanisms subject to DPIA.
compliance_evidence: Signed DPIA reports completed for each high-risk processing activity, including risk identification, mitigation actions, and residual risk assessment.
DPIA policy document defining high-risk processing criteria and approval workflows.
Data flow diagrams and system security assessments attached to DPIAs.
Approval sign-off from Data Protection Officer or nominated compliance authority with date and signature.
Register of completed DPIAs showing activity name, risk level, completion date, and approval status.
Evidence of vendor security assessments and processor DPA addenda addressing identified risks.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines up to THB 5 million; criminal penalties up to 1 year imprisonment for certain offences

regulations.name: Malaysia Personal Data Protection Act 2010
regulations.authority: Department of Personal Data Protection (JPDP)
regulations.summary: Malaysia's Personal Data Protection Act 2010 governs the processing of personal data in commercial transactions. Based on seven data protection principles. 2024 amendments introduced mandatory security breach notification and enhanced requirements for data processor contracts.

key_requirements.text: Process personal data only with consent or on another lawful basis
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: Audit all data processing activities and document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests) for each. 2. Compliance team: Create a data processing register mapping each processing activity to its documented legal basis. 3. IT/Operations teams: Implement technical controls to block processing outside approved lawful bases.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: Provide lawful basis assessment document for all processing activities; Compliance team: Deliver completed data processing register with lawful basis justification for each entry; IT team: Confirm implementation of role-based access controls limiting processing to authorised purposes only.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: Submit signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) confirming processing only occurs on documented instructions; Consent management platform vendors: Provide technical specification confirming consent capture and storage audit trails.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Processing Register with lawful basis column, signed DPAs with all processors, consent management system audit logs, internal legal memoranda documenting lawful basis analysis for high-risk processing.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals of the purpose of data processing at point of collection
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Marketing/Operations teams: Audit all data collection touchpoints (forms, websites, call centres, in-store) and identify where privacy notices are currently absent or incomplete. 2. Legal team: Draft or update privacy notices to clearly state purpose, data categories, retention period, and recipient organisation in plain language. 3. Product/IT teams: Integrate privacy notices at point of collection (web forms, app signup, paper forms) and document the implementation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Marketing team: Provide inventory of all data collection channels and current notice content; Legal team: Deliver finalised privacy notice template(s) compliant with PDPA requirements; IT team: Confirm deployment of notices on all digital collection points with screenshot evidence and version control.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Web platform/CRM vendors: Provide technical capability to display and update notices; Confirm notice content is captured and timestamped at collection point.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Privacy notice templates (signed by Legal), screenshots of notices on all collection channels dated, website accessibility audit showing notice placement, call centre script excerpts containing notice language, paper form samples with notice printed.

key_requirements.text: Implement security measures to protect personal data from loss, misuse, or unauthorised access
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT/Security team: Conduct a data security risk assessment identifying all personal data repositories, access points, and vulnerabilities; document findings in a Data Security Risk Register. 2. IT team: Implement or enhance technical controls (encryption at rest and in transit, access logging, MFA, network segmentation) aligned to risk assessment findings. 3. Compliance team: Document all implemented controls in a Security Measures Inventory and conduct annual security testing (penetration testing, vulnerability scanning).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: Provide Data Security Risk Register, network architecture diagram, encryption inventory, access control matrix, and results of annual vulnerability scans and penetration tests; Compliance team: Deliver Security Measures Inventory document mapping risks to controls.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: Submit current SOC 2 Type II report or ISO 27001 certificate; provide data centre security specification; confirm encryption standard (minimum AES-256) and backup security procedures. Third-party security tools: Provide product security datasheet and commitment to vulnerability disclosure.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Security Risk Register, Security Measures Inventory, encryption certificates/settings export, access control logs for 90-day sample period, latest vulnerability scan report (dated within 12 months), penetration test report (dated within 12 months), SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certificates from vendors.

key_requirements.text: Notify JPDP of data security breaches (mandatory under 2024 amendment)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT/Security team: Establish a data breach detection and logging system; define breach severity criteria (high/medium/low) and document decision criteria for JPDP notification threshold. 2. Compliance/Legal team: Draft a Breach Notification Procedure detailing reporting timelines (notification to JPDP without undue delay, to affected individuals within 30 days if likely to cause harm) and required information (data categories, number of individuals, probable impact). 3. Incident Response team: Designate a breach coordinator and conduct annual tabletop exercise simulating breach notification workflow.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: Provide breach detection system documentation and 12-month breach log (if any); Compliance team: Deliver signed Breach Notification Procedure; Incident Response team: Confirm breach coordinator appointment and provide tabletop exercise report with attendees and outcomes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Incident response/forensics vendors: Provide engagement SLA confirming response time and deliverables format; JPDP liaison consultant (if used): Confirm availability for breach notification drafting and submission.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Breach Notification Procedure (approved by Legal/Compliance), breach detection system configuration proof, incident response playbook, breach coordinator contact details and training records, proof of JPDP notification(s) submitted (copy of JPDP submission receipt if breach occurred), affected individual notification records (email/letter with date sent), tabletop exercise report.

key_requirements.text: Register as a data user with JPDP if processing in regulated sectors
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal/Compliance team: Determine if your organisation is a 'data user' under PDPA and if processing falls within regulated sectors (finance, healthcare, telecommunications, or large-scale commercial data processing). 2. Compliance team: If registration required, prepare the JPDP registration application form with organisational details, processing activities, security measures, and data protection officer designation. 3. Submit application to JPDP and maintain registration status; monitor for renewal deadlines and any JPDP regulatory notices.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: Provide Data User status assessment memo and sector classification analysis; Compliance team: Prepare and track JPDP registration application form with supporting documents; Finance team: Confirm registration fee payment and retain receipt.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: JPDP or local legal advisor: Confirm registration requirement and advise on application completeness; process registration submission if outsourced.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data User status assessment document signed by Legal, JPDP registration application form (executed copy), JPDP registration certificate (once issued), fee payment receipt, communication from JPDP confirming registration or confirmation of exemption.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access and correction of personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal/Compliance team: Document the organisation's process for handling Subject Access Requests (SARs) and correction requests, including response timeline (within 21 days of request or earlier where practicable), procedures for verifying identity, and escalation path. 2. IT/Operations teams: Implement a request tracking system and ensure all data custodians (HR, Finance, Customer Service, IT) can retrieve personal data held on an individual within the timeline. 3. Compliance team: Conduct annual testing by submitting test SAR internally and measuring response time compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: Provide Subject Access Request Procedure and Correction Request Procedure (both signed); IT team: Confirm SAR tracking system is deployed and functional; provide list of data custodians and their retrieval responsibilities; HR/Finance/Customer Service: Provide sample of historical SAR responses (redacted) demonstrating compliance with timelines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: Confirm in DPA their obligation to assist with SAR responses and provide data within specified timeline; SAR management platform vendors (if used): Provide system documentation and audit trail evidence.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Subject Access Request Procedure and Correction Request Procedure, SAR tracking system configuration/screenshot, audit report of SAR response times over past 12 months, sample of 10 SAR responses with timestamps (from receipt to dispatch), internal SAR test results, evidence of data custodian training on SAR obligations.

key_requirements.text: Ensure cross-border data transfers are to approved countries or with contractual protections
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all countries to which personal data is transferred and cross-reference against the approved list maintained by the Personal Data Protection Commissioner (PDPC), or verify Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are in place for non-approved destinations.
2. Document the legal basis for each cross-border transfer (approved country status or contractual protection mechanism) and maintain a register of all international data flows.
3. Conduct Data Transfer Impact Assessments (DTIA) for vendors operating in non-approved jurisdictions and document risk mitigation measures in data processing agreements.
internal_actions: Legal team: prepare and maintain a register of all cross-border data transfers with destination countries, transfer mechanisms, and approval dates.
Compliance team: develop and enforce a cross-border transfer approval procedure requiring review before any new international data movement.
IT team: provide system documentation identifying all data repositories and their geographic locations.
vendor_actions: Vendors: provide written confirmation of their data storage locations and agree to Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) where transfers occur to non-approved countries.
Vendors: sign a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) that explicitly addresses cross-border transfer mechanisms and jurisdictional restrictions.
Vendors: provide evidence of compliance with data localisation requirements if applicable to the vendor's jurisdiction.
compliance_evidence: Cross-border data transfer register listing destination countries, transfer dates, and approved mechanisms (country status or SCC reference).
Signed Data Processing Agreements with all vendors that include cross-border transfer clauses and Standard Contractual Clauses where applicable.
Data Transfer Impact Assessment reports documenting risk analysis and mitigation for non-approved country transfers.
Internal approval records and sign-off from Legal/Compliance teams for each cross-border transfer arrangement.

key_requirements.text: Retain personal data only as long as necessary and ensure secure disposal
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish and document a data retention schedule for each personal data category, specifying the retention period justified by business or legal necessity, and the secure disposal method.
2. Implement automated deletion or anonymisation processes in IT systems to dispose of data when retention periods expire, with audit logging of all disposal actions.
3. Conduct annual data inventory audits to identify and remove obsolete personal data, documenting the disposal method (secure deletion, incineration, encryption key destruction) for each batch.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: develop and approve a Data Retention and Disposal Policy specifying retention periods for each data type and justification based on business or legal requirements.
IT team: configure automated data deletion/purging rules in all systems holding personal data and provide quarterly reports of executed disposals with timestamps and record counts.
IT team: implement encryption key management procedures ensuring keys are destroyed when encrypted personal data reaches end of retention period.
Audit/Compliance team: conduct and document annual data inventory audits identifying orphaned or expired personal data and authorise disposal.
vendor_actions: Vendors: provide a detailed Data Retention and Disposal Schedule aligned with the organisation's policy and contractually commit to dispose of data within 30 days of contract termination or data expiration.
Vendors: sign a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) containing explicit disposal obligations and agree to provide written certification of secure disposal upon request.
Vendors: document and provide evidence of their disposal methods (e.g. certified data destruction certificates, incineration reports) for personal data held on the organisation's behalf.
compliance_evidence: Approved Data Retention and Disposal Policy document clearly specifying retention periods and disposal methods for each personal data category with business justification.
System configuration documentation showing automated deletion rules, retention period settings, and disposal triggers in all data repositories.
Quarterly IT disposal reports with transaction logs showing the number of records deleted, deletion date, and disposal method per system.
Signed Data Processing Agreements with all vendors containing data disposal obligations and termination protocols.
Annual data inventory audit reports documenting personal data holdings, identification of expired data, and authorisation for disposal.
Certified disposal evidence such as data destruction certificates from third-party vendors, encryption key destruction logs, or incineration confirmations.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to MYR 500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for violations

regulations.name: Philippines Data Privacy Act of 2012
regulations.authority: National Privacy Commission (NPC)
regulations.summary: The Philippines Data Privacy Act 2012 (Republic Act 10173) establishes comprehensive data protection obligations for government agencies and private entities processing personal information. Modeled on EU principles, it requires DPO registration with the NPC, breach notification within 72 hours, and implements data subject rights.

key_requirements.text: Process personal information lawfully, fairly, and with consent or legal basis
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, public task, or legitimate interest) for each category of personal information processed. 2. Create a Data Processing Register listing all processing activities, purposes, and lawful bases. 3. Obtain explicit, informed consent forms where consent is the lawful basis, with clear language and separate opt-in mechanisms per purpose.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft or update data processing inventory and consent templates; Compliance team: create and maintain the Data Processing Register with documented lawful bases for each processing activity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide Data Processing Addendums (DPAs) or contracts confirming they process data only on written instructions and on the same lawful basis as the organisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Register, executed consent forms with timestamps, documented lawful basis assessment, vendor Data Processing Addendums, privacy notices provided to data subjects.

key_requirements.text: Register Data Protection Officers with the National Privacy Commission (NPC)
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a qualified Data Protection Officer (DPO) with expertise in data protection law and technical knowledge. 2. Complete the NPC Data Protection Officer Registration Form with the DPO's details, qualifications, and contact information. 3. Submit the registration to the National Privacy Commission and retain the official registration certificate.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Legal team: identify and formally appoint the DPO with documented qualifications; Compliance team: prepare and submit NPC registration form; DPO: provide curriculum vitae and confirmation of independence from operational management.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not typically applicable; if engaging external DPO services, the service provider must supply proof of their own NPC registration or qualifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: NPC Data Protection Officer Registration Certificate, appointment letter, DPO CV with data protection credentials, proof of submission to NPC, ongoing DPO contact details record.

key_requirements.text: Notify NPC and affected individuals of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data breach detection and reporting procedure identifying who identifies breaches and who authorises notification. 2. Create breach notification templates for NPC and affected individuals specifying nature of breach, categories of data, likely consequences, and mitigation measures. 3. Log all breaches with timestamps and ensure notifications to NPC and individuals occur within 72 hours of discovery.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: implement breach detection monitoring and provide incident logs; Legal/Compliance team: draft breach notification templates and maintain a Breach Register with dates of discovery, notification, and evidence of timely reporting.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: contractually commit to notifying the organisation of any breach affecting personal data within 24 hours of discovery, and provide breach notification templates aligned with NPC requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Breach Register documenting all incidents with discovery dates, notification dates, copies of notifications sent to NPC and individuals, NPC acknowledgement letters, incident investigation reports, evidence of 72-hour compliance.

key_requirements.text: Implement organisational, physical, and technical security measures proportionate to nature of data
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Data Security Risk Assessment identifying the sensitivity of personal information and proportionate safeguards needed (encryption, access controls, firewalls, employee training). 2. Implement technical controls (encryption at rest and in transit, multi-factor authentication, intrusion detection) and organisational controls (staff confidentiality agreements, security training, access logs). 3. Document all security measures in a Security Controls Inventory and conduct annual reviews to ensure adequacy.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide security architecture documentation, encryption certificates, firewall/IDS logs, and access control matrix; Security/Compliance team: develop and maintain Security Controls Inventory and conduct annual penetration testing or security audits; HR team: provide staff confidentiality and non-disclosure agreement records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party vendors: provide SOC 2 Type II reports, ISO 27001 certificates, or equivalent security attestations; security assessment results; and contractual commitments to implement and maintain controls proportionate to data processed.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Security Risk Assessment report, Security Controls Inventory with implementation dates, encryption certificates, firewall/access control documentation, staff confidentiality agreements, vendor security certificates (SOC 2, ISO 27001), audit reports, annual security review records.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a Data Subject Rights Request Procedure defining timelines (typically 30 days), required verification steps, and processes for access, rectification, erasure, and objection requests. 2. Implement a request tracking system (register or ticketing system) to log all requests, responses, and timelines. 3. Train relevant staff (customer service, IT, legal) on handling each right and document responses with evidence of compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft and publish Data Subject Rights Request Procedure; IT team: implement request tracking system or register and provide data retrieval/deletion capabilities; all teams: attend training on rights requests and document training records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: contractually commit to assisting in fulfilling data subject rights requests (e.g., data retrieval, deletion) within the organisation's response timelines and provide documentation of actions taken.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published Data Subject Rights Request Procedure, Data Subject Rights Request Register with request dates and response dates, copies of responses sent to data subjects, proof of data rectification or erasure, training records, vendor commitment letters.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Privacy Impact Assessments before implementing new high-risk processing systems
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define high-risk processing activities (e.g., automated decision-making, large-scale processing, use of new technologies, processing of sensitive data) that trigger Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) requirements. 2. Create a PIA template addressing data necessity, purpose limitation, data minimisation, proportionality, and safeguards; conduct PIAs before system deployment. 3. Document PIA findings, mitigation measures, and sign-off from DPO and legal/compliance leadership before proceeding.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/DPO team: develop PIA template and maintain a PIA Register; Project teams: complete PIAs for new systems before implementation and provide system design documentation, data flows, and risk analysis; DPO: review and approve PIAs before system launch.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party solution providers: supply system documentation detailing data processing flows, security features, and audit trails; confirm whether the solution has undergone vendor-side PIA; provide recommendations for risk mitigation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed Privacy Impact Assessments with documented findings, PIA Register listing all assessments by system/project, DPO sign-off letters, documented mitigation measures, system design documentation, evidence of PIAs completed before deployment dates.

key_requirements.text: Impose contractual data protection obligations on third-party processors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third-party processors (vendors, cloud providers, BPO firms) who access or process personal data on your behalf.
2. Draft or amend Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) that explicitly bind each processor to confidentiality, security, and lawful processing obligations aligned with DPA 2012 requirements.
3. Execute signed DPAs with all active processors and maintain a central register of these agreements with renewal dates.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft standardized Data Processing Agreement template incorporating DPA 2012 obligations (purpose limitation, security measures, sub-processor approval requirements).
Compliance team: compile inventory of all current third-party data processors with contract execution dates and DPA status.
IT team: document all systems and databases where third-party processors have access credentials or data visibility.
vendor_actions: Each processor: sign executed Data Processing Agreement acknowledging specific processing activities, data categories, and commitment to implement security safeguards per DPA 2012 Section 12.
Each processor: provide written attestation of their own data security practices and any applicable ISO 27001 or equivalent certifications.
Each processor: commit in writing to notify the organization within 72 hours of any personal data breach or unauthorized access.
compliance_evidence: Executed Data Processing Agreements for all third-party processors bearing authorized signatures and dates.
Processor inventory register listing vendor name, processing scope, DPA execution date, and contract renewal schedule.
Email confirmations or signed acknowledgments from each processor confirming receipt and acceptance of DPA terms.
Evidence of security assessments or audits of critical processors (audit reports, SOC 2 certificates, or vendor security questionnaires).

key_requirements.text: Data transfer abroad requires adequacy finding or contractual safeguards
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Map all instances where personal data is transferred outside the Philippines, identifying the destination countries and the categories of data involved.

2. For each destination, determine whether the National Privacy Commission (NPC) has issued an adequacy finding; if not, implement contractual safeguards (Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules).
3. Document the legal basis for each transfer (adequacy decision or executed safeguard agreement) and communicate transfer restrictions to relevant staff.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: maintain a data transfer impact assessment for each non-Philippines destination, noting adequacy status and applicable safeguard mechanism.
Compliance team: obtain and review current NPC adequacy findings list to confirm which countries qualify as adequate.
Data Protection Officer: draft transfer approval procedures and communicate approved transfer destinations to data handlers.
IT team: configure data systems to prevent transfers to non-approved jurisdictions through technical controls or access restrictions.
vendor_actions: Each offshore processor or sub-processor: execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent contractual safeguards approved by the NPC, if transferring data to jurisdictions lacking adequacy findings.
Each offshore vendor: provide written confirmation of the country(ies) where data will be stored, processed, or backed up.
Each offshore vendor: commit to seek prior written approval before transferring data to any additional jurisdictions outside the original scope.
compliance_evidence: Data Transfer Register documenting each international transfer, destination country, data categories, and applicable adequacy or safeguard basis.
Executed Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules signed by both the organization and recipient processor.
NPC adequacy findings correspondence or certification confirming approved destination jurisdictions.
Internal memos or policies restricting data transfers to approved jurisdictions only and requiring DPO approval for new transfers.
Audit logs or system configuration records showing technical controls preventing unauthorized international data transfers.

regulations.penalties: Imprisonment 1-6 years and fines up to PHP 5 million depending on offence type

regulations.name: Vietnam Personal Data Protection Decree 13/2023/ND-CP
regulations.authority: Ministry of Public Security (MPS) — A05 Department
regulations.summary: Vietnam's first comprehensive personal data protection regulation, effective July 2023. Introduces broad data subject rights, consent requirements, data transfer restrictions, and a data localisation obligation for sensitive data. Foreign companies processing Vietnamese citizens' data must appoint a local representative and register with the Ministry of Public Security.

key_requirements.text: Obtain clear, voluntary, and specific consent before processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft consent forms specifying data purpose, categories, and retention period in plain Vietnamese; obtain signed acknowledgement before any processing begins. 2. Implement consent management system recording timestamp, form version, and individual identifier for each consent event. 3. Train staff to refuse processing requests lacking documented consent and log all refusals.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide template consent forms and consent withdrawal procedures. IT team: configure consent management system and provide audit logs of all consent records. HR team: deliver staff training completion certificates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: sign Data Processing Addendum confirming they process only on documented consent instructions and return/delete data upon expiry. Marketing vendors: provide proof of prior consent before receiving any contact lists.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms with timestamps, consent management system audit logs, Data Processing Addendum with each vendor, training completion records, and documented refusal logs.

key_requirements.text: Distinguish between basic and sensitive personal data with stronger protections for sensitive categories
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain a data inventory classifying all personal data fields as basic or sensitive (financial, health, biometric, identification numbers); assign data stewards to each category. 2. Apply stronger technical controls to sensitive data: encryption at rest (AES-256), role-based access restrictions, and enhanced audit logging. 3. Document classification rationale and review quarterly with Legal and IT teams.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide complete data asset inventory with classification tags and field-level encryption status. Legal team: deliver classification criteria and approve sensitive data handling procedures. Data stewards: confirm quarterly review sign-offs documenting any classification changes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers: confirm in writing encryption standards for sensitive data and restricted access controls. Third-party processors: sign attestation that they apply enhanced security measures (encryption, access logs) to sensitive categories.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed data inventory with classification stamps, encryption configuration reports, field-level access control matrices, quarterly review meeting minutes, and vendor attestations on sensitive data handling.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals and authorities of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a breach response plan naming the Incident Response Lead, defining breach severity thresholds, and requiring immediate breach notification to Legal and IT Security within 2 hours of discovery. 2. Within 72 hours of confirmation, issue written breach notification to affected individuals in Vietnamese detailing breach scope, data categories, and remediation steps. 3. Simultaneously submit breach report to A05 (Authority of Personal Data Protection under Ministry of Information and Communications) with incident timeline, number of individuals affected, and mitigating actions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: maintain breach detection logs, forensic reports, and incident timelines. Legal team: draft and send individual notification letters and A05 submission form. IT team: provide breach impact assessment and remediation completion evidence. HR team: brief affected staff members if employee data breached.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting and cloud providers: commit to breach detection and immediate (within 2 hours) notification to organisation. Third-party processors: sign clause requiring 24-hour breach disclosure to the organisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response plan, breach detection logs with timestamps, forensic investigation reports, copies of individual notification letters (dated within 72 hours), A05 submission receipts, and vendor breach disclosure agreements.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, objection, and portability
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a Data Subject Rights Portal allowing individuals to submit and track requests for access, correction, deletion, objection, and portability; assign a dedicated Data Rights Officer to review and respond. 2. Establish a 30-day response Service Level Agreement: acknowledge receipt within 2 days, provide decision within 30 days, and deny only with documented legal justification. 3. For deletion and portability requests, ensure technical capability to export data in machine-readable format (CSV/JSON) or permanently remove records within the timeframe.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: build or procure Data Subject Rights Portal with request tracking and provide extraction/deletion capability reports. Legal team: draft standard response templates and justify denials with statutory references. Data stewards: confirm technical feasibility to retrieve and delete data per category. Compliance officer: maintain request log with response timelines and denial reasons.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and hosting providers: commit to supporting data export, deletion, and portability within 15 days of organisation request. Data processors: sign clause guaranteeing deletion upon organisation instruction within 10 days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights Portal usage logs and request tracking records, response letters with timestamps (within 30 days), copies of exported data in machine-readable format, deletion/correction completion confirmations, vendor commitments in Data Processing Addendums, and denial justification documents.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers require explicit consent and impact assessment submitted to A05
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Before any cross-border transfer of personal data, conduct a Data Transfer Impact Assessment documenting the destination country's legal framework, adequacy of protections, and contractual safeguards (Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules). 2. Obtain explicit written consent from affected individuals specifying the destination country and purpose; do not infer consent from prior processing agreements. 3. Submit the completed assessment to A05 in Vietnamese with supporting legal opinions and obtain confirmation before transfer begins.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: prepare Data Transfer Impact Assessment template, conduct country law review, and submit to A05 with supporting documentation. IT team: identify all systems and processes involving cross-border data flows and provide data flow diagrams. Compliance: maintain registry of approved transfers and A05 decision letters.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: International service providers: execute Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent transfer agreement and provide legal attestation that their country permits receiving organisation's data import. Processors in other countries: sign Data Processing Addendum explicitly confirming transfer restrictions and onward transfer prohibitions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Transfer Impact Assessment, country legal opinion, Standard Contractual Clauses with each vendor, individual transfer consent forms, A05 submission and approval letter, data flow diagrams, and vendor transfer compliance attestations.

key_requirements.text: Sensitive personal data (financial, health, biometric) must be stored on servers within Vietnam
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Audit all current hosting and database locations; immediately migrate any sensitive personal data (financial account details, health records, biometric identifiers, identity card numbers) from offshore to Vietnam-based servers certified for personal data storage. 2. Execute contracts with Vietnamese data centre operators requiring physical servers to remain within Vietnam's borders and prohibiting cross-border replication or backups. 3. Document server locations, apply geolocation controls, and conduct quarterly compliance audits confirming data residence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: audit current data locations, migrate sensitive data to Vietnam servers, and provide server location certificates from Vietnamese hosting providers. Finance team: secure budget and contracts for Vietnam-based infrastructure. Compliance officer: maintain data residency audit logs and quarterly location verification reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vietnamese hosting providers: provide data centre location certificates, sign Data Processing Addendum with explicit data residency clause (no offshore replication or backups), and commit to quarterly compliance audits. International cloud providers: execute amendment prohibiting storage of sensitive data outside Vietnam or cease processing sensitive categories.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Server location certificates from Vietnamese data centres, signed contracts with data residency clauses, migration completion reports with before/after location documentation, geolocation control configurations, quarterly data residency audit reports, and vendor residency compliance attestations.

key_requirements.text: Foreign businesses must appoint a representative in Vietnam
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally appoint a natural person or legal entity resident in Vietnam to act as your organisation's representative for personal data protection matters.
2. Register the representative's full name, address, and contact details with the relevant Vietnamese regulatory authority (Ministry of Public Security or delegated body).
3. Ensure the representative has authority to receive and respond to data subject requests, regulatory inquiries, and complaints on behalf of the foreign business.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft appointment letter or power-of-attorney document designating the Vietnam representative with explicit personal data protection responsibilities.
Compliance team: maintain internal registry of the representative's contact details, appointment date, and scope of authority.
Finance team: establish payment arrangements and oversight mechanism for the representative's services.
vendor_actions: Vietnam representative (legal entity or individual): provide signed acceptance letter confirming their appointment, residency proof, and commitment to fulfil data protection liaison duties.
Vietnam representative: submit regulatory notification form (if required by local authority) and provide evidence of registration.
compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter or power-of-attorney with representative's full name and Vietnam address.
Representative's residency certificate or business registration in Vietnam.
Regulatory notification receipt or confirmation of registration with Vietnamese authority.
Internal appointment record with date of effect and scope of authority.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Create a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) template aligned with Decree 13/2023/ND-CP criteria and complete a DPIA for each high-risk processing activity (e.g. biometric processing, large-scale monitoring, automated decision-making).
2. Document the assessment findings including data categories, processing purposes, recipients, retention periods, risk identification, and mitigation measures.
3. Review and approve the DPIA results internally; escalate any residual high risks to legal/compliance leadership and implement recommended safeguards before processing commences.
internal_actions: Compliance team: develop DPIA methodology and risk-scoring matrix aligned with Decree 13/2023/ND-CP; maintain central register of all completed DPIAs with completion dates.
IT team: provide technical specifications of processing systems, data flows, security controls, and retention mechanisms relevant to each assessment.
Legal team: review DPIA findings for regulatory adequacy and document sign-off; recommend contractual or technical mitigations.
Business units: identify high-risk processing activities and supply processing justification and business requirements to DPIA team.
vendor_actions: Third-party processors or sub-processors: provide data security questionnaire, technical documentation, and signed Data Processing Agreement confirming their security and compliance commitments.
Vendors handling sensitive data: supply audit reports, certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), or security self-assessment relevant to the processing risk profile.
compliance_evidence: Completed DPIA reports for each high-risk processing activity, signed and dated by assessor and approver.
DPIA register or inventory listing all assessments performed, processing activity names, dates, and risk ratings.
Risk mitigation action plans with implementation dates and responsibility assignments.
Evidence of completion of recommended safeguards (e.g. technical controls deployed, contracts updated, training completed).
Processor Data Processing Agreements and vendor security documentation referenced in each DPIA.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines; criminal liability for intentional violations under Cybersecurity Law

regulations.name: Indonesia Personal Data Protection Law (UU PDP 2022)
regulations.authority: Ministry of Communication and Informatics (Kominfo) / Personal Data Protection Agency
regulations.summary: Indonesia's Personal Data Protection Law (Law No. 27/2022) passed in October 2022, with full enforcement from October 2024 after a 2-year transition. GDPR-influenced, it establishes data subject rights, controller and processor obligations, breach notification, international transfer rules, and a dedicated Personal Data Protection Agency.

key_requirements.text: Process personal data only with consent or other lawful basis
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all processing activities and map each to a lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests). 2. Create a Data Processing Register listing purpose, category, legal basis, and retention period for each activity. 3. Implement technical controls (e.g., consent management system) to enforce processing only under documented lawful basis.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed memo confirming lawful basis classification for each processing activity. IT team: configure access controls and logging to prevent processing outside documented scope. Compliance team: maintain Data Processing Register with sign-off from data controllers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: execute Data Processing Agreement (DPA) specifying lawful basis for their processing role, data categories, and permitted uses. Provide signed confirmation they process data only per contractual instructions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Processing Register (signed), executed DPA with vendors, consent management system audit logs, controller sign-off memo on lawful basis assessment.

key_requirements.text: Notify data subjects of purpose, type, and legal basis at collection
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create privacy notice template in Indonesian and English (if applicable) disclosing purpose, data categories, legal basis, and retention period at point of collection. 2. Integrate notice delivery into collection channels (web forms, paper forms, verbal scripts) with evidence of display/delivery. 3. Document when and how notice was provided to each data subject category.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and version-control privacy notice templates compliant with UU PDP 2022 Article 4 requirements. Marketing/Operations team: confirm notice is displayed before data collection on all touchpoints. Compliance team: maintain log of notice deployment dates and audience reach per collection channel.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: confirm in writing they display your provided privacy notice before collecting data on your behalf, or provide their own notice for your approval. Supply proof of notice integration into their collection interface.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed-off privacy notice templates, screenshots of notices displayed at collection points, vendor confirmation of notice integration, data subject acknowledgment records (e.g., checkbox logs, form submissions).

key_requirements.text: Mandatory data breach notification to authorities and data subjects within 14 calendar days
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a breach detection and escalation procedure requiring discovery reporting within 3 days of awareness. 2. Create a breach notification template (in Indonesian) and maintain authority contact list for OJK, BPK, or relevant sector regulator plus affected data subject communication log. 3. Document breach severity assessment criteria and track notification timeline (authority within 14 days, data subjects without undue delay).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: maintain breach detection logs and incident response runbook; provide breach discovery timeline and evidence (logs, alerts). Legal team: draft breach notification letter templates and regulator contact registry. Compliance team: establish breach notification tracker with dates and recipient lists.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: execute clause requiring breach notification to you within 24 hours of discovery; provide proof of their incident response procedure and authority notification template for your sector/regulator.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response procedure, breach notification tracker with authority and data subject letters (timestamp, delivery proof), regulator reference numbers, IT incident logs showing discovery date, severity assessment memo.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and document a Data Subject Rights Request (DSRR) handling process with 30-day response SLA, covering access (copy of data), correction, deletion, portability, and objection. 2. Implement a ticketing system to log requests, assign ownership, and track resolution; ensure Technical team can execute requests in systems. 3. Train relevant staff on request types and approval matrix (e.g., Legal approval for deletion, Data controller sign-off for portability).
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide inventory of systems holding personal data and document data extraction/deletion/correction capabilities per system; maintain request fulfillment logs. Legal team: define decision rules for each right type (e.g., when deletion is refused and why). Compliance team: operate DSRR ticketing system and report monthly on request volume and resolution time.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: commit in DPA to fulfill data subject rights requests on your behalf within your 30-day SLA; provide template rights request form and timeline for their sub-processor to respond.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DSRR procedure document (signed), DSRR ticketing system audit logs, sample fulfilled requests (data subject letter, data extract, deletion confirmation), vendor DPA clause on rights support, training attendance records.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for high-risk or large-scale processing
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Assess whether your organisation meets high-risk or large-scale criteria (e.g., public authority, financial data, health data, or processing >50% of target population in a region). 2. If yes, formally appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) with written designation, independence guarantee, and role description per UU PDP 2022 Article 5. 3. Publish DPO contact details on your website and provide to regulators if requested.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: issue signed DPO appointment letter confirming independence, no conflict of interest, and direct reporting line to Board/Executive. Compliance team: document high-risk/large-scale assessment criteria with sign-off; maintain DPO role description and monitor conflicts. Legal team: ensure DPO has adequate budget and authority.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: confirm in writing whether they process high-risk or large-scale data and, if so, confirm DPO appointment status and provide DPO contact details for coordination.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DPO appointment letter (signed), role description, high-risk/large-scale assessment memo, DPO contact published on website, Board/Executive confirmation of independence and budget allocation.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Privacy Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify high-risk processing activities (new technologies, large-scale data, sensitive categories, automated decision-making, vulnerable subjects) based on UU PDP 2022 Annex or sector guidance. 2. Conduct Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) for each high-risk activity: document processing purpose, data categories, risks (legal, technical, organizational), and mitigation measures. 3. Review PIA with Data Controller and Legal before processing launch; maintain PIA register and update annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/DPO team: define high-risk activity trigger list and PIA template. IT/Product team: provide processing technical specification (systems, data flows, retention) for PIA completion. Legal team: review PIA risk assessment and mitigation adequacy; sign off before processing launch.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendor: provide documented risk assessment of their processing (sub-processors, security measures, data location) for your PIA; confirm mitigation measures (encryption, access controls, audit logs) with evidence (SOC 2, ISO 27001).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: High-risk activity trigger list (signed), completed PIA templates per activity, risk register with mitigation actions, vendor security assessment (SOC 2, ISO 27001 cert or equivalent), Legal sign-off memo, PIA register with annual review dates.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border transfers require coordination agreements or contractual safeguards
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data flow audit to identify all cross-border transfers of personal data and their destination countries.
2. Execute Data Processing Agreements (DPA) or Standard Contractual Clauses (SCC) with each vendor or recipient outside Indonesia that includes adequacy safeguards.
3. Document the legal basis for each transfer (adequacy decision, contractual safeguards, or binding corporate rules) and maintain in a central registry.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft or procure Standard Contractual Clauses template and DPA addendum for cross-border transfers.
IT team: provide complete data flow mapping showing all destinations where personal data leaves Indonesia.
Compliance team: create and maintain a register of all cross-border transfers with destination country, safeguard type, and execution date.
vendor_actions: Third-party vendors/processors: execute Data Processing Agreement or Standard Contractual Clauses acknowledging receipt of personal data outside Indonesia and confirming equivalent protection measures.
Vendors: provide written confirmation of their country location and any sub-processor arrangements involving further cross-border transfers.
compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Agreements with contractual safeguards clauses for each cross-border transfer recipient.
Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent adequacy certification documents signed by both parties.
Cross-border data transfer register listing destination country, vendor name, data categories, transfer date, and safeguard mechanism.
Data flow documentation or architecture diagrams showing personal data movement across borders.

key_requirements.text: Retain personal data only as long as necessary for stated purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Document the retention period for each category of personal data aligned with its stated purpose (e.g., customer records for contract duration plus 3 years for tax compliance).
2. Establish automated or manual data deletion/anonymization procedures triggered at the end of the documented retention period.
3. Audit and delete excess personal data currently held beyond the documented retention window and document the disposal method.
internal_actions: Legal team: document the business and regulatory justification for each retention period by data category and purpose.
IT team: configure data lifecycle management tools or manual schedules to automatically flag and delete/anonymize data upon reaching retention expiry; provide audit logs of deletions.
HR/Records team: inventory all personal data stores (systems, databases, paper files) and assign retention periods to each; conduct initial purge of over-retained data.
vendor_actions: IT service providers/cloud vendors: confirm in writing their deletion or anonymization procedures and timeline, and commit to delete data upon organization's written request.
Data processors: provide evidence of technical controls enforcing retention limits (e.g., automated expiry triggers in systems).
compliance_evidence: Retention schedule document listing all personal data categories, purposes, and justified retention periods.
Deletion/anonymization policy describing the automated or manual process, responsible party, and method (secure erasure, anonymization, archiving).
System configuration screenshots or code showing automated data expiry or flagging mechanisms.
Audit logs or reports showing dates and volumes of personal data deleted or anonymized.
Evidence of initial data purge project (e.g., project report, deletion logs) confirming removal of excess historical data.

regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions up to 2% of annual revenue; criminal penalties up to IDR 60 billion

regulations.name: Hong Kong Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance
regulations.authority: Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data (PCPD)
