regulations.summary: Hong Kong's Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (Cap. 486), substantially amended in October 2021 to criminalise doxxing and enhance the PCPD's powers. Applies six Data Protection Principles covering data collection, accuracy, retention, use, security, and access. The PCPD can issue enforcement notices and initiate criminal prosecution for doxxing.

key_requirements.text: Collect personal data by lawful and fair means for a specified and lawful purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the lawful basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, or legitimate interest) for each data collection activity in a Data Collection Register. 2. Embed collection purpose statements in all forms, contracts, and system interfaces before capturing data. 3. Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for new collection activities to verify fairness and lawfulness.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed legal opinion on lawful basis for each data collection stream. IT team: supply inventory of all data collection points (forms, APIs, sensors) with associated purpose statements. HR team: document consent mechanisms and retention of signed consents.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: execute Data Processing Agreements (DPA) that specify the lawful basis they rely on and confirm they will not re-purpose data. Marketing/analytics vendors: provide written confirmation that their data processing is limited to the stated collection purpose.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DPAs with all vendors; Data Collection Register with documented lawful basis for each stream; completed DPIAs; screenshots of collection forms with embedded privacy notices; retained copies of signed consents.

key_requirements.text: Retain personal data no longer than necessary for the purpose of collection
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and publish a Data Retention Schedule that specifies retention periods for each data category (e.g. customer records: 7 years post-contract termination). 2. Implement automated deletion or anonymisation workflows in data systems triggered by the retention schedule. 3. Conduct annual data inventory audits to identify and securely dispose of data exceeding retention periods.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve Data Retention Schedule linked to business and legal requirements. IT team: configure deletion jobs, retention tags, and archival workflows in all systems holding personal data. Records Management team: provide evidence of secure destruction (certificates, logs, witness signatures).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/storage vendors: provide written confirmation of deletion capabilities and provide deletion completion certificates upon request. Data processors (outsourced services): sign addendum to DPA mandating deletion of personal data upon contract termination or retention period expiry.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Retention Schedule; system configuration screenshots showing automated deletion rules; deletion completion certificates from vendors; audit logs showing purge job executions; destruction certificates from secure disposal vendors.

key_requirements.text: Use personal data for the purpose for which it was collected or a directly related purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Maintain a Data Purpose Mapping document that traces each data element to its original collection purpose and lists any directly related secondary uses (e.g. fraud prevention, service improvement). 2. Audit all data queries and system exports quarterly to verify they align with documented purposes. 3. Implement technical controls (field-level access controls, query logging) to prevent unauthorised use beyond stated purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: create and maintain Data Purpose Mapping document approved by data controller. IT team: enable query logging and field-level access controls; generate quarterly reports of all data access by purpose. Data governance team: conduct quarterly use-case audits and sign-off on alignment with approved purposes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: execute amended DPA clauses that explicitly restrict secondary use and prohibit re-purposing without written consent. Analytics/BI vendors: provide contractual confirmation that dashboards and reports will only aggregate data for approved purposes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed and versioned Data Purpose Mapping; quarterly audit reports with sign-off; query logs filtered to show access by purpose; DPA clauses restricting secondary use; evidence of field-level access control configuration.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate security measures to prevent unauthorised access or processing
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a baseline information security risk assessment identifying personal data assets, threats, and vulnerabilities; document in a Risk Register. 2. Implement technical controls: encryption (data at rest and in transit), access control (role-based, principle of least privilege), multi-factor authentication, and audit logging on all systems storing personal data. 3. Establish an annual security testing program (penetration testing, vulnerability scanning) covering all personal data systems and document results.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: provide Risk Register, Information Security Policy, and System Security Plans (SSP) for each personal data system. IT team: supply encryption inventory, access control matrices, and MFA deployment status. IT Operations: provide security test reports (penetration test results, vulnerability scans) from an independent third party; provide access log exports and incident logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: supply Security Addendum, SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification, encryption key management procedures, and confirmation of encryption standards (AES-256 or equivalent). Third-party processors: provide evidence of their own security testing and incident response procedures via questionnaire or audit report.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Risk Register and controls matrix; Information Security Policy; SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificates from vendors; encryption inventory and configuration documentation; latest penetration test and vulnerability scan reports (dated within 12 months); DPA clauses mandating security obligations; incident response plan with documented tests.

key_requirements.text: Inform data subjects of their right to access and correct personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Publish a Privacy Notice / Personal Information Collection Statement (PICS) that clearly states data subjects' rights to access and correct personal data, including the process, timelines, and contact details. 2. Implement a Subject Access Request (SAR) and data correction workflow with documented procedures, timelines (typically 40 calendar days), and assigned responsibility. 3. Maintain a SAR Register logging all requests, response dates, and outcomes; audit responses annually for accuracy and timeliness.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft and publish Privacy Notice/PICS in plain language, available on website and at point of collection. IT team: provide a mechanism to handle SARs (helpdesk portal, email inbox, or dedicated system); configure workflows to retrieve and format personal data within 40 days. Customer Service/HR team: train staff on SAR handling procedures and establish a SAR Register with audit trail.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: execute DPA clause committing to assist with SARs by retrieving or correcting personal data they hold within 10 business days of request. Website/app vendors: integrate SAR submission forms or links to a central SAR inbox.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published Privacy Notice/PICS (with date of last update); SAR handling procedure document; SAR Register with entries showing request date, response date, and confirmation of compliance with 40-day timeline; sample SAR responses; staff training records; DPA sections on processor obligations to assist with SARs.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit doxxing: disclosing personal information to intimidate or harass individuals (criminal offence since 2021)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Incorporate an explicit policy clause into the Code of Conduct / Acceptable Use Policy prohibiting employees and contractors from intentionally disclosing personal data to intimidate, harass, or harm individuals; link to criminal penalties under PDPO section 64. 2. Conduct mandatory annual training on doxxing risks and reporting obligations; maintain attendance records and assessments. 3. Implement a whistleblower/incident reporting mechanism and investigate any reports of intentional disclosure within 10 business days; document findings and actions taken.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Legal team: incorporate doxxing prohibition into Code of Conduct and employment contracts with acknowledgement signatures. Compliance/Training team: develop and deliver annual training module on doxxing, criminal liability, and reporting procedures; maintain completion records. Compliance/Security team: establish incident investigation procedure; document all reports and investigations in a confidential log; provide quarterly summary reports to leadership.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers (especially call centres, outsourced support): execute contractual clause prohibiting doxxing and committing to implement equivalent training and reporting procedures; provide evidence of staff training annually.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Code of Conduct clauses prohibiting doxxing; employee acknowledgement signatures; training module content and completion records; incident report log (redacted if necessary) showing investigation summaries; documented corrective actions or disciplinary measures; vendor training certifications; DPA clauses mandating doxxing prohibition.

key_requirements.text: Regulate direct marketing use of personal data with strict opt-in requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Data Subject Consent Registry documenting explicit opt-in records (date, channel, and scope of consent) for each individual before any direct marketing communication.
2. Configure marketing automation systems to enforce consent validation at send-time, blocking messages to non-consented individuals and logging all rejection events.
3. Conduct quarterly audits of direct marketing lists against the Consent Registry to identify and remove non-consented contacts, with signed audit reports retained.
internal_actions: Marketing team: provide direct marketing campaign lists, consent mechanisms (e.g., web form code, email sign-up procedures), and historical opt-in records for the past 12 months.
IT team: export marketing automation system audit logs showing consent checks, message sends, and bounces; provide system configuration screenshots confirming opt-in enforcement rules.
Legal team: draft or update direct marketing policy specifying opt-in requirements, consent duration, and withdrawal procedures; prepare consent registry template.
vendor_actions: Email Service Provider / Marketing Automation Platform: provide written confirmation of opt-in validation capabilities, system audit logs for a sample period, and contractual undertaking to block sends to non-consented recipients.
Telemarketing or SMS vendor (if applicable): supply evidence of consent capture integration, proof of do-not-call list synchronization, and monthly compliance reports.
compliance_evidence: Signed Data Subject Consent Registry (with timestamps and consent method documented).
Marketing automation system audit logs showing consent validation checks and rejection events for a 3-month sample period.
Quarterly audit reports comparing campaign lists to the Consent Registry, signed by Marketing and Legal.
Direct marketing policy document approved by Legal/Management.
Vendor contracts or Data Processing Addenda including opt-in enforcement and audit rights clauses.
Screenshot evidence of system configuration enforcing opt-in rules.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers are subject to adequate protection requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Map all cross-border personal data transfers by recipient country and create a Transfer Risk Assessment documenting adequacy findings (e.g., legal framework, contractual safeguards, encryption in transit).
2. For transfers to jurisdictions without deemed adequacy, implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) as appropriate, executed with all recipient entities.
3. Conduct annual reviews of recipient country regulations and vendor compliance status; update transfer documentation and re-assess adequacy if legal frameworks or vendor practices change.
internal_actions: Data Protection Officer / Legal team: complete cross-border data flow mapping exercise identifying all transfers, destination countries, and data categories; produce Transfer Risk Assessment document.

IT team: provide system architecture diagrams showing data transmission paths, encryption standards (e.g., TLS 1.2+), and data residency configurations; confirm whether personal data is stored or merely transited.
Finance / Procurement team: supply list of all active third-party service providers receiving personal data and their registered country of operation.
vendor_actions: All vendors receiving personal data: execute Data Processing Addendum incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent adequacy mechanism; provide country of operation, data handling location, and sub-processor list.
Cloud service providers: supply data residency certification, compliance certifications relevant to destination country (e.g., SOC 2, ISO 27001), and confirmation of encryption standards used.
compliance_evidence: Completed Cross-Border Data Transfer Register or matrix documenting all transfers, recipient countries, data categories, and transfer mechanism (SCC/BCR/other).
Transfer Risk Assessment reports for each destination country, including adequacy findings and mitigation measures applied.
Executed Data Processing Addenda or contracts with all vendors containing SCC clauses (or equivalent legal mechanism) and approved by Legal.
System architecture documentation confirming encryption in transit and data residency controls.
Vendor compliance certifications and data handling attestations (SOC 2, ISO 27001, etc.).
Annual compliance review records re-assessing transfer adequacy and updating documentation.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to HKD 1 million and up to 5 years imprisonment for doxxing offences; enforcement notices for other violations

regulations.name: New Zealand Privacy Act 2020
regulations.authority: Office of the Privacy Commissioner (OPC)
regulations.summary: New Zealand's Privacy Act 2020 replaced the 1993 Act, modernising the framework with 13 Information Privacy Principles, mandatory breach notification, enhanced enforcement powers, and cross-border data transfer obligations. Aligns closely with Australia's Privacy Act and incorporates elements of GDPR.

key_requirements.text: Collect personal information only for a lawful purpose connected to your functions
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: document lawful purposes for each personal data collection point (e.g. employment verification, service delivery, statutory obligation). 2. Compliance team: map all data collection activities to documented purposes and conduct annual review. 3. All departments: sign-off confirming collections align with mapped purposes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written register of lawful purposes by data collection type. Compliance team: deliver data mapping matrix linking collection activity to lawful purpose and organisational function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide written confirmation that data processing for your organisation is limited to documented lawful purposes; certify no secondary use without authorisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed purpose register; data processing impact assessment (DPIA) documenting lawful basis; departmental sign-off records; vendor data processing agreements with purpose limitation clauses.

key_requirements.text: Collect information directly from the individual where practicable
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance team: audit all data collection channels and document instances where direct collection is not practicable (e.g. employer reference checks, fraud prevention). 2. Process owners: revise collection forms and workflows to capture data directly from individuals first, with fallback documented only when infeasible. 3. Communication team: draft privacy notices explaining why indirect collection occurs where applicable.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: provide audit report identifying all indirect collection points and documented justifications. Process owners: deliver updated collection procedures and forms showing direct collection as default. HR/Operations: confirm system configurations enforce direct collection prompts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data brokers or background check providers: certify they collect information directly from source individuals where possible and document exceptions; provide audit trail of collection methods used.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data collection audit report with 'direct vs. indirect' analysis; updated collection forms and workflows; privacy impact assessment justifying indirect collection; vendor certifications and audit trails.

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals of the purpose of collection and their rights at or before collection
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Communications team: design and deploy privacy notices (collection statements) at every point personal information is collected, covering purpose, rights, and recipients. 2. Compliance team: create master notice template aligned to NZ Privacy Act Principles 1–9 and embed in all forms, portals, and verbal collection scripts. 3. Quality assurance: test that notices are visible, plain language, and delivered before or at collection.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Communications team: deliver privacy notice templates (web, paper, verbal, email) signed-off by legal. Compliance team: audit all collection touchpoints and provide gap report. IT team: implement system controls to display notices before data entry.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Contractors and service providers collecting data on your behalf: provide signed commitment to deliver your approved privacy notice verbatim; return evidence of notice delivery with each data collection.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Master privacy notice template (approved by Legal); screenshots of deployed notices on all digital channels; signed contractor commitments; sample collection forms with notices; testing records confirming notice visibility.

key_requirements.text: Mandatory privacy breach notification to OPC and affected individuals when harm is likely
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Security/Incident team: establish breach detection and assessment process; define 'likely harm' threshold (reputational, financial, identity risk) and document decision criteria. 2. Legal team: draft breach notification template and escalation procedure to OPC (Office of Privacy Commissioner) and affected individuals; set 20 working day response target. 3. Leadership: approve breach response plan and conduct annual tabletop exercise.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security team: provide breach detection logs, incident response runbook, and harm assessment criteria. Legal team: deliver breach notification template, OPC contact procedure, and notification timeline document. IT team: confirm systems log access anomalies and support incident investigation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud providers and IT vendors: contractually commit to immediate breach notification (within 24–48 hours); provide incident response cooperation clause and log access to your data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response plan; OPC notification templates; incident response runbook with harm assessment matrix; vendor breach notification clauses in Data Processing Agreements; records of any breaches notified (redacted) and outcomes.

key_requirements.text: Store personal information securely and protect against unauthorised access
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT/Security team: conduct security baseline audit across storage systems (databases, file servers, cloud) and implement encryption-at-rest and encryption-in-transit for all personal data. 2. Access control: enforce role-based access, multi-factor authentication, and audit logging; limit access to 'need-to-know' basis. 3. Compliance team: document security controls in a register and validate annually via vulnerability scanning and penetration testing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide encryption inventory, access control policies, multi-factor authentication rollout plan, and audit log samples. Security team: deliver vulnerability assessment report and penetration test results. Compliance team: create security controls register and annual validation checklist.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and managed service providers: provide current SOC 2 Type II report (or equivalent); certify encryption standards (AES-256, TLS 1.2+); commit to vulnerability scanning cadence and incident response protocols.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Encryption audit report; access control policies and role definitions; multi-factor authentication enablement records; audit logs (sample 3-month extracts); SOC 2 certificates; vulnerability and penetration test reports; vendor security attestations.

key_requirements.text: Honour access and correction rights — respond within 20 working days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR/Compliance team: implement formal data subject access request (DSAR) process; designate single point of contact and define workflow (receipt → verification → compilation → response). 2. IT team: configure system to retrieve and export personal data in portable format within 10 working days of verified request. 3. Compliance team: track all requests in a register, log response timelines, and escalate any delays beyond 20 working days.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Compliance: draft DSAR process document and designate coordinator. IT team: document data export procedures and system query templates. Compliance team: create DSAR register template (request date, individual, status, response date, delay reason).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party data processors: contractually commit to provide your organisation with individual's data held with them within 5 working days on request; certify ability to retrieve and export in portable format (CSV, PDF).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed DSAR process procedure; DSAR register (12-month sample) showing all requests, response dates, and proof of 20 working day compliance; sample DSAR responses (redacted); vendor DSAR commitment clauses; system query logs.

key_requirements.text: Only use personal information for the purpose collected unless an exception applies
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document the primary purpose for each category of personal information collected (e.g. employment, marketing, service delivery) in a Data Purpose Register. 2. Establish a secondary use approval process requiring legal review before any repurposing, with documented justification against recognised exceptions (consent, legal obligation, public benefit). 3. Conduct annual audits of actual data uses against the register and maintain records of approval decisions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain the Data Purpose Register with all collection purposes and approved secondary uses. Compliance team: provide audit reports showing sampled data uses matched against documented purposes. IT team: generate reports of data access requests grouped by stated business purpose.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: provide contractual confirmation of permitted data uses and certify they will only process personal information for purposes specified in the Data Processing Agreement. Vendors must notify immediately if they receive requests to use data outside the agreed scope.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Purpose Register with date-stamped updates; secondary use approval decision logs with legal sign-off; annual use-vs-purpose audit reports; Data Processing Agreements with vendors containing permitted use restrictions; incident notification records showing vendor escalations for out-of-scope requests.

key_requirements.text: Ensure overseas transfers only to countries with comparable privacy protection
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain an Overseas Transfer Assessment document that maps each overseas recipient country against NZ Privacy Act adequacy criteria and documents the comparability determination (e.g. GDPR equivalence, local privacy law assessment, or contractual safeguards). 2. For transfers to non-adequate countries, implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) and document the mechanism in Data Processing Agreements. 3. Review assessments annually or when business arrangements change, and maintain audit evidence of due diligence conducted.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: complete Overseas Transfer Assessment for each country recipient, document comparability findings, and obtain sign-off from Privacy Lead. Compliance team: maintain current register of all overseas transfers with assessed countries and safeguard mechanisms. IT/Data team: provide inventory of systems and databases with overseas access or replication.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting vendors: provide Privacy Impact Assessment or third-party audit (SOC2 Type II) confirming data residency and access controls by jurisdiction. Vendors transferring data overseas: execute Standard Contractual Clauses and confirm sub-processor locations. All vendors: confirm country of operation and data centres used, and notify immediately if data location changes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Overseas Transfer Assessment document with legal determination of country adequacy; Data Processing Agreements with executed Standard Contractual Clauses or BCR references; vendor Privacy Impact Assessments or SOC2 Type II reports; register of overseas recipients with safeguard mechanism noted; annual review records with sign-off; vendor change notifications showing re-assessment of new locations.

regulations.penalties: Civil proceedings; fines up to NZD 10,000 for individuals; up to NZD 75,000 for organisations for offences

regulations.name: Turkey Law on the Protection of Personal Data (KVKK No. 6698)
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK)
regulations.summary: Turkey's Law No. 6698 on the Protection of Personal Data (KVKK), modeled on GDPR, governs the processing of personal data by natural and legal persons. Requires registration of data controllers in the VERBIS registry, lawful processing, data subject rights, and explicit consent for sensitive personal data categories.

key_requirements.text: Register as a data controller in the VERBIS (Data Controllers Registry) system
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate the Legal or Compliance team to prepare registration documentation including company details, data processing activities, and contact information. 2. Submit the registration application to the Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK) via the VERBIS online portal with all required supporting documents. 3. Maintain the registration certificate and update VERBIS within 30 days of any material changes to data processing activities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: prepare and sign VERBIS registration application form with company information, data processing purposes, and categories of personal data; Compliance team: document all data processing activities and maintain a current list of processing purposes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable—this is a direct regulatory filing requirement.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: VERBIS registration certificate issued by KVKK; copy of submitted registration form with official confirmation receipt; VERBIS account access credentials; dated internal memo confirming registration completion.

key_requirements.text: Process personal data only on a lawful basis (explicit consent or legal justification)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data processing audit to identify all personal data handling and document the lawful basis for each processing activity (consent, contract, legal obligation, or legitimate interest). 2. Create and maintain a Data Processing Register (Veri İşleme Kaydı) listing each processing activity, its legal basis, and retention period. 3. Embed lawful basis justification into all relevant data policies and contractual agreements with data subjects.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document lawful basis analysis for each processing activity and approve the Data Processing Register; Compliance team: create and maintain the Data Processing Register with supporting evidence for each lawful basis claim; Data Protection Officer: review register quarterly.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: sign Data Processing Agreements (DPA) confirming their role and lawful basis; third-party controllers: provide written confirmation of their independent lawful basis for any joint processing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Register listing all processing activities and their lawful basis; Data Processing Agreements with all processors; consent forms (if applicable); legal opinions supporting legitimate interest claims; audit trails showing when and why processing commenced.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit consent for processing special categories of personal data (health, biometric, criminal)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all processing activities involving special categories (health, genetic, biometric, criminal records) and confirm explicit written consent is obtained before any processing begins. 2. Draft clear, specific consent forms in Turkish that separately itemise special category processing and allow granular withdrawal; implement a consent management system to record signed forms. 3. Train all staff handling special category data on the heightened consent and security requirements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft special category consent forms and review for KVKK compliance; IT team: implement a consent management system with audit logs and timestamped records; HR team: conduct mandatory training for staff accessing special category data; Compliance team: maintain and audit consent records quarterly.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: sign a Special Category Data Processing Addendum confirming they process only on explicit instruction and under equivalent security measures; external consent platforms (if used): provide certified audit reports confirming secure storage and timestamped consent capture.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Turkish-language explicit consent forms with dated capture timestamps; consent management system audit logs; training attendance records and completion certificates; Data Processing Register section explicitly identifying special category processing and consent basis; processor contracts with special category addenda.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, objection, and data portability
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented Data Subject Rights Request procedure defining response timelines (max 30 days), responsible teams, and escalation protocols for access, correction, deletion, objection, and portability requests. 2. Implement a request tracking system (log, queue, and ticket system) to record receipt, processing, and resolution of each request with timestamps. 3. Audit the procedure semi-annually and report metrics (request volume, response time, denial rates) to the Data Protection Officer.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve the Data Subject Rights Request procedure; IT team: implement a ticketing and tracking system with audit logs; Customer Service/HR team (depending on data source): assign request handlers and document response timelines; Compliance team: maintain a register of all requests and resolutions; Data Protection Officer: review denial decisions and escalations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: confirm in writing that they will process and forward data subject requests within the 30-day window and provide supporting documentation (extraction logs, deletion certificates); external data portability platforms (if used): provide monthly reports of portability requests fulfilled and data formats supplied.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Subject Rights Request procedure document; dated requests and corresponding response letters; request tracking system exports showing receipt date, handling team, and resolution date; deletion certificates from IT; data portability reports with export formats and file hashes; semi-annual audit reports; denial decision justifications reviewed by Data Protection Officer.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Personal Data Protection Officer or designate responsible persons
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. If mandatory by law or organisational size, formally appoint a qualified Personal Data Protection Officer (DPO) or designate senior responsible persons in Legal, IT, and HR and document their roles in writing. 2. Publish the DPO or responsible persons' contact details on your privacy notice and ensure they have adequate authority, training, and resources to oversee compliance. 3. Establish a quarterly compliance review meeting chaired by the DPO/responsible persons to audit processing activities, assess risks, and review incidents.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Executive team: formally appoint DPO or designate responsible persons via board resolution or executive memo; Legal team: document roles, responsibilities, reporting lines, and contact details; HR team: ensure DPO/responsible persons receive annual KVKK and data protection training; Finance team: allocate budget for DPO activities and tools.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not typically applicable—appointment is internal. However, if external DPO counsel is retained: obtain a signed engagement letter confirming scope, authority, and confidentiality obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or executive memo formally appointing DPO or responsible persons; signed role descriptions; published contact details on privacy notice; training certificates for DPO/responsible persons; quarterly compliance meeting minutes and action logs; evidence of DPO access to systems and data processing records.

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to prevent unauthorised processing
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data security risk assessment covering all personal data systems (storage, transmission, access, backup) and document identified risks and mitigation controls. 2. Implement technical controls (encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, multi-factor authentication) and organisational controls (data classification, staff training, incident response procedures) aligned to the risk assessment. 3. Perform annual penetration testing and vulnerability scans, and maintain an audit trail of all access to personal data systems.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Security team: conduct annual data security risk assessment, implement encryption (TLS 1.2+, AES-256), configure role-based access control (RBAC), and enable multi-factor authentication (MFA); IT team: deploy audit logging with timestamped read/write/delete events; IT team: perform annual penetration testing and remediate findings; HR/Compliance team: deliver annual KVKK security training to all staff with attendance records; Incident Response team: document and test incident response procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide annual SOC 2 Type II reports or equivalent security audit reports confirming encryption, access controls, and logging; cloud providers: confirm in writing that encryption keys are held separately from data and that audit logs are tamper-proof and retained for minimum 12 months; backup vendors: provide certificates of encryption and off-site storage.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual data security risk assessment report; encryption configuration documentation with key management policy; RBAC matrix and access control audit logs (minimum 3 months); MFA enrollment records; penetration test reports and remediation logs; audit logs showing personal data access with timestamps and user identity; annual security training certificates; incident response procedure document and tabletop exercise records; processor security audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001, or equivalent).

key_requirements.text: Notify KVKK and data subjects of data breaches as soon as possible
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a data breach detection and classification procedure that defines what constitutes a notifiable breach under KVKK Article 4. 2. Create a breach notification workflow that mandates notification to the KVKK within 30 days and affected data subjects without undue delay, including a breach communication template with required information (nature, scope, likely consequences, measures taken). 3. Document all breach incidents, investigation findings, and notification evidence in a centralized breach register.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve breach notification template and response procedures. IT team: configure monitoring and logging to detect unauthorized access or data exfiltration; provide incident response playbook. Security team: conduct breach impact assessments and document root cause analyses for each incident.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors and service providers: provide written commitment to notify your organization of any breaches within 24 hours; submit incident response procedures and contact details for breach escalation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed breach response procedures document, breach notification template, breach register with timestamped entries showing detection date and notification dates to KVKK and subjects, copies of all breach notifications sent to KVKK and affected parties, incident investigation reports, vendor breach notification agreements.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers require adequate protection or explicit consent and KVKK approval
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all cross-border data transfers (destinations, data types, frequency) and assess whether recipient countries have adequacy decisions from KVKK or equivalent protection levels. 2. For transfers to countries without adequacy decisions, obtain explicit documented consent from data subjects or establish an alternative lawful basis; complete KVKK approval request with transfer impact assessment. 3. Execute Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or binding corporate rules with all third-country recipients and maintain evidence of KVKK pre-approval.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Data Protection Officer: document all cross-border transfer locations and create a transfer register; conduct adequacy assessments. Legal team: draft consent forms for data subjects and prepare KVKK approval application including transfer justification. IT team: provide system architecture diagrams showing data flows to third countries.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: International vendors and processors: execute Data Processing Addendum (DPA) incorporating Standard Contractual Clauses compliant with KVKK requirements; provide written confirmation of their country's data protection level or commitment to equivalent safeguards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-border transfer mapping document, KVKK adequacy decision references or approval letter, executed Standard Contractual Clauses with each third-country recipient, signed DPAs, explicit consent forms from data subjects (if applicable), KVKK pre-approval documentation, transfer risk assessment reports.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code

regulations.name: Switzerland Revised Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP/revDSG)
regulations.authority: Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC)
regulations.summary: Switzerland's revised Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP, revDSG), effective September 2023, modernises Swiss data protection law to align with GDPR standards while maintaining Swiss-specific elements. Introduces privacy by design and default, mandatory breach notification, data protection impact assessments, and enhanced individual rights.

key_requirements.text: Process personal data lawfully, in good faith, and proportionately to the declared purpose
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: draft and approve a Data Processing Charter that maps each processing activity to a specific lawful basis and declared purpose. 2. Compliance officer: establish a quarterly review cycle to audit active processing against declared purposes and document any deviations. 3. Data handlers: complete mandatory training on lawful processing principles and proportionality assessment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide executed Data Processing Charter with lawful basis justifications for each processing activity; Compliance team: provide audit logs and corrective action records from quarterly reviews; HR team: provide training attendance and completion certificates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPA) explicitly confirming lawful basis, purpose limitation, and data minimisation commitments; vendors handling personal data: provide contractual confirmation that processing aligns with stated purposes only.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Processing Charter; DPAs with all vendors; training completion records; audit review documentation; purpose limitation clauses in contracts; corrective action logs.

key_requirements.text: Implement privacy by design and privacy by default from the outset
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT and Product teams: embed privacy requirements into system design specifications and conduct privacy impact review before development begins. 2. Architecture team: document default settings (e.g., pseudonymisation, minimal data collection) in technical design documents and implement them in code. 3. Compliance officer: perform privacy-by-design checklist sign-off for all new systems or major updates before deployment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide system design documents with privacy requirements matrix and default security/minimisation settings; Product team: provide user interface screenshots showing privacy-first defaults (e.g., opt-in consent, data minimisation toggles); Development team: provide code review logs confirming privacy controls implementation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/SaaS vendors: provide architecture diagrams and technical specifications confirming privacy-by-design principles (encryption, access controls, data minimisation defaults); development partners: provide signed Privacy-by-Design confirmation and compliance checklist for deliverables.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: System design documents with privacy requirements; privacy-by-design checklists (signed off); technical architecture diagrams; code review records; default settings documentation; vendor architecture certifications.

key_requirements.text: Notify FDPIC and affected individuals of data security breaches as soon as possible
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. IT Security and Legal teams: jointly define a Data Breach Response Plan specifying notification timelines (FDPIC notification within 72 hours where required, individuals 'as soon as possible'), roles, and notification templates. 2. IT team: implement automated breach detection and logging system with documented escalation procedures. 3. Compliance officer: maintain a breach register and conduct post-incident reviews with documented remediation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: provide Data Breach Response Plan, breach detection logs, and escalation procedures; Legal team: provide notification templates, FDPIC submission records, and individual notification evidence (email logs, delivery confirmations); Compliance team: provide breach register with incident timeline, impact assessment, and remediation actions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting/cloud providers: provide contractual obligation to notify organisation of breaches within 24 hours; security monitoring vendors: provide logs of detected incidents and alert escalation evidence; third-party processors: provide signed commitment to report breaches immediately upon discovery.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Data Breach Response Plan; breach detection system logs; breach register; FDPIC notification letters and receipts; individual notification emails with delivery confirmations; post-incident review reports; remediation action records.

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance officer: establish a DPIA screening matrix to identify high-risk processing activities (e.g., large-scale collection, automated decision-making, vulnerable populations). 2. Cross-functional team: conduct formal DPIA for each identified activity, documenting risks, mitigation measures, and residual risk sign-off. 3. Legal/Compliance: maintain a DPIA register and update assessments annually or when processing significantly changes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: provide DPIA screening matrix and risk categorisation criteria; Cross-functional teams: provide completed DPIA templates with risk analysis, mitigation strategy, and sign-offs; Legal team: provide DPIA register and update log; Risk/Audit team: provide validation of risk assessment methodology.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: provide their own DPIA (or summary) for processing activities they perform on behalf of the organisation; third-party risk assessors: provide DPIA templates and assessment tools; consultants: provide DPIA review and validation reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: DPIA screening matrix; completed DPIA templates for all high-risk activities; risk mitigation plans; DPIA register with update log; sign-off records from legal/compliance; annual DPIA refresh documentation.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of processing activities for organisations processing personal data systematically
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance officer: create and maintain a Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) document or database capturing all systematic data processing (purpose, categories, retention, recipients, technical/organisational measures). 2. Data stewards: provide data inventory input and keep RoPA current when processing activities change. 3. Compliance team: perform quarterly RoPA audits to ensure completeness and accuracy, with documented sign-offs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: provide Records of Processing Activities template and completed RoPA for all systematic processing; IT team: provide data flow diagrams and system inventory supporting RoPA entries; Data stewards: provide data processing activity descriptions and retention schedules; Audit team: provide quarterly RoPA verification and update logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: provide processing activity descriptions, sub-processor lists, and processing locations; cloud/hosting providers: provide data location, retention, and technical security documentation; third-party service providers: provide processing scope and duration commitments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed Records of Processing Activities document (or database); data flow diagrams; system inventory; processing activity descriptions; retention schedules; quarterly RoPA audit logs; sign-off records; DPA appendices referencing RoPA.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, erasure, restriction, and data portability
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal and IT teams: document and implement procedures for each data subject right (access, correction, erasure, restriction, portability), including request intake, verification, response, and documentation. 2. Compliance officer: establish a Data Subject Rights register and define Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for responding to requests (typically 30 days). 3. IT/Database teams: ensure technical capability to fulfill rights requests (e.g., data export formats, deletion mechanisms, audit trails).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide Data Subject Rights Procedures document and request templates; IT team: provide technical capability assessment and export/deletion mechanism documentation; Compliance team: provide Data Subject Rights register template and SLA definition; Customer Service/HR: provide staff training on rights request intake and handling.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors: provide contractual commitment to assist with fulfilling data subject rights requests; cloud/database vendors: provide data export, deletion, and portability tooling documentation; third-party identity/access providers: provide audit and correction capabilities for identity data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Subject Rights Procedures document; request intake templates and process flows; Data Subject Rights register with all requests and responses; response letters and evidence of fulfillment (data exports, deletion confirmations); SLA compliance logs; staff training records; vendor commitment letters or DPA clauses on rights assistance.

key_requirements.text: Ensure adequate data protection before transferring personal data abroad
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) documenting the legal framework, technical safeguards, and contractual protections in the destination country.
2. Execute a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) or Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with the recipient that explicitly governs cross-border processing and includes adequacy findings.
3. Implement encryption, pseudonymization, or other technical measures to ensure data remains protected during and after transfer, with evidence of deployment.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft or obtain Transfer Impact Assessment and execute Data Processing Agreement with recipient entity.
IT team: provide documentation of encryption protocols, pseudonymization techniques, and access controls applied to transferred data.
Data Protection Officer: maintain register of all international transfers and produce a signed transfer approval matrix.
vendor_actions: Service provider or recipient: sign Data Processing Agreement confirming adequacy of safeguards and commitment to Swiss nFADP principles.
Third-party processor: provide written attestation of technical and organizational security measures (e.g., ISO 27001 certificate or SOC 2 report).
Foreign recipient: confirm legal basis for processing in destination jurisdiction and provide Privacy Shield, Standard Contractual Clauses, or Binding Corporate Rules documentation.
compliance_evidence: Executed Data Processing Agreement or Standard Contractual Clauses with recipient signature and date.
Transfer Impact Assessment documenting destination country legal framework and risk mitigation controls.
Encryption or pseudonymization technical specification sheets and implementation logs.
Data Protection Officer approval memo or transfer register entry authorizing the international transfer.
Third-party security certificate (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) or attestation letter confirming safeguards.

key_requirements.text: Designate a data protection representative in Switzerland for foreign companies targeting Swiss residents
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Identify the foreign company as subject to nFADP (actively offering goods/services to Swiss residents or monitoring their behaviour) and confirm designation requirement applies.
2. Appoint a named individual or legal entity resident in Switzerland with authority to act on behalf of the foreign company and formally document this appointment.

3. Register the designated representative's contact details with the Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC) and publish the representative's details on the company's website.
internal_actions: Legal team: perform nFADP applicability assessment and draft appointment letter designating the Swiss representative with scope of authority and responsibilities.
Compliance Officer: prepare and submit FDPIC registration form and maintain current contact register of all designated representatives.
Communications/Marketing team: update company website to prominently display designated representative's name, address, telephone, and email in German, French, or Italian.
vendor_actions: Swiss legal entity or individual (representative): sign and return formal Appointment and Power of Attorney confirming acceptance of designation role and authority to receive regulatory communications.
compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter or Power of Attorney authorizing the Swiss representative, dated and executed by foreign company officer.
FDPIC registration confirmation or receipt showing designated representative details and company applicability.
Screenshot or printout of company website footer or dedicated page showing representative contact information in one of Switzerland's official languages.
NFADP applicability assessment memo documenting targeting of Swiss residents or cross-border monitoring activities triggering designation requirement.

regulations.penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations

regulations.name: Russia Federal Law No. 152-FZ on Personal Data
regulations.authority: Roskomnadzor (Federal Service for Supervision in the Sphere of Telecom, IT and Mass Communications)
regulations.summary: Russia Federal Law No. 152-FZ on Personal Data, as amended, mandates that personal data of Russian citizens must be stored on servers within Russia (data localisation). Operators must register with Roskomnadzor, implement security measures, obtain consent, and respond to subject access requests. Significant fines and website blocking are enforcement tools.

key_requirements.text: Store all personal data of Russian citizens on servers physically located within Russia
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data mapping exercise to identify all systems storing Russian citizen personal data. 2. Audit current server locations and migrate any offshore infrastructure to Russian Federation territory. 3. Establish a quarterly location verification process with infrastructure teams to confirm ongoing compliance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Infrastructure team: provide documented server inventory with physical locations, datacenter contracts, and network topology diagrams. IT Security: confirm encryption and access controls for in-country storage systems.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/hosting providers: furnish certification of server physical location within Russia, SLA commitments to maintain domestic infrastructure, and audit rights for location verification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed datacenter contracts showing Russian Federation addresses, IP geolocation reports, infrastructure audit reports, and quarterly attestation letters from hosting providers confirming server locations.

key_requirements.text: Register as a personal data operator with Roskomnadzor before commencing data processing
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Prepare operator registration application with Roskomnadzor detailing data processing scope, categories, and purposes. 2. Submit application before any personal data processing commences. 3. Maintain registration status and notify Roskomnadzor of material changes to processing activities within required timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft registration application and maintain current list of processing activities. Compliance: assign responsibility for monitoring registration renewal deadlines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable for this requirement; internal registration obligation only.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Roskomnadzor registration certificate or confirmation letter with registration number, dated application submission proof, and amendment notifications if processing scope changed.

key_requirements.text: Obtain written consent from data subjects for processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop consent templates for each processing purpose, specifying data categories, use, and retention period in clear Russian language. 2. Implement collection mechanism (web forms, checkboxes, signed agreements) that captures affirmative, documented consent before processing begins. 3. Maintain audit logs and archive signed consents for minimum 3 years.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create consent templates for all processing activities. IT/Product: integrate consent capture into systems with audit logging. Compliance: establish consent record retention and retrieval procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: provide confirmation of consent collection mechanisms and commit to processing data only under valid consent authority provided by organisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or electronically timestamped consent records, consent templates, system logs showing consent capture, audit trail exports, and consent management policy document.

key_requirements.text: Implement organisational and technical security measures scaled to data sensitivity level
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct data sensitivity classification to categorize personal data (public, internal, confidential, highly sensitive). 2. Design and deploy technical controls (encryption at rest/transit, access controls, monitoring) and organisational measures (training, access policies, incident procedures) proportionate to each category. 3. Document all measures in a security baseline policy and perform annual security assessments.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security: conduct risk assessment, implement encryption standards, configure access controls, deploy logging/monitoring systems. Compliance: classify data categories and map controls to each. HR: deliver annual security awareness training to staff.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud and third-party processors: provide SOC 2 Type II or equivalent audit reports, encryption certificates, detailed control documentation, and vulnerability assessment results.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data classification matrix, security control documentation, encryption certificates, access control policies, system audit logs, annual security assessment reports, SOC 2 reports from vendors, and training completion records.

key_requirements.text: Notify Roskomnadzor of data security incidents within 24 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an incident response team and define a security incident detection process (logs, alerts, user reports). 2. Create a documented incident response procedure including investigation steps, Roskomnadzor notification template, and 24-hour reporting deadline. 3. Configure automated monitoring and alerting to detect potential breaches within hours.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security: deploy SIEM/monitoring tools, define incident classification criteria, establish on-call escalation. Legal: prepare notification template and process. Compliance: assign incident notification owner with authority to contact Roskomnadzor.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Hosting/security vendors: commit to alerting organisation immediately upon detecting anomalies, provide incident reporting templates, and confirm availability for emergency support.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Incident response policy, SIEM configuration and alert logs, incident notification template, Roskomnadzor incident report copies with submission timestamps, and post-incident investigation reports.

key_requirements.text: Destroy or anonymise personal data within 30 days of fulfilling the purpose of processing
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define retention periods for each data type aligned with processing purpose, then establish automated deletion/anonymisation rules in systems. 2. Document data lifecycle procedures specifying triggers for disposal (purpose fulfilled, consent withdrawn, retention period expired). 3. Perform quarterly audits to verify deletion/anonymisation completion and maintain destruction logs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: implement data lifecycle management (DLM) tools or scheduled deletion scripts with logging. Compliance: maintain retention schedule and document destruction procedures. Database admins: verify retention rules are active and generate quarterly destruction reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/database vendors: confirm deletion/anonymisation capabilities, provide deletion confirmation logs, and commit to secure disposal or return of data upon contract termination.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data retention policy with timelines, DLM tool configuration documentation, quarterly destruction/anonymisation logs with timestamps, sample deletion confirmation reports, and vendor data disposal agreements.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, and withdrawal of consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented Data Subject Rights Request procedure specifying timelines (typically 30 days), required verification steps, and approval workflows for each right type (access, rectification, erasure, consent withdrawal).
2. Implement a request intake and tracking system (log, ticketing, or case management tool) to record all incoming requests, actions taken, and evidence of fulfilment.
3. Train relevant staff (customer service, IT, data handlers) on recognising valid requests and escalation protocols to Legal/Compliance for complex cases.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve Data Subject Rights Request Policy and standard response templates for each right type.
IT team: configure access controls and data export capabilities to enable timely retrieval, rectification, and deletion of personal data from all systems.
Compliance team: establish and maintain a centralized register of all received requests, responses issued, and dates of fulfilment.
vendor_actions: Processors and sub-processors: sign or amend Data Processing Agreements to confirm they will honour data subject requests forwarded by the controller within the same timeline.
Vendors: provide written commitment that they will not retain deleted personal data beyond the contractually agreed erasure date and will provide written confirmation of deletion.
