compliance_evidence: Signed, board-approved Data Subject Rights Request Policy with defined SLAs and procedures for each right type.
Data Subject Rights Request register or log (sample entries) showing request date, type, subject identification, action taken, completion date, and staff approval.
Sample request response letters (redacted) demonstrating timely, documented replies (access extracts, rectification confirmations, erasure confirmations, consent withdrawal acknowledgements).
Training records (attendance lists, sign-off sheets) for staff handling data subject requests.
Amended Data Processing Agreements with vendors explicitly covering data subject rights obligations and deletion confirmation requirements.

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfers are restricted; data must first be stored in Russia
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Map all personal data flows and identify any transfers outside Russia; document where data is currently stored (primary location must be Russia for all Russian residents' personal data).

2. Implement technical and organisational controls to ensure all personal data collected from or relating to Russian data subjects is first stored on servers or infrastructure physically located within Russia before any cross-border transfer is considered.

3. For any legitimate cross-border transfer, obtain explicit legal basis (user consent, adequacy determination, or contractual necessity) and maintain evidence of the basis and recipient jurisdiction.
internal_actions: IT team: conduct data location audit and provide evidence of all personal data storage locations; confirm that Russian resident data resides first in Russia-based infrastructure.
IT team: implement or strengthen data residency controls (geofencing, encryption keys, backup locations) to prevent unauthorised data exfiltration.
Legal/Compliance team: maintain a Cross-Border Data Transfer Register documenting any transfer, legal basis, recipient country, and data categories transferred.
vendor_actions: Cloud providers, hosting vendors, and processors: certify in writing that personal data of Russian residents will be stored in Russia-based data centres and that any secondary or backup copies remain within Russia unless explicitly authorised.

Vendors: provide detailed Data Centre Location Attestations and regular compliance certificates (e.g., certified location audits) confirming physical storage location and confirming no automated replication to non-Russia jurisdictions.
Third-party data processors: amend Data Processing Agreements to restrict sub-processing and sub-contractor locations to Russia; require prior written approval for any cross-border flow.
compliance_evidence: Data Flow and Storage Location Map showing all systems, databases, and backup locations with explicit confirmation that Russian resident personal data is stored in Russia first.
Infrastructure audit or IT security assessment report confirming Russia-based data centre usage and absence of automated cross-border replication.
Data Processing Agreements and amendments with all vendors explicitly prohibiting cross-border transfer without prior controller authorisation and requiring Russia-first storage.
Cross-Border Data Transfer Register (if any transfers have been made) with documented legal basis (consent forms, adequacy determination, or contractual justification) for each transfer.
Vendor Data Centre Location Attestations and periodic compliance certificates from hosting providers confirming physical server location within Russia.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation

regulations.name: Ukraine Law on Protection of Personal Data No. 2297-VI
regulations.authority: Parliament Commissioner for Human Rights (Ombudsman)
regulations.summary: Ukraine's Law on Protection of Personal Data (No. 2297-VI) governs the collection, processing, and storage of personal data. Aligns with Council of Europe Convention 108. Requires consent-based processing, data subject rights, database registration, and cross-border transfer protections.

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent or establish another legal basis before processing personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a legal basis assessment for each processing activity and document which basis applies (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests). 2. Create consent forms or notices in Ukrainian that clearly describe the purpose, scope, and recipients of processing, then obtain signed consent before data collection begins. 3. Maintain records of when and how consent was obtained, including copies of signed forms or system logs showing active opt-in.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written legal basis assessment matrix for all processing activities. Compliance team: draft and review consent templates and privacy notices. IT team: configure systems to timestamp and log consent collection.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party processors: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (DPA) specifying their processing scope and confirming they process only on instruction. Marketing vendors: supply evidence of end-user consent they have collected on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed consent forms or screenshots of consent capture interfaces; dated legal basis assessment document; Data Processing Agreements with all vendors; audit log exports showing consent timestamps; privacy notices in Ukrainian.

key_requirements.text: Register personal data databases with the State Service of Special Communications
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compile a complete inventory of all personal data databases your organisation maintains (including system name, data categories, retention periods, and lawful basis). 2. Prepare the registration application form(s) required by the State Service of Special Communications and submit within the statutory timeframe. 3. Retain the official registration certificate(s) and update the registry whenever you add new databases or significantly change processing.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide detailed database inventory including technical specifications, data categories, and retention schedules. Compliance team: prepare and submit registration application to State Service; maintain registry update log. Legal team: confirm lawful basis for each database entry.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data centre/hosting providers: confirm they will support your ability to provide accurate technical information to the regulator (database location, backup location, encryption status).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Original registration certificate(s) from State Service of Special Communications; dated database inventory spreadsheet; submitted registration application forms; email confirmation from regulator; registry update records.

key_requirements.text: Implement technical and organisational measures to protect personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) to identify risks and required safeguards for high-risk processing. 2. Implement technical controls: encryption at rest and in transit, access controls (role-based), pseudonymization where feasible, and secure deletion procedures. 3. Establish organisational measures: data handling policies, staff training records, audit logs, and incident response procedures documented in a Data Security Policy.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Security/IT team: conduct DPIA and provide findings report; implement and document encryption standards, access control matrices, and audit logging. HR team: provide training completion records for all staff handling personal data. Compliance team: draft and maintain Data Security Policy and related procedures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Cloud/SaaS providers: supply security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) and signed addendum confirming encryption, access controls, and subprocessor notification. IT contractors: provide evidence of security training completion.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data Protection Impact Assessment report with mitigation plan; encryption implementation certificate from IT; role-based access control matrix; staff training attendance records; Data Security Policy signed by management; audit logs covering 12 months; incident response procedure document.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, blocking, and deletion
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and test a process for receiving, validating, and fulfilling data subject requests (access, correction, blocking, deletion) within 30 days. 2. Implement a ticketing system or register to log all requests with dates received, action taken, and date fulfilled. 3. Train staff and establish templates for responses; ensure deletion is technically enforced across all systems and backups.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure request management system with audit trail; implement technical deletion procedures across production, backups, and archives; provide deletion completion logs. Legal team: draft request acknowledgement and response templates. Compliance team: establish request SLA and maintain register of all requests.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Data processors/hosting providers: confirm they will execute deletion and blocking requests within agreed timelines and provide deletion certificates. Archive/backup vendors: confirm data deletion from their systems or provide retention schedules.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data subject request register with timestamps and outcomes; deletion certificates from all systems; test cases showing request processing; response letter templates; staff training materials; system audit logs showing access and deletion actions.

key_requirements.text: Notify data subjects of any processing activities affecting their rights
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Review all processing activities and identify where notification is required (e.g., collection from data subject, new purpose, sharing with third parties, security incidents). 2. Draft privacy notices and update notices in clear Ukrainian, specifying what data is collected, why, who it is shared with, and data subject rights. 3. Deliver notices before or at the time of data collection; maintain logs of when notices were provided (via email, website, form, etc.).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and update privacy notices for each processing context (website, app, recruitment, customer contracts, etc.). Compliance team: maintain version control and distribution log of all notices. IT team: integrate privacy notice display into data collection systems and log when users acknowledge receipt.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Website/app developers: implement notice banner or pop-up with timestamp logging. Email service providers: confirm delivery logs showing when notices were sent. Third-party marketers: provide evidence of how they notified end-users of data collection on your behalf.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Privacy notice documents in Ukrainian, dated and versioned; user acceptance logs (cookies, checkbox timestamps); email delivery reports; screenshots of in-app notices; audit log showing when notices were displayed to each data subject.

key_requirements.text: Restrict cross-border transfers to countries with adequate data protection
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all third countries where your personal data is transferred and assess their data protection adequacy (e.g., EU/EEA, UK, approved third countries per Ukrainian government guidance). 2. For transfers to countries without adequate protection, implement contractual safeguards: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) signed with all recipients. 3. Document all transfer routes and review contracts at least annually; block transfers to countries with inadequate protections.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: conduct cross-border transfer impact assessment; identify all destination countries; provide list of current and planned third-country recipients. Compliance team: maintain register of all cross-border transfers and adequacy determinations. IT team: configure systems to prevent unauthorised transfers or log all export events.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Overseas data processors: provide signed Data Processing Agreement including Standard Contractual Clauses (EU model clauses or UK equivalent); confirm data location and any onward transfers. Cloud providers: declare data residency options and provide assurance that transfers meet Ukrainian law requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Cross-border transfer register with destination countries and adequacy assessment; signed SCCs or BCRs with all third-country recipients; data residency attestations from vendors; legal opinion on adequacy of safeguards; audit log of data exports with destination IP/country metadata.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a responsible person for personal data protection
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
how_to_meet: 1. Identify and formally designate a named individual (or Data Protection Officer equivalent) with explicit responsibility for personal data protection oversight, documented in an internal appointment memo or board resolution.
2. Define and publish the appointee's role scope, reporting line, and authority in writing, including responsibilities for policy implementation, breach response, and regulatory liaison.
3. Ensure the appointee has dedicated time allocation and access to necessary training, tools, and budget to discharge their duties effectively.
internal_actions: HR team: provide employment contract or role description amendment for the appointed person, signed by management.
Legal team: draft and obtain board/management approval for the appointment resolution or memo confirming title, responsibilities, and reporting structure.
Finance team: confirm budget allocation and approval for the role (salary, training, tools, external advice).
vendor_actions: Not typically applicable; this is an internal governance requirement. If outsourcing DPO functions to a consultant: request a signed Statement of Work confirming scope, availability, and accountability.
compliance_evidence: Signed board resolution or management appointment memo naming the responsible person and their role.
Current role description or job specification document.
Evidence of role publication (internal directory, org chart, staff handbook).
Training records and budget approval documentation for the appointee.

key_requirements.text: Comply with Council of Europe Convention 108 requirements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a gap analysis mapping your current data protection practices against Council of Europe Convention 108 principles (lawfulness, fairness, accuracy, security, retention limits, and individual rights).

2. Update or create a comprehensive Data Protection Policy that explicitly references Convention 108 requirements and incorporates principles of data minimization, purpose limitation, and security safeguards.
3. Ensure all data processing agreements with third parties and data subject consent forms reference Convention 108 compliance obligations.
internal_actions: Legal team: conduct Convention 108 gap analysis and provide written findings; draft or revise Data Protection Policy to align with Convention 108 principles.
IT team: provide audit report on current technical and organisational security measures (encryption, access controls, incident logging) against Convention 108 security standards.
HR/Admin team: provide copy of all current privacy notices, consent forms, and data retention schedules for review against Convention 108 requirements.
vendor_actions: All data processors/vendors: provide signed Data Processing Agreements (or addenda) explicitly confirming compliance with Convention 108 and Ukraine LPPD obligations, including security, confidentiality, and sub-processor controls.
Third-party services (cloud, HR systems, CRM): provide security certification (ISO 27001, SOC 2, or equivalent) or detailed security questionnaire responses demonstrating Convention 108 alignment.
compliance_evidence: Completed Convention 108 gap analysis report with recommendations.
Signed and dated Data Protection Policy explicitly referencing Convention 108 compliance.
Updated Data Processing Agreements with all vendors, signed by both parties.
Current privacy notices and consent forms showing Convention 108 alignment.
IT security audit report or SOC 2/ISO 27001 certificates from key vendors.
Documented data retention schedule aligned with Convention 108 principles.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines and potential criminal liability for severe violations

regulations.name: Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA)
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) and Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
regulations.summary: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act prohibits US persons and issuers from bribing foreign government officials to obtain or retain business. It applies to US companies and citizens worldwide, as well as foreign companies listed on US exchanges. The FCPA also includes accounting provisions requiring accurate books and internal controls.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit payments of anything of value to foreign officials to obtain or retain business; apply to all issuers, domestic concerns, and agents acting in the US
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal: corporations up to $2M per count; individuals up to $250K and 5 years imprisonment; unlimited civil penalties
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-bribery policy; gift and hospitality register; third-party payment logs
how_to_meet: 1. Draft and approve a formal Anti-Bribery and Corruption Policy explicitly prohibiting payments of value to foreign officials, with clear definitions of prohibited conduct and applicable jurisdictions.
2. Distribute the policy to all employees, contractors, and agents globally, with signed acknowledgment forms retained centrally.
3. Establish a compliance hotline and escalation procedure for reporting suspected violations, with documented response protocols.
internal_actions: Legal team: produce final Anti-Bribery Policy document with board approval minutes.
HR team: compile signed employee acknowledgment forms and distribution records.
Compliance team: document hotline vendor contract and call/report logs for 12 months.
vendor_actions: All third-party agents and distributors: sign written acknowledgment of Anti-Bribery Policy and certify compliance.
Contract counterparties: provide written representations that they do not employ or engage foreign officials in any capacity.
compliance_evidence: Board-approved Anti-Bribery and Corruption Policy (dated and signed).
Signed acknowledgment forms from all current and new hires (HR records).
Vendor/agent certification letters and signed policy acknowledgments.
Compliance hotline contract and sample call logs redacted for privacy.

key_requirements.text: Issuers must maintain books and records that accurately reflect all transactions in reasonable detail (accounting provisions)
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; disgorgement; civil penalties
key_requirements.evidence_guide: GL reconciliation records; internal audit workpapers; financial controls documentation
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Chart of Accounts and transaction coding policy that requires all payments to foreign parties to be recorded with sufficient detail (payee name, business purpose, amount, date, approval level).
2. Implement mandatory approval workflows in the accounting system requiring pre-approval for all foreign vendor payments above a defined threshold.
3. Conduct a quarterly reconciliation between purchase orders, invoices, and bank payments to verify accuracy and identify anomalies.
internal_actions: Finance team: provide Chart of Accounts structure, GL account descriptions, and transaction coding standards documentation.
IT team: export 12 months of transaction logs showing approval routing, timestamps, and approval chain for foreign payments.
Finance team: deliver quarterly reconciliation reports and exception logs for the past 24 months.
vendor_actions: All foreign vendors and consultants: submit detailed invoices itemizing services rendered, dates, and business purpose in writing.
Service providers: provide monthly billing statements with clear description of work performed and no vague or round-number charges.
compliance_evidence: Chart of Accounts documentation and GL account coding policy (signed by CFO).
System-generated approval workflow audit trails for foreign payments (12 months).
Reconciliation reports matching POs, invoices, and bank statements (quarterly, signed by Finance Controller).
Vendor invoice samples showing itemized descriptions and supporting documentation.

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to prevent and detect bribery
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; deferred prosecution agreements
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Internal controls assessment; SOX 404 mapping; control testing results
how_to_meet: 1. Design and document an Internal Control Framework specific to FCPA risks, including segregation of duties (requester, approver, and payment processor roles must be separate), approval limits by transaction value, and exception handling.

2. Perform a risk assessment to identify high-risk payment categories (consulting fees, government tenders, hospitality) and implement supplementary controls (e.g., independent verification, manager sign-off).
3. Execute a Control Testing Plan semi-annually: sample transactions in each category, verify approval documentation, and document test results with sign-off from Finance and Compliance leadership.
internal_actions: Finance and Compliance teams: jointly produce an FCPA-focused Internal Control Framework document with risk assessment and control procedures.
IT team: provide system access matrix showing segregation of duties across accounts payable, GL posting, and payment processing modules.
Finance team: deliver Control Testing Reports (semi-annual) documenting sample size, exceptions found, remediation, and manager sign-off.
vendor_actions: Third-party auditors: provide SOC 2 reports or equivalent control attestations if processing payments or financial data on the company's behalf.
Vendors with high-risk classifications: submit to compliance due diligence questionnaire and allow internal audit visits if requested.
compliance_evidence: Internal Control Framework and FCPA Risk Assessment (dated, signed by CFO and Chief Compliance Officer).
System configuration documentation showing segregation of duties and approval workflows.
Control Testing Reports (semi-annual) with sample transactions, test results, and management sign-off.
Exception log and remediation tracking for any control failures identified.

key_requirements.text: Conduct adequate due diligence on all third parties (agents, consultants, distributors) acting on behalf of the company in foreign markets
key_requirements.consequence: Successor liability in M&A; prosecution for wilful blindness
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party DDQ responses; background check reports; red flag escalation records
how_to_meet: 1. Create and maintain a Vendor Risk Classification Matrix that assesses all third parties for FCPA risk based on jurisdiction, business function (agent, consultant, distributor), and government interaction level.

2. Require all foreign agents and consultants to complete a standardized Due Diligence Questionnaire covering beneficial ownership, sanctioned list screening, government relationships, and FCPA compliance commitment.
3. Perform sanctions screening (OFAC, BIS, UN lists) on all vendors before engagement and repeat annually; document results in a centralized Vendor Management System.
internal_actions: Compliance team: develop and publish Vendor Risk Classification Matrix and Due Diligence Questionnaire template.
Procurement team: maintain a centralized register of all third parties with risk ratings, DDQ completion dates, and screening results.
Compliance team: produce annual Vendor Screening Report documenting all new vendor onboarding and screening outcomes.
vendor_actions: All foreign agents, consultants, and distributors: complete a signed Due Diligence Questionnaire with beneficial ownership disclosure and certification of no government affiliation.
Vendors classified as high-risk: submit to background verification by a third-party vendor screening provider and provide results to the company.
compliance_evidence: Vendor Risk Classification Matrix and Due Diligence Questionnaire (version controlled, approved by Compliance and Legal).
Completed DDQ forms for all active third parties (signed by vendor authorized representative).
Sanctions screening reports (OFAC/BIS lookups) showing date run, results, and no-match confirmation.
Centralized Vendor Management System extract showing all vendors, risk ratings, and screening date stamps.

key_requirements.text: Train all employees and third-party agents on FCPA requirements, anti-bribery policy, and escalation procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Reduced mitigation credit in DOJ/SEC investigations
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; content; assessment results; refresher schedule
how_to_meet: 1. Develop a mandatory FCPA training curriculum covering the law's scope, prohibited conduct, real-world case studies, and company policy; require completion by all employees, board members, and agents within 30 days of hire and annually thereafter.
2. Implement role-specific training modules for high-risk functions (sales, government relations, procurement) covering red flags, due diligence processes, and escalation protocols.
3. Track and document training completion with signed attestations; measure effectiveness through annual compliance assessments and include FCPA topics in performance evaluations for key roles.
internal_actions: Learning & Development team: design and deliver FCPA training curriculum (online or in-person) with passing quiz and signed acknowledgment.
HR team: maintain a centralized Training Record System with completion dates, scores, and signed attestations for all employees and contractors.
Compliance team: produce an annual Training Completion Report showing completion rates by department and remediation of non-completions.
vendor_actions: All third-party agents and consultants: certify completion of FCPA training from the company or equivalent anti-corruption training, with signed confirmation.
Distributors and resellers operating in high-risk jurisdictions: undergo annual FCPA refresher training and provide proof of completion.
compliance_evidence: FCPA Training Curriculum document with learning objectives, case studies, and quiz (approved by Compliance and Legal).
Training Records System extract showing employee name, completion date, score, and signed acknowledgment for 100% of workforce.
Annual Training Completion Report documenting completion rates, remediation actions, and exceptions (signed by HR Director and Chief Compliance Officer).
Vendor training certifications and signed acknowledgments for all active third-party agents.

regulations.penalties: Corporate fines up to twice the benefit obtained; individuals up to USD 5 million fine and 5 years imprisonment; SEC can impose disgorgement and civil penalties

regulations.name: UK Bribery Act 2010
regulations.authority: Serious Fraud Office (SFO) and Crown Prosecution Service (CPS)
regulations.summary: The UK Bribery Act 2010 is one of the world's strictest anti-bribery laws. It criminalises active and passive bribery, bribery of foreign public officials, and — uniquely — the failure of commercial organisations to prevent bribery by associated persons. The 'adequate procedures' defence requires organisations to implement genuine anti-bribery controls.

key_requirements.text: Commercial organisations must have "adequate procedures" in place to prevent bribery by associated persons
key_requirements.consequence: Unlimited fine for organisation; up to 10 years imprisonment for individuals
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-bribery policy; six-step adequate procedures documentation; board commitment evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and document an Anti-Bribery Policy covering all associated persons (employees, agents, contractors, subsidiaries) with clear definitions of prohibited conduct and consequences. 2. Establish a governance framework designating accountability for procedure implementation, monitoring, and annual review. 3. Embed procedures into standard contracts, onboarding, and operational workflows with documented sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Anti-Bribery Policy and Procedures document with board-approved governance matrix. Compliance team: create procedure implementation checklist and assign ownership. HR team: provide evidence of policy communication to all employees (emails, training records, acknowledgements). Finance team: document controls in procurement and payment authorization processes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: sign Bribery Act Compliance Clause in contracts acknowledging their obligations as associated persons. Procurement partners: confirm receipt and understanding of Anti-Bribery Policy in writing. Third-party agents/distributors: provide formal anti-bribery compliance certifications.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Anti-Bribery Policy document with board approval date. Procedure implementation matrix with sign-off dates. Employee acknowledgement records (signed forms or learning management system completion logs). Vendor contract clauses with execution dates. Annual procedure review documentation showing updates and board re-approval.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit offering, promising, or giving a financial or other advantage to induce improper performance
key_requirements.consequence: Unlimited fine; 10 years imprisonment; SFO prosecution
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Gifts register; pre-approval records; hospitality log; escalation log
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Embed explicit prohibitions in the Anti-Bribery Policy defining impermissible inducements (cash, gifts, hospitality, facilitation payments) and improper performance (regulatory breaches, contract violations, preferential treatment). 2. Establish clear approval workflows requiring declaration and vetting of all business gifts, hospitality, and payments to public officials. 3. Communicate prohibitions through mandatory training and include non-compliance consequences in disciplinary procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft prohibition definitions and improper performance scenarios in Policy. Compliance team: create Gift and Hospitality Register template with approval thresholds and mandatory fields. HR team: develop mandatory annual anti-bribery training module with assessment. Finance team: implement transaction approval system flagging payments to high-risk individuals or countries.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: commit in writing to the same prohibition standards in supply agreements. Agents and distributors: complete Gifts and Hospitality Declaration annually. Third-party facilitators: certify they will not make payments or offer benefits on the organisation's behalf without prior written approval.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Policy document containing detailed prohibition definitions and examples. Signed Gift and Hospitality Register entries with approval authorizations. Training completion certificates for all employees (with dates and scores). Disciplinary records showing enforcement of prohibitions. Vendor compliance confirmations and signed gift/hospitality declarations.

key_requirements.text: Conduct a proportionate risk assessment of bribery risks across business activities and geographies
key_requirements.consequence: Adequate procedures defence unavailable without documented risk assessment
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Bribery risk assessment report; country risk ratings; annual review records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a documented risk assessment across all business units, geographies, and transaction types (procurement, sales, licensing, partnerships) identifying bribery risk factors (corruption indices, political exposure, third-party reliance). 2. Score and map risks by likelihood and impact, documenting findings in a Risk Register with mitigating control assignments. 3. Review and update the assessment annually and after material business changes (acquisitions, new markets, regulatory changes).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: design and execute risk assessment questionnaire covering all business functions and geographies. Risk team: compile external corruption indices and regulatory data by jurisdiction. Finance/Procurement teams: provide transaction volume and counterparty data for high-risk categories. Board/Audit committee: formally review and approve the Risk Register with documented sign-off.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vendors in high-risk jurisdictions: complete risk questionnaire disclosing beneficial ownership, regulatory history, and previous sanctions. Agents/distributors: provide information on markets served and competitor relationships. Third-party providers: disclose any previous government investigations or corruption allegations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Risk Assessment Report documenting methodology, risk factors, and scoring criteria. Completed Risk Register with entries for each identified risk, mitigation controls, and owner assignments. Corruption Perception Index or equivalent documentation for assessed geographies. Board/Audit Committee minutes approving the Risk Register. Annual review records showing reassessment dates and updates.

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence on individuals and entities who perform services on behalf of the organisation
key_requirements.consequence: Prosecution of organisation for failure to prevent bribery by associated person
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party due diligence policy; DDQ responses; background checks; ongoing monitoring
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish due diligence procedures proportionate to risk level, requiring beneficial ownership verification, regulatory sanctions screening, and corruption/fraud history checks for all agents, distributors, and service providers. 2. Document findings in a Due Diligence Register and obtain signed compliance certifications from each third party. 3. Conduct enhanced due diligence for high-risk jurisdictions or high-value relationships and repeat screening annually or when circumstances change.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/Legal team: design due diligence questionnaire and screening protocols aligned to risk assessment findings. Procurement team: establish sanctions screening tool (e.g., integration with OFAC, UN, EU lists) and document screening procedures. Finance team: verify third-party banking details and payment flows. HR/Recruitment: apply due diligence to consultant and contractor onboarding.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All new vendors: complete Due Diligence Questionnaire with beneficial ownership declarations and certification of no sanctions history. High-risk vendors: provide audited financial statements and evidence of regulatory compliance. Agents/distributors: certify no conflicts of interest and submit to enhanced background checks. Existing vendors: re-certify annually and notify of any material changes in ownership or regulatory status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Due Diligence Questionnaire template with completion instructions. Due Diligence Register with entries for each vendor including screening dates, results, and approval. Sanctions screening reports with dates and tool used (e.g., OFAC confirmation). Signed vendor compliance certifications and beneficial ownership declarations. Annual re-screening records. Enhanced due diligence reports for high-risk categories.

regulations.penalties: Unlimited corporate fines; individuals up to 10 years imprisonment; debarment from public contracts

regulations.name: France Sapin II Law — Anti-Corruption and Transparency
regulations.authority: Agence Française Anticorruption (AFA)
regulations.summary: France's Sapin II Law imposes mandatory anti-corruption compliance programmes on large companies (500+ employees, €100M+ turnover). Enforced by the Agence Française Anticorruption (AFA), it requires a specific eight-pillar compliance programme. AFA can conduct controls and impose sanctions without criminal proceedings.

key_requirements.text: Entities with 500+ employees and €100M+ turnover must implement an anti-corruption compliance programme (8 prescribed measures)
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; fines up to €200K for individuals and €1M for entities; compulsory compliance programme order
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption programme documentation; all 8 measure implementation evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a compliance officer and establish an anti-corruption steering committee with representatives from Legal, HR, Finance, and business units. 2. Document and implement the eight prescribed measures: risk mapping, due diligence procedures, code of conduct, training, internal controls, whistleblowing system, disciplinary policy, and third-party management framework. 3. Obtain board approval for the compliance programme and assign accountability for each measure to specific departments.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft anti-corruption code of conduct and disciplinary framework; Compliance Officer: prepare written compliance programme document detailing all eight measures and governance structure; Finance team: establish internal audit protocols for corruption risk controls; HR team: design mandatory anti-corruption training curriculum.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External compliance consultant: review and validate the eight-measure programme for adequacy; Training provider: deliver certified anti-corruption training module; External auditor: conduct baseline compliance assessment.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed board resolution approving compliance programme; documented anti-corruption code of conduct; compliance officer appointment letter; training attendance records; internal audit reports; written procedures for each of the eight measures.

key_requirements.text: Establish a risk mapping identifying and prioritising corruption risks exposure
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; programme inadequacy finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk mapping report; risk assessment methodology; annual review records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a structured risk assessment across all business functions, geographies, and customer segments to identify corruption exposure (bribery, facilitation payments, conflicts of interest, sanctions evasion). 2. Document findings in a formal risk register and map each identified risk to business unit ownership and existing controls. 3. Prioritise risks by likelihood and impact, and review and update the mapping annually or when significant business changes occur.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance Officer: design and execute risk mapping questionnaire across all departments; Finance/Audit team: analyse transaction data and procurement patterns for anomalies; Legal team: assess jurisdictional and sectoral corruption risk exposure; Business unit heads: provide risk assessment input for their areas.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External risk consultant: facilitate risk mapping workshops and validate findings; Third-party intelligence provider: supply sanctions and PEP screening data to inform risk ratings.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Formal risk mapping document with identified risks, assessed likelihood/impact ratings, and assigned risk owners; annual risk review and update records; board or audit committee meeting minutes approving the risk map; supporting questionnaires and analysis.

key_requirements.text: Implement a secure and confidential whistleblowing alert system
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; Sapin II non-compliance finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Alert system documentation; confidentiality procedures; investigation handling records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a dedicated whistleblowing channel (hotline, email, online portal) that is independent, confidential, and accessible 24/7 to employees and external stakeholders. 2. Document clear procedures for receiving, logging, investigating, and resolving reports; ensure anonymity protections and non-retaliation guarantees are legally enforceable. 3. Designate an independent officer (internal or external) to manage the system and report findings to the audit committee or board quarterly.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft whistleblowing policy with confidentiality and anti-retaliation clauses; IT team: procure and configure secure whistleblowing platform with encryption and audit logs; Compliance Officer: establish investigation and escalation procedures; HR team: communicate policy to all employees and embed in onboarding.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Whistleblowing platform provider: deliver secure system with confidentiality guarantees and provide incident logging reports; External ombudsperson or investigator: provide independent investigation and reporting service.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published whistleblowing policy and procedures; platform provider contract and security certification; whistleblowing register with anonymised reports received; investigation summary reports; evidence of non-retaliation (e.g., HR records); audit committee meeting minutes reviewing reports and outcomes.

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence on third parties (clients, suppliers, intermediaries) presenting corruption risk
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; programme inadequacy
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party due diligence procedures; DDQ responses; risk-based screening records
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a third-party due diligence procedure defining risk criteria (jurisdiction, industry, transaction value, business relationship type) and assessment methods (questionnaires, background checks, sanctions screening, beneficial ownership verification). 2. Apply the procedure to all material suppliers, clients, intermediaries, and agents before contract signature and at periodic intervals (annually or as risk profile changes). 3. Document due diligence findings and maintain approval records; escalate medium/high-risk third parties for enhanced review by senior management or legal counsel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: integrate due diligence questionnaires into vendor onboarding workflow and maintain assessment records; Legal team: draft third-party due diligence policy and contract clauses requiring compliance with anti-corruption standards; Compliance Officer: establish risk scoring methodology and review procedures; Finance team: maintain third-party registry with due diligence status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party screening provider: deliver sanctions, PEP, and adverse media screening reports for all third parties; Background check service: provide criminal, financial, and corporate registration verification; Third-party intelligence vendor: supply beneficial ownership and conflict-of-interest data.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Documented third-party due diligence policy and risk assessment criteria; completed due diligence questionnaires and screening reports for representative sample of third parties; approval records and risk ratings; contracts containing anti-corruption representations and warranties; periodic due diligence review documentation; audit log of changes to third-party risk status.

regulations.penalties: AFA injunctions; personal liability of executive: fines up to EUR 200,000; corporate fines up to EUR 1 million; judicial public compliance monitoring

regulations.name: Malaysia Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2009 — Section 17A Corporate Liability
regulations.authority: Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC)
regulations.summary: Section 17A of Malaysia's Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2009, effective June 2020, introduces corporate liability for corruption by associated persons. Organisations can avoid liability by proving 'adequate procedures' were in place. The Guidelines on Adequate Procedures (T.R.U.S.T.) provide the compliance framework.

key_requirements.text: Implement T.R.U.S.T. adequate procedures: Top-level commitment, Risk assessment, Undertake control measures, Systematic review, Training and communication
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Secure written commitment from Board/C-suite endorsing anti-corruption framework and allocate dedicated compliance resources. 2. Conduct structured corruption risk assessment across all business units and document control gaps. 3. Design and roll out mandatory training aligned to risk profiles, establish review cycle (quarterly/annually), and maintain training records and completion certificates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: obtain Board resolution or signed commitment letter; Legal team: compile risk assessment report with identified vulnerabilities and control recommendations; HR team: provide training attendance registers and assessment scores; Internal Audit: document systematic review schedule and findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign acknowledgement of anti-corruption code of conduct; key vendors: provide corruption risk certifications and compliance attestations relevant to their contracted services.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board resolution or CEO/MD signed anti-corruption commitment letter; enterprise-wide risk assessment report with date and sign-off; training curriculum and participant attendance logs with dates; internal audit review reports showing systematic monitoring; T.R.U.S.T. framework implementation checklist signed by compliance lead.

key_requirements.text: Top management must actively lead and endorse anti-corruption culture
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Obtain formal Board resolution or C-suite declaration explicitly committing to anti-corruption leadership and tone-from-the-top. 2. Communicate leadership commitment through company-wide channels (email, intranet, town halls) with documented evidence of dissemination. 3. Embed anti-corruption expectations into executive performance objectives and compensation frameworks.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretariat: draft and circulate Board resolution or management declaration; Communications team: prepare and distribute leadership message to all staff with read receipts or acknowledgement mechanism; HR team: update executive KPIs and compensation policy to reflect anti-corruption accountability; Compliance team: maintain records of all communications.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Senior management and Board members: sign individual anti-corruption pledge or attestation documents confirming personal commitment to ethical conduct.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Board resolution or management declaration dated and minutes-recorded; email distribution records showing leadership message sent to all staff with open/read confirmations; updated executive performance agreements reflecting anti-corruption KPIs; signed individual pledges from C-suite executives; internal communications (intranet posts, newsletters) showcasing leadership anti-corruption messaging.

key_requirements.text: Conduct enterprise-wide corruption risk assessment and document findings
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage external or internal audit to facilitate enterprise-wide corruption risk assessment covering all departments, geographies, and business processes. 2. Document identified risks, inherent risk ratings, and residual risk after control measures using a structured risk register. 3. Obtain formal sign-off from Board/Audit Committee and establish remediation timeline for high-risk gaps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Internal Audit or Compliance team: coordinate assessment across all business units (procurement, sales, HR, operations); Finance team: provide transaction data and vendor/customer relationship information; Department heads: participate in risk workshops and provide process documentation; Legal team: document legal and regulatory exposure.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors or risk consultants (if engaged): deliver signed risk assessment report with methodology and findings; key third-party service providers: complete vendor risk questionnaires assessing their corruption exposure and controls.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed corruption risk assessment report with assessment date, scope, and methodology; enterprise-wide risk register documenting identified risks, ratings (high/medium/low), and ownership; Board or Audit Committee meeting minutes approving the assessment; remediation action plan with timelines and responsible parties; evidence of stakeholder consultation (attendance lists, workshop notes).

key_requirements.text: Implement controls proportionate to identified risks including third-party due diligence
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map identified corruption risks to specific control activities (e.g., approval hierarchies, segregation of duties, competitive bidding) and document in a controls matrix. 2. Establish third-party due diligence procedures including vendor risk questionnaires, beneficial ownership checks, and sanctions screening before engagement. 3. Embed anti-corruption clauses into all vendor contracts and monitor compliance through periodic audits or certifications.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain controls matrix linking risks to mitigating controls; Procurement team: design and implement vendor due diligence checklist and sanctions screening process; Legal team: draft standard anti-corruption contract clauses; Finance team: establish vendor approval workflow requiring compliance clearance; Internal Audit: schedule vendor compliance audits.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors/service providers: complete anti-corruption risk questionnaire, provide evidence of beneficial ownership, and undergo sanctions/PEP screening; key vendors: sign anti-corruption addendum to master service agreement; third-party intermediaries: provide compliance certifications and undertakings on corruption risks.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Risk-to-control mapping matrix or controls assessment document; vendor due diligence policy and procedures document; signed vendor risk questionnaires and screening reports (sanctions, PEP, adverse media); executed vendor contracts containing anti-corruption clauses; vendor approval workflow documentation; internal audit reports on vendor compliance; signed anti-corruption addenda or vendor certifications.

key_requirements.text: Establish whistleblowing and grievance reporting mechanisms
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and launch confidential whistleblowing mechanism (hotline, email, online portal) accessible to all employees and external parties with clear escalation protocols. 2. Establish documented grievance handling procedures ensuring impartial investigation, confidentiality, and protection against retaliation. 3. Publicise the reporting channels through induction, training, posters, and intranet with clear guidance on what constitutes reportable conduct.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Compliance team: engage whistleblowing platform provider or establish hotline infrastructure; Legal team: draft whistleblowing and investigation procedures including retaliation protections; Compliance team: create reporting channel user guide; Communications team: design awareness campaign materials; Internal Audit: maintain log of all reports received and investigation status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Whistleblowing platform provider: sign service agreement guaranteeing confidentiality, independent investigation support, and audit trail generation; external investigators (if used): sign NDA and commit to documented investigation protocol.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published whistleblowing policy and procedures document; screenshots or documentation of reporting channels (hotline, email, portal) with access instructions; induction materials and training slides referencing reporting channels; awareness posters and intranet content; investigation log recording reports received, investigation outcomes, and timelines; sample investigation reports with conclusions and actions taken; staff survey or testing results confirming awareness of reporting mechanisms.

key_requirements.text: Provide regular anti-corruption training tailored to roles and risk exposure
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design role-based anti-corruption training curriculum differentiated by risk exposure (high-risk roles: procurement, sales, finance receive enhanced content; all staff receive foundational training). 2. Deliver training in multiple formats (classroom, e-learning, refresher sessions) with documented attendance, comprehension assessments, and completion certificates. 3. Implement mandatory annual or periodic retraining with updates reflecting new risks, regulatory changes, and case studies relevant to the industry.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Learning & Development team: develop training content and learning management system (LMS) enrollment; Compliance team: define role-based training requirements and assessment criteria; Department heads: nominate participants and track completion rates; Internal Audit: monitor training compliance and non-completion escalation; HR team: maintain training records for audit.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training provider (if external): deliver customized curriculum aligned to organisation's risk profile and deliver completion certificates; LMS vendor: provide attendance reports, assessment scores, and audit trail functionality.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Role-based training curriculum and content documents; training delivery records showing dates, attendees, and completion status; LMS reports with enrollment and completion metrics; training assessment or quiz results; completion certificates issued to participants; evidence of refresher training cycles; annual training compliance summary report; training effectiveness survey results showing staff understanding of anti-corruption obligations.

key_requirements.text: Undertake periodic reviews and audits of the anti-corruption programme effectiveness
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Establish an annual audit schedule conducted by internal audit or external firm, documenting scope, methodology, and risk areas covered (e.g. procurement, gifts & hospitality, conflict of interest declarations).
2. Review audit findings against baseline metrics (e.g. number of breaches, control gaps, training completion rates) and document remediation actions with assigned owners and deadlines.
3. Present consolidated audit reports to the Board or Audit Committee quarterly, including trend analysis and effectiveness ratings of anti-corruption controls.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Internal Audit team: prepare annual audit plan with risk-based scope; document testing procedures and sample sizes used. Legal team: retain audit reports, management responses, and evidence of Board/Committee approval and discussion minutes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External Audit firm (if engaged): deliver formal audit report with detailed findings, control ratings, and remediation recommendations; provide access to working papers upon request.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed annual audit plans, completed audit reports with findings and ratings, management action plans with sign-off dates, Board/Audit Committee minutes recording review and approval, trend analysis reports showing year-on-year control improvements or degradation.

key_requirements.text: Ensure contractors, agents, and subsidiaries are subject to equivalent standards
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Develop a standardised anti-corruption clause for all contractor, agent, and subsidiary agreements, requiring compliance with MACC Act Section 17A and equivalent internal standards; include audit rights and termination provisions.

2. Conduct baseline due diligence on all existing and new contractors/agents/subsidiaries, documenting their anti-corruption policies, POCA/PCA status, and beneficial ownership; retain records in a central register.

3. Include anti-corruption compliance as a standing agenda item in quarterly governance reviews with contractors/subsidiaries; request annual certifications of compliance and copies of any investigations or breaches.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain template anti-corruption clauses and due diligence checklists; create and update contractor/subsidiary register with compliance status. Compliance team: document baseline assessments and maintain certification logs with dates and signatories.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Contractors/Agents: sign acknowledgement of anti-corruption terms; provide annual written certification of compliance and copies of their own anti-corruption policies. Subsidiaries: submit annual compliance reports, audit results (if conducted), and declaration of any investigations or sanctions.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed contractor/agent agreements with anti-corruption clauses, completed due diligence checklists and risk assessments, contractor/subsidiary compliance register (dated and reviewed), annual certifications from contractors/agents/subsidiaries with signatory details, audit committee minutes recording oversight of third-party compliance.

regulations.penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years

regulations.name: Italy Legislative Decree 231/2001 — Corporate Administrative Liability
regulations.authority: Italian Judicial Authorities (Public Prosecutor's Offices)
regulations.summary: Italy's Legislative Decree 231/2001 establishes administrative liability for legal entities for certain crimes (including corruption, fraud, market manipulation, environmental crimes) committed by directors, employees, or persons under their control. Organisations can avoid liability by demonstrating an effective Organisational, Management, and Control Model (Modello 231) was in place.

key_requirements.text: Adopt an Organisational, Management and Control Model (Modello 231) to prevent predicate offences by company personnel
key_requirements.consequence: Administrative fines; disqualification from contracts; seizure of profits; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Adopted Modello 231 document; board resolution; model review dates
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a risk assessment identifying predicate offences applicable to your industry and company operations (e.g. corruption, fraud, environmental crimes).
2. Draft a comprehensive Modello 231 document specifying prohibited conduct, control procedures, roles/responsibilities, and sanctions for breach.
3. Formally adopt the Model by board resolution and communicate it to all employees with mandatory acknowledgement of receipt.
internal_actions: Legal team: produce risk mapping document and draft Modello 231 tailored to company size and sector.
HR team: maintain signed employee acknowledgement forms and training attendance records.
Finance team: document budget allocation and audit schedule for Model oversight.
vendor_actions: External counsel: provide written opinion on Model adequacy under D.Lgs. 231/2001.
Third-party service providers (e.g. payroll, logistics): return signed Data Processing Agreements confirming compliance with Model requirements.
compliance_evidence: Board resolution approving Modello 231 with date and signatures.
Final Modello 231 document (Italian version) with version control and approval sign-off.
Risk assessment report identifying applicable predicate offences.
Signed employee acknowledgement records (dated within 30 days of adoption).
External counsel legal opinion letter.

key_requirements.text: Appoint an independent Supervisory Body (Organismo di Vigilanza) to oversee implementation of the Model
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of OdV removes the shield of organisational liability defence
key_requirements.evidence_guide: OdV appointment; OdV meeting minutes; annual OdV report to board
how_to_meet: 1. Define the Supervisory Body structure (individual or committee), independence criteria, and reporting line—ensuring members have no conflicts of interest with management.
2. Appoint qualified members with compliance/legal expertise via formal board resolution, documenting their independence and absence of prior violations.
3. Establish written Terms of Reference specifying the Body's powers, investigative authority, confidentiality protections, and direct access to the board/audit committee.
internal_actions: Board/Governance team: pass formal resolution appointing Supervisory Body members with curriculum vitae and conflict-of-interest declarations.
Legal team: draft and approve Terms of Reference and Operating Charter for the Body.
Finance team: allocate dedicated budget and resources (e.g. investigation tools, legal support) to the Body.
vendor_actions: External audit firm or compliance consultant: provide background checks and independence certification for appointed members.
Legal counsel: review Terms of Reference for regulatory adequacy and provide written confirmation.
compliance_evidence: Board resolution appointing Supervisory Body members with effective date.
Signed declaration of independence and conflict-of-interest disclosures from each member.
Formal Terms of Reference/Charter document approved by board.
Curriculum vitae demonstrating compliance/legal expertise of each member.
Evidence of dedicated resources (budget codes, office allocation, tool subscriptions).

key_requirements.text: Implement a whistleblowing channel allowing confidential reporting of potential offences under D.Lgs. 231/2001
key_requirements.consequence: Weakened liability defence; Spain Whistleblowing non-compliance parallel
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Whistleblowing channel documentation; confidentiality procedures; report investigation records
how_to_meet: 1. Select or procure a confidential whistleblowing platform (internal hotline, external service provider, or dedicated email address) that ensures anonymity, non-retaliation, and secure storage of reports.
2. Publish clear, multilingual whistleblowing procedures in the Modello 231 and on company intranet/handbook, specifying how to report, expected response timelines, and protection from retaliation.
3. Establish escalation protocols ensuring reports go directly to the Supervisory Body (bypassing line management) and maintain a secure log of all reports with investigation status and outcomes.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft whistleblowing policy with reporting procedures, confidentiality assurances, and non-retaliation guarantees.
IT team: implement secure channel (encrypted email, ticketing system, or third-party platform) with access logs and audit trails; ensure data retention per company policy.
HR team: communicate policy to all staff via email and training; maintain evidence of distribution and acknowledgement.
Supervisory Body: establish investigation log, assign investigation owners, and document closure with findings.
vendor_actions: Whistleblowing platform provider: supply Service Level Agreement including uptime, confidentiality, data protection compliance (GDPR), and incident response procedures.
External counsel: review whistleblowing procedures for legal compliance with D.Lgs. 231/2001 and labour law (non-retaliation).
compliance_evidence: Whistleblowing policy document (published in Modello 231 and employee handbook).
Platform contract and SLA showing confidentiality and non-disclosure clauses.
Technical documentation of whistleblowing channel (architecture, encryption, audit logs).
Evidence of staff communication (email announcements, training slides, posters).
Signed employee acknowledgement of whistleblowing policy.
Whistleblowing register with timestamps, reporter identifiers (anonymised), report summaries, investigation owner, and closure date/findings.
External counsel opinion letter confirming legal compliance.

regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions: fines up to EUR 1.5 million; temporary suspension of business activities; disqualification from public contracts; confiscation of profits

regulations.name: Brazil Clean Company Act (Lei Anticorrupcao No. 12.846/2013)
regulations.authority: Comptroller General of the Union (CGU) and Attorney General's Office (AGU)
regulations.summary: Brazil's Clean Company Act (Lei 12.846/2013) introduces strict corporate liability for acts of corruption against national or foreign public administrations. Companies can receive fine reductions of up to 4% by demonstrating an effective integrity programme. The Leniency Agreement mechanism allows voluntary disclosure in exchange for reduced penalties.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit offering or paying bribes to public officials for business advantage
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a written anti-bribery policy explicitly prohibiting direct or indirect payments, gifts, or benefits to public officials, with clear definitions of 'business advantage'. 2. Implement transaction approval workflows requiring documented business justification for all payments to government-related entities. 3. Conduct quarterly audits of high-risk transaction categories (permits, licenses, regulatory approvals) to detect anomalies.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and sign-off anti-bribery policy with specific examples and escalation procedures. Finance team: provide transaction logs filtered by public official interactions and approval audit trails. Compliance team: document policy distribution and acknowledgment records from all staff.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party consultants and agents: provide signed declarations of compliance with anti-bribery obligations and certify no payments will be made to public officials on the company's behalf. Agents: submit detailed engagement letters naming specific public officials or agencies they will interact with.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed anti-bribery policy with effective date, transaction approval records showing documented business rationale, quarterly audit reports with zero or documented exceptions, signed vendor declarations, engagement letters with agents, and training attendance logs.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit financing or sponsoring acts of corruption
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a formal financial control policy prohibiting use of corporate funds for donations, sponsorships, or grants that serve as disguised corruption payments to benefit a public official or their associates. 2. Require pre-approval by Legal and Compliance for all charitable donations and event sponsorships above a defined threshold (e.g., BRL 50,000). 3. Perform semi-annual reviews of all donations and sponsorships against beneficial ownership and public official relationship databases.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: maintain a central register of all donations and sponsorships with business justification, beneficiary identity verification, and approval sign-offs. Legal team: develop approval matrix and vetted list of eligible charitable organizations. Compliance team: conduct beneficial ownership screening using third-party databases and document results.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Charitable organizations and event organizers: provide organizational registration documents, tax-exempt status certificates, and confirmation that no public officials hold beneficial ownership or control interests.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Donations and sponsorships register with approval documentation, pre-approval sign-off records from Legal and Compliance, beneficial ownership screening reports, charity registration and tax-exempt certificates, and semi-annual review summary reports.

key_requirements.text: Implement a corporate integrity programme covering risk mapping, code of conduct, training, and third-party due diligence
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document a formal Integrity Programme charter that maps corruption risks by business unit, geography, and transaction type; include the charter in Board-approved governance documents. 2. Publish a Code of Conduct covering anti-corruption, conflicts of interest, and third-party conduct; require annual signed acknowledgment from all employees. 3. Implement mandatory anti-corruption training for all staff at hire and annually, with completion tracking; customize training for government-facing roles (procurement, licensing, regulatory affairs).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: develop risk mapping document identifying high-risk functions, departments, and geographies with mitigation controls. Legal team: draft Code of Conduct with specific anti-corruption provisions and obtain Board approval. HR team: establish mandatory training curriculum, track completion records, and maintain signed acknowledgments. Finance team: provide transaction data to support risk mapping.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party training providers: deliver customized anti-corruption training modules and provide completion certificates. External auditors: perform control testing on the Integrity Programme design and operating effectiveness.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved Integrity Programme charter with risk map, signed Code of Conduct with employee acknowledgments, training curriculum and completion records by employee and date, annual training attendance certificates, and external audit reports validating Programme design.

key_requirements.text: Establish a confidential reporting channel for suspected violations
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish an independent, confidential reporting channel (hotline, email, or third-party platform) accessible 24/7 with multi-language support, promoting it widely to employees and contractors. 2. Develop a documented investigation procedure requiring all reports to be logged, acknowledged within 5 business days, investigated impartially, and resolved within a documented timeframe. 3.
