Implement a non-retaliation policy protecting reporters, communicated alongside the reporting channel and tested through periodic training.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: procure and configure a third-party confidential reporting platform or establish an independent internal hotline; create and publish investigation procedures with defined roles and timelines. Legal team: draft non-retaliation policy and ensure integration into HR policies. HR team: maintain investigation logs and resolution tracking, provide quarterly statistics on reports received.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party hotline provider: deliver platform with encryption, anonymity, multi-language support, and monthly reporting dashboards. External counsel: provide investigation support for sensitive allegations and advisory on investigation completeness.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Confidential reporting channel set-up documentation and proof of accessibility, investigation procedure manual with sign-off, non-retaliation policy in employee handbook, quarterly reporting logs showing receipt and investigation status, investigation summaries and closure documentation, and proof of channel promotion (intranet postings, email campaigns, training materials).

key_requirements.text: Conduct enhanced due diligence on agents, consultants, and government-facing third parties
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a due diligence questionnaire and risk assessment matrix tailored to agents, consultants, and government-facing service providers, requiring submission before engagement and annually thereafter. 2. Conduct beneficial ownership screening, sanctions list checks, and media screening using third-party due diligence platforms; document results in a centralized vendor file. 3. Obtain signed anti-corruption representations and warranties from all third parties, and include anti-corruption and compliance audit rights in all engagement contracts.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: maintain a centralized vendor due diligence file with completion dates, screening results, and risk ratings. Legal team: draft due diligence questionnaire, representations/warranties template, and contract clauses for compliance audit rights. Compliance team: execute third-party screening, interpret results, and approve/reject vendors with documented rationale.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Agents and consultants: complete due diligence questionnaire, provide beneficial ownership declarations, and submit to sanctions and media screening. All vendors: sign representations and warranties confirming no corruption, criminal history, or public official affiliations; grant audit rights allowing Compliance to review engagement records.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed due diligence questionnaires, third-party screening reports (beneficial ownership, sanctions, media), signed vendor due diligence files with approval sign-offs, executed representations and warranties agreements, contract amendments with compliance audit clauses, and annual re-screening documentation.

key_requirements.text: Maintain accurate financial records and internal controls
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a Chart of Accounts and financial policies requiring segregation of duties, with all payments coded by category, recipient type, and purpose; maintain transaction detail supporting documentation. 2. Establish a monthly financial reconciliation process with documented reviews and sign-offs; perform quarterly management reviews of high-risk accounts (agent payments, discretionary spending, government-related transactions). 3. Conduct an annual internal audit of key financial controls, including testing of a sample of transactions for proper approval, documentation, and business justification.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: document Chart of Accounts structure, transaction coding standards, and approval matrices; maintain transaction registers with supporting invoices and approvals. Controller: perform monthly reconciliations with documented sign-offs and investigate exceptions. Internal Audit team: develop annual audit plan testing financial controls, transaction samples, and segregation of duties; issue audit reports with findings and management responses.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors: perform annual financial statement audit with specific testing of high-risk transaction categories and assessment of financial control design. Accounting software vendor: provide audit trail and exception reports demonstrating transaction logging and approval tracking.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Chart of Accounts documentation with transaction coding policy, monthly reconciliation sign-offs with supporting work papers, quarterly management review reports of high-risk accounts, annual internal audit reports with transaction testing samples and control assessments, external audit reports, and accounting system audit trail reports.

key_requirements.text: Consider leniency agreement mechanism for voluntary disclosure to CGU
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a confidential disclosure channel (hotline or secure portal) staffed by external counsel to receive and document potential violations without immediate reporting triggers.
2. Create a leniency application protocol defining scope, timing, and required supporting evidence for voluntary disclosure submissions to the Comptroller General of Brazil (CGU).
3. Maintain a sealed register of all disclosures received and their status (under review, submitted to CGU, resolved) to evidence good-faith cooperation if audited.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft leniency agreement template and disclosure protocol aligned with CGU guidelines; provide written opinion on timing and privilege protections.
Compliance team: establish disclosure intake log with case tracking, evidence retention procedures, and decision documentation for each submission.
Finance team: quantify financial exposure and remediation costs associated with disclosed violations for leniency negotiation support.
vendor_actions: External counsel (specializing in CGU leniency): provide written guidance on admissibility requirements, negotiation strategy, and settlement terms; commit to confidential handling of submissions.
Third-party investigator (if needed): execute NDA and deliver preliminary investigation report to support materiality assessment before CGU submission.
compliance_evidence: Signed leniency protocol document approved by board or audit committee.
Sealed disclosure register with timestamped entries, submitter identities, and CGU submission confirmations.
CGU correspondence (acknowledgment letters, leniency agreement if executed, settlement documentation).
External counsel opinion letter confirming alignment with Anti-Corruption Law requirements and privilege protections.

key_requirements.text: Ensure anti-corruption obligations flow down to subsidiaries and controlled companies
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
how_to_meet: 1. Insert mandatory anti-corruption compliance clauses into all subsidiary governance documents (bylaws, shareholder agreements, board policies) requiring adoption of parent company standards.

2. Roll out annual certification requirement for each subsidiary and controlled company wherein management formally attests to anti-corruption policy implementation, training completion, and absence of violations.
3. Conduct annual audit or compliance assessment of a representative sample of subsidiaries to verify policy enforcement and document findings in a consolidated group compliance report.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft anti-corruption addendum or amendment for each subsidiary's incorporation documents and shareholder agreements; maintain master register of all executed documents.
Compliance team: develop standardized annual attestation template for all subsidiaries; compile certification submissions and create group-wide compliance dashboard summarizing policy adoption.
Internal audit team: perform risk-based testing of subsidiary compliance controls; document scope, methodology, and findings in audit workpapers retained for 5+ years.
vendor_actions: External auditors: include subsidiary anti-corruption compliance procedures in the group audit scope; deliver separate management letters detailing any control deficiencies or non-compliant practices.

Subsidiary management (if different from parent): execute written acknowledgment and commitment to parent company anti-corruption policies; provide signed confirmation of training completion and policy communication to their staff.
compliance_evidence: Amended subsidiary bylaws, articles of association, or shareholder agreements bearing anti-corruption obligation language and execution dates.
Annual signed attestations from each subsidiary CEO/CFO confirming policy implementation and compliance status.
Consolidated group compliance report summarizing subsidiary adoption metrics, training attendance records, and audit findings.
Internal audit reports and external auditor management letters documenting subsidiary control testing and remediation action plans where deficiencies were identified.

regulations.penalties: Administrative fines from 0.1% to 20% of annual gross revenue; extraordinary fines of up to BRL 60 million if revenue cannot be determined; debarment from public contracts

regulations.name: Canada Corruption of Foreign Public Officials Act (CFPOA)
regulations.authority: Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) and Public Prosecution Service of Canada
regulations.summary: Canada's Corruption of Foreign Public Officials Act criminalises the bribery of foreign public officials by Canadian companies and individuals. Amended in 2013 to remove the facilitation payments exception and introduce a nationality-based jurisdiction. Canada is a signatory to the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on offering or paying bribes to foreign public officials to obtain or retain business
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team drafts and publishes a written Anti-Bribery Policy explicitly prohibiting any offer, promise, or payment of value to foreign public officials. 2. Establish a pre-engagement due diligence checklist requiring sign-off before engaging intermediaries, consultants, or agents in foreign markets. 3. Create a gifts and hospitality register with approval thresholds, requiring declaration and business justification for any payment to persons in official capacity.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide signed Anti-Bribery Policy document and board approval memo. Compliance team: establish and maintain gifts/hospitality register with approval audit trail. Finance team: configure transaction coding to flag payments to foreign government entities or officials.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party intermediaries and agents: sign Anti-Bribery Certification confirming no payments to officials on the organisation's behalf. Distributors and resellers: complete Foreign Official Dealings Declaration form annually.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Anti-Bribery Policy, board resolution approving policy, gifts register with monthly certifications, executed vendor Anti-Bribery Certifications, transaction logs showing blocked or flagged payments, training attendance records.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on possessing or hiding property that is proceeds of bribery
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Finance and Legal teams jointly create a proceeds-of-bribery detection procedure integrated into transaction monitoring and AML screening. 2. Establish account opening and ongoing beneficial ownership verification protocols to prevent receipt and concealment of bribes as company assets. 3. Mandate quarterly asset and cash reconciliation statements signed by CFO confirming no unexplained or illicit property flows.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: implement transaction screening rules and provide monthly AML exception reports. Internal Audit team: conduct quarterly spot checks on high-risk geographic entities and payment patterns. Legal team: document beneficial ownership policies and provide certification of compliance.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External AML/compliance service provider: supply monthly transaction screening reports and alerts for suspicious patterns. Banks and payment processors: provide certified statements confirming no frozen or flagged accounts related to bribery investigations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: AML screening procedures manual, quarterly CFO asset certifications, transaction screening reports with alert logs, beneficial ownership documentation, bank correspondence, Internal Audit reports with findings and remediation.

key_requirements.text: Maintain accurate books and records — prohibits accounting offences that obscure bribery
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Finance team implements segregation of duties in the accounting system with automated reconciliation controls and an audit trail capturing user ID, timestamp, and amount for all payments. 2. Establish a monthly books-and-records review procedure requiring Finance Controller certification that all transactions are recorded completely and accurately with supporting documentation. 3. Internal Audit conducts annual testing of high-risk transaction categories (foreign payments, consulting fees, gifts) to confirm proper journal entries and supporting evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide accounting policy manual, system access logs, and reconciliation exception reports monthly. Controller: sign monthly books-and-records certification memo. Internal Audit team: deliver annual transaction testing workpapers and management letter.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors: include CFPOA-specific audit procedures in annual audit plan and confirm in management letter that no indicators of accounting manipulation detected.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Accounting policies manual with CFPOA controls, monthly reconciliation reports signed by Controller, system audit logs covering user access and transaction posting, annual Internal Audit testing workpapers, external audit management letter, supporting documentation repository for sample transactions.

key_requirements.text: Extraterritorial reach: applies to Canadians and Canadian companies operating anywhere in the world
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team issues a Jurisdictional Scope Memo confirming that the Anti-Bribery Policy applies to all employees, officers, and agents of the Canadian parent and all subsidiaries, branches, and joint ventures globally, regardless of local law. 2. Compliance team creates a Global Employee Register mapping all entities and individuals covered by CFPOA obligations by jurisdiction. 3. HR team mandates anti-corruption training for all covered staff annually and maintains completion records by location.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: issue Jurisdictional Scope Memo and update Code of Conduct with global applicability statement. Compliance team: maintain Global Employee Coverage Register updated quarterly. HR team: provide annual training enrollment and completion certificates by location and employee category.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All subsidiaries and joint venture partners: sign Parent Company Compliance Undertaking confirming acceptance of Anti-Bribery Policy and CFPOA obligations. Local legal counsel in high-risk jurisdictions: provide written opinion on CFPOA extraterritorial applicability.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Jurisdictional Scope Memo with legal opinion, Global Employee Coverage Register with quarterly updates, Code of Conduct with global applicability clause, subsidiary/JV compliance undertakings, annual training certificates, HR records showing coverage confirmation by location.

key_requirements.text: No exception for facilitation payments (removed by 2013 amendment)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team issues a Facilitation Payments Prohibition Notice to all employees, agents, and vendors explicitly stating that no payments—regardless of amount or characterization as 'facilitation', 'courtesy', or 'expediting fees'—are permitted to foreign officials for routine services. 2. Finance establishes a categorical ban in the payment authorization system preventing approval of transactions coded as facilitation payments or expediting fees to foreign government entities. 3. Compliance team includes specific facilitation payment prohibition testing in quarterly transaction reviews and annual Internal Audit procedures.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: distribute Facilitation Payments Prohibition Notice to all staff and maintain acknowledgment records. Finance team: configure payment system rules blocking or flagging facilitation payment descriptions. Compliance team: include facilitation payment testing in quarterly transaction reviews with documented findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All agents, distributors, and service providers in foreign markets: sign Facilitation Payments Attestation acknowledging zero tolerance and committing to report any requests from officials.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Facilitation Payments Prohibition Notice, employee acknowledgment records, payment system configuration screenshots and rule logs, quarterly transaction review reports with facilitation payment testing details, vendor attestations, training records covering facilitation payment prohibition.

key_requirements.text: Implement an anti-corruption compliance programme covering due diligence, training, and monitoring
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance team drafts a comprehensive Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme document covering due diligence procedures, training schedule, and monitoring mechanisms, approved by Board and Legal. 2. Establish a formal due diligence process requiring completion of a Third-Party Risk Assessment questionnaire (covering ownership, sanctions, PEP status, prior misconduct) before engagement of consultants, intermediaries, distributors, and vendors in foreign markets. 3. Implement annual anti-corruption training for all relevant staff (Board, management, sales, procurement) with documented attendance and sign-off, and quarterly monitoring of high-risk transactions and entities with documented review findings.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme document approved by Board and provide annual effectiveness report. Legal team: maintain Third-Party Risk Assessment template and approval workflow. HR/Compliance: manage training calendar, track completion, and provide annual training statistics. Internal Audit: conduct quarterly high-risk transaction and vendor reviews with written findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third parties: complete Third-Party Risk Assessment questionnaire and provide supporting documents (beneficial ownership, sanctions checks, reference checks). High-risk vendors: undergo enhanced due diligence including on-site audits and ongoing compliance monitoring with quarterly certifications. Service providers: acknowledge receipt of Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme and confirm acceptance of compliance obligations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme document, Third-Party Risk Assessment questionnaires and approval records for all active vendors, annual training plan with attendance rosters and completion certificates, trainer credentials, annual Internal Audit monitoring reports with transaction testing results, quarterly high-risk transaction reviews, vendor certifications, Board compliance reporting memo.

key_requirements.text: Conduct risk assessments for operations in high-risk jurisdictions
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Identify all jurisdictions where the organisation operates and classify them by corruption risk using established indices (e.g., Transparency International CPI, World Bank governance indicators).

2. Document a formal risk assessment for each high-risk jurisdiction covering: foreign official interaction frequency, transaction types, third-party intermediaries, and historical corruption patterns in relevant sectors.
3. Update assessments annually or when material business changes occur in high-risk markets, with documented sign-off by Legal and Finance leadership.
internal_actions: Legal team: prepare jurisdiction risk classification matrix with supporting corruption index data.
Finance team: provide transaction volumes, payment methods, and counterparty details for high-risk markets.
Compliance team: maintain risk assessment register with dated reviews and remediation actions documented.
vendor_actions: Third-party service providers in high-risk jurisdictions: supply background verification reports, beneficial ownership disclosure, and anti-corruption certifications.
Local agents/distributors: provide documented acknowledgment of CFPOA compliance obligations and their corruption risk exposure in their operating market.
compliance_evidence: Formal written risk assessment documents for each high-risk jurisdiction, dated and signed by Legal/Finance.
Corruption index reports or third-party risk data used to justify jurisdiction classifications.
Risk register with annual review sign-offs and audit trail of assessment updates.
Board or committee minutes approving high-risk jurisdiction risk mitigation strategies.

key_requirements.text: Provide anti-corruption training to employees and representatives in high-risk roles
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
how_to_meet: 1. Develop anti-corruption training curriculum covering CFPOA prohibitions, foreign official identification, red flags, and reporting obligations; mandate completion for all employees in high-risk roles (sales, procurement, government relations, compliance) within 90 days of hire.
2. Deliver training via documented platform (LMS) or instructor-led sessions with signed attendance records; require annual refresher for high-risk roles and upon appointment to sensitive positions.
3. Track completion metrics and non-compliance by role/jurisdiction; escalate incomplete training to managers with documented remediation timelines.
internal_actions: HR team: maintain roster of high-risk role designations and new hire onboarding training assignment records.
Learning & Development team: provide LMS training completion reports with timestamps, pass/fail results, and attestation of content coverage for anti-corruption modules.
Compliance team: generate quarterly training completion dashboards and document any waivers or exceptions with business justification.
vendor_actions: Training platform vendor: provide audit logs confirming user access, content delivery, assessment scores, and completion timestamps for anti-corruption modules.
Third-party trainers (if used): deliver trainer credentials, course curriculum approval, and sign-off confirming CFPOA Act content was delivered as agreed.
compliance_evidence: Signed training completion certificates or LMS export showing all high-risk role employees completed anti-corruption training with dates.
Training curriculum document with specific CFPOA Act sections, foreign official definition, and prohibited conduct scenarios covered.
Attendance records or sign-in sheets for instructor-led sessions, or LMS completion logs for online training.
Annual certification from Compliance/HR confirming training completion rate by role/jurisdiction and remediation of any lapses.

regulations.penalties: Individuals: up to 14 years imprisonment; corporations: unlimited fines; debarment from government contracts

regulations.name: Mexico General Law of Administrative Responsibilities (LGRA)
regulations.authority: Secretariat of Anti-Corruption and Good Governance (SABG) / Superior Audit Office (ASF)
regulations.summary: Mexico's General Law of Administrative Responsibilities (LGRA) creates a system for preventing and sanctioning corruption involving public officials and private parties. Part of the National Anti-Corruption System, it introduces corporate liability for participating in corruption acts and incentivises compliance programmes with fine reductions.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on private parties offering bribes, gifts, or undue benefits to public officials
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Finance team: establish a gifts and entertainment policy with monetary thresholds and pre-approval requirements for all offerings to public officials. 2. Compliance officer: implement a central register recording all gifts, meals, or benefits offered to government representatives with business justification. 3. Internal audit: perform quarterly reviews of the register and conduct surprise testing of procurement and sales transactions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide gifts and entertainment policy, pre-approval workflows, and transaction approval matrices. Compliance officer: deliver the central gifts register with sample entries and approval evidence. Internal audit: supply quarterly testing reports showing sample transactions reviewed.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All sales and procurement vendors: sign an anti-bribery addendum confirming they will not offer gifts, entertainment, or benefits to Mexican public officials on behalf of the organisation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed gifts and entertainment policy, centralized gifts register with timestamps and approvals, quarterly audit reports, executed vendor anti-bribery addenda, training attendance records for employees with government contact.

key_requirements.text: Corporate liability for participation in serious administrative offences by related persons
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal and HR teams: map all roles with authority to engage with public officials (sales, regulatory affairs, government relations) and document decision-making chains. 2. Compliance officer: establish a responsibility matrix showing which executives and managers are accountable for employee and contractor conduct in government-facing activities. 3. Audit and legal: conduct annual certification of high-risk roles, requiring them to acknowledge accountability for related persons' conduct.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide organisational chart with government-facing roles and escalation procedures. HR team: deliver role responsibility matrix and list of contractors/agents authorized to represent the organisation. Compliance officer: produce annual role certification sign-offs with acknowledgment of liability.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All government relations agents and contractors: sign a liability acknowledgment confirming they understand the organisation bears corporate liability for their violations and they accept personal responsibility.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Organisational chart annotated with government-facing roles, signed responsibility matrix, annual role certifications with executive signatures, contractor liability acknowledgments, documented escalation procedures.

key_requirements.text: Integrity programmes can reduce sanctions: code of conduct, training, compliance officer, reporting channels, whistleblower protections
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance officer: author and board-approve a comprehensive code of conduct covering anti-corruption, conflicts of interest, and proper dealings with public officials; establish a standalone ethics hotline and whistleblower protection policy. 2. HR and training team: deliver mandatory anti-corruption training to all employees within 30 days of hire and annually thereafter, with completion records retained. 3. Designate and empower a compliance officer with direct board reporting access and adequate budget; publish their contact details and independence statement.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance: draft and obtain board approval for code of conduct and whistleblower protection policy. HR: establish training curriculum, schedule annual delivery, and maintain attendance/completion records. Compliance officer: produce annual independence report and ethics hotline activity summary (anonymized).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All material vendors and partners: acknowledge receipt and acceptance of the organisation's code of conduct; sign confirmation of compliance with anti-corruption expectations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved code of conduct, published whistleblower protection policy, ethics hotline setup documentation and call logs (anonymized), employee training attendance certificates, annual compliance officer independence report, vendor acknowledgment letters.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on influencing public officials' decisions through improper means
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Compliance and legal teams: prohibit use of intermediaries, conditional offers, or side arrangements to influence public officials' decisions; document this in the code of conduct and government affairs procedure manual. 2. Establish a pre-submission review process for all government permit applications, contract bids, and licensing requests requiring compliance sign-off before filing. 3. Train all government-facing staff on proper communication channels and prohibited influence tactics; document and maintain training records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft government affairs procedure manual with case examples of prohibited conduct. Compliance officer: design and implement a pre-submission compliance checklist for all government filings. HR and training: deliver targeted training to sales, regulatory, and legal staff; record attendance.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Government relations consultants and agents: provide written commitment that all advice and services comply with Mexican law and the organisation's anti-corruption standards; sign a detailed scope of work excluding prohibited influence methods.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Government affairs procedure manual with board approval, signed compliance checklists for each government submission, training attendance records with content summary, consultant engagement agreements with anti-corruption clauses, email trails demonstrating pre-approval of government communications.

key_requirements.text: Due diligence on government-facing third parties and supply chain partners
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Procurement and compliance teams: develop a third-party risk assessment questionnaire covering government relationships, beneficial ownership, sanctions status, and prior regulatory violations; require completion before contract signature. 2. Legal team: insert standard anti-corruption, sanctions compliance, and indemnification clauses into all vendor contracts for government-facing services (e.g., lobbying, permits, licensing support). 3. Compliance officer: maintain a vendor risk registry and perform annual re-certification of high-risk partners (government relations firms, customs brokers, licensing consultants).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: provide vendor onboarding checklist and risk assessment template. Compliance officer: create and maintain vendor risk registry with assessment scores and re-certification dates. Legal team: supply standard contract clauses for government-facing vendor agreements.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All government-facing vendors: complete a detailed due diligence questionnaire (ultimate beneficial owners, government affiliations, prior compliance violations), provide corporate governance documents, and sign anti-corruption and indemnification contract clauses.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed vendor due diligence questionnaires with supporting documentation, signed vendor contracts with anti-corruption clauses, vendor risk registry with assessment dates and scores, annual re-certification sign-offs, copies of beneficial ownership verifications (e.g., corporate registry extracts).

key_requirements.text: Maintain controls over government contracts, permits, and licensing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal, compliance, and procurement teams: establish a government contracts register capturing all permits, licenses, and public contracts; document decision stages, approvals, and applicable regulations for each. 2. Create a control checklist for each contract stage (application, award, renewal, performance) requiring compliance review and sign-off before action. 3. Audit and compliance: perform semi-annual reviews of the contracts register to verify approvals were obtained and no irregularities occurred.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: design government contracts register template and document control procedures. Compliance officer: produce stage-gated approval checklists for permit, license, and contract renewals. Internal audit: deliver semi-annual compliance review reports on government contracts activity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors involved in obtaining or managing government permits, licenses, or contracts: provide monthly activity reports detailing all communications with government agencies, permit status, and compliance measures taken.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Government contracts register with all active and closed permits/licenses, signed stage-gated approval checklists, semi-annual audit reports, vendor activity reports showing government interactions, documented renewal and performance monitoring records.

key_requirements.text: Cooperate with anti-corruption investigations; self-reporting can reduce penalties
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal reporting channel (hotline, email, portal) accessible to all employees and third parties for suspected violations, with documented intake and escalation procedures.
2. Create a documented cooperation protocol with internal Legal and Compliance teams that defines timelines, disclosure scope, and evidence preservation when violations are reported.
3. Implement a self-reporting mechanism to relevant Mexican authorities (FEPN or applicable agency) within regulatory timeframes when material violations are identified internally.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain a written cooperation and self-reporting policy with clear decision criteria for when to notify authorities.
Compliance team: establish investigation procedures documenting findings, remediation actions, and communications with authorities.
HR team: provide records of employee training on reporting obligations and whistleblower protections.
IT team: configure and maintain secure reporting portal with audit logs of submissions and status tracking.
vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign a clause acknowledging cooperation obligations and committing to notify the organisation of any violations within 5 business days of discovery.
External legal counsel: provide written opinions on self-reporting strategy and timing to reduce penalty exposure.
compliance_evidence: Signed anti-corruption cooperation policy and self-reporting protocol.
Documented investigation files with findings, remediation steps, and authority notifications (with proof of submission).
Hotline/portal audit logs showing receipt and handling of reports.
Written correspondence with FEPN or relevant authorities confirming self-disclosure.
Employee training records and attestation of policy acknowledgement.

key_requirements.text: Annual integrity programme review and risk assessment
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct an annual risk assessment identifying corruption, bribery, and conflicts of interest vulnerabilities across all departments and business units.
2. Document the integrity programme review in a formal annual report, including control testing results, gaps identified, and remediation plan with assigned owners and deadlines.
3. Present the review findings and updated programme to senior management and board/governance body for approval and sign-off annually.
internal_actions: Compliance team: design and execute annual risk assessment questionnaire covering fraud, asset misuse, and regulatory breach risks; document results in a formal Risk Assessment Report.
Internal audit team: perform control testing on key integrity controls (procurement, expenses, conflicts of interest declarations) and provide audit report with findings.
Legal team: review regulatory changes and update the integrity programme; prepare annual review summary for executive sign-off.
HR team: provide headcount, department structure, and staff turnover data to support risk stratification.
vendor_actions: External auditor or compliance consultant: conduct independent assessment of integrity programme design and operating effectiveness; deliver formal report with recommendations.
Compliance software vendors: provide certification that monitoring and control tools are functioning as configured for the review period.
compliance_evidence: Annual Integrity Programme Review Report signed by Compliance Officer and approved by board/senior management.
Risk Assessment Report documenting identified risks, inherent and residual risk ratings, and control design.
Internal audit or external consultant report with control testing results and observations.
Board or management committee meeting minutes approving the annual review and any programme updates.
Documented remediation plan with responsible parties, timelines, and completion evidence for prior-year findings.

regulations.penalties: Corporate fines up to 2x the benefit obtained or up to 2% of annual revenue; temporary or permanent debarment from government contracts

regulations.name: South Africa Prevention and Combating of Corrupt Activities Act (PRECCA)
regulations.authority: Directorate for Priority Crime Investigation (Hawks) / National Prosecuting Authority
regulations.summary: South Africa's PRECCA creates a general offence of corruption applicable to any person and introduces a duty to report corrupt activities on persons in positions of authority. It targets bribery, extortion, fraud, money laundering, and procurement fraud in both public and private sectors.

key_requirements.text: General corruption offence: prohibition on any gratification that improperly influences any person
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a gifts and entertainment policy that defines prohibited gratifications and sets monetary thresholds for approval. 2. Implement expense management controls requiring itemized receipts, business purpose documentation, and manager sign-off for all gifts, meals, and hospitality. 3. Conduct quarterly expense audits to identify patterns of improper gratifications to employees, clients, or government officials.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide expense transaction reports and gift register logs for the past 24 months; Legal team: draft or review gifts and entertainment policy with specific exclusions and approval matrices; Compliance team: establish and maintain a centralized gifts register with donor, recipient, value, and business purpose.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All vendors: sign anti-corruption and gifts/hospitality clause addendum confirming they will not offer or provide gratifications to your employees; third-party event organizers: provide itemized invoices and attendee lists for any corporate events or hospitality.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed gifts and entertainment policy document with approval date; quarterly expense audit reports with exception summaries; centralized gifts register (electronic or paper) showing all recorded gifts above threshold; signed vendor anti-corruption addenda; sample expense claim forms with supporting receipts and approvals.

key_requirements.text: Specific offences for public officers, foreign public officials, agents, and members of the public
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all employees, contractors, and board members who interact with public officials or hold decision-making authority over government contracts. 2. Provide targeted role-specific anti-corruption training covering offences under PRECCA for public officers, foreign officials, agents, and public-facing roles. 3. Establish a conflicts-of-interest register requiring disclosure of any relationships, financial interests, or previous roles in government.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide org chart with role classifications and list of staff with government-facing responsibilities; Legal team: prepare role-specific training materials (e.g., for procurement, compliance, and board); Compliance team: create and maintain a signed conflicts-of-interest register updated annually or on hire.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All contractors and agents: complete a conflicts-of-interest declaration form and confirm they hold no financial interest in your organization; agents in particular: provide evidence of independence (e.g., no prior government appointment, no undisclosed family relationships with public officials).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed role-specific PRECCA training certificates with attendance records and dates; conflicts-of-interest register with employee/contractor signatures and declaration dates; org chart clearly identifying public-sector-facing roles; signed contractor/agent conflicts declarations; evidence of annual re-certification.

key_requirements.text: Duty to report: persons in authority who know or suspect corruption must report to SAPS
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented corruption reporting procedure that clearly designates persons in authority (e.g., compliance officer, board member) as mandatory reporters and includes escalation to SAPS. 2. Create a confidential reporting hotline or secure mailbox, separate from line management, to enable staff and third parties to report suspected corruption without fear of retaliation. 3. Develop a written log documenting all corruption reports received, including date, nature, reporter identity (if disclosed), and action taken within 14 days.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft and publish the corruption reporting procedure with SAPS contact details and mandatory reporter designations; IT team: implement and configure a confidential reporting channel (e.g., secure email, third-party hotline service, or anonymous portal); HR team: include mandatory reporting obligations in leadership induction and annual performance contracts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party hotline provider (if used): provide system access logs, call/submission records, and confirmation of confidentiality and non-retaliation controls; external audit firm: confirm receipt of reporting procedure and testing protocol.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published corruption reporting procedure signed by board/CEO with effective date; hotline system setup documentation and sample submission records; corruption report log with dates, summaries, and action records (redacted for privacy); board minutes documenting receipt and escalation of reports; SAPS case reference numbers for any reports filed.

key_requirements.text: Failure to report corruption is itself a criminal offence
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Ensure all persons in authority (board, senior management, compliance officers) receive written notice of their legal duty to report corruption and the criminal penalties for failure to report. 2. Maintain a centralized record of all corruption allegations received, including dates, reporter details (if disclosed), and documentary evidence of action taken and SAPS notification. 3. Conduct annual compliance certification where persons in authority affirm they have complied with their reporting obligations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide written notification to all board and senior management with copies of PRECCA sections on failure to report and penalties; Compliance team: maintain a master corruption allegations log with decision trail (investigation result, SAPS referral status, closure date); HR team: include failure-to-report clause in leadership contracts and annual performance review templates.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External legal counsel: provide annual opinion letter confirming the organization's reporting compliance and any gaps; external audit firm: verify a sample of reports and confirm SAPS notification occurred or document reasons for non-notification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed letters sent to all persons in authority confirming receipt of duty to report notice; master corruption allegations log with supporting investigation files and SAPS notification records; annual certification forms signed by board and senior management confirming no unreported suspicions; external legal compliance opinion; audit testing confirmation.

key_requirements.text: Maintain anti-corruption policies, procedures, and training for employees
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a comprehensive anti-corruption policy covering PRECCA offences, gifts, conflicts of interest, and reporting channels; obtain board approval and publish to all employees. 2. Deliver mandatory anti-corruption training to all new hires within 30 days and annual refresher training to all staff, with content tailored to role risk levels. 3. Document attendance, test comprehension via quiz or attestation, and maintain training records with dates and employee signatures for minimum three years.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft anti-corruption policy with PRECCA definitions, examples, and reporting procedures; HR team: schedule and deliver annual training (via workshop, e-learning, or video) and collect signed attendance records; Learning & Development: prepare role-specific modules (e.g., enhanced content for procurement, finance, board members) and maintain training register with pass/fail status.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External training provider (if used): deliver customized PRECCA training and provide attendance certificates, test results, and learner feedback; policy template provider: confirm template is South Africa–specific and PRECCA-compliant.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved anti-corruption policy document dated and signed; training attendance register with employee names, dates, signatures, and role classifications; training materials (slides, videos, handouts) covering PRECCA offences and examples; training assessment/comprehension test results; annual training compliance report showing percentage of staff trained; policy distribution log (email, intranet, handbook) with read receipts or acknowledgement.

key_requirements.text: Due diligence on agents, contractors, and public-sector-facing third parties
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a third-party risk assessment checklist covering PRECCA compliance, beneficial ownership transparency, sanctions, and litigation history for all agents, contractors, and public-sector-facing service providers. 2. Conduct due diligence before engagement, including background checks, regulatory searches, and signed compliance certifications; escalate any adverse findings to the board or compliance committee. 3. Maintain a due diligence file for each third party with assessment date, findings, approvals, and annual re-certification or monitoring.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: document all third-party engagements and provide names and contract values to Compliance; Compliance team: conduct or commission background checks (adverse media, sanctions, litigation, SAPS records checks where available) and create a due diligence template; Finance team: ensure contracts with public-sector vendors include PRECCA compliance clauses and audit rights.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third parties: complete anti-corruption compliance questionnaire, sign a PRECCA compliance certification, and provide beneficial ownership declaration and director/shareholder details; agents in particular: provide evidence of independence and confirm no undisclosed government connections; public-sector contractors: provide proof of absence from SAPS corruption database (or equivalent).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed third-party due diligence assessment template for each vendor, contractor, and agent; background check reports (in-house or from third-party compliance service) dated and filed; beneficial ownership declarations and director identity checks; signed PRECCA compliance certifications from all third parties; contract addenda requiring PRECCA compliance and audit rights; due diligence exceptions register showing any risks identified and remediation or rejection decisions; annual third-party re-certification records.

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain a whistleblowing and reporting channel
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a dedicated, confidential reporting channel (telephone hotline, email, web portal, or third-party service) that allows employees and external parties to report suspected corrupt activities without fear of retaliation.
2. Document and publish a whistleblowing policy that specifies reporting procedures, confidentiality protections, non-retaliation guarantees, and investigation timelines.
3. Maintain a centralised log of all reports received, investigations conducted, outcomes, and corrective actions taken, with records retained for at least five years.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and approve the whistleblowing policy and non-retaliation statement; ensure alignment with PRECCA and King IV governance principles.
HR team: implement non-retaliation protocols and train managers on handling disclosures; maintain confidential reporter identity records.
Compliance team: establish reporting intake procedures, assign investigation ownership, and create a centralised case management register with closure documentation.
IT team: configure secure, anonymous reporting platform with encrypted data storage and access controls; provide technical audit logs of platform usage.
vendor_actions: External hotline provider (if used): supply service level agreement (SLA) confirming confidentiality, 24/7 availability, call/email logging, and monthly statistical reports.
Investigation service provider (if outsourced): provide written engagement terms detailing investigation methodology, reporting timeline, and confidentiality obligations.
compliance_evidence: Signed and dated whistleblowing policy document published on intranet or employee handbook.
Evidence of policy communication (email distribution, training attendance sheets, acknowledgement forms from staff).
Centralised whistleblowing register with sample entries showing report date, nature of allegation, investigator assigned, and closure status.
Investigation files (anonymised) documenting findings and corrective actions.
Platform audit logs and hotline provider monthly reports demonstrating operational continuity.
Non-retaliation attestation or HR policy extract.

key_requirements.text: Register on the National Register for Tender Defaulters if convicted
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Upon conviction of any PRECCA offence, immediately notify the National Treasury's Office of the Chief Procurement Officer (OCPO) and request registration on the National Register for Tender Defaulters (NRTD).

2. Complete the official NRTD registration form with full organisational details, conviction date, offence description, and sentence; submit within the prescribed timeframe (typically 30 days post-conviction).
3. Maintain a compliance register documenting the conviction, NRTD registration submission date, registration confirmation, and the exclusion period (typically five years from conviction date).
internal_actions: Legal team: immediately notify Compliance and Finance teams of any conviction; provide court order documentation and sentencing details for NRTD submission.
Compliance team: prepare and submit NRTD application form to the OCPO within statutory deadlines; retain proof of submission and acknowledgement.
Finance team: ensure finance and procurement systems are flagged to reject new tender applications during the exclusion period; document system restrictions.
Company Secretary: maintain a confidential conviction and NRTD registration record in the compliance register with filing evidence.
vendor_actions: No direct vendor actions required; however, convict organisation must communicate NRTD registration status to all existing service providers and procurement partners for transparency.
compliance_evidence: Court judgment or conviction order with date and offence description.
Completed NRTD registration form (Form TR-1 or equivalent) signed by authorised officer.
OCPO acknowledgement letter or email confirming NRTD registration and exclusion end date.
Centralised compliance register entry documenting conviction date, NRTD submission date, and registration confirmation.
Procurement system audit logs showing tender restriction flags applied during exclusion period.
Internal communication to stakeholders regarding NRTD status (if applicable).

regulations.penalties: Up to 15 years imprisonment; fines; asset forfeiture; blacklisting from public procurement

regulations.name: Singapore Prevention of Corruption Act (PCA)
regulations.authority: Corrupt Practices Investigation Bureau (CPIB)
regulations.summary: Singapore's Prevention of Corruption Act enforced by the CPIB is one of the most effective anti-corruption frameworks in the world, contributing to Singapore's top global anti-corruption rankings. Covers both public and private sector corruption. CPIB operates independently with extensive investigative powers.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on offering, receiving, or soliciting corrupt gratification in public and private sectors
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a written anti-corruption policy explicitly prohibiting the offer, receipt, or solicitation of any gratification (cash, gifts, favours, entertainment) to or from any person in connection with business. 2. Implement a gifts and entertainment register requiring pre-approval and documentation of all gifts above a defined threshold (e.g. SGD 50). 3. Create a confidential whistleblowing channel with independent third-party handling to report suspected violations without fear of retaliation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft anti-corruption policy and gifts/entertainment guidelines; HR team: establish whistleblower hotline with external vendor and publish reporting procedures; Finance team: create gifts register template and approval workflow; Compliance team: document all approvals and maintain records for 5 years.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External whistleblowing service provider: confirm confidentiality protocols, case handling procedures, and independence from management; Third-party investigators (if engaged): sign confidentiality and conflict-of-interest declaration.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed anti-corruption policy document dated and approved by Board/senior management; Gifts and entertainment register with entries showing date, recipient, value, business rationale, and approval signature; Whistleblower hotline contract and annual case log summary; Employee acknowledgement forms confirming receipt and understanding of policy.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on corrupt acts involving agents and principals (private sector bribery)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define and document all agent, distributor, and intermediary relationships in a central register including scope, fees, and approval authority. 2. Implement contractual clauses in all third-party agreements explicitly prohibiting corrupt acts and requiring compliance with PCA and anti-bribery laws. 3. Conduct quarterly transaction reviews comparing agent invoices and payments against documented work deliverables and market rates to detect anomalous patterns.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: standardise agent/distributor contract template with anti-corruption clauses and integrity warranties; Procurement team: maintain a current register of all third-party agents with contract dates and fee structures; Finance team: establish payment approval matrix and provide quarterly reports comparing payments to deliverables; Compliance team: design and execute quarterly compliance audit sampling of 10-15% of agent transactions.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All agents and distributors: sign anti-corruption declaration and certification of no undisclosed beneficial owners; agents operating in high-risk sectors: provide evidence of own sanctions screening and financial due diligence practices.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Master agent/distributor register with signed contracts containing PCA compliance clauses; Signed anti-corruption certifications from each agent; Quarterly audit reports showing sampling methodology, findings, and sign-off by CFO/Compliance Officer; Payment reconciliation reports with annotations explaining any variances >10% from prior periods.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on bribing public officials — Singapore's clean government reputation is legally enforced
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Publish a clear organisational policy that strictly prohibits any form of bribery, facilitation payments, or improper inducements to any public official in Singapore or overseas, with explicit reference to PCA penalties. 2. Map all business interactions with public officials (government agencies, statutory boards, GLCs) and require documented approval and record-keeping for all such engagements. 3. Train all staff with public official contact responsibilities on PCA offences, real case examples, and mandatory reporting of suspicious requests.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create supplementary guidance document on PCA offences with Singapore case law examples and cross-border applicability; Compliance team: maintain a log of all planned interactions with public officials including the nature, attendees, and business purpose; HR team: mandate annual PCA e-learning for all staff; Risk and Audit team: conduct annual risk assessment of public official interaction touchpoints (procurement, licensing, permits).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All third-party facilitators or consultants engaging with government on company behalf: sign declaration of understanding PCA and indemnification clause confirming no bribes will be offered.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved policy on public official interactions dated and version-controlled; Public official interaction log with dates, names, titles, agencies, and business rationale; Annual PCA training completion certificates for all staff; Signed declarations from third-party representatives; Audit report documenting review of high-risk government interactions for the past 2 years.

key_requirements.text: Implement effective anti-corruption policies and controls
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented Anti-Corruption Governance Framework approved by the Board, specifying roles (Compliance Officer, CFO, audit committee), responsibilities, and escalation procedures. 2. Implement preventative controls across procurement, expense approval, and agent management including transaction limits, segregation of duties, and exception reporting. 3. Conduct an annual corruption risk assessment identifying high-risk business areas (e.g. government procurement, cash-intensive operations) and design targeted controls for each.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Audit Committee: approve and formally adopt Anti-Corruption Governance Charter; Compliance Officer: produce annual corruption risk assessment report with heat-map of high-risk functions; Finance and Procurement teams: document existing segregation of duties, approval matrices, and system exception reports; Internal Audit: design and execute annual testing plan for key controls (e.g. 30 expense approvals, 20 procurement authorisations, 15 agent payments) and report findings.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor or compliance consultant: conduct independent assessment of adequacy of anti-corruption controls and provide written recommendations and sign-off.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board-approved Anti-Corruption Governance Charter; Annual corruption risk assessment report (signed and dated); Internal control procedures manual with process flowcharts and approval matrices; Annual internal audit plan and execution report with tested controls and remediation status; External auditor's assessment letter confirming control adequacy.

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence on agents, distributors, and partners operating in high-risk jurisdictions
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a tiered due diligence framework categorising partners/vendors by risk (high-risk jurisdictions, government relationships, cash-intensive services) and applying proportionate screening. 2. For all high-risk third parties, obtain certified beneficial ownership declarations, conduct sanctions screening against UN/OFAC/Singapore MAS lists, and request references from previous clients. 3. Document all due diligence findings in a signed compliance assessment and obtain Management sign-off before contract execution; repeat screening annually.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement/Vendor Management team: document the due diligence framework including risk criteria and screening procedures; Compliance team: establish screening checklist for each risk tier and create a due diligence file template; Finance team: integrate vendor screening into payment authorization workflows ensuring only screened vendors are paid; Legal team: build mandatory due diligence completion evidence into contract execution checklist.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All high-risk vendors: complete beneficial ownership declaration form (declaring all individuals owning >25% interest) signed by company officer; provide evidence of sanctions clearance or allow company to conduct third-party screening (via Refinitiv, Dun & Bradstreet, or equivalent); provide 2-3 client references for verification; sign anti-corruption and sanctions compliance certification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Due diligence framework document with risk categorisation matrix; Completed due diligence checklists for all high-risk vendors signed by Compliance Officer; Beneficial ownership declarations from vendors (notarised copies); Sanctions screening reports dated and showing vendor name match and clear results; Evidence of reference checks (email confirmations from referees); Compliance assessment sign-off by CFO/Compliance Officer prior to contract execution; Annual re-screening reports for ongoing vendors.

key_requirements.text: Provide anti-corruption training to employees especially in high-risk roles
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design a mandatory annual anti-corruption training programme tailored to role-based risk: general staff receive overview of PCA and reporting channels; high-risk roles (procurement, sales, government relations, finance) receive detailed case study-based training covering red flags and decision frameworks. 2. Document training completion with signed attendance records and pass certification (e.g. minimum 80% on post-training assessment). 3. Include PCA training in onboarding for all new hires; refresh biannually for high-risk roles and whenever policies are materially updated.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: integrate PCA training into mandatory onboarding programme and maintain training records for all staff with completion dates and assessment scores; Learning & Development team: commission external training provider to develop role-specific modules (use Singapore PCA case law and CPIB examples); Compliance Officer: maintain a training schedule and escalate any non-completion to business unit heads monthly; Finance team: budget for biennial training refresh.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External training provider: deliver customised e-learning and/or instructor-led sessions for high-risk roles covering PCA offences, real Singapore cases, and company-specific risk scenarios; provide certificates of completion and aggregate completion reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Training curriculum document showing differentiated content by role and PCA topics covered; Master training register with employee names, roles, dates completed, and assessment scores; Signed training attendance records or e-learning completion logs; Training certification letters from external provider; Evidence of biennial refresher scheduling and completion; Audit trail showing escalation of non-completion to managers.

key_requirements.text: Gifts and hospitality policies must be clearly defined and enforced
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
how_to_meet: 1. Draft a written Gifts and Hospitality Policy specifying monetary thresholds (e.g., SGD 50 limit per gift), prohibited categories (cash, luxury items), and mandatory pre-approval for gifts exceeding the threshold.
2. Establish a Gifts Register requiring employees to log all gifts received with donor name, item description, value, and business justification within 5 working days.
3. Conduct mandatory training for all staff and management on policy requirements, sign-off records, and consequences of non-compliance; refresh annually.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft and finalize Gifts and Hospitality Policy with clear approval workflows and delegation matrix.
HR team: design and deploy mandatory e-learning module with completion and assessment records; maintain training attendance logs.
Finance team: set up and maintain the Gifts Register (digital or manual) with documented review sign-offs by line managers monthly.
vendor_actions: Third-party vendors and business partners: provide signed acknowledgement of the organisation's Gifts and Hospitality Policy as part of onboarding agreements.
