Vendors: disclose any gifts, hospitality, or benefits offered to employees in writing upon request for audit purposes.
compliance_evidence: Signed and dated Gifts and Hospitality Policy document with version control and board/management approval.
Completed Gifts Register (minimum 12 months) showing donor, item, value, date, and approval sign-offs.
Training completion certificates or e-learning platform reports showing 100% staff participation with pass assessments.
Vendor acknowledgement letters or signed policy acceptance forms retained in vendor master files.

key_requirements.text: Cooperate with CPIB investigations — obstruction is a criminal offence
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented CPIB Cooperation Protocol defining roles, escalation paths, and designated response officers who will interface with CPIB investigators.
2. Brief all staff that obstruction of CPIB investigations is a criminal offence under the PCA; include this in induction and annual compliance refresher training.
3. Designate a legal point of contact and ensure all document requests from CPIB are actioned promptly (typically within 5 business days) with an audit trail maintained by the Legal team.
internal_actions: Legal team: create and publish a CPIB Cooperation Protocol document; appoint and communicate the designated CPIB liaison officer(s) to all staff.
Compliance/HR team: include CPIB cooperation and obstruction penalties (up to SGD 100,000 and 5 years imprisonment) in staff induction materials and annual compliance training.
IT team: provide document retrieval capabilities and maintain access logs for any records provided to CPIB; ensure data preservation holds are applied when notified of investigations.
vendor_actions: Vendors: commit contractually to cooperate with CPIB inquiries and provide requested information or records without delay.
Vendors: acknowledge in writing that they will not obstruct, hinder, or deny access to information required by CPIB.
compliance_evidence: Signed CPIB Cooperation Protocol document with named liaison officers and their contact details.
Training records (e-learning completion, certificates, or sign-off sheets) showing all staff trained on CPIB cooperation obligations and obstruction offences.
Documented evidence of CPIB requests received (e.g., letters, emails) and a dated response log showing timely provision of documents or records.
Vendor contract clauses or signed commitment letters confirming cooperation with CPIB investigations.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years per offence

regulations.name: EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) 2022/2464
regulations.authority: National Competent Authorities / European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG)
regulations.summary: The EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) mandates comprehensive ESG reporting aligned with European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). Phased implementation starting FY2024 for large listed companies, expanding to all large EU companies, listed SMEs, and eventually non-EU companies with significant EU business. Requires assurance on sustainability information.

key_requirements.text: Report on material sustainability matters using European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a sustainability reporting task force to map organisational activities against ESRS E1–E5 and S1–S3 standards. 2. Design data collection templates and assign data owners across functions (environment, HR, supply chain) aligned to ESRS metrics. 3. Document the materiality assessment process and create a sustainability disclosure register linking each ESRS standard to reported metrics.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide baseline financial and operational data for materiality thresholds. Legal team: confirm regulatory requirements and reporting scope. IT team: provide data architecture documentation and system access for metric extraction. Sustainability/ESG office: develop ESRS mapping matrix and define KPI calculation methodologies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: confirm ESRS audit methodology and required documentation format. Third-party data providers (energy, water, waste): supply historical datasets and certification of measurement standards. Consultant (if engaged): provide ESRS-compliant reporting templates and quality assurance checklist.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: ESRS materiality assessment report with stakeholder engagement records; ESRS mapping matrix cross-referencing organisational metrics to standards; Signed ESRS data collection protocols from each data owner; External auditor engagement letter confirming ESRS audit scope; Sustainability disclosure register with completeness sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Apply double materiality: report on both impact on society/environment AND financial risks from ESG
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a double materiality assessment: identify topics where the organisation impacts the environment/society, and separately where ESG factors create financial risks to the business. 2. Document both impact and financial risk ratings using a defined scale, validated by internal stakeholders (Finance, Operations, Legal). 3. Produce a materiality matrix showing both axes and justify inclusion/exclusion of topics in the sustainability report.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: quantify potential financial exposure (liability, asset impairment, revenue loss) from identified ESG risks. Risk/Compliance team: provide enterprise risk register mapping ESG exposures. Strategy team: confirm business model dependencies on social/environmental factors. Board/Audit Committee: validate materiality thresholds and sign-off on final matrix.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External consultant or auditor: facilitate materiality assessment workshops and validate double materiality methodology against CSRD expectations. Data providers: supply peer benchmarking data and impact quantification methodologies for sector-specific issues.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Double materiality assessment report with documented methodology and stakeholder workshop minutes; Materiality matrix (impact vs. financial risk) with quantified thresholds and topic justifications; Board-approved materiality policy and decision log; Written confirmation of materiality scope boundaries; External auditor sign-off on materiality assessment robustness.

key_requirements.text: Include Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions in climate disclosures
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all emission sources across Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and Scope 3 (value chain) using GHG Protocol guidance; assign responsibility to operational teams. 2. Implement data collection systems: meters/invoices for Scope 1&2; supplier engagement framework and emission factors for Scope 3. 3. Establish annual emission baselines, calculate year-on-year trends, and document all assumptions and emission factors used.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Facilities/Energy team: provide utility bills, fuel consumption records, and meter data for Scope 1&2. Procurement team: compile supplier list and obtain energy/emission data from key suppliers for Scope 3 upstream. Operations team: document production volumes and process-specific emission coefficients. IT team: deploy or integrate emissions accounting software with audit trail and version control.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Energy suppliers: provide detailed consumption and emissions data invoices. Logistics/transport providers: supply shipping distance, mode, and fuel data for downstream emissions. Suppliers: complete CSRD/CDP emissions questionnaire or provide audited Scope 1&2 data. Third-party emissions calculator vendor: certify tool compliance with GHG Protocol and provide methodology documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas inventory report with GHG Protocol alignment statement; Emission factor source documentation (IPCC, regional grid factors, supplier-specific data); Data collection templates signed by operational owners; Scope 3 supplier engagement log and questionnaire responses; Annual emissions baseline and year-on-year comparison with variance explanations; External auditor limited assurance report on emissions calculations.

key_requirements.text: Disclose social topics: own workforce, workers in value chain, affected communities, consumers
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define disclosure scope: own workforce (headcount, diversity, pay equity, health/safety), value chain workers (supplier labour practices, child/forced labour risk), affected communities (land rights, indigenous engagement), and consumers (product safety, data privacy). 2. Design KPIs for each category using CSRD/ESRS S1–S3 metrics; assign data owners in HR, Procurement, and relevant business units. 3. Implement annual data collection and validation cycles with supporting documentation (policies, audit reports, certifications).
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide workforce demographic data (age, gender, ethnicity), pay equity analysis, turnover, health & safety incident logs, and training hours. Legal/Compliance: document grievance mechanisms and remediation records. Procurement team: conduct supplier labour audits or collect third-party audit reports on labour standards. Community Relations/Government Affairs: document community impact assessments and stakeholder consultation records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Suppliers: provide labour practice certifications (SA8000, SEDEX, audit reports) or complete labour standards questionnaire. NGO/auditor: conduct independent labour audits in high-risk supplier facilities. Union representatives: confirm engagement records and collective bargaining outcomes. Community representatives: provide feedback on engagement activities and grievances.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Workforce composition and pay equity analysis with variance explanations; Health and safety policy with incident records and investigation reports; Supplier labour audit reports or SEDEX/similar certifications; Community stakeholder engagement records and impact assessments; Grievance mechanism policy and remediation case files; External auditor limited assurance report on social disclosures; Board resolution confirming social KPI targets and oversight.

key_requirements.text: Governance disclosures including board oversight of sustainability and anti-corruption
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document board/governance structure: identify sustainability committee or board member(s) with explicit ESG oversight; define decision-making authority and reporting cadence. 2. Establish governance policies covering sustainability strategy approval, executive remuneration linked to ESG targets, and regular board reporting on material ESG risks. 3. Implement anti-corruption controls: code of conduct, training records, conflict-of-interest declarations, and investigation logs; disclose governance processes and outcomes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Company Secretary: provide board charter, committee terms of reference, and minutes showing ESG oversight and approvals. HR/Compliance: compile anti-corruption/code of conduct training completion records, signed acknowledgements, and breach investigation files. Remuneration Committee: provide executive remuneration policy and ESG KPI scorecards linked to bonuses. Internal Audit: deliver anti-corruption audit reports and conflict-of-interest declaration forms.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External counsel: provide anti-corruption due diligence reports (sanctions screening, beneficial ownership checks) for key suppliers and customers. Third-party ethics hotline vendor: supply annual ethics reports, complaint volume summaries, and investigation status updates. Auditor: confirm governance compliance with CSRD expectations and best-practice comparisons.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board charter and sustainability committee terms of reference with signed approval. Minutes of board/committee meetings showing ESG oversight decisions. Anti-corruption and code of conduct policy with distribution records. Training completion certificates for all board members and senior management. Executive remuneration policy linking ESG KPIs to compensation. Conflict-of-interest declarations (annual). Sanctions screening and beneficial ownership due diligence reports. Ethics hotline annual summary and investigation closure reports. External auditor governance review and sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Obtain limited assurance from an accredited statutory auditor on sustainability statements
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Select and engage a statutory auditor (or auditor firm) accredited under national law and with ESRS/CSRD audit competency; sign an engagement letter specifying limited assurance scope and timeline. 2. Prepare a draft sustainability statement using ESRS standards, compile supporting documentation, and provide auditor access to data owners and systems. 3. Address auditor observations, revise disclosures as needed, and obtain signed limited assurance report confirming ESRS compliance and absence of material misstatements.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance/Sustainability team: compile complete sustainability statement draft with ESRS references and supporting schedules. Data owners: make available all underlying data, methodologies, and calculation workpapers (emissions, workforce, supply chain). IT team: grant auditor secure access to relevant systems and data repositories. Management: respond to auditor enquiries and implement agreed revisions before report finalisation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Statutory auditor: provide ESRS limited assurance engagement letter, audit plan, and detailed audit procedures; conduct fieldwork and issue signed limited assurance report on sustainability statement. Where data is held by third parties (e.g., supplier emissions), auditor may request direct confirmations or access to third-party audit reports.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed audit engagement letter specifying limited assurance scope, ESRS standards, and reporting date. Auditor's final limited assurance report (standalone or integrated into annual report) with explicit ESRS compliance statement. Management representation letter signed by CEO/CFO confirming sustainability data accuracy. Workpaper bundle showing audit procedures, evidence, and auditor conclusions. Auditor's credentials/accreditation proof (national registry or audit firm license). Any management letters or audit observations and management responses.

key_requirements.text: Publish sustainability statement in the management report in XHTML-tagged digital format
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Prepare the sustainability statement content (including double materiality assessment, strategy, governance, and performance metrics) and designate a single authoritative document version.

2. Engage IT/publishing team to convert the management report to valid XHTML format with all required semantic tags (article, section, h1-h6, table, figure) and validate against XHTML 1.0 Strict or HTML5 schema.
3. Embed the XHTML-tagged file within the digital annual report submission package and conduct accessibility testing (WCAG 2.1 AA compliance) before publication.
internal_actions: Finance team: provide final sustainability statement text and management report sign-off documentation.
IT team: generate XHTML-tagged file from source, maintain XML/XHTML schema validation logs, and provide file encoding and character set documentation.
Legal team: confirm no confidentiality conflicts and provide sign-off that sustainability statement meets disclosure standards.
vendor_actions: Publishing/Digital platform vendor: confirm XHTML schema compliance, provide DTD/schema validation certificate, and confirm accessibility audit capability.
External auditor: confirm audit procedures include validation of XHTML structure and tagging completeness.
compliance_evidence: Valid XHTML-tagged digital file (.xhtml or .xml) with embedded semantic markup and DOCTYPE declaration.
XHTML validation report from W3C validator or equivalent tool showing zero schema errors.
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility audit report from independent third party.
Management sign-off memo authorizing publication of sustainability statement in specified digital format.
Published annual report file with embedded XHTML statement and download/access metadata.

key_requirements.text: Report on EU Taxonomy alignment for environmentally sustainable activities
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Map all company activities, products, and services against the EU Taxonomy Technical Screening Criteria (6 environmental objectives) and identify which are taxonomy-eligible and taxonomy-aligned.

2. Collect operational data (revenue, capex, opex) by activity and assess compliance with both environmental performance thresholds and governance safeguards; quantify and document the percentage of revenue/capex/opex in each category.
3. Disclose alignment percentages with detailed methodology notes, assumptions, activity-level breakdowns, and a clear statement of any activities excluded or not yet assessed.
internal_actions: Finance team: extract and validate revenue, capex, opex segmentation by business activity; provide data reconciliation to audited financial statements.
Sustainability/ESG team: conduct EU Taxonomy mapping workshop, document screening criteria application per activity, and prepare alignment assessment report with supporting calculations.
Operations/Business Unit heads: confirm activity descriptions, provide technical specifications (energy sources, emissions data, manufacturing processes) needed for technical screening.
IT team: provide systems audit documenting data lineage and completeness of operational metrics used in taxonomy calculations.
vendor_actions: External taxonomy consultant or auditor: provide independent taxonomy alignment assessment, validate screening criteria application, and issue opinion letter on methodology soundness.
Data providers (emissions, energy, supply chain partners): supply certified environmental performance data (e.g., scope 1/2/3 emissions, renewable energy %) with signed attestation of accuracy.
ERP/financial systems vendor: confirm ability to extract and reconcile revenue/capex/opex data by activity and provide technical documentation of data export processes.
compliance_evidence: EU Taxonomy Alignment Report detailing eligible and aligned activities with revenue/capex/opex percentages, calculation methodology, and assumptions.
Activity-level mapping document cross-referencing each business line to specific Taxonomy environmental objective(s) and Technical Screening Criteria.
Financial data reconciliation schedule linking taxonomy-reported figures to audited annual financial statements.
Technical screening worksheets (per activity) showing compliance assessment against Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) criteria and minimum safeguards.
External auditor or consultant sign-off letter confirming taxonomy methodology and alignment percentages.
Management sign-off memo authorizing disclosure of EU Taxonomy alignment figures in the annual sustainability/management report.

regulations.penalties: Member state-imposed sanctions; enforcement by national securities and auditing regulators

milestones.label: Large companies not previously subject to NFRD
Listed SMEs, small & non-complex credit institutions

regulations.name: EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) 2024/1760
regulations.authority: National Supervisory Authorities
regulations.summary: The EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D/CSDDD) requires large companies to identify and address adverse human rights and environmental impacts in their own operations and value chains. Full implementation from 2027-2029 depending on company size. Directors have a duty of care for sustainability impacts.

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence across own operations, subsidiaries, and established business relationships in value chain
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map all direct suppliers, sub-contractors, and subsidiaries with geographic locations and product categories. 2. Conduct initial risk assessment using sector-specific vulnerability matrices and supplier financial stability checks. 3. Establish quarterly review cycles to update the supplier risk inventory and identify new or changed business relationships.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide current supplier contracts and subsidiary ownership documentation. Procurement team: export complete supplier database with tiers, locations, and product classifications. Finance team: supply subsidiary financial statements and ownership structure charts.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: complete sustainability questionnaire covering operations location, workforce demographics, environmental certifications, and subcontractor disclosure. Subsidiaries: submit governance structure, key operational metrics, and list of material suppliers.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Supplier risk assessment matrix (dated and signed), supplier questionnaire responses with audit trails, subsidiary ownership documentation, quarterly due diligence review minutes, and supply chain mapping diagrams.

key_requirements.text: Identify actual and potential adverse human rights and environmental impacts
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct human rights and environmental impact assessment across priority value chain segments using recognized frameworks (UN Guiding Principles, ILO standards). 2. Collect operational data on labor practices, resource use, and waste streams from own operations and obtain equivalent data from tier-1 and high-risk suppliers. 3. Document findings in a structured impact register categorized by likelihood, severity, and geographic concentration.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: develop impact assessment methodology and template aligned with CSRD requirements. HR team: provide workforce composition data, incident logs, and grievance records. Operations team: supply environmental monitoring data (emissions, waste, water use), health and safety audit reports, and facility inspection records.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: High-risk suppliers: complete detailed impact questionnaire covering child labor risks, forced labor indicators, wage compliance, environmental permits, and waste management practices. Third-party auditors: deliver completed assessment reports for sampled suppliers using standardized evaluation criteria.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Impact assessment methodology document, completed impact register with risk ratings, supplier impact questionnaire responses, third-party audit reports, environmental monitoring dashboards, and HR incident summaries.

key_requirements.text: Prevent, mitigate, and remediate identified adverse impacts with action plans
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. For each identified actual or potential adverse impact, develop a prioritized action plan specifying remediation timeline, responsible parties, success metrics, and budget allocation. 2. Establish monitoring mechanisms to track progress (monthly KPI reviews, supplier compliance audits, worker interviews). 3. Document lessons learned and adjust plans quarterly based on effectiveness data and stakeholder feedback.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create action plan templates with impact linkages and approval workflows. Finance team: allocate and track remediation budgets by impact category. Operations/HR teams: implement monitoring controls (audit schedules, worker hotlines, environmental sensors) and maintain compliance logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Suppliers with identified impacts: sign remediation commitment letters and provide monthly progress reports with supporting evidence (training certificates, facility upgrades, payroll audits). Auditors: conduct baseline and follow-up assessments at 6 and 12-month intervals.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed remediation action plans with timelines, monthly progress dashboards, supplier commitment letters, audit reports (baseline and follow-up), training completion certificates, facility upgrade photographs, wage audits, and evidence of worker outreach.

key_requirements.text: Establish and operate accessible grievance mechanism for affected stakeholders
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a confidential, multi-channel grievance mechanism (phone hotline, email, web portal, in-person channels) accessible to all value chain workers and affected communities in local languages. 2. Define clear intake, investigation, and resolution procedures with documented timelines (acknowledgment within 5 days, investigation within 30 days). 3. Publish transparency reports quarterly disclosing complaint volumes, categories, investigation status, and remediation outcomes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft grievance mechanism policy and resolution procedures compliant with UN Guiding Principles. IT team: procure and configure anonymous reporting platform with audit logging. Compliance team: establish investigation and tracking protocols; assign dedicated grievance officer.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: adopt equivalent grievance mechanisms or agree to route complaints through parent company platform. Third-party operator: manage hotline/portal, provide monthly intake and resolution statistics, and maintain strict confidentiality protocols.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published grievance mechanism policy and procedures, grievance platform system specifications and audit logs, quarterly transparency reports with complaint statistics, investigation case files (anonymized), resolution closure documentation, and worker awareness campaign materials.

key_requirements.text: Publish annual due diligence report aligned with CSRD sustainability statement
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Prepare annual due diligence statement covering due diligence process, identified impacts, remediation actions, and grievance mechanism performance, mapped to CSRD sustainability statement requirements. 2. Obtain sign-off from board/directors and legal review to confirm alignment with corporate strategy disclosures. 3. Publish report on company website and file with relevant regulatory authority as required by jurisdiction.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: compile due diligence findings from all teams into standardized report template with CSRD cross-references. Board Secretary: schedule director review and sign-off; maintain approval records. Finance/Sustainability team: ensure data consistency between CSDDD report and CSRD statement.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditor: provide limited assurance or audit opinion on selected due diligence disclosures if required by local regulations. Translation vendor: provide certified translations into required EU languages.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published annual due diligence report with board sign-off, auditor assurance reports, regulatory filing confirmation, website publication evidence (screenshots with dates), CSRD statement cross-reference index, and director approval minutes.

key_requirements.text: Directors must integrate due diligence into corporate strategy and are personally accountable
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Insert explicit accountability clauses in director job descriptions and board governance charters assigning responsibility for due diligence oversight and integration into strategy. 2. Establish a board-level committee (or designate existing committee) to review due diligence performance quarterly and escalate material impacts to full board. 3. Link director compensation or performance evaluation to due diligence and impact mitigation KPIs to ensure continuous executive engagement.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board Secretary/HR team: amend director contracts, governance charters, and committee terms of reference to include due diligence accountability. Compliance team: develop quarterly board reporting templates and KPI dashboards. Compensation team: design metrics linking director incentives to remediation outcomes.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External governance advisor (if used): provide template language for director accountability clauses and board committee charters aligned with EU best practice.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Updated director contracts with accountability clauses, board committee charter with due diligence responsibilities, quarterly board meeting minutes discussing due diligence, KPI dashboards presented to board, director compensation documentation showing due diligence linkage, and governance disclosure in annual report.

key_requirements.text: Adopt a Paris Agreement-aligned climate transition plan
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a cross-functional decarbonisation committee (Finance, Operations, Sustainability) to set science-based targets aligned with Paris Agreement 1.5°C pathway using SBTi methodology.
2. Develop a detailed transition plan documenting baseline emissions (Scope 1, 2, 3), interim targets by 2030, and final net-zero target by 2050 with annual milestone review cycles.
3. Integrate climate KPIs into executive remuneration and board-level governance to ensure accountability and resource allocation to transition activities.
internal_actions: Finance team: provide baseline GHG inventory (Scope 1, 2, 3) for past 3 years, capital expenditure forecasts, and carbon accounting software outputs.
Operations team: supply detailed energy consumption data by site/facility, supplier emission profiles, and decarbonisation project pipelines with timelines and costs.
HR/Remuneration team: confirm integration of climate transition KPIs into annual bonus structures and long-term incentive plans with weighting percentages.
vendor_actions: Tier-1 suppliers: submit Scope 1 and 2 GHG inventory and confirm commitment to science-based emissions reduction targets within 12 months.
Energy/logistics providers: provide decarbonisation roadmaps showing transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy, electric vehicle fleets, or equivalent low-carbon alternatives.
Auditors/consultants: deliver third-party verified baseline emissions assessment and confirmation of alignment with SBTi Net Zero Standard.
compliance_evidence: Published or board-approved climate transition plan (5–10 year horizon) with quantified annual emission reduction targets and capex allocation.
SBTi validation letter or equivalent third-party science-based target certification.
Board resolutions and committee minutes documenting climate governance oversight and approval of transition milestones.
Annual GHG inventory reports (third-party verified) aligned with GHG Protocol Corporate Standard.

key_requirements.text: Engage with affected stakeholders including workers, trade unions, and communities
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal multi-stakeholder engagement framework defining workers, trade unions, affected communities, and vulnerable groups; create documented protocols for consultation frequency, feedback channels, and escalation.
2. Conduct baseline stakeholder impact assessments (SIAs) or human rights due diligence for all material operations and supply chains, documenting identified risks and affected communities.
3. Implement grievance mechanisms (whistleblower hotlines, ombudsperson, community feedback portals) with mandatory response timelines and remediation tracking accessible to all stakeholders.
internal_actions: Legal/Compliance team: draft stakeholder engagement policy, grievance procedure terms, and impact assessment templates; maintain a stakeholder register with contact details and engagement history.
HR/Labour Relations team: document employee consultation records, trade union meeting minutes, and internal communication logs demonstrating worker voice in sustainability/risk decisions.
Community Relations/CSR team: produce stakeholder impact assessment reports, community consultation records (signed attendance sheets, feedback summaries), and evidence of remediation actions taken.
IT team: configure and audit grievance system (ticketing logs, response times, resolution tracking) to demonstrate accessibility and non-retaliation safeguards.
vendor_actions: Suppliers: provide evidence of worker representation mechanisms, trade union agreements or collective bargaining clauses, and confirmation of freedom of association policies.

Community-adjacent operators (mining, manufacturing, agricultural suppliers): submit community stakeholder maps, consultation records, and formal consent/impact mitigation agreements with local authorities and communities.
Third-party auditors: deliver stakeholder engagement verification reports and independent grievance mechanism assessments.
compliance_evidence: Documented stakeholder engagement policy and multi-stakeholder engagement calendar showing scheduled consultations, attendees, and outcomes.
Signed stakeholder impact assessments or human rights due diligence reports covering workers, unions, communities, and vulnerable groups by material geography/operation.
Grievance mechanism operating procedures, case logs (anonymised), response times, and evidence of remediation or corrective action closure.
Minutes and attendance records from worker forums, trade union negotiation sessions, and community consultation meetings with documented feedback and management responses.
Annual stakeholder engagement report published (internally or externally) summarising engagement outcomes and actions taken in response to stakeholder concerns.

regulations.penalties: Supervisory authority administrative fines; civil liability to persons harmed by failure to comply; fines up to 5% of global net turnover

milestones.label: Applies to EU companies: turnover >€1.5B, 5000+ employees
Applies to EU companies: turnover >€900M, 3000+ employees

regulations.name: Germany Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (Lieferkettensorgfaltspflichtengesetz — LkSG)
regulations.authority: Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control (BAFA)
regulations.summary: Germany's LkSG requires large companies (originally 3,000+ employees from 2023, expanded to 1,000+ from 2024) to conduct due diligence on human rights and environmental risks throughout their supply chains. Companies must appoint a human rights officer, conduct risk analysis, implement remedial measures, and report annually.

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Human Rights Officer responsible for monitoring LkSG compliance
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Formally designate a named Human Rights Officer with documented responsibility matrix and reporting line to senior management. 2. Define the officer's scope covering supply chain monitoring, risk assessment, and remediation oversight across all tiers. 3. Allocate budget and resources; record appointment in board minutes or equivalent governance record.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide appointment letter and role description; Legal team: document officer's reporting structure and escalation authority; Finance team: confirm budget allocation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: acknowledge receipt of Human Rights Officer contact details and confirm communication channel for compliance inquiries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed appointment letter, board resolution or equivalent governance record, job description with LkSG-specific duties, organizational chart showing reporting line, written acknowledgment from suppliers of officer contact information.

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual supply chain risk analysis across direct and indirect suppliers
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and document a risk assessment methodology covering human rights, environmental, and labour practice risks across Tier 1 and indirect suppliers (document scope and criteria). 2. Conduct annual assessment using supplier questionnaires, audit data, and external intelligence; record findings in a risk register by supplier and risk category. 3. Classify suppliers into risk bands and document remediation plans for medium and high-risk entities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: provide complete supplier list with annual spend and country of operation; Legal team: define risk assessment framework and scoring matrix; Compliance team: maintain and update risk register with assessment dates and owner assignments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: complete annual self-assessment questionnaire covering labour practices, environmental controls, and subcontractor management; high-risk suppliers: provide audit reports or third-party certifications (SA8000, SEDEX, ISO 14001) where applicable.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Risk assessment methodology document, completed supplier questionnaires with timestamps, risk register with risk ratings and dates, annual assessment report signed by Human Rights Officer, remediation plans with owner and target dates, copies of supplier audit reports and certifications.

key_requirements.text: Issue a Policy Statement on respect for human rights signed by management
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Draft a formal Policy Statement on human rights and labour standards covering commitment to ILO conventions, environmental responsibility, and zero tolerance for violations; include specific LkSG-relevant topics (forced labour, child labour, fair wages). 2. Obtain signed approval from CEO or Board; ensure statement is published and accessible to all employees and external stakeholders. 3. Communicate policy to all suppliers and embed in standard procurement terms.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft policy statement aligned with UNGP and ILO standards; Communications team: design publication and distribution strategy; HR team: integrate into employee handbook and onboarding materials.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: sign acknowledgment of policy receipt and confirm commitment to compliance; incorporate policy requirements into amended supplier agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed policy statement document with approval date and CEO/Board signature, evidence of publication (website, intranet, printed copies), distribution log to suppliers with signed acknowledgments, policy integration into supplier contracts, employee training records demonstrating communication.

key_requirements.text: Implement preventive measures and remedial action for identified risks and violations
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a standardized preventive action protocol: map identified risks by root cause, assign owner, define control implementation (e.g. audit schedules, training, contract amendments), and set completion deadlines. 2. For violations or breaches, establish a remedial action process including impact assessment, corrective steps, verification timeline, and escalation to Human Rights Officer. 3. Maintain a violations log with incident type, supplier, action taken, and closure evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: develop preventive and remedial action templates with timelines; Risk team: maintain violations log and track closure; Procurement team: update supplier contracts to include control requirements and audit rights.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Non-compliant suppliers: submit detailed remedial action plan with root cause analysis, corrective steps, timeline, and responsible party; submit evidence of completion (training certificates, audit confirmations, policy updates) within agreed timeframe.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Preventive and remedial action protocols (documented procedures), violations log with incident details and closure evidence, supplier remedial action plans with approval signatures, follow-up audit reports or compliance verification documents, correspondence trail showing escalation and resolution.

key_requirements.text: Establish a complaints procedure for affected persons to report human rights risks
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal grievance mechanism accessible to workers, affected communities, and external parties (online portal, dedicated email, telephone hotline, or third-party intermediary); ensure confidentiality and non-retaliation protections are documented. 2. Define intake, investigation, and resolution procedures; assign ownership to Human Rights Officer or Compliance team with defined response timelines (e.g. acknowledgment within 5 days). 3. Publish mechanism details in multiple languages on website and communicate to all suppliers and their workforces.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure secure complaint management system with audit trails; Legal team: draft grievance procedure and non-retaliation policy; Communications team: prepare multilingual information materials; HR team: train staff on complaint handling and investigation protocols.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: acknowledge mechanism and commit to communicating it to their workforce; high-risk suppliers: provide evidence of internal grievance mechanism or agree to use company-provided mechanism; commit to cooperate in investigations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Grievance mechanism policy document with procedures and timelines, screenshots or documentation of accessible submission channels (portal, email, hotline), non-retaliation and confidentiality policy signed by management, complaint log with intake dates and case identifiers, investigation reports and closure documentation, evidence of communication to suppliers and workers (notices, multilingual materials), third-party provider agreement (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Ensure procurement practices do not contribute to human rights or environmental violations
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Embed human rights and environmental due diligence criteria into procurement specifications and supplier selection scorecards; require documented evidence (certifications, audit reports, policy commitments) before contract award. 2. Amend all supplier agreements to include mandatory human rights and environmental clauses, audit rights, and termination provisions for non-compliance. 3. Train procurement team on risk-based supplier evaluation and conduct quarterly audits of procurement decisions against due diligence criteria.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: revise RFQ templates and supplier scorecards to include human rights and environmental criteria; Legal team: update standard supplier agreements with human rights, environmental, and audit clause; Training team: deliver procurement staff training on LkSG criteria; Compliance team: conduct quarterly procurement audits.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: certify human rights and environmental compliance in supplier master record; provide evidence (certifications, audits, environmental policies) at contract negotiation; new suppliers: complete pre-award assessment questionnaire on human rights and environmental practices before agreement signature.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Updated procurement specifications and supplier scorecards with human rights/environmental criteria, amended supplier agreement template with human rights and audit clauses, training attendance records and materials, pre-award assessment questionnaires completed and signed by suppliers, supplier master record database with compliance field populated, quarterly procurement audit reports documenting compliance review, evidence of rejected bids or terminations due to non-compliance.

key_requirements.text: Document due diligence activities and publish an annual report submitted to BAFA
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a centralised due diligence documentation system (spreadsheet or module within ERP/compliance software) that logs all risk assessments, supplier audits, corrective actions, and communications conducted during the fiscal year.

2. Compile findings into a structured annual LkSG report (German or English) detailing risk identification methodology, affected suppliers, remediation measures, and outcomes; have Legal review for accuracy and completeness.
3. Submit the signed annual report to BAFA (Bundesamt für Außenwirtschaft und Ausfuhrkontrolle) by the statutory deadline (typically by 31 March of the following year) and retain proof of submission.
internal_actions: Legal team: prepare annual LkSG compliance report template and submission checklist aligned with BAFA requirements.
Compliance/Procurement team: compile all due diligence records, audit findings, and corrective action registers from the preceding 12 months.
IT team: configure centralised documentation system with version control and audit trail to track all entries and amendments.
Finance team: confirm budget allocation and resource availability for annual reporting process.
vendor_actions: Suppliers: provide written responses to risk questionnaires and audit findings; submit evidence of any corrective actions taken during the reporting period.
Third-party auditors (if engaged): deliver audit reports and risk assessments by 31 December to allow time for consolidation into annual report.
compliance_evidence: Signed annual LkSG report submitted to BAFA with submission confirmation/receipt.
Internal due diligence documentation register (Excel or software export) showing all risk assessments, supplier communications, and corrective actions logged chronologically.
Audit reports, risk assessment matrices, and supplier correspondence files.
BAFA submission letter with date stamp and reference number.

key_requirements.text: Extend due diligence to indirect suppliers when there is substantiated knowledge of risks
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Define 'substantiated knowledge' criteria (e.g. media reports, NGO alerts, worker complaints, audit findings, financial instability indicators) and integrate into your supplier risk assessment methodology; document this in your due diligence policy.

2. When substantiated knowledge of risks (forced labour, child labour, environmental harm, unsafe conditions) is identified at a direct supplier, systematically trace supply chains upstream to identify indirect suppliers and conduct proportionate due diligence (desk research, questionnaires, or on-site audits depending on risk severity).

3. Issue formal requests to direct suppliers for supply chain transparency data (supplier lists, manufacturing locations, subcontractor details) and contractually require them to cascade due diligence obligations to tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers.
internal_actions: Compliance/Procurement team: develop and document 'substantiated knowledge' trigger criteria and integrate into supplier risk assessment workflow; create a risk escalation log.
Legal team: amend standard supplier contracts to include mandatory supply chain transparency clauses and indirect supplier due diligence requirements.
IT team: establish a supply chain mapping tool or database to track indirect suppliers identified through tracing; configure alerts when substantiated knowledge triggers are met.
Risk/Compliance team: create indirect supplier due diligence templates (questionnaires, audit protocols) scaled to risk level.
vendor_actions: Direct suppliers: provide complete supply chain maps (names, locations, contact details of all tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers); sign amended contracts acknowledging indirect due diligence obligations.
Direct suppliers: submit responses to supply chain transparency questionnaires and pass due diligence requirements down to indirect suppliers in writing.
Indirect suppliers (when substantiated knowledge is triggered): complete due diligence questionnaires or facilitate audits; provide corrective action plans if risks are identified.
compliance_evidence: Due diligence policy document defining 'substantiated knowledge' criteria and indirect supplier inclusion thresholds.
Risk escalation log showing trigger events (media reports, complaints) that activated indirect supplier due diligence.
Amended supplier contracts with supply chain transparency and cascading due diligence clauses.
Supply chain mapping reports and indirect supplier lists; questionnaire responses from tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers.
Audit reports or risk assessments for indirect suppliers identified as higher-risk.
Corrective action plans signed by indirect suppliers and evidence of implementation monitoring.

regulations.penalties: BAFA fines up to EUR 8 million or 2% of global annual revenue; exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years

regulations.name: UK Modern Slavery Act 2015
regulations.authority: Home Office / Independent Anti-Slavery Commissioner
regulations.summary: The UK Modern Slavery Act 2015 requires large commercial organisations with annual UK turnover of £36 million or more to publish an annual Transparency Statement disclosing steps taken to ensure no modern slavery or human trafficking in their supply chains and operations. It also criminalises modern slavery and human trafficking with severe penalties.

key_requirements.text: Publish an annual Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Transparency Statement signed by a director
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: draft the annual Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Transparency Statement incorporating all mandatory disclosures. 2. Board: review, approve and obtain signed sign-off from a serving director. 3. Compliance: maintain signed statement with director approval evidence and publish on corporate website with effective date.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide draft statement template and final signed director approval letter; Company Secretary: confirm director signatory authority and execution date; Compliance team: maintain master copy with signature evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: No external vendor involvement required for statement publication and execution.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Transparency Statement bearing director name, title, date and signature; Board approval minutes or resolution; website publication audit trail showing homepage link and publication date.

key_requirements.text: Statement must cover at least: organisation structure and supply chains, policies, due diligence processes, identified risk areas, key performance indicators
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Supply Chain & Procurement: document organisation structure chart, supply chain tiers, and map all high-risk categories (labour-intensive sectors, high-migration regions). 2. Governance & Compliance: compile existing modern slavery policies, due diligence procedures, audit findings and remediation actions. 3. Finance & HR: identify and document key performance indicators such as supplier audit completion rate, training attendance and incident reports.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: provide supply chain mapping document and risk assessment matrix; Legal/Compliance: deliver copies of modern slavery policy, due diligence framework and audit checklists; Finance: supply metrics on supplier audits completed and non-conformances identified; HR: provide training completion and incident reporting logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: High-risk suppliers: provide certificates of compliance, audit reports, labour practice certifications and attestations of code-of-conduct adherence.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Supply chain mapping document with risk categories and tiered supplier list; Modern Slavery Policy and Due Diligence Procedure documents; Supplier audit reports and compliance certificates; KPI dashboard or scorecard showing audit completion, training metrics and incident trends; Risk assessment matrix with scoring methodology.

key_requirements.text: Publish the Statement on the company website with a prominent link from the homepage
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Web team: identify or create a prominent webpage location (e.g. Corporate Responsibility, Governance, or Compliance section) dedicated to modern slavery transparency. 2. IT/Web: implement a direct hyperlink from the homepage to the published statement with clear labelling. 3. Compliance: verify monthly that the link remains active and statement is current.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT/Web team: provide website configuration audit showing homepage link implementation, URL routing and page analytics; Compliance team: maintain checklist of quarterly website spot-checks confirming link functionality.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Website hosting provider or CMS vendor: confirm statement page is publicly accessible and provide server access logs if required for audit purposes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Screenshot of company homepage showing prominent Modern Slavery Statement link; Web analytics report confirming page visibility and access statistics; URL structure documentation; Monthly website compliance audit checklist with sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Conduct supply chain mapping and risk assessment to identify modern slavery exposure
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Procurement & Risk: conduct a comprehensive supply chain mapping exercise identifying all direct suppliers, sub-tier suppliers and labour-intensive categories, then score each against modern slavery risk indicators (geography, industry, worker demographics). 2. Compliance & Legal: document risk assessment methodology and create a risk register with ratings (high/medium/low) and remediation timelines. 3. Compliance: review and update the assessment annually or when supply chain changes occur.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: deliver complete supplier register with geographic and sector classification; Risk team: provide documented risk assessment methodology and scoring framework; Finance: supply supplier spend analysis by risk tier; Compliance: maintain supply chain risk register with assessment dates and owner sign-off.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: complete supplier information questionnaire (SIQ) or self-assessment form capturing location, worker count, labour practices and certification status.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Supply chain mapping document with multi-tier supplier list; Risk assessment methodology document; Completed supply chain risk register with risk ratings and dates; Supplier self-assessment questionnaires and responses; Annual review and re-assessment sign-off from Procurement and Compliance leadership.

key_requirements.text: Implement supplier codes of conduct and audit mechanisms for high-risk supply chains
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal & Procurement: develop and publish a Supplier Code of Conduct that explicitly prohibits forced labour, child labour and human trafficking, and incorporate it into all supplier contracts and onboarding documentation. 2. Compliance & Audit: establish audit mechanisms for high-risk suppliers including on-site audits, questionnaires and third-party audit programme, with audit frequency based on risk rating. 3. Procurement: document audit findings, non-conformances and corrective actions in a central audit log and enforce remediation timelines.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft Supplier Code of Conduct and document incorporation into standard contract terms; Procurement: maintain supplier contract register showing code-of-conduct attachment; Compliance/Audit: provide annual audit plan, audit reports and non-conformance log with corrective action tracking; Finance: allocate budget for third-party audit services.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: High-risk suppliers: sign code-of-conduct attestation form, submit to on-site audits by internal or third-party auditors, respond to audit findings within agreed timelines and evidence corrective actions taken.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Supplier Code of Conduct document; Sample supplier contracts showing code-of-conduct attachment and signature; Audit plan and schedule by supplier risk rating; Completed audit reports with findings and corrective action notices; Non-conformance log with closure evidence; Third-party audit certifications or reports.

key_requirements.text: Provide training to relevant employees on identifying and reporting modern slavery indicators
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. HR & Learning: identify roles with supply chain visibility (Procurement, HR, Finance, Operations, Compliance) and design targeted training on recognising modern slavery indicators (wage theft, restricted movement, debt bondage, excessive hours) and internal reporting procedures. 2. HR: deliver training annually via classroom, e-learning or workshop format and maintain attendance records. 3. Compliance: track completion rates and assess training effectiveness through post-training assessments or incident reporting trends.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR/Learning team: provide training curriculum, delivery schedule and attendance registers; Compliance team: maintain training completion database and evidence of post-training assessments; Internal audit: assess training effectiveness through knowledge testing or scenario-based exercises.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External training provider (if applicable): deliver accredited modern slavery awareness course and provide certificates of completion and participant feedback.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Modern Slavery Training Curriculum document; Annual training delivery schedule and calendar invites; Attendance registers or LMS completion reports by employee and role; Training delivery evidence (sign-in sheets, e-learning logs); Post-training assessment scores or knowledge tests; Completion certificate; Training effectiveness report showing % of target roles trained.

key_requirements.text: Establish a reporting mechanism for employees and suppliers to raise concerns
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a dedicated reporting channel (e.g. anonymous hotline, secure web portal, or designated email) accessible to both employees and external suppliers, with clear escalation protocols to senior management and legal.
2. Create and distribute a written policy documenting the reporting mechanism, confidentiality protections, non-retaliation guarantees, and investigation procedures.
3. Implement intake and tracking logs to record all reports received, investigations initiated, and remedial actions taken.
internal_actions: Legal team: draft whistleblower policy and reporting procedure documentation.
HR team: design intake log template and train designated report handlers on investigation protocols.
IT team: configure secure reporting portal or hotline infrastructure with audit trail capabilities.
vendor_actions: Vendors and suppliers: sign acknowledgement confirming receipt of reporting mechanism details and commitment to encourage their own workforce to use the channel if applicable.
Key suppliers: provide evidence of their own internal reporting mechanisms for Modern Slavery concerns.
compliance_evidence: Whistleblower/grievance reporting policy (published and dated).
Hotline or portal screenshots and configuration documentation.
Sample intake and investigation logs covering the reporting period.
Vendor acknowledgement letters and signed commitments.
Annual summary report detailing number of reports received, investigations completed, and outcomes.

key_requirements.text: Applies to all legal entities supplying goods or services in the UK with £36 million+ global turnover
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a global turnover assessment by consolidating audited financial statements from all legal entities within your corporate group to determine if the £36 million threshold is met.
2. Document the organisational structure and identify all entities subject to the Act; clarify which single legal entity or parent company bears the duty to publish a Modern Slavery Statement.
3. Maintain a compliance register recording the assessment outcome, threshold determination date, and the designated responsible entity, reviewed annually.
internal_actions: Finance team: provide consolidated group revenue figures and audited accounts for the financial year; confirm global turnover calculation and threshold crossing evidence.
Legal team: document organisational structure, entity registration details, and confirmation of which entity holds UK commercial presence triggering the duty.
Compliance team: establish and maintain a Modern Slavery Act applicability register with annual review sign-off.
vendor_actions: Not directly applicable; however, document and retain evidence of any acquisitions or significant changes to business structure that affect UK turnover or entity status.
compliance_evidence: Audited consolidated financial statements showing global turnover.
Written threshold assessment memorandum with calculation and date.
Corporate structure diagram or organisational chart identifying all legal entities.
Compliance register entry confirming applicability status and responsible entity.
Annual review and re-assessment documentation signed by Finance and Legal.
Company registration details (Companies House extract) for the designated responsible entity.

regulations.penalties: Criminal penalties for modern slavery: up to life imprisonment. Civil penalties for statement non-compliance: unlimited fines; government may apply for injunction

regulations.name: Australia Modern Slavery Act 2018
regulations.authority: Department of Home Affairs (Australian Border Force)
regulations.summary: Australia's Modern Slavery Act 2018 requires entities based or operating in Australia with annual consolidated revenue over AUD 100 million to report annually on modern slavery risks in their operations and supply chains. Reports must be submitted to the government-managed Modern Slavery Register and are publicly available.

key_requirements.text: Submit an annual modern slavery statement covering required criteria to the online Register
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Public naming for non-compliant entities; expected enhanced penalties under planned amendments.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Legal team: draft annual modern slavery statement addressing the six mandatory criteria (risks identified, actions taken, effectiveness measures, consultation, process governance). 2. Compliance team: submit the signed statement to the Modern Slavery Register (www.modernslaveryregister.gov.au) before the statutory deadline each financial year. 3. Document and retain proof of submission including confirmation receipt and timestamp.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide draft modern slavery statement incorporating mandatory criteria and entity-specific risk assessments; Compliance team: maintain submission records, confirmation emails, and register login audit trails; Company Secretary: retain board approval documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External legal counsel (if engaged): review statement for regulatory compliance and alignment with reporting standards; external auditors: confirm statement completeness against Modern Slavery Act 2018 section 16 requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed modern slavery statement, Modern Slavery Register submission confirmation receipt, internal version control logs showing drafting and approval workflow, board minutes referencing statement approval.

key_requirements.text: Statement must be approved by the board and signed by a principal officer
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Public naming for non-compliant entities; expected enhanced penalties under planned amendments.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Board Secretary: schedule formal board meeting to review and approve the modern slavery statement before submission. 2. Principal officer (CEO, MD, or equivalent) must read, attest to accuracy, and sign the statement with full legal title and date. 3. Retain signed original and board resolution formally documenting approval.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Company Secretary: prepare board minutes documenting statement review and approval resolution; HR/Executive Office: confirm principal officer identity, title, and authority to sign; Legal team: obtain wet-ink or certified digital signature from principal officer on final statement.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External board advisor (if applicable): provide guidance on board approval best practice and governance documentation standards.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board minutes or board resolution approving the statement, principal officer's signed statement with name and title, corporate records confirming principal officer's appointment and authority, declaration of accuracy signed by principal officer.

key_requirements.text: Identify modern slavery risks in operations and supply chains across all tiers
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Public naming for non-compliant entities; expected enhanced penalties under planned amendments.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Procurement and Supply Chain team: map all direct suppliers and sub-tier contractors, categorising by industry sector, geography, and product/service type. 2. Risk team: conduct modern slavery risk assessment using sector-specific vulnerability indicators (labour exploitation, forced labour, child labour risks) across operations and supply chains. 3.
