key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Active CSA licence certificate, completed application and supporting documentation, practitioner CV and certifications register, annual licence renewal receipts, and CSA compliance audit reports.

key_requirements.text: Implement cybersecurity codes of practice issued by CSA for CII sectors
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 2 years for CII owners; fines up to SGD 500,000 for unlicensed cybersecurity services.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Download the applicable CSA Code of Practice for your sector (e.g., Energy, Healthcare, Finance, Water) from the CSA website and map requirements to existing controls. 2. Document gaps between current state and Code requirements, develop remediation roadmap with timelines and owner assignments. 3. Implement missing technical and process controls and maintain evidence of compliance through configuration reviews and internal audits.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: obtain and distribute applicable CSA Code of Practice documents. IT Security team: conduct control gap assessment and provide implementation roadmap with timelines. Management: approve budget and resource allocation for implementation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Technology vendors: provide documentation confirming products/services meet Code of Practice requirements (e.g., encryption standards, access control features, logging capabilities).
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Gap assessment report mapping Code of Practice requirements to implemented controls, remediation plan with completion dates, configuration evidence (firewall policies, encryption certificates, access control lists), and internal audit findings demonstrating control operation.

key_requirements.text: Report significant vulnerabilities discovered in systems to CSA
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 2 years for CII owners; fines up to SGD 500,000 for unlicensed cybersecurity services.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vulnerability identification and triage process that flags significant vulnerabilities (CVSS ≥7.0 or affecting CII systems) for CSA reporting consideration. 2. Create a vulnerability disclosure template including system details, vulnerability description, remediation plan, and business impact assessment. 3. Report to CSA through the designated channel within a defined timeframe (typically before public disclosure) and maintain a log of reported vulnerabilities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT Security team: configure vulnerability scanning tools (Nessus, Qualys) with automated alerting for high-risk findings. Vulnerability Management team: maintain vulnerability tracking spreadsheet with CSA reporting status. Legal/Compliance: define significance threshold and CSA reporting timeline.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Vulnerability disclosure platforms and security researchers: ensure responsible disclosure agreements direct submissions to your vulnerability management contact and enforce CSA reporting timelines.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Vulnerability disclosure policy signed by management, CSA vulnerability report submissions with dated delivery confirmations, vulnerability tracking log with severity ratings and report status, and evidence of remediation timelines.

key_requirements.text: Systems of Temporary Cybersecurity Concern (STCC) must comply with direction from Commissioner
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 2 years for CII owners; fines up to SGD 500,000 for unlicensed cybersecurity services.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Designate a Cybersecurity Incident Response Lead to monitor CSA Commissioner notices and maintain a log of all directives received. 2. Establish a documented process to acknowledge receipt of Commissioner directions within 24 hours and escalate to executive management. 3. Implement remediation actions per Commissioner guidance and maintain records of compliance steps taken within specified timeframes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: establish a register of all CSA Commissioner directives received, including date, content, and deadline; Legal team: draft internal escalation procedures and approval workflows for implementing Commissioner directions; Security team: document all remediation actions and their completion dates with evidence.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: sign an addendum confirming they will comply with any CSA Commissioner directions applicable to their systems or data processing on your behalf, and will provide cooperation notices to your organisation within 48 hours of receipt.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed register of Commissioner directives with dated acknowledgement stamps; copies of all Commissioner notices and correspondence; remediation action logs with completion dates and sign-offs; evidence artefacts (configuration changes, patches, audit reports) demonstrating compliance with each direction.

key_requirements.text: Cooperate with CSA investigations and provide access to information and systems
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 2 years for CII owners; fines up to SGD 500,000 for unlicensed cybersecurity services.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a CSA Liaison Officer responsible for responding to CSA investigation requests and maintaining a secure communication channel with the regulator. 2. Establish a documented procedure requiring all investigation requests to be logged, reviewed by Legal within 48 hours, and actioned with full cooperation unless legally constrained. 3. Ensure IT and Security teams maintain continuous access to all systems, logs, and data repositories required for investigation without unnecessary delay.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: create CSA Investigation Response Protocol defining roles, escalation paths, and approval authorities; IT team: document and maintain current inventories of system architectures, data locations, and access credentials; Security team: preserve and index all security logs, incident records, and forensic artefacts in a dedicated investigation repository; Compliance team: maintain a register of all CSA investigation requests received with response timelines.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party service providers: execute a CSA Cooperation Agreement confirming they will provide investigation access within 24 hours of your request, preserve all relevant logs and data, and indemnify your organisation for cooperation with CSA inquiries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: CSA Liaison Officer appointment letter; Investigation Response Protocol document with version control and approval signatures; register of investigation requests with dates, subjects, and closure evidence; timestamped logs showing system access provided to CSA within specified timelines; certificates or audit reports confirming data preservation and forensic integrity during investigations.

regulations.penalties: Fines up to SGD 100,000 and/or imprisonment up to 2 years for CII owners; fines up to SGD 500,000 for unlicensed cybersecurity services

regulations.name: China Export Control Law 2020
regulations.authority: Ministry of Commerce of China (MOFCOM)
regulations.summary: China's Export Control Law 2020 establishes a unified legal framework for controlling exports of dual-use items, military goods, nuclear materials, and other items related to national security. Imposes end-user and end-use controls, requires export licences, and introduces an unreliable entity list.

key_requirements.text: Obtain export licences from MOFCOM for controlled items on the export control list
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Maintain an updated inventory of products and technologies mapped to the MOFCON Export Control Commodity List, categorised by control status. 2. Establish a pre-export review process requiring Legal to assess each transaction and submit licence applications to MOFCOM at least 30 days before shipment. 3. Document approval numbers and validity periods; do not export without valid licence in hand.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide completed MOFCOM licence application templates, commodity classification worksheets, and a signed export control procedure manual. Finance team: maintain export transaction ledger with licence reference numbers.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Freight forwarder: provide signed undertaking that they will not ship without valid licence documentation; Customs broker: confirm they have received and verified original MOFCOM licence before customs clearance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Original MOFCOM export licence certificates with validity dates, signed export control procedure manual, completed commodity classification worksheets, export transaction ledger entries matched to licence numbers, copies of all licence applications and approvals.

key_requirements.text: Conduct end-user and end-use verification for controlled items before export
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a due diligence questionnaire covering end-user identity, business legitimacy, end-use purpose, and non-proliferation undertakings; require documentary evidence (business registration, bank statements, customer references). 2. Conduct secondary verification through third-party databases or direct contact with end-users before releasing goods. 3. Document all findings in a signed end-use certificate before shipment.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: design end-user verification checklist and due diligence questionnaire template. Legal team: review and approve questionnaire language and create end-use certificate template. Sales team: maintain a centralised database of verified end-user profiles with evidence attached.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customer/end-user: complete signed due diligence questionnaire, provide business registration certificate, bank statement, and written end-use declaration. Third-party verification provider: deliver verification report confirming legitimacy of end-user and stated end-use.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed due diligence questionnaires for each transaction, copies of end-user business registration and supporting documents, signed end-use certificates, third-party verification reports, sales team database with approval sign-off records, correspondence demonstrating secondary verification steps.

key_requirements.text: Implement internal compliance programme for export control (screening, recordkeeping, training)
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and document a formal export control compliance programme covering commodity screening, transaction review, record retention (minimum 5 years), and escalation procedures. 2. Deliver mandatory annual export control training to all staff with export responsibilities (Sales, Legal, Customs, Finance); maintain signed attendance records and test results. 3. Conduct quarterly compliance audits and issue findings reports to management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance/Legal team: draft and approve export control compliance programme manual and training curriculum. HR team: schedule and log annual training sessions; collect signed training attendance sheets and test scores. Compliance team: prepare quarterly internal audit reports documenting screening results and any breaches.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training provider (if external): deliver compliance training tailored to export control law and company procedures; provide signed certificate of completion and participant roster.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed export control compliance programme manual, training curriculum document, signed attendance sheets for all personnel for past 3 years, training test scores, certificates of completion, quarterly internal audit reports with remediation actions, evidence of management review and sign-off.

key_requirements.text: Establish internal export control systems for significant exporters
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. If organisation qualifies as significant exporter, establish a dedicated export control compliance function with defined roles, responsibilities, and reporting lines to senior management. 2. Implement automated screening tools integrated into the order-to-cash process to flag controlled items and high-risk customers at point of sale. 3. Create and maintain a centralised export control documentation repository with version control and audit trails.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: procure and configure export control screening software; provide system access logs and audit trail exports. Compliance team: hire or designate export control officer; draft role description and reporting structure. Legal/Compliance team: design and document system configuration, control matrices, and risk rating thresholds.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Software provider: deliver system with automated commodity screening, customer screening, and audit logging capabilities; provide signed statement of system security and data protection compliance.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Organisational chart showing dedicated export control function and reporting lines, signed role descriptions, system configuration documentation, system access and audit logs, automated screening alert records, centralised export control documentation repository with version history, proof of senior management oversight (board minutes or steering committee records).

key_requirements.text: Prohibit re-export of controlled items without Chinese government approval
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Include a contractual clause in all export agreements prohibiting downstream re-export of controlled items without prior written approval from the Chinese government and notification to the original exporter. 2. Require customers to warrant they are the legitimate end-user and will not re-export or transfer the goods to third parties. 3. Maintain a registry of re-export requests received and corresponding government approvals (or denials).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: embed re-export prohibition clause in standard export contract terms; obtain internal sign-off. Sales team: ensure all export contracts contain signed clause and maintain contract register. Compliance team: maintain re-export request log with dates, parties, and government response.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customer/distributor: sign export agreement including re-export prohibition clause and non-transfer warranty; provide written undertaking not to re-export without Chinese government approval.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed export agreements containing re-export prohibition clause for all transactions, contract register, signed non-transfer warranties, re-export request log with supporting correspondence, evidence of any government approvals for re-exports, copies of customer undertakings.

key_requirements.text: Screen customers and transactions against the Unreliable Entity List
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Integrate screening against the official Unreliable Entity List (maintained by MOFCOM) and equivalent lists (SDN, EU sanctions, etc.) into the customer onboarding and transaction approval process. 2. Conduct screening at point of new customer registration and quarterly for existing customers; document all screening hits and escalations. 3. Deny or suspend business with any customer matching a listed entity unless formal sanctions exemption or delisting is obtained.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: subscribe to MOFCOM Unreliable Entity List and other sanctions databases; document subscription and update frequency. IT team: integrate screening tool into CRM/ERP system; configure automated flagging and alert workflow. Compliance team: maintain screening results log with dates, entities screened, match status, and action taken.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Sanctions screening provider: deliver automated screening service with real-time updates to MOFCOM and other lists; provide signed SLA confirming update frequency and accuracy guarantees.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Proof of subscription to MOFCOM Unreliable Entity List and other sanctions databases, system configuration documentation showing screening integration, screening results log for all transactions (past 2 years), evidence of all screening hits and escalations, documentation of denied/suspended customers and reasons, copies of any exemption requests or delisting evidence.

key_requirements.text: Report suspicious export inquiries or diversion attempts to MOFCOM
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a written procedure defining what constitutes a suspicious export inquiry or diversion attempt (e.g., unusual destinations, end-use inconsistencies, intermediary involvement). 2. Designate a compliance officer responsible for reviewing export requests against this procedure and escalating flagged cases. 3. Submit written reports to MOFCOM within the statutory timeframe (typically 5 working days) with supporting documentation of the concern.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: draft and maintain a 'Red Flag Indicator' matrix documenting criteria for suspicious inquiries; Legal team: establish MOFCOM reporting protocol and retention schedule; Sales team: provide copies of all flagged customer inquiries and correspondence for internal case file.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Freight forwarders and customs brokers: provide signed attestation of commitment to report diversion risks to the exporter immediately upon detection; distributors: commit to immediately notify the company of unusual customer requests or re-export inquiries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Dated MOFCOM written reports (with case reference numbers and receipt confirmation); internal case files with Red Flag Indicator scoring; meeting minutes from compliance review sessions; email trails showing timely escalation and decision-making.

key_requirements.text: Comply with catch-all controls for items not on the list but intended for WMD or military end-use
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a due diligence procedure that screens all export inquiries—even for unlisted items—against WMD and military end-use indicators, including customer background, stated use, and destination country controls. 2. Implement a catch-all compliance checklist requiring sign-off by compliance and legal before shipment approval. 3. Maintain documented evidence of the due diligence assessment and retain all customer declarations and end-use certifications.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: create and maintain a master control matrix of WMD-related technologies and military end-uses aligned with MOFCOM guidance; IT team: configure export management system to flag transactions matching catch-all criteria and generate audit logs; Legal team: draft and update customer end-use certification templates and obtain signed declarations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Distributors and resellers: provide written commitment to prohibit onward transfer for WMD or military purposes and to notify the company of any such requests; freight forwarders: confirm they have verified ultimate destination and end-user and will not accept shipments with suspicious characteristics.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed customer end-use certificates and declarations; completed catch-all compliance checklists with approval sign-offs; system-generated transaction audit logs showing flag triggers and resolution; copies of MOFCOM catch-all guidance and internal control matrix versions with dates; correspondence with vendors confirming catch-all commitments.

regulations.penalties: Fines from RMB 500,000 to 10x the value of goods; licence revocation; addition to Unreliable Entity List; criminal prosecution for severe violations

regulations.name: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA)
regulations.authority: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) / Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force (FLETF)
regulations.summary: The Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act creates a rebuttable presumption that all goods mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part in China's Xinjiang region were made using forced labor and are barred from US imports. Importers must provide clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption.

key_requirements.text: Do not import goods produced wholly or in part in Xinjiang or by UFLPA-listed entities
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a restricted supplier list excluding all entities with known Xinjiang operations or UFLPA sanctions. 2. Implement automated screening of purchase orders against this list before approval. 3. Document all sourcing decisions with geographic origin certification from suppliers.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: maintain updated list of blocked suppliers and UFLPA-listed entities; Finance team: provide purchase order records for audit trail; Legal team: draft supplier restriction policy with enforcement mechanism.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: sign declaration of non-sourcing from Xinjiang; provide corporate registration documents confirming registered office location outside Xinjiang; certify no use of materials or components from UFLPA-listed manufacturers.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed supplier declarations; restricted supplier list with dates of implementation; purchase order audit logs showing automatic rejection of non-compliant vendors; UFLPA Entity List screening reports dated and timestamped.

key_requirements.text: Conduct comprehensive supply chain tracing to identify any Xinjiang-sourced materials
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map supply chain for all product categories to identify material origins, starting with high-risk goods. 2. Collect bills of lading, customs invoices, and certificates of origin for all materials tracing back to raw material producer. 3. Conduct quarterly audits of tier-1, tier-2, and tier-3 suppliers with documented supplier responses.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Supply Chain team: create supply chain mapping template and maintain master database of all suppliers by tier; Procurement team: collect and organize origin documentation; Compliance team: design quarterly audit checklist and schedule vendor questionnaires.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Tier-1 suppliers: complete detailed supply chain questionnaire identifying all sub-suppliers and material sources; provide bills of lading and customs documentation; commit to flow-down of tracing requirements to tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers; designate compliance contact for ongoing inquiries.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed supply chain mapping database; supplier questionnaire responses signed by authorized vendor representative; bills of lading and certificates of origin; audit meeting notes and evidence of supplier corrective actions; documented communication trail showing quarterly verification cycles.

key_requirements.text: Maintain records demonstrating supply chain due diligence and absence of forced labour
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Create a due diligence documentation repository organized by supplier and product SKU. 2. Collect baseline due diligence assessments (audit reports, certifications, signed attestations) for all active suppliers. 3. Maintain a control log recording review dates, findings, and approval sign-offs by a designated compliance officer.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: design due diligence file template with required documentation checklist; Compliance team: establish document retention schedule (minimum 7 years); IT team: set up secure repository with access controls and audit logging; Finance team: confirm supplier communication records are retained.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: provide third-party audit reports (SMETA, SA8000, or equivalent) covering labour practices; submit signed attestation of compliance with forced labour prohibitions; provide evidence of worker interviews or grievance mechanisms; commit to annual recertification.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Due diligence file for each supplier containing: signed attestations, audit reports with dates, certificates of compliance, supply chain mapping documents, correspondence confirming receipt and review; master control log showing review dates and approval signatures; document retention policy signed by Records Management.

key_requirements.text: If goods are detained by CBP, provide clear and convincing evidence against forced labour to obtain release
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a CBP detention response protocol with pre-prepared documentation templates and escalation contacts. 2. When detention occurs, immediately initiate evidence collection from all relevant suppliers covering raw material sourcing through manufacturing. 3. Compile a written submission to CBP with certificates of origin, audit reports, worker interviews, and supply chain mapping proving absence of forced labour.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft detention response template and train key personnel on evidence gathering procedures; Compliance team: maintain contact list for suppliers and logistics partners; IT team: establish expedited document retrieval process; Finance team: secure supplier invoices and payment records showing commercial relationships.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Affected suppliers: within 48 hours of request, provide detailed written statement on labour sourcing and worker treatment; supply worker pay records, employment contracts, and evidence of voluntary employment; arrange for third-party site inspection or worker interviews if necessary; provide any existing audit reports or certification documents.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Dated CBP detention notice; written submission to CBP with supporting documents (certificates of origin, audit reports, supplier attestations, worker interview summaries, supply chain diagrams); supplier written statements with authorization signatures; correspondence log showing dates of evidence requests and supplier responses; legal memorandum summarizing findings and conclusions.

key_requirements.text: Screen suppliers against the UFLPA Entity List (companies sanctioned for using forced labour)
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Download and import the current UFLPA Entity List into procurement systems with automated matching against new suppliers. 2. Screen all existing suppliers against the list and document results with dates and responsible parties. 3. Establish a monthly review cycle to check for list updates and re-screen flagged entities.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: integrate UFLPA Entity List into vendor management system with automated matching alerts; Procurement team: conduct baseline screening of all active suppliers and document results; Compliance team: schedule monthly list update checks and assign responsibility for review and escalation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: All suppliers: complete vendor information form confirming no listed entity is a parent company, subsidiary, or operating division; provide corporate family tree and list of affiliated entities; certify compliance with screening request; agree to immediate notification if status changes.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: UFLPA Entity List screening report showing all suppliers checked with dates and results; copies of vendor information forms signed by suppliers; audit log from vendor management system showing automated screening alerts and date/time stamps; monthly screening update records; escalation documentation for any matches or unclear cases.

key_requirements.text: Implement supply chain mapping to at least the third tier for high-risk categories (cotton, polysilicon, tomatoes, PPE, electronics)
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all products in high-risk categories (cotton, polysilicon, tomatoes, PPE, electronics) and classify by sourcing geography. 2. Require tier-1 suppliers to map their supply chain to tier-3 with supporting documentation (contracts, invoices, certifications). 3. Conduct annual deep-dive audits of identified tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers in high-risk categories, prioritizing Xinjiang-adjacent regions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Supply Chain team: create product classification matrix identifying high-risk category items; develop three-tier mapping template with required data fields; Procurement team: collect and organize supplier contracts showing sub-supplier relationships; Compliance team: design tier-3 audit questionnaire and schedule site visits.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Tier-1 suppliers: provide signed supply chain diagram identifying tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers by name and location; supply purchase orders or contracts with tier-2 suppliers; facilitate access to tier-2 and tier-3 contact information; support on-site audits or allow third-party verification visits.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed three-tier supply chain maps for all high-risk products with dates; contracts and purchase orders between tiers; completed tier-2 and tier-3 supplier questionnaires with signatures; audit reports from site visits to high-risk tier-3 facilities; photos or inspection evidence from audits; corrective action plans for any non-compliance findings; annual recertification sign-offs from tier-1 suppliers.

key_requirements.text: Engage with suppliers on their own supply chain transparency and forced labour policies
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Issue a formal Supplier Forced Labour Policy Questionnaire to all active suppliers requesting documented evidence of their own supply chain due diligence processes and forced labour risk assessments.
2. Conduct annual review meetings with Tier-1 suppliers to discuss their supply chain transparency measures, audit findings, and corrective actions related to forced labour risks.
3. Document all supplier responses and maintain a register of which suppliers have provided compliant attestations and which require remediation or escalation.
internal_actions: Procurement team: draft and issue standardized Supplier Forced Labour Policy Questionnaire template and track response deadlines and completion rates.
Legal team: establish criteria for acceptable supplier responses and define escalation procedures for non-compliant or unresponsive suppliers.
Compliance team: create and maintain a Supplier Policy Compliance Register with dates of questionnaire issuance, responses received, and follow-up actions.
vendor_actions: All suppliers: complete and sign the Forced Labour Policy Attestation Form certifying their own supply chain due diligence and forced labour prevention policies.
Tier-1 suppliers: provide copies of their written supply chain transparency and forced labour risk assessment policies, and commit to annual review meetings.
High-risk suppliers: provide evidence of third-party audits or certifications (e.g., SMETA, SA8000) demonstrating forced labour controls in their supply chain.
compliance_evidence: Completed and signed Supplier Forced Labour Policy Questionnaires and Attestation Forms from all active suppliers, with dated submission records.
Supplier Policy Compliance Register showing questionnaire distribution dates, response dates, compliance status, and any corrective action plans.
Meeting minutes or written summaries from annual supplier review discussions documenting forced labour policy commitments and supply chain transparency measures.
Copies of supplier-provided forced labour prevention policies and supply chain due diligence documents retained in a centralized vendor file.

key_requirements.text: Maintain and provide to CBP upon request: supply chain tracing, supply chain due diligence policies, and business records
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a Supply Chain Tracing System that documents the origin, movement, and processing of all products and raw materials from source through final distribution, with particular focus on Xinjiang-region sourcing.

2. Compile a master Supply Chain Due Diligence Policy document that outlines your organization's forced labour risk identification, supplier vetting, audit procedures, and remediation steps, and ensure it is applied consistently across all procurement.

3. Implement a Business Records Retention Protocol to preserve all relevant documentation (supplier contracts, audit reports, communications, test results, shipment records) in a retrievable format for minimum 3 years, with designated custodians.
internal_actions: Supply Chain/Operations team: create and maintain a detailed Supply Chain Tracing Register (spreadsheet or database) for each product line showing supplier names, material origins, processing locations, and countries of manufacture.

Legal team: draft and finalize the organization's formal Supply Chain Due Diligence Policy (document clearly defining forced labour risks, due diligence procedures, corrective action protocols, and supplier accountability measures).

IT/Records Management team: establish a centralized Business Records Repository (secure shared drive or document management system) with folder structure, access controls, and retention schedules for all CBP-relevant records.
Compliance team: create a CBP Request Response Protocol and assign a primary custodian responsible for coordinating document retrieval and timely submission to U.S. Customs and Border Protection.
vendor_actions: All suppliers: provide detailed supply chain tracing information (source of raw materials, sub-suppliers, processing locations, and final manufacturing locations) in response to the organization's supply chain transparency request.
All suppliers: confirm in writing their alignment with the organization's Supply Chain Due Diligence Policy or provide their own equivalent policy for review and acceptance.

Suppliers: maintain and make available upon request their own business records (audit reports, test certificates, labour certifications, quality documentation) that demonstrate compliance with forced labour standards.
compliance_evidence: Complete Supply Chain Tracing Register for all product categories showing documented origin, sub-suppliers, processing locations, and manufacturing locations (accessible and date-stamped).
Signed formal Supply Chain Due Diligence Policy document (bearing effective date and management sign-off) that details the organization's forced labour risk assessment and remediation procedures.

Indexed Business Records Repository with documented file structure, retention schedule, and custodian assignment clearly demonstrating ability to locate and retrieve specific records within 5 business days.
CBP Request Log recording all Customs inquiries, request dates, documents provided, and submission dates, demonstrating consistent and timely response capability.
Sample retrieval test results or audit finding showing the organization successfully located and produced requested supply chain and due diligence records within a specified timeframe.

regulations.penalties: Detention and seizure of imported goods; exclusion from US market; commercial disruption; potential reputational damage

regulations.name: FuelEU Maritime Regulation (EU) 2023/1805
regulations.authority: European Maritime Safety Agency (EMSA) and national Maritime Authorities
regulations.summary: FuelEU Maritime mandates progressive reduction of the GHG intensity of energy used on board ships calling at EU ports. Starting 2025, ships must comply with declining GHG intensity targets (2%, 6%, 13%, 31%, 62%, 80% reductions by 2025-2050) and promotes adoption of sustainable maritime fuels (methanol, ammonia, hydrogen).

key_requirements.text: Comply with GHG intensity limits for energy used on board ships calling at EU ports from 2025
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a baseline GHG intensity audit of all vessels calling at EU ports, calculating grams of CO2 per megajoule of energy consumed. 2. Establish annual reduction targets aligned with EU limits (93.5% of baseline in 2025, declining to 80% by 2035) and map vessel fuel sourcing against these targets. 3. Implement fleet-wide fuel selection and operational procedures to ensure compliance before each EU port call.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide fuel purchase invoices and energy consumption records by vessel for 2024–2025. Operations team: submit vessel routing logs, engine performance data, and voyage fuel burn estimates. Compliance team: develop and document GHG intensity calculation methodology and reduction roadmap.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Fuel suppliers: provide certification of fuel GHG intensity values and lifecycle assessment data. Port authorities: confirm deadlines and calculation methods for GHG reporting at each EU port. Classification society: validate vessel energy efficiency baseline and calculations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: GHG intensity baseline report per vessel; fuel purchase certificates with GHG intensity values; annual fleet GHG intensity compliance statement; voyage logs showing fuel consumption and energy output; third-party verified GHG calculation reports.

key_requirements.text: Use EU Monitoring, Reporting and Verification (MRV) system to report fuel consumption and GHG data
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Register all vessels with the EU MRV system and obtain unique IMO numbers and monitoring plan identifications. 2. Install or upgrade on-board monitoring systems to automatically capture fuel consumption, distance travelled, cargo, and voyage-related variables in real time. 3. Compile and submit quarterly fuel consumption and GHG data reports to the EU MRV portal, with annual verification by an independent third party.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: establish secure data pipeline from vessel monitoring systems to EU MRV submission software; provide audit logs of all data uploads. Compliance team: create MRV monitoring plan template and assign vessel-level data owners. Finance team: reconcile fuel purchase data against reported MRV consumption figures.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: MRV software provider: confirm system integration with EU portal, data validation rules, and audit trail capabilities. Vessel equipment suppliers: certify fuel flow meters and bunker tank monitoring systems meet ISO 4217 accuracy standards. Independent verifier: provide third-party assurance protocol and annual verification certification template.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: EU MRV system registration confirmation; completed monitoring plans per vessel; quarterly MRV data submission receipts from EU portal; fuel meter calibration certificates; independent third-party verification reports; data reconciliation statements comparing fuel purchases to reported consumption.

key_requirements.text: Obtain a FuelEU Maritime Document of Compliance verified by an accredited organisation
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a Document of Compliance (DoC) administrator and select an accredited Classification Society or Recognized Organization to conduct the initial audit. 2. Compile evidence of GHG intensity compliance, fuel sourcing documentation, MRV reporting, and operational procedures into a single DoC submission package. 3. Submit the package to the accredited verifier and obtain signed DoC; maintain DoC validity through annual audits and updates.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Compliance team: designate DoC administrator and establish audit file with all supporting evidence (fuel certificates, GHG calculations, MRV reports, crew training records). Legal team: draft and approve company fuel policy and operational procedures document. Quality team: schedule and document internal audit to verify readiness before third-party verification.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Accredited verifier (Classification Society/RO): provide FuelEU DoC audit checklist, fees, and timeline; conduct initial and annual audits; issue and sign the official DoC certificate. Flag state authority: confirm notification of DoC holder and any relevant state-level requirements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed FuelEU Maritime Document of Compliance; accredited verifier's audit report and certification; supporting evidence file containing fuel GHG certificates, GHG intensity calculations, MRV annual reports, vessel inspection records, crew training certificates, and company procedures; annual audit reports and DoC renewal confirmations.

key_requirements.text: Comply with the On-shore Power Supply (OPS) obligation at designated EU ports from 2030
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Identify all designated EU ports where OPS is available (as published in the EU list) and assess which vessels in the fleet will call at those ports from 2030 onward. 2. Negotiate OPS connection agreements with port authorities and ensure vessels have compatible shore-side power reception equipment installed or retrofit by the 2030 deadline. 3. Establish operational procedures to mandate OPS use whenever available at designated ports, and maintain records of OPS usage and any exemption requests.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: audit current fleet OPS compatibility, schedule retrofits for non-compliant vessels, and assign vessel captains responsibility for OPS connection at designated ports. Finance team: budget OPS retrofit costs and connection fees; track OPS usage charges. Compliance team: document exemption criteria (technical failure, unavailable connection, safety risk) and create a formal exemption request template.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Port authorities at designated EU ports: provide OPS availability confirmation, technical specifications, and connection fee schedules. Equipment suppliers: install OPS reception systems, provide certification of compatibility, and deliver installation and maintenance manuals. OPS service providers: confirm booking procedures, connection protocols, and supply of power quality documentation.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Fleet OPS compatibility audit report; retrofit project plans and completion certificates for each vessel; signed OPS connection agreements with designated EU ports; operational procedures manual requiring OPS use; quarterly OPS usage logs showing connection events at designated ports; records of any exemption requests with supporting evidence (technical reports, port correspondence); independent audit confirming OPS readiness by 2030.

key_requirements.text: Use FuelEU balance pooling mechanism to share surplus and deficit compliance across fleets
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Calculate annual GHG intensity surplus or deficit for the entire fleet and determine eligibility for pooling under EU rules (multi-vessel operator fleets, or contractual pooling groups). 2. If surplus exists, formally register the fleet in the EU pooling mechanism and document surplus/deficit allocation rules in a signed pooling agreement. 3. Report pool-level compliance to the EU MRV authority and maintain updated records of all fleet members in the pool and their individual contribution to the collective GHG target.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: conduct annual fleet-wide GHG intensity reconciliation (aggregate fuel consumption and GHG by all vessels, divide by total energy output). Compliance team: model surplus/deficit scenarios and document pooling eligibility. Legal team: draft or review pooling agreement if joining a third-party pool, defining cost-sharing and liability terms.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: EU MRV authority: confirm pooling registration procedures and reporting templates. Pooling intermediaries (if applicable): provide pooling agreement terms, cost allocation methodology, and annual reconciliation reports. External fleet operators (in multi-operator pools): share individual fleet GHG data and agree to data-sharing protocol.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual fleet GHG intensity reconciliation report with aggregate calculations; EU pooling mechanism registration confirmation (if applicable); signed pooling agreement (if multi-party); individual vessel GHG intensity reports for all fleet members; pool-level compliance report submitted to EU MRV; annual surplus/deficit allocation statement and cost settlement documentation.

key_requirements.text: Increase use of sustainable fuels including renewable fuels of non-biological origin (RFNBOs)
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a sustainable fuel procurement strategy specifying annual volume targets for advanced biofuels, synthetic fuels (RFNBOs), and other low-carbon fuels, with escalating targets toward 2030+ (starting at 2% by 2025, rising to 70% by 2050). 2. Identify and contract qualified sustainable fuel suppliers with valid EU sustainability certification (ISCC, RSB, or equivalent) and obtain fuel GHG intensity documentation for each delivery. 3. Monitor annual sustainable fuel consumption against targets, adjust procurement contracts if shortfalls occur, and document all purchases and GHG savings in compliance records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: create sustainable fuel supplier scorecard, qualify vendors against EU sustainability standards, and negotiate long-term supply agreements with volume commitments. Finance team: track sustainable fuel purchase volumes, costs, and price differentials; budget for potential cost premiums. Operations team: ensure fuel handling procedures accommodate different fuel types (blends, synthetic fuels) and maintain consumption tracking by fuel type.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Sustainable fuel suppliers: provide valid EU sustainability certification (ISCC+, RSB, ECOCERT, or equivalent), GHG intensity documentation per batch, and annual volume availability forecasts. Certification bodies: issue and renew sustainability certificates covering production pathways, emissions calculations, and traceability. Fuel traders/intermediaries: confirm chain-of-custody documentation and batch-level GHG values.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Sustainable fuel procurement policy with annual volume targets; supplier sustainability certification copies; signed contracts with minimum sustainable fuel volume commitments; fuel delivery invoices and blending receipts showing sustainable fuel percentage and type; batch-level GHG intensity certificates; annual sustainable fuel consumption report with calculation of GHG reduction achieved; third-party audit of sustainable fuel sourcing and traceability.

key_requirements.text: Ships with non-compliance balances must remediate by paying penalties or pooling arrangements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish internal fuel compliance tracking system to monitor compliance balance monthly against regulatory thresholds. 2. Establish written procedures for non-compliance scenarios: either direct penalty payment to competent authority or formal pooling agreement execution with other ship operators. 3. Maintain documented remediation pathway with timeline and responsible parties for each non-compliant vessel.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: establish GL account codes for fuel compliance penalties and create monthly reconciliation process showing compliance balance by vessel. Legal team: draft template pooling agreement and non-compliance remediation procedures. Operations team: configure vessel monitoring system to flag compliance shortfalls 30 days before reporting deadline.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Fuel supplier: provide monthly bunker delivery invoices with fuel carbon intensity certificates (EU MRV data). Classification society or maritime consultant: provide third-party verification of fuel compliance calculations and pooling eligibility assessments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Monthly fuel compliance balance spreadsheets signed by Finance Manager; executed pooling agreements with counterparty signatures and stamps; penalty payment evidence (bank statements, payment confirmations to competent authority); third-party compliance verification reports with auditor credentials.

key_requirements.text: Applies to all ships over 5,000 GT calling at EU/EEA ports regardless of flag state
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct vessel registry audit to identify all ships in fleet over 5,000 GT, regardless of flag state, that call at EU/EEA ports. 2. Document port call schedule and frequency for each identified vessel using AIS data and port authority records. 3. Apply FuelEU Maritime requirements to all identified vessels in compliance scope regardless of ownership or registration flag.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Fleet Management team: provide complete vessel registry with GT measurements, flag states, and EU/EEA port calling patterns for last 24 months. IT team: generate AIS port call reports and extract EU/EEA port visit frequency by vessel. Legal team: confirm scope assessment and document vessels in/out of regulatory perimeter.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: AIS data provider: deliver certified port call logs showing each vessel's EU/EEA port entries for audit period. Port authorities: provide official port call records and vessel entry documentation for verification. Classification society: confirm GT calculations and vessel specifications from official class records.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Fleet Scope Assessment Report listing all vessels ≥5,000 GT with flag states and EU/EEA port call frequencies; AIS port call data exports certified by data provider; official port authority port call records; vessel classification certificates showing GT measurements; board-approved scope decision documenting rationale for inclusion/exclusion.

regulations.penalties: Annual penalty of EUR 2,400 per tonne of Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent for non-compliance balance; additional sanctions from Member States

milestones.label: Reporting period begins for 2025 fuel data

regulations.name: EU Import Control System 2 (ICS2)
regulations.authority: European Commission — Directorate-General for Taxation and Customs Union (DG TAXUD)
regulations.summary: EU Import Control System 2 (ICS2) is a phased customs pre-arrival safety and security programme replacing ICS1. It requires economic operators to file Entry Summary Declarations (ENS) with pre-loading/pre-arrival data enabling customs risk analysis before goods arrive in the EU. Phase 3 (full scope for all maritime/air/road) is effective March 2025.

key_requirements.text: File Entry Summary Declarations (ENS) with accurate pre-loading data before goods depart for EU
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a pre-loading data capture process requiring shipper/freight forwarder submission of commercial invoices, packing lists, and HS codes minimum 24 hours before vessel/aircraft departure. 2. Route all captured data through your customs broker or directly to the EU Customs trader portal to generate and file the Entry Summary Declaration (ENS). 3. Implement a checklist-driven QA step to verify shipper-provided HS codes and descriptions against Harmonized System rules before submission.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Customs/Trade Compliance team: document the pre-loading data collection workflow, SOP for HS code validation, and proof of ENS filing timestamps. Finance/Supply Chain: confirm shipper instructions are embedded in purchase orders and freight booking terms.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customs broker or freight forwarder: provide written confirmation of ENS filing capability and undertake to file ENS at least 24 hours pre-departure with supporting commercial documents attached. Shippers/suppliers: commit to supplying complete product description, quantity, weight, and preliminary HS code classification with each commercial invoice.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed ENS filing confirmations from customs broker, timestamped ENS submissions from trader portal, sample commercial invoices and packing lists annotated with HS codes, internal SOP document for pre-loading data validation.

key_requirements.text: Provide minimum dataset at pre-loading; full dataset at pre-arrival (PLACI obligations)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define the 'minimum dataset' (shipper, consignee, commodity description, container count, total weight, vessel/flight details) and map it to your IT system fields; ensure capture at pre-loading stage. 2. Establish a pre-arrival data enrichment process (typically 24 hours before EU port arrival) to add the full dataset including commercial invoice detail, weight per item, and final HS classification. 3. File the PLACI (Pre-Loading and Pre-Arrival Cargo Information) submission via the EU Customs portal confirming both minimum and full datasets before vessel/aircraft arrival.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: provide data dictionary mapping minimum dataset fields to full dataset fields, and log exports showing pre-loading vs. pre-arrival submission timestamps. Customs/Trade Compliance: create and document PLACI submission checklist and schedule.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customs broker: provide written undertaking to file minimum dataset at pre-loading and full PLACI dataset at pre-arrival stages, with evidence of portal submissions. Freight forwarder: confirm supply of complete commercial invoice (value, Incoterms, final HS codes) no later than 48 hours before EU port arrival.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Timestamped minimum dataset submissions, timestamped full PLACI dataset submissions, sample completed PLACI forms, portal audit logs showing progression from pre-loading to pre-arrival filing, signed broker undertaking.

key_requirements.text: Postal and express carriers must file ENS data before loading in non-EU origin countries
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. For all postal and express shipments, identify the carrier (DHL, FedEx, UPS, etc.) and confirm in writing their ENS filing responsibility and process. 2. Build a pre-dispatch data supply process: ensure your shipper provides full product detail, weights, and preliminary HS codes to the carrier at point of handoff. 3. Implement a spot-check audit (monthly sample) of carrier ENS filings by reviewing tracking reference numbers cross-referenced to EU Customs ENS logs.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Supply Chain/Shipping team: document the pre-dispatch data package template provided to carriers and maintain a register of all shipments with tracking numbers and dispatch dates. Compliance team: establish and run monthly carrier ENS filing audit with findings log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Postal/Express carriers (DHL, FedEx, UPS, etc.): provide written confirmation of ENS filing capability, guaranteed filing timeline (before loading in non-EU origin), and undertake to supply monthly audit reports showing all ENS filings originating from your shipments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Carrier written undertaking confirming ENS filing responsibility and timeline, monthly carrier audit reports, sample shipment data transmissions (shipper to carrier), cross-referenced tracking numbers and ENS filing confirmations from EU Customs logs.

key_requirements.text: All economic operators in the supply chain may be required to contribute ENS data
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Map your supply chain (shipper, freight forwarder, customs broker, carrier, consignee) and document each party's role and ENS data contribution obligation. 2. Issue a formal data-sharing and responsibility matrix to all upstream suppliers and freight partners, clearly stating who is responsible for providing HS codes, weights, shipper/consignee details. 3. Embed ENS data requirements into your standard trading terms and supplier agreements, with annual sign-off.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft and maintain a supply-chain responsibility matrix and amend standard terms to include ENS data provision clauses. Procurement/Supply Chain: distribute the responsibility matrix to all active suppliers and freight partners; maintain signed acknowledgments.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Suppliers: sign acknowledgment confirming they will provide product HS code, harmonized commodity description, and net weight with every shipment document. Freight forwarders/customs brokers: provide written confirmation they will solicit and consolidate ENS data from all supply-chain parties and identify data gaps in writing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed supply-chain responsibility matrix, amended terms and conditions with ENS clauses, signed acknowledgments from suppliers and freight partners, documented examples of ENS data requests issued to supply-chain parties.

key_requirements.text: Respond to 'Do Not Load' or 'Authorisation Needed' messages from EU customs within specified timeframes
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a 24/7 monitoring protocol: assign custody of the EU Customs trader portal account to a named compliance officer and deputy; set up automatic email notifications or API monitoring for 'Do Not Load' (DNL) and 'Authorisation Needed' (AN) messages. 2. Create a response SOP specifying: DNL requires immediate shipment hold and written response to customs within the specified timeframe (typically 12–24 hours); AN requires submission of requested documentation or proof of compliance within timeframe. 3. Log every DNL/AN message received, action taken, and response submitted in a centralized incident register with timestamps.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: configure portal alert routing and API log exports; ensure 24/7 escalation chain is documented. Compliance/Customs team: author DNL/AN response SOP, assign roles and responsibility, establish incident log, and run quarterly drills to test response time.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customs broker: undertake to monitor trader portal continuously on behalf of the organization and escalate any DNL/AN message within 1 hour of receipt; provide written emergency contact protocol. Freight forwarder/carrier: commit to immediately halt loading of any shipment flagged DNL pending your authorization.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed SOP for DNL/AN response with defined timeframes, incident log excerpts showing received messages, dates/times of responses, and corrective actions taken, email/SMS alerts proving 24/7 notification setup, sample response letters to customs, broker escalation protocol.

key_requirements.text: Maintain data accuracy and completeness — use of EU Customs trader portal or APIs
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a data quality audit: map all ENS fields (shipper, consignee, HS code, description, weight) to their source systems (ERP, freight management, supplier data) and implement automated validation rules (e.g., HS code length, weight unit consistency, required-field flags). 2. Designate direct ownership of ENS data accuracy: assign accountability to your freight/customs team (for submission via portal/API) and to suppliers (for product classification). 3. Perform monthly reconciliation: compare ENS data filed against supporting commercial documents; document and correct any discrepancies.
key_requirements.internal_actions: IT team: implement automated data validation rules in your systems, provide quarterly data quality reports, and establish API audit logs if using direct customs portal integration. Finance/Supply Chain: provide sample invoices and packing lists monthly for reconciliation against filed ENS. Compliance team: author and maintain data quality SOP and discrepancy log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Customs broker/freight forwarder: sign undertaking to validate all ENS data against supporting commercial documents before filing, flag discrepancies to you in writing, and provide quarterly data accuracy attestation. ERP/System vendor: confirm API or data export functionality supporting real-time validation against HS code and product databases.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Data quality SOP document, automated validation rule configuration (screenshots), monthly data quality audit reports, sample commercial documents cross-referenced to filed ENS records, discrepancy log and corrections, broker data accuracy attestations, portal/API audit logs showing validation checkpoints.

key_requirements.text: Implement ICS2 trader portal access or integrate via certified third-party software
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
how_to_meet: 1. Evaluate and select either direct ICS2 trader portal access or a certified third-party software provider that holds EU Customs certification for ICS2 submissions.
2. Deploy the chosen solution in your customs declaration workflow and configure user roles with appropriate access controls.
3. Conduct end-to-end testing of pre-clearance data submission and confirm successful transmission to EU Customs authorities.
internal_actions: IT team: document the technical architecture, authentication mechanism, and audit trail capabilities of the selected ICS2 solution.
IT team: provide user access provisioning records and role-based access control configuration documentation.
Legal team: confirm contractual terms for certification validity period and support SLAs for the chosen platform.
vendor_actions: Software vendor: provide current EU Customs ICS2 certification or accreditation certificate with validity dates.
Software vendor: supply technical integration documentation, API specifications, and error handling protocols.
Software vendor: commit to maintaining certification status and notify immediately of any suspension or withdrawal.
compliance_evidence: Vendor's EU Customs ICS2 certification or approved software list entry.
System configuration screenshots showing user roles and access controls.
Test submission reports confirming successful transmission of pre-clearance data to EU Customs.
Signed Service Level Agreement specifying uptime, security, and certification maintenance obligations.

key_requirements.text: Economic operators must register in EU Customs Trader Portal and hold EORI number
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. Register your economic operator entity in the EU Customs Trader Portal (ECTP) using authorised signatory credentials and complete all mandatory fields.
2. Obtain and document the assigned EORI number provided by the EU Member State competent authority.
3. Update all customs declaration processes, commercial contracts, and logistics instructions to reference the active EORI number.
internal_actions: Legal team: confirm signatory authority and collect evidence of authorisation to register as an economic operator in the Trader Portal.
Compliance team: establish a register recording the EORI number, registration date, and responsible individual.
Finance/Trade team: update all vendor master data, shipping instructions, and customs declaration templates with the registered EORI.
vendor_actions: Customs brokers or freight forwarders: request confirmation that they have received and recorded your EORI number in their systems.
Third-party software providers: request that they have configured your EORI in their customs declaration module.
compliance_evidence: EORI registration certificate or official acknowledgement letter from the EU Member State Customs Authority.
Screenshot or export from ECTP showing active registration status and EORI details.
Internal EORI register with registration date and responsible officer name.
Copies of updated commercial invoices or shipping documents showing EORI in the importer/exporter field.

regulations.penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations

regulations.name: Ocean Shipping Reform Act of 2022 (OSRA)
regulations.authority: Federal Maritime Commission (FMC)
regulations.summary: The Ocean Shipping Reform Act of 2022 strengthens the Shipping Act of 1984 to address unfair practices by ocean common carriers. It provides new FMC enforcement tools, requires carriers to publish service contracts, prohibits unreasonable refusals of export cargo bookings, and mandates transparency in detention and demurrage charges.

key_requirements.text: Ocean carriers must maintain public tariffs and service contracts on file with FMC
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a document management system to collect, version, and archive all tariffs and service contracts from ocean carrier partners. 2. Create a quarterly reconciliation process to verify all active tariffs and contracts are current and filed with FMC. 3. Designate a compliance owner to monitor FMC filing deadlines and maintain a filing log with submission confirmations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: maintain a centralized register of all ocean carrier tariffs and service contracts with effective dates and FMC filing references.
