Finance team: provide billing records to cross-verify tariff terms against invoices. Compliance team: establish and document the quarterly review and filing verification process.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: provide certified copies of all tariffs and service contracts filed with FMC, including filing confirmation numbers and effective dates. Carriers must notify in writing within 5 business days of any tariff or contract amendments.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: FMC filing confirmation receipts, dated tariff and service contract documents with version control, quarterly reconciliation certifications signed by compliance owner, and a maintained filing log showing submission dates and FMC reference numbers.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on ocean carriers unreasonably refusing to negotiate service contracts with exporters
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document all service contract negotiation requests and carrier responses in a centralized tracking system with dates, terms offered, and reasons for any rejections. 2. Establish an escalation procedure for disputed refusals, including internal review by Legal and a formal written response to the carrier. 3. Conduct quarterly audits to identify patterns of unreasonable refusals and prepare summary reports for FMC if required.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: define criteria for 'reasonable' refusal based on OSRA guidance and create a template decision log. Procurement team: implement a negotiation request tracking system with rejection reason codes. Compliance team: perform quarterly analysis and prepare exception reports for management review.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: provide written justification for any refusal to negotiate, citing specific operational, safety, or contractual constraints. Carriers must respond to all negotiation requests in writing within 10 business days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed negotiation request tracking log with carrier responses, documented refusal justifications, escalation decision memos, quarterly audit reports with trend analysis, and correspondence demonstrating good-faith negotiation attempts.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on unreasonable refusal of requests for vessel space for US exports
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vessel space request procedure requiring carriers to confirm receipt and provide a written response (approval or detailed reason for refusal) within 5 business days. 2. Maintain a log of all space requests, approvals, and refusals with supporting justification. 3. Implement a quarterly review to identify any patterns of unreasonable refusals and escalate to carrier management if needed.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: design and implement a vessel space request submission and tracking system with timestamp records. Legal team: develop criteria for evaluating 'reasonable' refusal reasons (safety, capacity, regulatory restrictions). Compliance team: execute quarterly analysis and exception reporting.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: issue written confirmation of all space requests within 2 business days and provide explicit reasons for any refusal (e.g., vessel capacity, safety constraints, regulatory holds). Carriers must provide alternative sailing dates or routes when refusing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Vessel space request tracking system with timestamps and carrier responses, written refusal justifications from carriers, quarterly trend reports on approval and refusal rates, and documented escalations to carrier management with responses.

key_requirements.text: Ocean carriers must provide detailed billing for detention and demurrage charges
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Require ocean carriers to provide itemized billing invoices that separately list detention charges, demurrage charges, duration of hold, applicable rate per day, and total amount due. 2. Create an invoice validation process comparing charges against the filed tariff rates and service contract terms. 3. Maintain all billing documentation in a centralized repository with dispute resolution tracking.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: establish an invoice audit protocol requiring line-item verification against carrier tariffs and contracts before payment. Accounting team: create a general ledger code structure segregating detention and demurrage charges for reporting. Compliance team: maintain a disputes and resolution log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: provide itemized billing statements showing detention duration (date/time in and out), demurrage duration, applicable rate per day, and FMC-filed tariff reference. Invoices must be issued within 10 business days of container release and include a detailed charge breakdown.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Sample itemized invoices from carriers with detailed charge breakdowns, tariff comparison spreadsheets showing rate validation, invoice audit approval records, segregated general ledger reports for detention and demurrage, and a maintained disputes log with resolution notes.

key_requirements.text: Detention and demurrage charges must be just and reasonable — FMC can review and refund excess
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a quarterly rate-reasonableness review comparing detention and demurrage charges against FMC-published benchmarks and industry standards. 2. Document any charges that exceed reasonable thresholds and prepare a summary for FMC review if required. 3. Establish a refund request procedure and maintain records of all refund claims submitted to carriers and their outcomes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: perform quarterly benchmark analysis of detention and demurrage charges, comparing against FMC published rates and peer benchmarks. Legal team: develop internal policy defining 'just and reasonable' thresholds. Compliance team: prepare quarterly rate-reasonableness reports and maintain a refund request log.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: provide historical rate data and methodology for calculating detention and demurrage charges. Carriers must acknowledge and process refund requests within 15 business days, providing written justification if refund is denied.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Quarterly rate-reasonableness analysis reports with benchmark comparisons, internal policy document defining 'just and reasonable' criteria, refund request letters and carrier responses, credit memos or refund receipts, and summary documentation for FMC submission if needed.

key_requirements.text: Common carriers must establish accessible complaint procedures for shippers
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Design and publish a clear, accessible complaint procedure on the company website and in carrier agreements, specifying how shippers submit complaints, expected response timeframes (7-10 business days), and escalation paths. 2. Establish a complaint intake and tracking system with a unique reference number, documentation of complaint details, carrier response, and resolution. 3. Conduct quarterly reviews of complaint trends and prepare summary reports for management and FMC if required.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Communications team: draft and publish a customer-facing complaint procedure in plain language, with multiple submission channels (email, phone, online form). Customer Service team: implement a complaint tracking system (ticketing software) with automated acknowledgment, assigned owner, and resolution deadline. Compliance team: perform quarterly analysis of complaint themes and prepare trend reports.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Ocean carriers: acknowledge receipt of all shipper complaints within 2 business days and provide substantive written response within 10 business days. Carriers must designate a primary contact for complaint handling and confirm procedures in their service agreements.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published complaint procedure document (web page screenshot, printed materials), complaint tracking system access logs and sample ticket records, completed complaint tickets showing intake date, owner assignment, carrier response, and resolution, and quarterly complaint trend analysis reports with closure metrics.

key_requirements.text: FMC has enhanced investigative powers and can initiate enforcement without complaint
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Map all FMC-regulated activities within your shipping operations and document current compliance posture across ocean shipping services, detention charges, and demurrage practices.
2. Establish a monitoring protocol to track FMC enforcement actions, guidance updates, and investigative trends; assign compliance ownership for proactive remediation.
3. Create and maintain comprehensive audit trails, transaction records, and communication logs for all FMC-regulated conduct to support rapid disclosure during unsolicited FMC inquiries.
internal_actions: Legal team: conduct FMC jurisdiction audit identifying all services and entities subject to enhanced investigative scope.
Compliance team: establish FMC regulatory intelligence monitoring and create an enforcement response playbook.
IT team: implement immutable logging and data retention systems covering all FMC-regulated transactions for minimum 3 years.
vendor_actions: Third-party logistics providers: confirm they maintain independent FMC compliance records and audit trails for shared operations.
External counsel: deliver annual memo on FMC investigative authority expansion and recommended internal control enhancements.
compliance_evidence: Documented FMC jurisdiction mapping with sign-off from Legal.
Audit trail export logs showing transaction capture and retention across all regulated service lines.
FMC monitoring protocol and enforcement response procedures (dated and version-controlled).
Annual external counsel memo on investigative powers and compliance readiness.

key_requirements.text: Shipping exchanges (non-vessel operators) must register and comply with regulations
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
how_to_meet: 1. If your organisation operates as a shipping exchange (intermediary arranging ocean transportation services without vessel ownership), confirm your business model classification and initiate FMC registration or renewal immediately.

2. Document your non-vessel operator status, service offerings, and customer relationships; establish written policies for fair access, service terms, and complaint handling aligned with FMC regulations.
3. Implement annual compliance certification process requiring sign-off from Legal and Finance confirming adherence to FMC tariff, service rules, and financial responsibility requirements.
internal_actions: Legal team: prepare FMC registration application or renewal documentation, including detailed business model description and service scope.
Compliance team: draft written operating procedures for service rules, customer transparency, tariff filing, and complaint handling; ensure alignment with OSRA provisions.
Finance team: document and certify financial responsibility mechanism (bonding, insurance, or escrow) required for non-vessel operator status.
vendor_actions: Freight forwarders and NVOs (if subcontracting): provide signed attestation confirming their own FMC registration status and regulatory compliance.
Customs brokers and service partners: confirm they will not circumvent your FMC registration obligations or misrepresent your regulatory standing.
compliance_evidence: FMC registration certificate or active renewal confirmation.
Signed written service rules and tariff documentation filed with FMC.
Certificate of financial responsibility (bonding agent or insurance policy) with current expiry date.
Annual compliance certification memo from Legal and Finance.
Customer-facing transparency documents (service agreements, complaint procedures) dated and version-controlled.

regulations.penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders

regulations.name: Corporate Transparency Act — Beneficial Ownership Information Reporting
regulations.authority: Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) — U.S. Treasury
regulations.summary: The Corporate Transparency Act requires most US domestic and foreign companies registered to do business in the US to report their beneficial owners (persons owning 25%+ or exercising substantial control) to FinCEN. Note: as of March 2025, FinCEN temporarily exempted domestic US companies pending regulatory review.

key_requirements.text: Report beneficial ownership information (BOI) to FinCEN — name, DOB, address, and ID document for each beneficial owner
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct internal audit to identify all beneficial owners meeting 25%+ equity or control threshold. 2. Collect verified personal identifiers (legal name, DOB, residential address, state-issued ID number) from each beneficial owner through formal declaration forms. 3. Submit FinCEN Form 114 with complete BOI data via the secure FinCEN reporting portal.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: provide articles of incorporation, cap table, and org chart showing ownership structure; Finance team: provide shareholder register and equity ledger; HR/Compliance: issue and collect beneficial owner declaration forms with identity verification documents.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Corporate registered agent: confirm beneficial owner information on file and provide any documentation held; if using identity verification services: obtain attestation letters confirming identity verification performed.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Completed and filed FinCEN Form 114 with submission receipt; beneficial owner declaration forms signed by each owner; copies of verified state-issued photo ID and address verification for each beneficial owner; shareholder register showing ownership percentages.

key_requirements.text: Existing companies formed before 2024 had a reporting deadline of January 1, 2025 (domestic companies currently exempt pending review)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Verify company formation date in articles of incorporation or state business registration records. 2. Determine if domestic or foreign entity status applies, checking current FinCEN guidance on domestic company exemptions. 3. Document decision rationale and maintain evidence of timely filing or exemption claim by January 1, 2025 deadline.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: confirm entity formation date and jurisdiction; Compliance: establish deadline tracking log and document exemption eligibility assessment; Finance: maintain copy of state certificate of formation or registration.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Corporate registered agent or formation service: provide certified formation documents and confirmation of entity establishment date.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Dated articles of incorporation or state certificate of formation; FinCEN Form 114 submission receipt (if filed) or documented exemption determination memo; internal compliance checklist showing deadline assessment completed.

key_requirements.text: New companies formed after 2024 must report within 30 days of formation
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a 30-day tracking process from entity formation approval by state authority. 2. Within the 30-day window, collect beneficial owner information and prepare FinCEN Form 114 submission. 3. File completed form with FinCEN and retain submission confirmation and formation date evidence.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: flag formation dates and establish calendar alerts for 30-day reporting deadlines; Compliance: create intake checklist for new entities specifying required BOI documentation; Finance: maintain formation date records in entity management system.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Formation service provider or registered agent: provide state approval notice with exact formation date and trigger immediate notification to Compliance team within 24 hours of approval.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: State certificate of formation or approval notice with date stamped; FinCEN Form 114 submission receipt showing filing date within 30 days; internal deadline tracking log with formation date and filing date documented.

key_requirements.text: Report changes to beneficial ownership or company information within 30 days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Implement a change management process requiring legal and finance teams to flag any changes to beneficial ownership (equity transfers, control shifts) or company information within 5 business days of occurrence. 2. Within 30 days, prepare amended FinCEN Form 114 with updated information. 3. File amendment with FinCEN and maintain version control of all BOI submissions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: establish change notification procedure and issue alerts upon ownership transfers or control changes; Finance: update shareholder register immediately upon transaction settlement; Compliance: maintain change log with dates and file amended Forms 114.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Corporate registered agent: notify of any address or jurisdiction changes; transfer agents (if applicable): provide notification of equity transfers within 2 business days.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Timestamped change notification log showing date change identified; original and amended FinCEN Form 114 filings with submission receipts; supporting documentation (equity transfer agreements, board resolutions, amended cap table).

key_requirements.text: Foreign companies registered to do business in the US must report
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Verify foreign entity status by reviewing state-level registration for business in the US and FinCEN guidance on reporting triggers. 2. If registered to conduct US business, collect beneficial owner information using same process as domestic entities. 3. Submit FinCEN Form 114 with foreign entity designation and parent company information.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: obtain state business registration certificates for all US jurisdictions where entity conducts business; review foreign entity status and parent company structure; Compliance: confirm reporting requirement applicability for each foreign entity.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Foreign registered agent or state filing service: provide evidence of US business registration and confirmation of jurisdictions where entity is registered to conduct business.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: State certificates of foreign business registration for each US jurisdiction; FinCEN Form 114 filed with foreign entity designation; documentation showing beneficial owner information collection and filing within applicable deadline.

key_requirements.text: Beneficial owners are individuals owning 25%+ equity interest or exercising substantial control
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define beneficial owner eligibility using FinCEN's 25%+ equity threshold or substantial control test (voting rights, board seats, officer roles, material operational control). 2. Audit cap table and organizational structure to identify all individuals meeting either criterion. 3. Document the beneficial owner determination for each identified person with supporting evidence of ownership percentage or control basis.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide detailed cap table showing ownership percentage for all shareholders; Legal team: document control indicators (board representation, executive positions, voting agreements); Compliance: prepare beneficial owner determination memo for each identified owner with threshold justification.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: None typically required; if using third-party cap table management tool: request export of current ownership structure with percentage calculations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Audited cap table with ownership percentages; beneficial owner determination matrix listing each owner with calculated equity %, control indicators, and threshold justification; board resolutions or organizational charts documenting control relationships.

key_requirements.text: Implement internal processes to identify, verify, and update beneficial ownership records
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a beneficial ownership identification workflow requiring Legal and Finance teams to document all individuals with 25%+ direct or indirect ownership stakes, cross-referenced against cap table and shareholder registry.

2. Implement a semi-annual verification process where identified beneficial owners confirm their ownership status and provide updated identity documents (government-issued ID, address proof) through a formal attestation form.
3. Create a change management protocol requiring immediate notification to Compliance when ownership thresholds are crossed, with supporting transaction documentation retained for audit trail.
internal_actions: Legal team: deliver current cap table with all registered shareholders, trust beneficiaries, and control chain documentation.
Finance team: provide bank statements and investment account records showing fund source and ownership percentages.
HR/Compliance team: maintain beneficial ownership register with dated entries, verification checklists, and attestation sign-offs from each beneficial owner.
vendor_actions: Registered agent or corporate secretary service: confirm filing of beneficial ownership updates and provide certified copies of registered agent declarations.
Third-party beneficial ownership verification service: deliver identity verification reports and KYC documentation for each identified beneficial owner.
compliance_evidence: Signed beneficial ownership certification forms from each identified owner (dated within current reporting period).
Cap table audit trail showing ownership changes with supporting transaction documents.
Beneficial ownership register extract stamped by Legal/Compliance with verification completion dates.
Identity verification reports and address confirmations for all beneficial owners on file.

key_requirements.text: Certify that reported information is accurate and complete
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Designate a single Compliance Officer or Legal representative as the certifying authority responsible for validating all beneficial ownership data against source documents before FinCEN filing.

2. Execute a formal certification protocol requiring the designated officer to review the complete beneficial ownership report line-by-line against the beneficial ownership register, cap table, and identity documentation, with documented sign-off.

3. Implement a pre-filing review checklist covering completeness (all beneficial owners included), accuracy (names, titles, ownership percentages match source records), and consistency (data aligns across all internal systems).
internal_actions: Compliance/Legal team: produce a beneficial ownership data validation matrix comparing reported names, titles, ownership percentages, and addresses against source documents (cap table, shareholder agreements, beneficial owner attestations).
Finance team: certify that ownership percentages in the report reconcile to equity records and investment account balances.

Records Management: compile a master file containing all source documents used in the certification (attestation forms, ID copies, cap table versions, corporate resolutions) with version dates and reviewer initials.
vendor_actions: Legal counsel or compliance consultant (if external): provide a certification opinion letter confirming that reported beneficial ownership information is accurate and complete based on available documentation.
Registered agent service: confirm receipt of beneficial ownership filing and provide electronic proof of submission to FinCEN with filing timestamp and tracking number.
compliance_evidence: Signed certification statement by designated officer attesting to accuracy and completeness of beneficial ownership report.
Pre-filing validation checklist completed by Compliance, signed and dated before submission.
Data reconciliation schedule showing comparison of reported data to source documents with reviewer sign-offs.
FinCEN filing receipt and timestamp confirmation from registered agent or electronic filing portal.
Master supporting documentation file with indexed reference to each beneficial owner's source verification records.

regulations.penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations

milestones.label: Beneficial ownership reporting begins (entities formed before 2024)
Treasury paused enforcement — court injunction lifted
New reporting deadline under FinCEN interim rule

regulations.name: Brazil CVM Resolution 193 — ESG Reporting (ISSB Standards)
regulations.authority: Securities and Exchange Commission of Brazil (CVM)
regulations.summary: Brazil's CVM Resolution 193 requires publicly listed companies to report on sustainability matters using ISSB Standards (IFRS S1 and S2), making Brazil an early adopter of mandatory climate and sustainability disclosure aligned with international standards. Mandatory for listed companies reporting on fiscal year 2026.

key_requirements.text: Disclose sustainability-related financial information using IFRS S1 (General Requirements)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Engage Finance and Accounting to map material sustainability costs, liabilities, and impacts to IFRS S1 core content areas (governance, strategy, risk management, metrics). 2. Establish a data consolidation process linking general ledger accounts to sustainability-related financial line items. 3. Prepare sustainability accounting policies aligned with IFRS S1 and include in the annual financial statement notes.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Finance team: provide general ledger extracts, capital expenditure schedules, and contingent liabilities register; Legal team: furnish materiality assessment documentation and ESG-related contract terms; Accounting team: deliver accounting policy drafts and financial impact reconciliations.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External auditors: confirm readiness to audit IFRS S1 disclosures and specify required working paper formats; Software providers (ERP/consolidation systems): confirm IFRS S1 reporting module availability and data field mappings.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: IFRS S1 disclosure statement with cross-references to financial statements; sustainability accounting policies document; materiality matrix linking financial amounts to ESG themes; auditor's assurance report on IFRS S1 disclosures.

key_requirements.text: Disclose climate-related risks and opportunities using IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct climate scenario analysis (1.5°C, 2°C, 4°C) across revenue-generating and capital-intensive operations to identify material climate risks and opportunities. 2. Document governance and risk management processes for climate oversight, including board committee charters and management accountability. 3. Disclose in annual report the strategy, governance, and key metrics aligned with IFRS S2 requirements (governance, strategy, risk management, metrics & targets).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Risk/Compliance team: provide climate risk register, scenario analysis workpapers, and risk assessment methodologies; Board/Governance team: furnish board and committee charters, minutes showing climate oversight discussions, and delegation matrices; Strategy team: deliver strategic plans incorporating climate transition and physical risk mitigation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Climate science/advisory consultants: provide scenario analysis reports, climate risk quantification models, and peer benchmarking; Insurance brokers: confirm coverage adequacy and provide risk transfer documentation for climate-related exposures.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: IFRS S2 climate disclosure statement; board/committee charters and approved minutes; climate scenario analysis report with assumptions; risk register with climate risks rated; strategy documentation addressing climate risks and opportunities.

key_requirements.text: Report Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions following GHG Protocol
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish organizational boundaries (equity consolidation approach) and identify Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and Scope 3 (value chain) emission sources using GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard. 2. Deploy energy, fuel, and process data collection from all facilities, suppliers, and logistics partners; implement third-party verification of calculation methodologies. 3. Publish annual GHG inventory with emission factors, calculation methods, and year-over-year comparisons in the sustainability report.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Facilities/Operations team: provide utility bills, fuel consumption records, equipment registers, and renewable energy certificates; Procurement team: collect Scope 3 supplier emission data templates and engagement logs; Finance team: allocate budget codes for carbon accounting software and third-party verification services.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Energy service providers: supply detailed consumption data in standardized formats; Logistics/transportation partners: furnish distance travelled, vehicle fleet data, and fuel consumption; Third-party verification body: provide GHG inventory assurance statement and audit workpapers; Software vendor: confirm GHG Protocol Scope 1/2/3 calculation templates and reporting outputs.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Annual GHG inventory report with detailed Scope 1/2/3 breakdowns; organizational boundaries and consolidation approach document; emission factor sources and calculation workpapers; third-party assurance/verification statement; historical trend analysis and restatement policy.

key_requirements.text: Describe governance processes for oversight of sustainability-related risks and opportunities
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define board committee or full board responsibility for sustainability/ESG oversight; document charter, meeting frequency, and key decision authorities. 2. Establish management committees (ESG steering, risk, sustainability) with clear roles, reporting lines, and escalation protocols to the board. 3. Disclose governance structure, roles, and accountability mechanisms in the annual report with evidence of active oversight (meeting minutes, approvals, performance reviews).
key_requirements.internal_actions: Board/Corporate Governance team: draft or update board/committee charters defining ESG oversight scope and authority; HR/Compensation team: incorporate ESG metrics into executive and board performance evaluations; Legal/Compliance team: document escalation protocols and decision-making authorities in governance policies.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Board advisory/governance consultants: provide governance structure review and best-practice recommendations aligned with ISSB standards; External auditors: confirm governance documentation requirements and audit scope for board oversight.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board or committee charter explicitly addressing sustainability-related risks and opportunities; org chart showing management ESG committees and reporting lines; meeting minutes documenting ESG discussions, decisions, and approvals; executive compensation policy linking ESG targets to remuneration; board member competency assessments for ESG topics.

key_requirements.text: Disclose strategy for managing climate transition and physical risks
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop climate transition strategy document addressing decarbonization pathways, capital allocation, and operational changes across 5-, 10-, and 30-year horizons aligned with net-zero or Paris Agreement targets. 2. Identify and map physical climate risks (flooding, extreme weather, supply chain disruption) by geography and facility; design adaptation and resilience measures. 3. Disclose transition and physical risk strategies in the sustainability or integrated report with financial implications and dependencies on external factors.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Strategy team: prepare transition roadmap with interim milestones, capex requirements, and technology deployment plans; Risk team: complete physical risk assessment for all operating facilities and key suppliers; Finance team: quantify transition capex, stranded assets, and cost of capital implications; Operations team: identify process improvements and energy efficiency initiatives.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Climate strategy/net-zero advisory firms: deliver transition strategy recommendations, pathway modelling, and financial impact analysis; Physical risk assessment providers: supply climate hazard mapping, facility vulnerability assessments, and adaptation cost estimates; Green finance providers: confirm financing options and ESG-linked lending terms.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Climate transition strategy document with decarbonization pathways and financial implications; physical risk assessment report by location and asset; board-approved transition roadmap and capex allocation; dependencies and assumption documentation; stakeholder engagement records informing strategy development.

key_requirements.text: Provide quantitative metrics and targets for climate performance
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define climate performance metrics (emissions intensity, renewable energy percentage, energy efficiency improvements) and establish science-based or net-zero targets with interim milestones (e.g., 2025, 2030, 2050). 2. Align targets with external frameworks (Science Based Targets initiative, net-zero alliances) and validate through third-party review. 3. Disclose metrics, targets, and year-over-year progress in sustainability report with reconciliation to base year and explanations for variance.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Sustainability/ESG team: establish metric definitions, calculation methodologies, and data governance procedures; Finance team: set capital budgets and KPI tracking systems for climate targets; Operations team: define departmental emissions reduction initiatives and accountability; Data team: implement automated metric reporting dashboards with monthly reviews.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Third-party validators (e.g., Science Based Targets initiative, Assurance providers): review and validate target-setting methodology; Data analytics platform providers: configure dashboards and automated metric calculation; External auditors: confirm audit scope for quantitative metrics and targets.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Climate metrics and targets policy document; science-based or net-zero target validation letter from third party; baseline year emissions and historical trend analysis; annual performance against targets with variance explanations; metric calculation methodologies and data audit trails; board approval of targets and annual performance reviews.

key_requirements.text: Ensure sustainability disclosures are consistent with financial statements
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a reconciliation process between sustainability metrics (e.g., energy consumption, emissions, water usage) and corresponding financial line items (e.g., utilities expense, carbon credits, environmental remediation costs) documented in a sustainability-to-financials mapping matrix.

2. Conduct quarterly cross-functional reviews between the sustainability reporting team and financial reporting team to validate that material ESG data reflected in public disclosures aligns with amounts, periods, and classifications in audited financial statements.
3. Document all adjustments, reclassifications, or exclusions made to ESG data during the reconciliation process in a formal change log maintained by the CFO's office.
internal_actions: Finance team: provide trial balance extracts, general ledger detail, and expense allocation records for all sustainability-related cost centers and accounts.
Sustainability team: deliver monthly sustainability data registers with source documentation (meter readings, invoices, operational records) and variance explanations.
Internal audit team: perform a control walkthrough and sign-off on the reconciliation methodology and frequency.
vendor_actions: External auditor: confirm in the audit planning letter that the scope includes substantive testing of material sustainability disclosures against underlying financial data.
Data providers/utilities vendors: furnish certified consumption statements and invoices that support reported sustainability metrics.
compliance_evidence: Signed sustainability-to-financials mapping matrix (updated quarterly).
Reconciliation worksheets showing line-by-line traceability from ESG metrics to financial statement accounts.
Cross-functional sign-off memo from CFO and Chief Sustainability Officer confirming consistency.
Change log documenting all adjustments with business justification and approver signature.
External auditor's audit planning letter confirming ESG disclosure scope.
Utility vendor certified statements matching reported consumption figures.

key_requirements.text: Obtain external assurance on sustainability disclosures (timeline to be defined)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Select and engage an external assurance provider (Big Four firm or specialist ESG assurance firm) qualified to perform reasonable or limited assurance over sustainability disclosures per ISAE 3000 (or equivalent) standards, and establish a formal Statement of Work defining scope, materiality thresholds, and timeline.

2. Define the assurance perimeter in writing, specifying which ESG metrics, sustainability claims, and disclosure locations (annual report, standalone sustainability report, website) will be subject to external review, and align with ISSB Standards (IFRS S1/S2) disclosure requirements.

3. Obtain and document the external assurance report (with opinion or conclusion) prior to public disclosure of sustainability information; embed any auditor-identified exceptions or management responses into the final disclosure document.
internal_actions: Sustainability team: compile complete sustainability data schedules, system reports, control documentation, and source evidence (invoices, meter readings, certifications, third-party reports) in a single assurance data room accessible to auditors.
Legal team: draft and execute the external assurance engagement letter and define disclosure restrictions or confidentiality requirements.
Finance/Internal Audit team: provide a control narrative and risk assessment identifying which sustainability data streams are highest risk and require deepest auditor focus.
vendor_actions: External assurance firm: issue a fully executed engagement letter specifying ISAE 3000 (or equivalent) standard, assurance level (reasonable or limited), materiality definition, timeline for fieldwork and reporting, and deliverable format.
External assurance firm: provide a detailed assurance plan outlining sample sizes, testing procedures, and key control evaluation points at least 30 days before fieldwork begins.
compliance_evidence: Signed external assurance engagement letter naming the assurance provider and referencing applicable standards (ISAE 3000, ISSB guidance).
Assurance provider's detailed assurance plan and materiality memorandum.
Final external assurance report containing explicit opinion or conclusion on the sustainability disclosures.
Management representation letter signed by CEO/CFO acknowledging responsibility for ESG data accuracy.
Auditor's communication letter detailing any exceptions, misstatements identified, or management responses.
Evidence of corrective actions taken on any auditor findings before final disclosure publication.
Timeline attestation confirming assurance completion prior to regulatory filing deadline.

regulations.penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact

regulations.name: Hong Kong International Convention for Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships (HKC)
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: The Hong Kong International Convention (2009) establishes safe and environmentally sound requirements for the recycling of ships at end-of-life. Entered into force June 26, 2025, it requires ships to carry an Inventory of Hazardous Materials (IHM), obtain certification, and be recycled only at approved facilities listed by their flag state.

key_requirements.text: Ships must maintain and certify an Inventory of Hazardous Materials (IHM) — Part I for new ships, Parts II and III for recycling
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a comprehensive material audit of the vessel to identify all hazardous substances present in structure, equipment, and systems. 2. Engage a certified surveyor to verify and certify the Inventory of Hazardous Materials (IHM) in accordance with HKC Part I. 3. Maintain the certified IHM document and ensure it is updated throughout the ship's operational life and referenced during recycling operations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: document the appointment of certified surveyor and maintain records of IHM certification. IT team: establish secure storage and version control for all IHM documentation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Surveyor/Classification Society: provide signed certification of IHM completion and detailed hazardous materials inventory report with material locations and quantities.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Certified IHM document (Parts I, II, or III as applicable), surveyor's certification letter, material audit report, update logs showing document maintenance throughout vessel lifecycle.

key_requirements.text: Flag states must authorise ship recycling facilities and maintain a national list
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. As a flag state, establish formal authorisation criteria aligned with HKC standards for ship recycling facilities seeking national list inclusion. 2. Conduct rigorous pre-approval inspections and audits of applying facilities against mandatory safety and environmental standards. 3. Maintain and publish an updated national register of authorised recycling facilities with periodic re-certification cycles.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft authorisation procedure, standards, and criteria documents. Regulatory Affairs team: maintain centralised database of approved facilities with inspection dates and certification status. Internal Audit: schedule and document annual compliance reviews of listed facilities.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Recycling Facility applicants: submit completed application forms, facility plans, safety management systems, environmental compliance documentation, and worker training records for review.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Published national list of authorised recycling facilities, facility approval letters with reference numbers, inspection reports and checklists, signed facility certifications with validity dates, audit trail of approvals and rejections.

key_requirements.text: Ship Recycling Facilities must maintain a Ship Recycling Facility Plan (SRFP)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop a comprehensive Ship Recycling Facility Plan (SRFP) covering waste management, worker safety, environmental protection, and hazardous material handling procedures. 2. Document operational procedures, emergency response protocols, and environmental controls for each stage of ship recycling. 3. Ensure the SRFP is approved by the flag state authority and made available to incoming vessel operators prior to recycling commencement.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: draft detailed SRFP with all operational procedures and hazard mitigation controls. Health & Safety team: specify worker protection measures and emergency response protocols. Quality Assurance: schedule annual SRFP reviews and update logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Not applicable — facility must develop internally; however, flag state authority must formally approve the SRFP in writing before operations commence.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed and dated SRFP document, flag state approval letter, facility layout diagrams, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for each recycling phase, amendment history log, approval expiration date and re-certification schedule.

key_requirements.text: Issue a Statement of Completion from the recycling facility upon completion
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Upon completion of ship recycling, conduct a final compliance verification to confirm all hazardous materials have been appropriately handled and waste streams processed. 2. Issue a signed Statement of Completion (SoC) documenting the date of completion, final waste inventory, and confirmation of regulatory compliance. 3. Provide the original SoC to the ship owner and submit a copy to the flag state authority for regulatory records.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Operations team: compile completion checklist and final waste documentation. Quality Assurance: verify final inspection against original IHM and SRFP requirements. Finance team: reconcile material disposal records and waste management invoices.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Waste management contractors: provide certificates of final disposal or recycling for all waste streams generated during ship recycling.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed Statement of Completion document with completion date, final waste inventory report, flag state receipt confirmation, waste disposal certificates from third-party handlers, photographic documentation of facility post-recycling, final inspection checklist signed by authorised representative.

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on use of hazardous materials listed in Annex I (asbestos, PCBs, ozone-depleting substances, etc.)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a formal prohibition policy naming all hazardous materials listed in HKC Annex I (asbestos, PCBs, ozone-depleting substances, heavy metals above threshold levels, etc.) and ban their use in facility operations and incoming ship materials. 2. Implement material intake inspection procedures to detect and reject any incoming ship components containing listed substances. 3. Document all detected hazardous materials and maintain records of proper disposal or isolation.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Procurement team: update purchasing specifications to exclude all Annex I substances. Compliance team: create and maintain a reference guide of prohibited materials for staff awareness. Receiving/QA team: establish material intake inspection logs.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Material suppliers and recycling contractors: confirm in writing that supplied materials and services do not involve Annex I hazardous substances; provide Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) where applicable.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Formalised Annex I prohibition policy, material intake inspection checklist and logs, rejected material documentation with disposal records, supplier confirmation letters stating non-use of prohibited substances, MSDS files for all materials handled, inspection photographic evidence.

key_requirements.text: Workers at recycling facilities must receive hazardous materials training and PPE
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Develop and deliver mandatory hazardous materials training programme covering identification, handling, health risks, and emergency response to all workers at the recycling facility. 2. Maintain training records with documented attendance, assessment scores, and certification dates for each worker. 3. Provide Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) appropriate to identified hazards and conduct periodic PPE fit-testing and replacement cycles.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: maintain training attendance records and certification database. Health & Safety team: conduct quarterly training refreshers and maintain attendance logs. Finance team: budget for PPE procurement and replacement cycles.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Training provider: deliver HKC-compliant hazardous materials training and issue certificates; PPE supplier: provide health and safety data sheets, fit-testing services, and replacement equipment on schedule.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Training course curriculum aligned to HKC requirements, signed worker training certificates with dates and trainer names, training attendance registers, worker assessment scores, PPE inventory and issuance records, fit-testing reports, incident logs demonstrating proper training application, annual training schedule and completion reports.

key_requirements.text: Vessels must obtain Ready for Recycling Certificate before entering a recycling facility
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Establish a vessel pre-recycling audit procedure that verifies hazardous material inventory (asbestos, PCBs, heavy metals) complies with HKC Annex standards before facility entry.
2. Maintain a register of all vessels entering recycling facilities with their corresponding Ready for Recycling Certificate (RfRC) reference numbers and issue dates.
3. Implement gate controls at recycling facilities to block vessel entry unless RfRC is electronically validated against the IMO Ship Recycling Information System (SRIS).
internal_actions: Fleet Management/Operations team: provide vessel manifests, hazardous material audit reports, and RfRC copies for each vessel scheduled for recycling.
IT team: configure automated alerts when vessels lack valid RfRC documentation and enable SRIS system access for certificate validation.
Legal team: prepare template documentation for RfRC applications and maintain signed declarations from recycling facilities confirming certificate receipt.
vendor_actions: Certified Surveyor: deliver signed Ready for Recycling Certificate with vessel-specific hazardous material inventory listing and remediation confirmations.
Recycling Facility: provide written gate acceptance log confirming RfRC review prior to vessel entry and return a stamped copy to the vessel owner.
compliance_evidence: Original or certified Ready for Recycling Certificates (one per vessel) with surveyor credentials and vessel identifiers.
Gate entry logs from recycling facilities showing RfRC number, certificate validation date, and facility authorised signatory.
Vessel recycling register excerpt showing RfRC cross-references for all vessels entered during the audit period.
SRIS transaction records or screenshots showing successful certificate validation attempts for each vessel.

key_requirements.text: Flag states must report annually on ship recycling activities to IMO
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
how_to_meet: 1. Compile annual ship recycling activity data (vessel names, IMO numbers, gross tonnage, recycling facility used, hazardous materials quantities) from all vessels flying your flag that entered recycling facilities.
2. Cross-validate vessel recycling records against Port State Control (PSC) data and flag state ship registry to ensure no under-reporting.
3. Submit the completed IMO Annual Report on Ship Recycling (Form IORS) to the flag state maritime authority by the required deadline for onward transmission to IMO.
internal_actions: Ship Registry/Flag State Liaison team: extract list of all flagged vessels that entered recycling during the reporting year with their current registration status.
Fleet Management/Operations team: consolidate hazardous material inventories, RfRC certificates, and recycling facility confirmations for each vessel.
Finance/Compliance team: prepare signed attestation certifying the completeness and accuracy of reported data and retain working papers for audit trail.
vendor_actions: Recycling Facilities: provide written confirmation of vessel receipts with IMO number, gross tonnage, reception facility capacity status, and any hazardous material non-compliance observations.
Certified Surveyors: supply final RfRC data sheets and post-recycling completion certificates (if applicable) to support annual activity reporting.
compliance_evidence: Signed IMO Annual Report on Ship Recycling (IORS form) submitted to flag state with timestamp of receipt acknowledgement.
Supporting schedules listing vessel IMO numbers, names, gross tonnage, recycling facility name, and date of entry for each vessel reported.
Flag state maritime authority confirmation letter showing report receipt and IMO forwarding reference number.
Internal sign-off memo from Fleet Operations and Compliance confirming data completeness and accuracy for the reported period.

regulations.penalties: Flag state penalties and detention of non-compliant ships; facility certification withdrawal

regulations.name: South Korea Serious Accidents Punishment Act (SAPA)
regulations.authority: Ministry of Employment and Labor (MOEL)
regulations.summary: South Korea's Serious Accidents Punishment Act criminalises fatal industrial accidents and serious occupational diseases resulting from management failures. CEOs and management may face personal imprisonment. Applies to workplaces with 50+ employees and all public transportation and multi-use facilities.

key_requirements.text: Implement a workplace safety and health management system as the responsible business operator
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Appoint a designated safety and health manager (or committee if required by operation scale) with documented authority and reporting lines to senior management. 2. Establish a written Occupational Safety and Health Management System (OSHMS) framework aligned with Korean industrial safety standards. 3. Obtain Board approval and sign-off on the system charter, designating the responsible business operator.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft OSHMS charter and governance structure; HR team: provide organizational chart showing safety manager appointment and escalation paths; Finance team: confirm budget allocation for safety function.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Occupational health and safety consultant: provide written OSHMS design document or certification audit report confirming system alignment with Korean regulations.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board meeting minutes approving OSHMS; signed appointment letter for safety manager; OSHMS policy document with version control and approval dates; organizational chart showing safety function reporting structure.

key_requirements.text: Establish and maintain documented safety and health management goals and execution plans
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Document specific, measurable safety and health goals (e.g., accident rate reduction targets, hazard elimination timelines) aligned with business operations. 2. Create and maintain detailed execution plans identifying responsible parties, resource requirements, and completion milestones for each goal. 3. Establish a review schedule (minimum quarterly) with documented updates tracking progress and corrective actions.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Safety manager: prepare signed safety and health goals document with baseline metrics; Operations team: provide execution plans with task owners and timelines; Finance team: confirm allocated resources and budget per plan.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: External safety auditor or ISO 45001 consultant: provide third-party validation that goals and plans are appropriate for the organization's risk profile.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed safety and health goals statement with performance metrics; detailed execution plans (Gantt charts or project schedules); quarterly review meeting minutes with progress tracking; resource allocation documentation (budget, headcount, equipment).

key_requirements.text: Secure adequate budget and personnel for safety management proportionate to operations
key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a proportionality analysis documenting the number of employees, hazard levels, and operational scope to establish baseline safety personnel and budget requirements. 2. Allocate dedicated headcount (safety officer, inspectors, coordinators as applicable) and secure multi-year budget approval from Finance. 3. Document the allocation decision in Board minutes or executive approval memo with explicit linkage to operational risk.
key_requirements.internal_actions: HR team: provide current headcount, job descriptions, and salary costs for safety roles; Finance team: provide approved budget allocation for safety personnel, training, equipment, and audit costs; Operations team: document hazard profile and operational complexity justifying resource level.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Industry safety benchmarking firm: provide report comparing allocated resources to peer organizations of similar size and risk; occupational health consultant: confirm adequacy of proposed staffing model in writing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Board or executive approval memo authorizing safety budget and headcount; proportionality analysis memo linking operations to resource requirements; headcount roster showing safety personnel and reporting lines; annual safety budget line item approval from Finance; copy of safety budget vs. actual spend reports.

key_requirements.text: Conduct regular workplace inspections and address identified safety hazards
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Establish a documented inspection schedule (e.g., monthly facility walkthroughs, quarterly departmental reviews, annual comprehensive audits) with assignment of responsibility. 2. Use a standardized inspection checklist aligned with Korean safety regulations and organization-specific hazards; document findings with photographic evidence where applicable. 3. Create a formal hazard log with remediation timelines, assigned owners, and sign-off upon completion; report status monthly to management.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Safety manager: prepare inspection schedule and checklist templates; Operations/Facilities team: execute inspections and provide completed checklists with findings; IT team: maintain centralized hazard log database with status tracking and audit trail.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Occupational safety consultant: design or validate inspection checklist against Korean KOSHA standards; external auditor: conduct independent quarterly or annual safety audit with written report identifying gaps.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed inspection schedule and checklist templates; completed inspection forms (dated, signed by inspector); photographs of hazards and corrective actions; centralized hazard log extract showing hazard ID, date identified, remediation due date, owner, and closure date; management review minutes discussing inspection findings; records of hazard remediation (work orders, completion photos, sign-offs).

key_requirements.text: Immediately report serious industrial accidents to Ministry of Employment and Labor
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Define 'serious accident' according to Korean SAPA (death, permanent disability, hospitalization ≥3 days, or multiple injuries) in a written incident classification procedure. 2. Establish a 24-hour incident notification protocol specifying the Ministry of Employment and Labor contact method, required information, and responsible parties. 3. Designate a document control owner to maintain a register of all reported incidents with confirmation receipts from the Ministry.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Legal team: draft incident classification procedure aligned with SAPA definitions; HR/Safety team: provide 24-hour escalation contact list and notification template; IT team: establish incident reporting system with timestamp logging and Ministry submission audit trail.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Korean labor law consultant: provide written guidance on incident classification and Ministry reporting requirements; occupational health provider: confirm incident severity assessment process in writing.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed incident classification procedure document; incident notification template; 24-hour escalation contact list (approved and dated); incident register (register of all reported serious accidents with Ministry case numbers); Ministry of Employment and Labor confirmation receipts or acknowledgment emails; incident report copies submitted to Ministry with timestamps; internal incident escalation logs showing time from discovery to Ministry notification.

key_requirements.text: Take immediate protective measures when a serious accident occurs to prevent recurrence
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Upon occurrence of a serious accident, immediately execute a written 'stop-work' or containment order and deploy the pre-established emergency response team to secure the affected area. 2. Conduct a formal root-cause investigation within 7 days, documenting the sequence of events, contributing factors, and corrective actions required to prevent recurrence. 3. Implement and verify corrective actions (engineering controls, procedural changes, training) with completion sign-off and evidence of effectiveness before resuming normal operations.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Safety manager: activate incident response protocol and document containment actions; Operations team: execute work stoppage in affected area; Investigations team: complete root-cause analysis report (5-why analysis or fault tree); HR team: deliver retraining to affected personnel upon corrective action implementation.
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Occupational safety consultant: conduct independent root-cause investigation or peer-review the internal investigation; engineering firm: assess and design engineering control measures if required; training provider: deliver incident-specific safety training with attendance roster.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Timestamped incident containment order and photographs; emergency response team activation log; root-cause investigation report (signed, dated) with findings and corrective action plan; list of corrective actions with assigned owners and due dates; photographs or work orders showing implementation of controls; completion sign-off from Safety manager; post-corrective-action effectiveness verification (re-inspection, testing, or follow-up audit); Ministry of Employment and Labor follow-up correspondence acknowledging corrective actions.

key_requirements.text: Implement remediation measures for identified safety risks within specified timelines
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis
key_requirements.how_to_meet: 1. Conduct a documented risk assessment identifying all safety hazards with severity ratings and regulatory deadlines per SAPA timeline requirements. 2. Assign ownership and budget to each remediation action with target completion dates, tracked in a centralized remediation register. 3. Execute corrective actions and validate closure through independent verification (audit or third-party assessment) before deadline expiry.
key_requirements.internal_actions: Health & Safety team: provide completed risk assessment matrix with identified hazards, risk ratings, and assigned owners. Finance team: confirm budget allocation and expenditure tracking for each remediation item. Operations team: deliver remediation completion reports with evidence of corrective implementation (photographs, test results, certifications).
key_requirements.vendor_actions: Safety consultants or equipment suppliers: provide corrective action quotations and timelines, and issue completion certificates or inspection reports upon remediation delivery. Equipment vendors: supply maintenance records and safety compliance documentation for any third-party systems being remediated.
key_requirements.compliance_evidence: Signed risk assessment report with SAPA-aligned timelines; remediation register with start/end dates and owner signatures; photographic evidence or inspection reports of completed corrections; budget allocation memos; completion sign-off from Health & Safety leadership; independent auditor verification report (if applicable).

key_requirements.text: Maintain records of safety management activities and accident response
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: CEOs and responsible managers: up to 1 year imprisonment (mandatory) and fines up to KRW 1 billion for fatal accidents; company fines up to KRW 5 billion.
